Tarte à la tomate is a French savory tart made with a pastry crust filled with a mixture of tomatoes, onions, and herbs, typically baked until the crust is golden and the filling is softened. It is commonly served as a first course or light lunch dish, either warm or at room temperature.

Mouri potol is a Bengali vegetable dish in which pointed gourd (potol) is cooked with fennel seeds (mouri), along with spices such as turmeric, ginger, and green chilies in mustard oil. It is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Khoslar bora is a Bengali fritter typically prepared by grinding a main ingredient into a paste or batter, shaping it into small rounds, and deep-frying until golden and crisp. It is commonly served as a side dish or snack alongside rice and dal, often accompanied by chutney or a wedge of lime.

Mulo fuler bora are fritters made from radish flowers (mulo phul), which are coated in a spiced chickpea flour or rice flour batter and deep-fried until crisp. They are a traditional Bengali preparation typically served as a snack or side dish, making use of the edible blossoms of the white radish plant.

Kulekhara patar bora are fritters made by coating leaves of the kulekhara plant (Hygrophila auriculata) in a spiced chickpea flour batter and deep-frying them until crisp. They are commonly served as a snack or side dish in Bengali cuisine, often accompanied by rice or eaten on their own.

Ofe Ujuju is a Nigerian soup originating from the Igbo people, prepared with ujuju leaves (Myrianthus arboreus), fish, and meat, seasoned with table salt and black pepper. It is typically served as a main dish accompanied by a starchy staple such as fufu or pounded yam.

Muger Jilipi is a Bangladeshi sweet made from a batter of ground mung bean paste and flour, which is piped into spiral shapes, deep-fried until crisp, and then soaked in sugar syrup. It is typically served as a dessert or festive snack, commonly associated with occasions such as Eid, weddings, and other celebrations in Bangladesh.

Shiuli patar bora is a Bengali fritter made by coating the leaves of the Shiuli (night-jasmine, Nyctanthes arbor-tristis) plant in a seasoned gram flour (besan) batter and deep-frying them until crisp. The dish is typically served as a snack or appetizer, often accompanied by mustard-based or green chutney.

Oler bora are fritters made from taro stem ( ol or elephant foot yam stem) that is chopped, mixed with spices such as turmeric, green chili, and mustard, then shaped into small patties and shallow- or deep-fried. They are a traditional Bengali snack or side dish, typically served with rice or as an accompaniment to a main meal.

Gaudes is a traditional French cornmeal porridge made by cooking cornmeal with milk and butter until thickened, typically seasoned with salt and sometimes sugar. It is served as a warm side dish or dessert, often accompanied by fresh fruit or eaten plain as comfort food.

Westfälisches Blindhuhn is a traditional German stew from Westphalia made with potatoes, beans, and bacon or salt pork, cooked together until tender. The dish is typically served as a hearty main course, often accompanied by bread or additional vegetables, and represents regional peasant cuisine from western Germany.

Uchchhe patar bora is a Bengali fritter made by coating young bitter melon leaves in a spiced gram flour batter and deep-frying until crispy. The dish is typically served as a snack or appetizer, often accompanied by chutney or as part of a Bengali meal.

Kumro bijer bora is a Bengali fritter made from roasted pumpkin seeds (kumro bij) that are ground into a paste, mixed with spices and gram flour, then deep-fried until golden. The dish is typically served as a savory snack or appetizer, often accompanying rice-based meals or eaten on its own as street food.

Kachki machher bora is a Bengali appetizer made from raw fish that is marinated in spices and then deep-fried until crispy. The dish is typically served as a starter or snack, often accompanied by lime wedges and green chutney.

Barbajuan is a traditional Monégasque fried pastry filled with chard, Swiss chard, spinach, or other leafy greens mixed with meat, cheese, and herbs. The pastry parcels are deep-fried until golden and crispy, and are typically served as an appetizer or snack in Monaco and the surrounding Liguria region.

Thieboudienne is a Senegalese rice dish prepared by cooking rice in fish broth with vegetables such as carrots, cabbage, and eggplant, typically layered with fried fish. The dish is traditionally served as a complete meal, with the fish and vegetables arranged on top of the seasoned rice.

Shkmeruli is a Georgian chicken dish prepared by braising chicken pieces in a sauce made with garlic, fresh cilantro, and sometimes white wine or vinegar, then finished with melted cheese that creates a creamy consistency. The dish is traditionally served hot as a main course, often with fresh bread or rice to accompany the flavorful sauce.

Mertoxe is a traditional Kurdish dish prepared by combining flour with lard and cooking the mixture until thickened, then topped with kaymak (clotted cream). It is typically served as a hearty breakfast or accompaniment, especially during colder months or festive gatherings.

Cholera is a traditional Swiss savory pie from the Valais region, made with a shortcrust or puff pastry shell filled with layers of potatoes, leeks, onions, cheese (typically Gruyère or Raclette), and sometimes apple or bacon. It is baked until the filling is cooked through and the pastry is golden, and is commonly served as a main course, particularly during winter months.

Lychee pork is a Chinese dish in which pieces of pork, typically belly or shoulder, are deep-fried and then coated in a sweet and sour sauce alongside lychee fruit, giving the dish its characteristic flavor and name. It is commonly served with steamed rice and is associated with Cantonese cuisine, where the combination of fruit with meat in sweet-savory preparations is traditional.

Shaplar vela bhaja is a Bengali vegetable fritter made from the stems of the water lily (shapla), which are sliced, coated in a spiced batter of gram or rice flour with turmeric, salt, and chili, and shallow-fried until crisp. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice and dal in traditional Bengali meals.

Kitawa is typically prepared as a soft, pancake-like or fritter-style dish made from a batter of mashed banana, milk, and baking powder, often pan-fried or griddled until golden. It is commonly served warm as a breakfast item or light snack, sometimes accompanied by honey, syrup, or fresh fruit.

Kupat Sumpil is an Indonesian dish made from glutinous rice packed into a triangular or conical wrap formed from young coconut leaves or bamboo leaves, then boiled until cooked through. It is traditionally served during Islamic celebrations, particularly around Eid al-Fitr, and is typically accompanied by grated coconut or savory sauces.

Quanta firfir is an Ethiopian dish made by crumbling injera bread and mixing it with a spiced sauce of berbere, dried meat, and tomato. It is traditionally served as a communal dish, often eaten for breakfast or lunch, with the ingredients combined directly on a plate or platter.

Şirdan is a traditional Turkish dish made from minced meat and onions that are typically stuffed into intestines or a casing and then cooked by boiling or grilling. It is commonly served as street food or a main course, often accompanied by bread and vegetables.

Ote ote is an Indonesian street food consisting of vegetables and other ingredients coated in wheat flour batter and deep-fried until golden. The fritters are typically served as a snack or appetizer, often accompanied by a sweet and spicy dipping sauce.

Pisang ijo is a South Sulawesi dessert made from green bananas that are wrapped in a green rice flour batter and steamed until cooked through. The dish is typically served warm or at room temperature, often accompanied by a sweet palm sugar syrup or coconut milk sauce for dipping.

Lembarang is a Javanese dish made by simmering chicken with coconut milk, shallots, and onions until tender and flavorful. It is typically served as a main course with rice as part of traditional Javanese meals.

Kumpir is a Turkish baked potato dish prepared by splitting a large potato and filling it with various toppings such as butter, cheese, vegetables, and sometimes meat or legumes. It is typically served hot as a street food or casual meal, often accompanied by additional toppings that diners can customize according to preference.

Strapačky is a Slovak potato dish made by mixing grated or shredded potatoes with halušky (potato dumplings) and typically binding them with eggs before pan-frying until golden. The dish is traditionally served as a main course, often topped with sour cream and crispy bacon or fried onions.

Eggplant dolma turşusu consists of eggplants that are hollowed out and stuffed with a mixture of rice, herbs, and spices, then pickled in vinegar-based brine. The dish is served cold as a meze or appetizer, typically accompanied by bread.

Komplet lepinja is a Serbian flatbread sandwich made by filling a round lepinja bread with kajmak (a dairy spread similar to clotted cream) and a fried egg. It is typically served warm as a breakfast or light meal item.

Roti sai mai is a Thai dessert made from roti dough that is fried until crispy and then coated with a mixture of condensed milk and sugar, creating a sweet and crispy treat. It is typically served warm as a street food or dessert, often enjoyed with tea or coffee.

Scagliozzi are fried polenta fritters originating from Naples, made by slicing cooled, solidified polenta into rectangular or triangular pieces and deep-frying them in oil until golden and crisp. They are typically served as street food or a snack, often lightly salted, and are a common fixture of Neapolitan fried food traditions.

Ol Kochur Dhokla is a Bengali vegetable dish prepared by cooking elephant foot yam (ol) and taro (kochu) with spices such as turmeric, cumin, and mustard oil, often thickened to a dry or semi-dry consistency. It is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Mazzerelle is a traditional dish from the Teramo area of Abruzzo, Italy, consisting of lamb offal (typically liver, lung, and heart) wrapped in young lamb intestines along with wild herbs such as marjoram and parsley, then tied and braised or roasted. It is commonly prepared during Easter celebrations and served as a main course, reflecting the region's pastoral culinary traditions.

Three Pies is a traditional Ossetian dish consisting of three individual savory pies typically filled with different ingredients such as cheese and herbs, potato, or meat, baked until golden. The pies are usually served warm as a main course or substantial appetizer in Ossetian cuisine.

Ekmek kadayıfı is a Turkish dessert made by layering shredded phyllo dough (kadayıf) with cream and baking until golden, then soaking in sugar syrup. It is typically served warm with whipped cream or vanilla ice cream on top and garnished with nuts.

The B.C. roll is a sushi roll typically filled with imitation crab, avocado, and cucumber, wrapped in nori and rice with the rice on the outside. It is served chilled, usually cut into six to eight pieces and accompanied by soy sauce, wasabi, and pickled ginger.

Tandyr kebab is a Uyghur dish consisting of marinated meat cooked in a tandoor, a traditional clay oven, until charred on the outside while remaining tender inside. The meat is typically served with flatbread and accompanied by onions and vinegar as condiments.

Amala is a traditional Yoruba staple made by mixing cassava flour or yam flour with hot water or broth to form a smooth, dough-like paste. It is typically served alongside soups such as ewedu or gbegiri soup, with meat or fish added for protein.

Şekerpare is a Turkish pastry made from semolina dough mixed with melted butter, yogurt, and baking powder, shaped into small balls or mounds and typically topped with a whole clove or almond before baking until golden. The baked pastries are immediately soaked in ice-cold sugar syrup, creating a sweet, moist confection commonly served as a dessert or with tea and coffee.

aliases: gnocchi in the style of sorrento
description: Gnocchi alla sorrentina consists of potato gnocchi layered with tomato sauce, mozzarella, and basil, then baked until the cheese melts. This Southern Italian dish is typically served hot as a main course, originating from the Campania region.

Caldoso is a Spanish rice dish cooked in a broth that results in a creamy, soupy consistency, typically prepared with seafood, meat, or vegetables mixed throughout the rice. The dish is served hot in bowls and commonly features saffron-infused broth, creating a dish that falls between paella and risotto in texture.

Nävgröt is a Swedish porridge made by mixing flour with melted butter and then adding milk or broth, creating a creamy paste-like consistency. It is traditionally served hot as a comforting main dish or side, often accompanied by lingonberry jam, pickled cucumbers, or cured meats.

Pane frattau is a Sardinian dish made by layering crispy sheets of pane carasau (thin flatbread) with tomato sauce and cheese, then baking until heated through. It is traditionally served as a first course or light main dish in Sardinian cuisine.

Parrozzo is an Italian almond cake from the Abruzzo region made with ground almonds, sugar, eggs, and flour, typically shaped into a dome and coated with white royal icing. The cake is traditionally served as a dessert or sweet treat, often during festive occasions and holidays.

Sardenaira is a traditional Ligurian focaccia topped with tomatoes, anchovies, olives, and garlic, then drizzled with olive oil before baking. This savory flatbread is typically served as an appetizer or light meal in Italian coastal regions.

Lawar is a traditional Balinese dish made by finely chopping or mincing raw or cooked meat (typically pork, chicken, or duck), then mixing it with grated coconut, spices, and a paste made from chilies, garlic, shallots, and shrimp paste. The mixture is bound together with coconut milk and typically includes chopped herbs and sometimes raw blood, then served as a side dish or appetizer, often at ceremonial meals and celebrations.

aliases: aloo shim begun bori diye motor shaker torkari
description: Motor shaker charchari is a Bengali vegetable dish prepared by stir-cooking fresh pea pods (motor shaker) with mustard oil, panch phoron or nigella seeds, green chilies, and turmeric until the pods are tender and lightly dry. It is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice and dal as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Cimpa is a traditional Karo Batak cake from North Sumatra, Indonesia, made from glutinous rice flour filled with a sweetened grated coconut and palm sugar mixture, then wrapped in turmeric-tinted young sugar palm leaves before being steamed or grilled. It is commonly prepared and served during traditional Karo ceremonies, harvest festivals, and cultural celebrations.

Cushta is a traditional Nicaraguan dish typically prepared with cocoa beans, often roasted and ground as part of the preparation process, reflecting the indigenous culinary heritage of the region. It is commonly served as a rustic regional specialty, sometimes accompanied by local staples such as corn-based sides or beverages, and is encountered mainly in areas where traditional Nicaraguan cooking is preserved.

Bata machher vapa is a Bengali fish preparation where fish is wrapped in banana leaves with spices and steamed or baked until cooked through. The dish typically features freshwater fish seasoned with mustard paste, turmeric, and green chilies, served as a traditional Bengali main course.

Sichenyky are Ukrainian cutlets made from finely chopped or ground meat or fish, bound with egg, shaped into flat oval patties, and pan-fried or baked until cooked through. They are typically served as a main course, often accompanied by side dishes such as mashed potatoes, buckwheat, or vegetables.

Achma is a traditional Abkhazian layered pastry dish made by alternating thin sheets of dough with melted cheese, typically produced by stretching dough very thin and cooking it on a griddle or in an oven until golden. The dish is served warm, cut into portions, and often accompanied by additional melted butter or cheese on the side.

Urap is an Indonesian salad made by mixing blanched or lightly cooked vegetables such as bean sprouts, cabbage, and long beans with a paste of grated coconut, garlic, chilies, and shrimp paste, then tossing everything together until well combined. It is typically served as a side dish or light appetizer at room temperature, often accompanying rice-based meals in Indonesian cuisine.

Pasta mollicata is a Sicilian pasta dish prepared with a topping of toasted breadcrumbs mixed with garlic, anchovies, and olive oil, sometimes including grated cheese and herbs. The dish is typically served as a primo (first course) and represents a traditional preparation from Sicily that uses simple, pantry-stable ingredients.

Carne salada is a traditional Italian cured meat made from beef that is salted and aged for several weeks to develop its characteristic flavor and texture. It is typically sliced thin and served cold as an antipasto or appetizer, often accompanied by bread, cheese, or pickled vegetables.

Cipollata is an Italian sausage dish made with ground pork, cheese, and spices, traditionally formed into links and cooked by grilling, frying, or roasting until browned. It is typically served as a main course or appetizer, often accompanied by bread, vegetables, or as part of a mixed meat platter.

Geplak is an Indonesian sweet made by cooking grated coconut with brown sugar and spices until the mixture hardens, then cut into small pieces or diamond shapes. It is traditionally served as a snack or dessert and is particularly popular in Central Java.

Enfrijoladas are Mexican tortillas dipped in a bean sauce made from cooked and blended Phaseolus vulgaris (pinto or black beans) and topped with cheese and cream. The dish is typically served as a main course, often accompanied by additional toppings such as onions and sour cream.

Casado is a traditional Central American meal consisting of a plate that typically includes grilled or fried meat, beans (Phaseolus vulgaris), rice, salad, and a tortilla, served as a complete lunch or dinner. The components are arranged on a single plate and represent a balanced, everyday meal common throughout Costa Rica and neighboring countries.

Tah Chin is an Iranian rice dish where parboiled rice is layered with meat (typically chicken or lamb), yogurt, and saffron in a pot, then cooked until a golden crust forms on the bottom. The dish is inverted onto a plate for serving, presenting the crispy rice layer on top, and is typically accompanied by butter and fresh herbs.

Spam musubi consists of a slice of grilled or fried Spam placed on top of a block of seasoned sushi rice, typically wrapped with a band of nori (seaweed). It is a popular Hawaiian-Japanese fusion dish commonly served as a handheld snack or appetizer, often accompanied by soy sauce or sriracha for dipping.

Suimono is a clear Japanese soup made by simmering dashi (fish and kelp stock) with minimal seasonings such as salt or soy sauce, typically garnished with seasonal vegetables, mushrooms, and occasionally seafood or tofu. It is traditionally served in individual bowls as part of a formal Japanese meal, often following the sashimi course.

Chanchamito is a Guatemalan tamale made from masa (corn dough) mixed with recado (a seasoned tomato-based sauce) and filled with small pieces of pork or chicken, then wrapped in corn husks and steamed until cooked through. It is smaller than a standard tamale and is commonly served as a street food or snack, often accompanied by salsa or chili sauce.

Draw soup is a viscous Yoruba soup made with okra or other mucilaginous ingredients such as ogbono (wild mango seeds) or iru (locust beans), combined with leafy vegetables, palm oil, and various proteins including fish, meat, or stockfish. It is typically served over pounded yam or other starchy swallows, with its characteristic slippery, stretchy texture achieved through the natural thickening properties of its key ingredients.

Bánh bèo are small, delicate steamed rice cakes topped with a savory shrimp and pork topping, crispy fried shallots, and a drizzle of fish sauce-based sauce. These bite-sized cakes are typically served as a snack or appetizer and eaten with the provided spoon.

Bánh tét is a cylindrical glutinous rice cake filled with mung beans and pork, wrapped tightly in banana leaves and boiled until cooked through. It is traditionally served sliced during Tet (Lunar New Year) celebrations and other special occasions in Vietnamese cuisine.

Biscuits and gravy consists of warm, fluffy baked biscuits served with a savory gravy made from meat drippings, flour, and milk or cream, typically thickened and seasoned with salt and pepper. This dish is commonly served as a breakfast or brunch item, with the gravy ladled over split or whole biscuits.

Flama bread is a Spanish family of soft wheat breads made from flour, water, yeast, and salt, shaped into small elongated loaves and baked until the crust is thin and lightly golden. It is commonly served sliced for sandwiches (bocadillos) or as an accompaniment to meals in Spanish bars and cafés.

Cơm hến is a Vietnamese rice dish made with small clams (hến) that are typically stir-fried with aromatics like shallots, garlic, and dill before being mixed into cooked rice. The dish is commonly served as a casual meal or street food, often accompanied by fresh herbs and a savory broth made from the clam cooking liquid.

Gigandes plaki is a Greek dish of large lima beans baked in tomato sauce with onions, garlic, and olive oil until tender. The beans are typically served warm as a main course or side dish, often accompanied by feta cheese and crusty bread.

Meatballs are seasoned ground meat formed into balls, typically browned in a pan or oven, then simmered in broth or sauce until cooked through. They are traditionally served over fluffy cooked rice, making for a complete protein-and-carbohydrate dish common across many cuisines.

Arroz con bogavante is a seafood rice dish made by cooking rice with European lobster (Homarus gammarus), typically prepared in a saffron-infused broth with vegetables and seafood stock. The dish is traditionally served as a main course in Spanish and Mediterranean cuisine, with the lobster pieces arranged on top of or mixed throughout the rice.

Chocolate marquise is a frozen or chilled dessert made from a mousse-like preparation of dark chocolate, butter, and eggs, typically set in a terrine mold and sliced into portions. It is served cold, often accompanied by whipped cream or a fruit coulis, and originated in French cuisine as an elegant plated dessert.

City chicken consists of cubed pork and veal (or sometimes beef) threaded onto skewers, breaded, and deep-fried until golden brown. It is typically served with a gravy or sauce and accompaniments such as mashed potatoes or noodles, originating as a Depression-era dish from the American Midwest.

Dahi baigana is an Indian dish consisting of eggplant cooked with yogurt-based sauce, typically prepared by roasting or frying eggplant and then simmering it with seasoned yogurt and spices. It is commonly served as a side dish or curry accompaniment to rice or bread in Indian cuisine.

Reshteh polo is a Persian rice dish made by cooking long-grain rice with fresh herbs (typically parsley, dill, and fenugreek) and dried green lentils, then layering and steaming until fluffy. The dish is traditionally served as a main course, often accompanied by meat or kebab and a dollop of yogurt.

Dobin mushi is a Japanese soup served in a small earthenware teapot, typically containing dashi broth with chicken, seafood, mushrooms, and vegetables like matsutake mushrooms and bamboo shoots. The dish is traditionally presented at the table in its brewing vessel and poured into cups for consumption, often finished with a squeeze of citrus.

Robatayaki is a Japanese grilling technique where various ingredients including vegetables, seafood, and meats are cooked over a high-heat charcoal fire, typically at a counter where diners watch the preparation. The cooked items are traditionally served with salt, soy sauce, or other dipping sauces and eaten immediately while hot.

Tain is a dense, long-lasting Serbian military bread developed in the early 20th century, baked from a mixture of wheat and other grain flours with minimal leavening to ensure durability during storage and transport. It was issued as field rations to soldiers, typically consumed on its own or alongside basic provisions during military campaigns.

Finocchini are small, dry Italian biscuits flavored with fennel seeds, typically made from a simple dough of flour, sugar, eggs, and whole or ground fennel seeds. They are baked until firm and are commonly served as a snack or alongside dessert wines, particularly in central and southern Italian culinary tradition.

Table cream is a thick, pourable dairy product made from pasteurized cream with a fat content typically ranging from 18% to 25%, produced by separating cream from whole milk and heat-treating it for preservation. It is commonly served as a topping or accompaniment for desserts, fresh fruits, cakes, and hot beverages such as coffee.

Metro is typically an international-style layered or assembled dish, often featuring a combination of proteins, vegetables, and sauces arranged in sections reminiscent of its namesake transit system. It is commonly served as a main course on a large plate or platter, suitable for sharing and accompanied by bread, rice, or a side salad.

Poutine à trou is a traditional Québécois baked dessert made by wrapping a whole apple in a soft dough, sometimes with sugar, butter, and spices, then baking until the pastry is cooked through and the apple is tender. It is typically served warm as a home-style dessert, often accompanied by cream or a sweet sauce.

Tatar böreği is a fried pastry filled with mashed potato and cheese, with the filled dough typically folded into a half-moon or rectangular shape before deep frying until golden. It is commonly served as a hot appetizer or snack, often accompanied by yogurt or a tomato-based sauce for dipping.

Pijama is a Spanish dessert composed of several chilled components served together on a single plate, typically including crème caramel, ice cream, whipped cream, and canned or fresh fruit. It is commonly found in traditional Spanish restaurants and cafeterias, served cold as a composed dessert platter rather than a single unified preparation.

Nozza is an Italian dish that is typically prepared using regional ingredients and traditional cooking methods characteristic of Italian cuisine, which often emphasize fresh produce, olive oil, herbs, and simple but flavorful combinations. It is commonly served as part of a multi-course meal, often accompanied by bread or wine, and may be enjoyed either as a starter or a main course depending on local custom.

Onde-onde are small round glutinous rice cakes made from sticky rice flour dough, typically filled with palm sugar and coated with sesame seeds. They are boiled in water until they float to the surface, then served as a sweet snack or dessert, commonly enjoyed in Indonesia and Malaysia.

Hawawshi is an Egyptian sandwich made by splitting flatbread and filling it with minced meat (typically beef or lamb), onions, and spices, then grilling or pan-frying until the bread is crispy and the meat is cooked through. It is commonly served as a street food or casual meal, often accompanied by tahini sauce, fresh vegetables, or pickled items.

Raviola di ricotta of Caltanissetta is a Sicilian pasta filled with creamy ricotta cheese, typically enclosed in thin pasta sheets and sealed into small pillow-shaped parcels. The raviola are boiled and traditionally served with butter, cheese, or tomato-based sauce as a first course in Italian cuisine.

Bustrengo is a traditional Italian sweet bread made from a mixture of flour, eggs, sugar, and butter, often enriched with dried fruits and sometimes nuts or spices. It is typically baked until golden and served as a dessert or sweet course, particularly in Liguria and other northern Italian regions.

Chhena Mudki is an Odia sweet made from freshly curdled milk solids (chhena) that are kneaded, shaped into small balls or cylindrical forms, and deep-fried until golden brown. The fried pieces are typically coated with a sugar syrup or jaggery coating and served as a traditional dessert or festive sweet in Odisha cuisine.

Sagu is a starch-based pudding made from sago pearls that are cooked until translucent and then combined with brown sugar syrup, coconut milk, and vanilla flavoring. The dish is typically served chilled as a dessert or sweet snack, often in individual bowls or glasses with the pearls suspended in the creamy liquid.

Goan nevri is a sweet dumpling made from rice flour dough filled with jaggery, coconut, and nuts, then deep-fried until golden. It is traditionally served as a dessert or festive treat in Goan cuisine, often during celebrations and special occasions.

Chausson aux pommes is a French pastry made from puff pastry folded around a filling of spiced apple compote or diced apples. It is typically baked until golden and served as a dessert or pastry course, often dusted with powdered sugar.

Gochtial is a traditional Breton pastry from France, made from an enriched yeast dough combining elements of bread and brioche, typically prepared with flour, butter, eggs, sugar, and sometimes raisins before being shaped and baked. It is commonly served sliced at breakfast or as an afternoon snack, often accompanied by coffee, tea, or butter and jam.

Kelane is a Kurdish flatbread prepared by rolling out dough and stuffing it with fillings such as herbs, leeks, onions, or spiced meat before cooking it on a griddle or pan until the exterior is lightly browned. It is typically served as a main or accompanying dish, often eaten with yogurt, tea, or pickles during breakfast or communal meals.

Peanut tofu (jimāmi-dōfu) is a traditional Okinawan preparation made by extracting milk from ground peanuts, then cooking it with starch—typically kudzu or sweet potato starch—until it sets into a smooth, firm block resembling conventional tofu despite containing no soybeans. It is commonly served chilled, cut into small cubes, and accompanied by soy sauce, dashi-based sauce, or wasabi as a side dish or appetizer in Okinawan cuisine.

Gatatan is a Filipino soup dish originating from the Ilocos region, made by simmering a mixture of various vegetables, bamboo shoots, jackfruit, and often pork or other proteins in coconut milk until thickened. It is typically served as a main course accompanied by steamed rice and is a common feature of everyday home cooking in the Ilocos provinces of the Philippines.

Mariah's Cookies are freshly baked cookies typically prepared in a variety of flavors such as chocolate chip, oatmeal, and sugar, often made with standard ingredients like flour, butter, sugar, and eggs. They are commonly served warm as a sweet snack or dessert, and are usually offered individually or in assorted boxes suitable for sharing.

Grisbì is an Italian shortbread biscuit produced by Matilde Vicenzi, filled with a smooth chocolate cream and encased in a crumbly, buttery pastry shell. The biscuits are individually wrapped and typically served as a snack or dessert accompaniment, often paired with coffee or tea.

Scattone is a traditional preparation from the Ciociaria region of Lazio, Italy, made by pouring red wine over the hot cooking water left after draining pasta, often sweetened with sugar. It is consumed as a rustic drink at the end of a meal, typically sipped directly from the bowl.

Ming'oko are wild edible tubers of the Dioscorea hirtiflora plant, foraged in southern Tanzania and prepared by boiling until tender. They are typically served as a snack or side dish, often accompanying tea or eaten on their own after peeling.

Atama soup is a Nigerian soup prepared by cooking palm fruit extract with fish, meat, and crayfish, seasoned with salt, onions, and ground capsicum peppers, with atama leaves (Heinsia crinita) typically used as the defining aromatic herb. It is a specialty of the Efik and Ibibio peoples of southeastern Nigeria and is commonly served with fufu, pounded yam, or other starchy accompaniments.

Tbikha is a North African vegetable stew, commonly prepared by slow-cooking a combination of vegetables such as tomatoes, peppers, zucchini, and onions with olive oil and spices including cumin and paprika. It is typically served as a side dish or light main course, often accompanied by bread, and is found across Tunisian and broader Maghrebi cuisine.

Lontong Mi is an Indonesian noodle dish that combines soft rice cakes (lontong) with noodles, bean sprouts, and typically a savory broth or sauce. The dish is commonly served as a breakfast or light meal in Indonesia, with the lontong and noodles providing a filling base of starches and textures.

Biscotto della monaca is a traditional Italian twice-baked almond biscuit that originated in convents, made by combining flour, sugar, eggs, and almonds before being sliced and toasted until crisp. The hard, crunchy biscuits are typically served as an accompaniment to coffee or dessert wine.

Eshkeneh is an Iranian soup made by sautéing onions until caramelized, then simmering them with turmeric, dried limes, and broth, typically finished with beaten eggs stirred in to create a creamy texture. The soup is traditionally served hot as a main course or appetizer, often accompanied by bread and sometimes topped with crispy fried onions.

Toasted ravioli consists of pasta parcels filled with meat, cheese, or vegetable mixtures that are breaded and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The dish is typically served as an appetizer with marinara sauce for dipping, originating from Italian-American cuisine.

Gorditas de nata are small, thick corn cakes made from masa (corn dough) filled or topped with nata (Mexican sour cream) and typically served with savory accompaniments such as cheese, beans, or meat. They are a traditional Mexican street food and appetizer, often eaten warm as a snack or light meal.

Yerma is an Uzbek soup prepared by simmering lamb meat with wheat grains and onions until the ingredients form a thick, hearty broth. It is typically served hot as a main course, often accompanied by flatbread in traditional Central Asian meals.

Maxim Pasta is typically a pasta-based dish made with noodles produced by the Luxembourgish brand Maxim, commonly prepared by boiling the pasta until al dente and tossing it with a sauce such as tomato, cream, or butter-based preparations. It is often served as a main course alongside grated cheese, fresh herbs, or a simple side salad, reflecting broad international pasta conventions.

The Butter Pecan Blondie is a warm blondie brownie made with butter, brown sugar, and pecans, baked until dense and chewy. It is typically served at Applebee's as a dessert, topped with vanilla ice cream and a caramel or butter pecan sauce.

Katla machher tel jhal is a Bengali preparation of katla fish (Catla catla) cooked in a pungent, oil-based sauce made with mustard paste, green chilies, nigella seeds (kalonji), and turmeric, typically shallow-fried before being simmered in the spiced oil. It is commonly served as a main course alongside steamed white rice in Bengali households across West Bengal and Bangladesh.

Loitta shutki jhal is a spiced dish made from dried Bombay duck fish (loitta shutki), cooked with mustard oil, green chilies, onions, garlic, and a blend of pungent spices to create an intensely flavored, fiery preparation. It is a traditional Bengali dish commonly served with steamed rice as a main course, particularly popular in Bangladesh and the coastal regions of West Bengal.

Muboora is a Zimbabwean dish made from pumpkin leaves, which are cleaned, chopped, and cooked with onions, tomatoes, and groundnut powder or peanut butter to form a thick, protein-rich stew. It is typically served as a relish alongside sadza, the staple maize porridge of Zimbabwe.

Jhinge diye tilapia machh is a Bengali preparation in which tilapia fish is cooked with ridge gourd (jhinge) in a light mustard oil-based sauce, typically seasoned with turmeric, green chilies, and nigella seeds (kalonji). It is commonly served as a main course alongside steamed rice in everyday Bengali household meals.

Chingrir rosa is a Bengali prawn curry prepared by cooking shrimp in a spiced gravy typically made with onions, garlic, ginger, green chilies, and whole spices such as cardamom, cinnamon, and cloves. It is generally served with steamed rice or pulao as a main course.

Vinegret is a traditional Russian salad made by combining diced beets, potatoes, sauerkraut, and onions, then dressing the mixture with vegetable oil and vinegar. The dish is typically served as a cold appetizer or side dish, often accompanying main courses in Russian cuisine.

Piracaldo is a Paraguayan empanada typically filled with fish, onions, and sometimes hard-boiled eggs, then fried until golden. It is traditionally served as a main course or appetizer, particularly during periods of religious observance when meat consumption is restricted.

Agnolini are small pasta parcels made from egg pasta dough that are filled with various stuffings such as meat, cheese, or vegetables, then sealed by folding or crimping the edges. They are typically served in broth or with sauce as a first course in Italian cuisine.

Palada Pradhaman is a Kerala dessert made by slow-cooking rice flakes with jaggery, ghee, and coconut milk until the ingredients amalgamate into a thick, creamy consistency. The dish is traditionally served warm as a festive sweet preparation, often during celebrations and religious occasions in South Indian cuisine.

Bhetki machher stew is a Bengali fish stew made with bhetki (Asian sea bass), cooked with potatoes, onions, and tomatoes in a light, seasoned broth. The dish is typically served with rice or bread as a main course in Bengali cuisine.

Chitol macher jhal is a Bengali fish curry prepared with chitol fish (an Indian freshwater fish) cooked in a spiced gravy typically made with onions, tomatoes, ginger, garlic, and traditional Bengali spices. The dish is commonly served with steamed rice as a main course in Bengali cuisine.

Chingrir machher chop is a Bengali fried cutlet made from shrimp and fish that are minced, mixed with spices and binders, formed into patties, and deep-fried until golden. The dish is typically served as an appetizer or snack, often accompanied by sliced onions and lime.

Macher pur diye begun korma is a Bengali curry where fish is stuffed with eggplant and cooked in a spiced tomato-based sauce with onions, garlic, and traditional Bengali spices. The dish is typically served with rice or bread as part of Bengali cuisine.

Three-pointed bread is a Peruvian wheat bread shaped by folding or pinching the dough into a distinctive three-pointed form before baking. It is typically served as a table bread accompanying meals or at breakfast, often with butter, cheese, or traditional Peruvian spreads.

Mourala Machher Tok is a Bengali sour fish dish made with mourala (a small freshwater fish, also known as mola carplet), cooked in a tangy tamarind-based sauce along with mustard, green chilies, and spices. It is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice in traditional Bengali households, particularly in the regions of West Bengal and Bangladesh.

Sorse katla is a Bengali fish dish prepared by cooking katla (rohu carp) in a mustard-based sauce made from ground mustard seeds, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. It is typically served with steamed white rice as a main course in Bengali households and restaurants.

Jhal koi is a Bengali spiced fish dish prepared by cooking koi (climbing perch, Anabas testudineus) in a pungent sauce made with mustard paste, green chilies, nigella seeds, and turmeric, typically finished with mustard oil. It is commonly served with steamed rice and is a traditional preparation found in Bengali households across Bangladesh and West Bengal, India.

Parshe machh bhaja is a Bengali preparation in which parshe fish (Mugil parsiah, a small mullet found in brackish and freshwater) is marinated with turmeric and salt, then shallow- or deep-fried in mustard oil until the skin turns crisp and golden. It is typically served as a starter or alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Doi mouri parshe is a Bengali dish in which parshe fish (Mullet) is cooked in a yogurt-based sauce seasoned with mustard, turmeric, green chili, and aromatic spices. It is typically served with steamed rice as part of a multi-course Bengali meal.

Rui posto is a Bengali fish curry made with rohu steaks that are lightly marinated in turmeric and salt, pan-fried, and then simmered in a spiced gravy of onion, ginger, garlic, cumin, and tomato or yogurt. It is typically served with steamed rice as part of a main meal in Bengali households.

Parshe jhal is a Bengali fish curry made with parshe (mullet) fish cooked in a pungent mustard paste sauce, typically seasoned with green chilies, turmeric, mustard oil, and nigella seeds. It is commonly served with steamed white rice as a traditional everyday meal in Bengali households of West Bengal and Bangladesh.

Lonka pora rui is a Bengali preparation of rohu fish (Rui, Labeo rohita) in which dried red chilies (lonka) are charred or fire-roasted and incorporated into a spiced gravy, giving the dish its characteristic smoky heat. It is typically served with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Katla machher tel bhaja is a Bengali fish preparation made by frying catla fish in oil until the flesh becomes crispy and golden brown. The dish is typically served as a main course or side dish in Bengali cuisine, often accompanied by rice and dal.

Bata machh bhaja is a Bengali fried fish dish made by coating fish pieces in a spiced batter and deep-frying until crispy. The dish is typically served as an appetizer or side course, often accompanied by rice or bread in Bengali cuisine.

Doi bhetki is a Bengali fish curry dish featuring bhetki (Asian sea bass) cooked in a yogurt-based gravy with spices, onions, and tomatoes. The fish is typically simmered gently in the creamy sauce until tender and is traditionally served with steamed rice or bread.

canonicalName: roshur sorshe diye rui machher jhal
displayName: Roshur sorshe diye rui machher jhal
aliases: roshur sorshe diye rui machher jhal
description: Roshur sorshe diye rui machher jhal is a Bengali fish curry made with rui (rohu) fish cooked in a spiced mustard-based sauce, typically prepared with mustard paste, green chilies, and aromatic spices. The dish is traditionally served with steamed rice as a main course in Bengali cuisine.

Aspic is a savory jelly made by boiling meat, poultry, or fish bones and connective tissue to extract gelatin, then clarifying the stock and allowing it to set. It is typically served cold as a coating or glaze for charcuterie, terrines, and other prepared meats, or cut into cubes as a garnish.

Blanklefse is a Norwegian potato flatbread made from a simple dough of mashed potatoes, flour, and butter, then pan-fried until golden on both sides. It is traditionally served warm with butter, sugar, or savory toppings, and is commonly eaten as a breakfast or snack item in Norwegian cuisine.

Pyeonsu are Korean dumplings with a flat, rectangular shape typically filled with meat, vegetables, or tofu, then boiled or pan-fried until the wrapper develops a golden crust. They are commonly served as an appetizer or side dish in North Korean cuisine, often accompanied by a dipping sauce of vinegar and soy sauce.

Chama is a traditional Bolivian wheat bread made from a dough of wheat flour, water, salt, fat, and yeast, shaped into small round loaves and baked in an oven until the crust is firm and golden. It is commonly served at breakfast or tea time, often accompanied by coffee, mate, or cheese.

Apio is a Sephardic Jewish dish made from celery root (celeriac) braised with lemon juice, olive oil, and sometimes carrots or potatoes until tender. It is typically served cold or at room temperature as a meze or side dish, particularly during Shabbat and holiday meals in Sephardic communities.

Babarsa is a traditional sweet confection from West Bengal, India, prepared from a batter of fermented rice flour and lentils that is deep-fried and then soaked in sugar syrup. It is typically served as a dessert or festive treat, often enjoyed during regional celebrations and family gatherings.

Khao Kriep Pak Mor is a Thai steamed dumpling made from a thin, translucent wrapper prepared with rice flour, arrowroot, and cassava starch, which together produce its characteristic delicate texture. The dumplings are filled with a savory mixture, typically including pork and vegetables, and are commonly served as a snack or appetizer in Thai cuisine.

Baddare is a traditional Swedish pastry, typically prepared as a small baked sweet made from a buttery dough enriched with ingredients such as sugar, eggs, and sometimes almonds or cardamom for flavor. It is commonly served alongside coffee as part of the Swedish 'fika' tradition, often presented on a platter with other assorted cookies and pastries.

Patîle is a traditional Kurdish flatbread, typically prepared from a simple dough of flour, water, and salt that is rolled thin and baked on a hot griddle or stone. It is commonly served warm alongside stews, cheeses, or grilled meats, and often used to scoop up dips and accompany tea during meals.

Bodyk is a traditional Kazakh soup prepared by cooking dried meat, typically lamb or beef, with grains such as millet or wheat in a broth-based liquid. It is commonly served as a hearty main course, particularly during colder months or festive gatherings in Kazakhstan.

Saumaise is an Austrian preparation consisting of pork offal and meat scraps cooked and preserved in a seasoned aspic or brine, resulting in a firm, sliceable terrine-like product. It is typically served cold as a cured meat, cut into slices and accompanied by bread, onions, vinegar, and oil as part of a Jause or cold platter.

Sfenj is a Moroccan fried pastry dough that is deep-fried until golden and puffy, typically shaped into a ring or coil. It is traditionally served warm with honey or sugar for dipping, often eaten for breakfast or as a street food snack.

Dobrada is a traditional Portuguese dish made from beef tripe that is cleaned, cut into strips, and cooked slowly with beans, onions, and spices until tender. It is typically served as a hearty stew or main course, often accompanied by rice or bread.

Anginara is a Greek dish made from artichoke hearts that are typically braised or stewed with olive oil, lemon juice, and herbs until tender. It is commonly served as a warm or room-temperature appetizer or side dish in Mediterranean cuisine.

Spicy cow foot is prepared by simmering beef foot with red peppers, onions, crayfish, and palm oil, seasoned with salt and nutmeg for depth of flavor. The dish is commonly served as a stew or soup in West African cuisine, often accompanied by rice or fufu.

Kategna is an Ethiopian bread made by mixing flour, water, and salt into a batter that is cooked on a large circular griddle called a mitad until golden and crispy on the edges. It is typically served as an accompaniment to stews and dishes, or can be enjoyed on its own as a snack or breakfast item.

Chapli kebab is a flattened, round patty made from minced meat (typically beef or mutton) mixed with onions, tomatoes, coriander, cumin, and chili peppers, then shallow-fried until golden brown. It is traditionally served hot with naan bread, yogurt, and fresh vegetables as a popular street food and appetizer in Pakistani cuisine.

Kuivaliha is a Finnish dried meat dish prepared by curing and air-drying beef or reindeer, traditionally sliced thin and served as an appetizer or snack. The meat is typically seasoned with salt and spices during the curing process, resulting in a concentrated, chewy texture that is eaten cold, often accompanied by bread and butter.

Neobiani is a Georgian grilled beef dish where marinated meat is cooked over an open flame or on a grill until charred on the outside while remaining tender inside. The beef is typically seasoned with spices and herbs, then served hot, often accompanied by bread and fresh vegetables as part of a traditional Georgian meal.

Bi29 is a South Korean snack confection produced by Crown Confectionery, made from baked wheat flour dough seasoned with curry powder to give it a distinctive savory-sweet curry flavor. It is sold in small packaged portions and typically eaten as a shelf-stable snack on its own or alongside beverages.

Zuppa mitonata is a Piedmontese bread soup prepared by layering slices of stale bread with broth and simmering or baking until the bread absorbs the liquid and the mixture becomes thick and cohesive. It is typically made with meat or vegetable broth and stale bread, and served as a simple, rustic first course.

Chicken Plus Vietnam is typically a chicken-based dish prepared with Vietnamese-inspired flavors, commonly featuring ingredients such as lemongrass, garlic, fish sauce, and fresh herbs like cilantro or mint. It is often served with steamed rice or rice noodles and accompanied by vegetables or a light dipping sauce, reflecting the balance of savory, sweet, and aromatic elements typical of Vietnamese-influenced cooking.

Kolot is a traditional Turkish bread roll made from wheat flour, water, salt, and yeast, shaped into small rounds and baked until firm. It is commonly served as an accompaniment to meals or eaten with cheese, butter, or jam at breakfast.

Bittergarnituur is a Dutch assortment of deep-fried savory snacks typically served as appetizers or bar snacks alongside drinks. The selection commonly includes items such as bitterballen, kaassoufflés, and other breaded and fried bites, served on a plate or in a basket for shared snacking.

Bazzoffia is a traditional Italian soup from the Lazio region, prepared by simmering spring vegetables such as peas, fava beans, artichokes, and lettuce together with onion and sometimes pancetta or lard. It is typically served as a hearty first course during spring, when the fresh legumes and vegetables used in the dish are in season.

Mutton intestine soup is a broth-based dish prepared by cleaning and simmering sheep intestines with aromatics such as ginger, garlic, and spices until tender. It is served hot as a main course or street food across various cuisines in Asia, the Middle East, and Africa, often accompanied by bread, rice, or noodles.

Stone soup (caldo de pedra) is a traditional Portuguese dish originating in the Ribatejo region, made by heating stones in a fire and placing them directly into a broth of water, pork, blood sausage, beans, and various vegetables to cook the ingredients. It is traditionally prepared communally outdoors and served as a hearty main course, with the hot stones remaining in the pot during service.

Apple crumble pie consists of a pastry pie crust filled with sliced apples mixed with sugar and spices, topped with a crumbly mixture of flour, butter, sugar, and oats. It is typically baked until golden and served warm, often with whipped cream or vanilla ice cream as an accompaniment.

Sup tulang is an Indonesian bone soup made by simmering beef or chicken bones with aromatic spices, turmeric, and vegetables to create a rich, flavorful broth. The dish is traditionally served hot as a comforting main course, often accompanied by rice and fresh garnishes such as scallions and fried shallots.

Celeriac soup is prepared by cooking peeled celeriac root with stock, onions, and aromatics, then blending until smooth. The soup is typically served hot as a starter or light course, often finished with cream and seasoned with salt and pepper.

White bean soup is prepared by simmering dried or canned white beans with vegetables such as onions, celery, and carrots, typically seasoned with herbs and broth until the beans soften and flavors meld. The soup is commonly served hot as a starter or light main course, often finished with olive oil or accompanied by bread.

A hamburger consists of a ground beef patty, typically seasoned with salt and pepper, cooked on a griddle or grill until the desired doneness is reached, and served on a soft bun. Common accompaniments include lettuce, tomato, onion, pickles, and condiments such as ketchup, mustard, or mayonnaise, and it is typically served as a casual meal or quick lunch.

Ofe Achara is a Nigerian soup prepared by combining palm oil, egusi seeds, smoked fish, beef, crayfish, and vegetables such as onions and capsicum in a water-based broth. The dish is traditionally served with cassava fufu, garri, or pounded yam as an accompaniment.

Fideuà is a Spanish noodle dish from the Valencia region made by toasting short, thin pasta (fideos) before cooking it in a seafood or meat broth, similar to paella preparation. The dish is traditionally served as a main course with seafood such as shrimp, squid, or other shellfish, and finished with garlic aioli and lemon.

Lobster bisque is a creamy soup made by simmering lobster shells and meat with aromatic vegetables, then straining and enriching the broth with cream and butter to create a smooth, velvety texture. It is typically served hot as an appetizer or first course in fine dining settings.

Pasta marquesa is a traditional Spanish Christmas pastry made primarily from ground almonds, sugar, and egg yolks, forming a marzipan-based dough that is shaped into small individual pieces and baked until set. It is typically served during the holiday season alongside other festive sweets such as turrón and polvorones, often accompanied by coffee or sweet wine.

Pera Sandesh is a Bengali sweet made by kneading chenna (fresh curdled milk solids) with sugar and pear pulp or pear flavoring, then shaping the mixture into small portions, often molded into decorative forms. It is typically served as a dessert or festive confection, chilled or at room temperature, and belongs to the sandesh family of milk-based sweets common in West Bengal.

Turkish ring dessert is a circular pastry made from a choux-like dough that is piped into a ring shape, deep-fried or baked, and soaked or coated in syrup, sometimes finished with powdered sugar or sesame seeds. It is typically served as a sweet snack or dessert in Turkish cuisine, often accompanied by tea.

Kawari is a Sudanese soup prepared by slow-simmering cattle or sheep hoofs with vegetables and spices until the broth becomes rich and gelatinous. It is typically served hot as a hearty dish, often accompanied by bread.

Louhouré noun coco is a traditional Guinean dessert that typically features coconut as a central ingredient, often combined with sugar and milk to create a sweet, rich preparation. It is commonly served chilled or at room temperature as a finishing course, and may be enjoyed during festive occasions or family gatherings.

Mohnmingilan is typically prepared as a sweet Italian-style pancake featuring poppy seeds (Mohn) as a key ingredient, often mixed into a light batter of flour, eggs, milk, and sugar before being pan-fried until golden. It is commonly served as a dessert or afternoon treat, often dusted with powdered sugar and accompanied by fruit compote, jam, or a drizzle of honey.

Meascia is a rustic bread from the Lake Como area of Lombardy, made from a mixture of cornmeal and wheat flour combined with milk, butter, and studded with pieces of apple or pear, raisins, and sometimes dried figs or walnuts. It is typically baked until firm and served in slices as a snack, dessert, or accompaniment to coffee or wine.

Chhena Mandua is a traditional Indian sweet typically made from chhena, a fresh, unripened cheese produced by curdling milk with a mild acid, which is then kneaded and shaped into small rounds or patties. It is commonly sweetened with sugar or jaggery and flavored with cardamom, and is often served at festivals, religious occasions, or as a dessert following a meal.

Ofe ogbolor is a Nigerian soup made primarily from ogbolor leaves (Gnetum africanum), cooked with palm oil, crayfish, stockfish, assorted meats, and traditional seasonings such as ogiri or iru. It is typically served alongside starchy accompaniments like pounded yam, eba, or fufu in Igbo culinary tradition.

Kıvratma is a Turkish fried pastry made from a batter of wheat flour, eggs, yogurt, and a small amount of sugar, which is cooked in hot oil until golden. It is typically served as a snack or dessert, often dusted with powdered sugar or accompanied by a sweet topping.

Húsleves is a traditional Hungarian beef broth made by simmering beef with bones, onions, and seasonings for several hours until the meat is tender and the broth is deeply flavored. The dish is typically served hot as a soup course, often with egg noodles, potatoes, or other vegetables, and represents a foundational element of Hungarian cuisine.

Escarole soup is prepared by simmering escarole (a leafy green vegetable) in a broth base, typically combined with ingredients such as beans, garlic, and olive oil. The soup is commonly served as a first course in Italian and Mediterranean cuisines, often with crusty bread on the side.

Chestnut soup is prepared by simmering roasted chestnuts with stock, aromatics, and often cream or butter to create a smooth puree, with seasonings adjusted to taste. The soup is typically served hot as a starter course, sometimes garnished with croutons, roasted chestnut pieces, or fresh herbs.

Torta de leche is a sponge cake soaked in a mixture of evaporated milk, condensed milk, and heavy cream, creating a moist and rich texture throughout. The cake is typically topped with whipped cream and meringue, and is served chilled as a dessert in Spanish-speaking countries.

Ajiaco is a hearty stew made by simmering meat (typically chicken or beef), potatoes, and vegetables in a seasoned broth, with regional variations across Latin America. The dish is traditionally served hot in bowls and often accompanied by corn, avocado, cream, or fresh herbs depending on the specific regional preparation.

Skippy is an American brand of peanut butter made by grinding roasted peanuts with added salt, sugar, and hydrogenated vegetable oils to produce a smooth or chunky spread. It is commonly served spread on bread for sandwiches, such as the peanut butter and jelly sandwich, or used as a dip for fruit and an ingredient in baked goods and sauces.

S'mores Bake is a layered dessert assembled with a graham cracker base, a chocolate brownie or fudge layer, and topped with marshmallows, then baked or broiled until the marshmallows are toasted. It is typically served warm as a shareable dessert, replicating the classic campfire s'more combination in a baked dish format.

Torta 900 is a layered chocolate cake created in Ivrea, Italy, consisting of two discs of cocoa sponge filled with a cold chocolate cream, with the original recipe kept secret since its creation in the early 20th century. It is typically served chilled as a dessert, cut into slices and often accompanied by coffee.

Sospiro is an Italian dessert made from a baked meringue base of whipped egg whites and sugar, often flavored with lemon and topped or filled with a sweet icing or glaze. It is typically served in small individual portions as a sweet treat with coffee or at the end of a meal.

Kabissé is a dessert prepared by combining curdled milk with wheat semolina, which is cooked together to form a thick, pudding-like consistency. It is typically served as a sweet course at the end of a meal.

Gaja is a traditional Indian dessert typically prepared by deep-frying strips or pieces of wheat flour dough, which are then coated or soaked in a sugar syrup to give them a sweet, crisp finish. It is commonly served as a festive treat or sweet snack alongside tea, and is often enjoyed during celebrations and religious occasions.

Şöbiyet is a Turkish pastry made from thin phyllo dough layered with clotted cream (kaymak) and ground pistachios, then baked and soaked in sugar syrup. It is typically served as a dessert, cut into square or rectangular portions, and is closely related to baklava but distinguished by its cream filling.

A jam tart consists of a pastry case filled with jam, typically baked until the pastry is golden and crisp. It is commonly served as a dessert or afternoon tea item, either warm or at room temperature, often with a dusting of sugar on top.

Mađarica is a Croatian layered chocolate cake made by stacking multiple thin pastry sheets with a chocolate cream filling, then cutting into rectangular slices. It is a common dessert in Croatian households and bakeries, typically served at room temperature and associated with festive occasions.

Apple crumble is a baked dessert consisting of stewed apples topped with a crumbly mixture of flour, butter, and sugar. It is typically served warm, often with custard, cream, or ice cream on the side.

Besan laddu is a spherical Indian sweet made from roasted chickpea flour (besan) mixed with ghee, sugar, and cardamom, then shaped into balls and often garnished with nuts. It is a traditional dessert commonly served at celebrations, festivals, and as a homemade confection.

White chocolate cake is made by incorporating melted white chocolate and cocoa butter into a light batter, typically combined with eggs, sugar, flour, and butter, then baked until golden. It is commonly served as a dessert, either plain or topped with white chocolate frosting, ganache, or fresh berries.

Maboké is a Central African dish in which fish is wrapped in banana leaves and cooked by steaming or grilling, allowing the fish to cook gently in its own juices while absorbing flavors from the wrapping. The dish is typically seasoned with spices and served hot as a main course, often accompanied by starchy sides such as cassava or plantains.

Sulu köfte is a Turkish meatball dish prepared by simmering ground meat mixed with rice, onions, and spices in a broth or sauce until cooked through. The dish is typically served hot as a main course, often accompanied by rice or bread to absorb the surrounding liquid.

Ginger duck is a Chinese dish in which duck meat is stir-fried with fresh ginger, soy sauce, and aromatics such as garlic and scallions, creating a savory and slightly spicy preparation. The dish is typically served over steamed rice as a main course in Cantonese cuisine.

Peyajkoli diye katla machh is a Bengali fish dish in which katla (Catla catla), a freshwater carp, is cooked with peyajkoli (green onion stalks or scallion greens) in a mustard oil-based preparation typically seasoned with turmeric, green chilies, and whole spices. The dish is commonly served as a main course alongside steamed rice in Bengali households.

Samsa dessert is a baked pastry made from layered or folded dough filled with a sweet mixture of crushed nuts, sugar, and sometimes honey or spices, then shaped into triangles or parcels before being oven-baked until golden. It is typically served as a dessert or accompaniment to tea, often soaked in or drizzled with sugar syrup or honey after baking.

Torró d'Ocata is a Catalan variety of turrón made from toasted almonds bound with honey and sugar, producing a hard, brittle texture that is typically broken into irregular pieces. It is traditionally served as a sweet during Christmas celebrations, often alongside other festive confections and accompanied by cava or dessert wine.

Cherry kebab is a dish in which cherries are combined with seasoned ground or chunks of meat, typically skewered and grilled or cooked over an open flame. It is served in various regional preparations where the tartness of cherries balances the savory meat, often presented alongside rice or flatbread.

Aftir oukessoul is typically prepared as a savory dish combining slow-cooked components, likely featuring seasoned meat or vegetables simmered with aromatic spices and herbs common to international fusion cooking. It is often served warm as a main course, commonly accompanied by bread, rice, or a side of steamed grains to balance the flavors.

Usi is a stiff porridge made from cassava root, which is peeled, dried, and ground into flour before being cooked with boiling water and stirred to a thick, smooth consistency. It is a staple food in parts of Central and Southern Africa, typically served as an accompaniment to relishes, stews, or vegetables.

Otaguri is a Japanese dish from Nagano Prefecture made by simmering horse intestines with miso, ginger, and green onions until tender. It is typically served as an izakaya dish or accompaniment to sake and shochu.

A California salad typically consists of mixed greens combined with ingredients such as avocado, tomato, bacon, and hard-boiled egg, often dressed with a vinaigrette or creamy dressing. It is commonly served as a starter or light main course in casual dining settings.

Itawasa is a Japanese appetizer consisting of a slice of wasabi (Japanese horseradish rhizome) served on a thin piece of kamaboko (processed fish cake), typically accompanied by soy sauce. It is commonly served as a small side dish or bar snack (otsumami) alongside sake or other beverages.

Mie ayam is an Indonesian noodle dish made with wheat noodles tossed in a savory chicken-based sauce flavored with soy sauce, garlic, and spices, typically topped with shredded cooked chicken. It is commonly served with a side of chicken broth, fresh vegetables such as bok choy or bean sprouts, and often accompanied by sambal or chili paste for additional heat.

Thiakry is a Senegalese dessert made from millet couscous mixed with yogurt, milk, and sugar, creating a creamy pudding-like consistency. The dish is typically served chilled as a sweet treat or dessert, often garnished with peanuts or other nuts.

Keshi yena is a hollowed-out cheese (typically Edam) stuffed with a savory filling of seasoned meat, vegetables, and spices, then baked until the cheese is melted and the exterior is golden. This Curaçaoan dish is traditionally served as a main course, often accompanied by rice or beans.

Soparnik is a traditional Croatian pastry consisting of a thin dough filled with cheese and Swiss chard or spinach, typically baked until golden. The dish is commonly served as a savory pie or appetizer, often cut into squares or triangles, and may be accompanied by sour cream.

Pelau is a one-pot dish made by cooking rice with meat, pigeon peas, and coconut milk together with caramelized sugar, creating a savory and slightly sweet flavor profile. It is a staple comfort food in Caribbean cuisines, particularly in Trinidad and Tobago, typically served as a main course.

Empal gepuk is an Indonesian dish made from beef that is boiled until tender, then flattened and fried until crispy on the outside while remaining soft inside, typically seasoned with garlic, shallots, and spices. It is commonly served as a main dish with rice and sambal, popular in Javanese cuisine particularly from the Central Java region.

Vigorón is a Nicaraguan dish consisting of fried pork belly (chicharrón) served alongside boiled yuca and a cabbage slaw dressed with a tomato-based vinegar sauce. It is traditionally eaten as a street food or casual meal, often served on a banana leaf.

Barongko pisang is an Indonesian steamed cake made from mashed bananas mixed with flour, sugar, and coconut milk, then wrapped in banana leaves and steamed until cooked through. It is a traditional dessert from South Sulawesi typically served as a sweet snack or breakfast item.

Putri salju cake is an Indonesian sponge cake made with egg whites, sugar, and flour, often flavored with vanilla and topped with shredded coconut. The cake is baked until light and fluffy, then coated with a thick layer of grated coconut, giving it a snow-like appearance that inspired its name.

Bakso Balungan is an Indonesian soup made by simmering beef bones to create a rich broth, which is then served with beef meatballs and typically includes noodles, vegetables, and aromatics such as garlic and shallots. This dish is commonly consumed as a main course for lunch or dinner and is popular throughout Indonesia as a casual, hearty meal.

Twist is a brand of bite-sized confectionery consisting of small chocolate-coated pieces with varied fillings, individually wrapped and sold in assorted mixed bags. It is typically served as a snack or shared confection at social gatherings and is commonly found in retail stores internationally.

Köpoğlu mancası is a Turkish dish made by layering fried or roasted eggplant with a tomato-based sauce, often incorporating garlic-seasoned yogurt as a topping. It is typically served as a meze or side dish, with the components arranged together and presented at room temperature.

Bulung is a traditional Balinese dish made from fresh seaweed, typically of the species Caulerpa or similar varieties, dressed with a spiced coconut or sambal-based sauce incorporating shallots, chili, lime juice, and shrimp paste. It is commonly served as a side dish or salad accompaniment in Balinese meals, particularly in coastal regions of Bali where seaweed is locally harvested.

Sambal asam udueng is an Indonesian condiment or side dish made from shrimp (udueng) combined with asam (tamarind) and chili peppers, typically ground or pounded together to create a sour, spicy paste. It is commonly served as an accompaniment to rice and other main dishes in Indonesian cuisine, particularly in regions where fermented or fresh shrimp-based sambals are traditional.

Sengkulun is a traditional Indonesian steamed cake made primarily from glutinous rice flour, palm sugar, and coconut milk, which give it a dense, chewy texture and a naturally sweet flavor. It is commonly found as a traditional snack or ceremonial food in Betawi and Javanese communities, often wrapped in banana leaves during preparation.

Sie reuboh is an Acehnese dish from Indonesia made from beef simmered with vinegar, chili, garlic, shallots, turmeric, and other spices until the liquid reduces and the meat absorbs the seasoning. It is traditionally prepared in large batches, stored in clay pots, and served with steamed rice, often during Ramadan and communal gatherings in Aceh Besar.

Soto tangkar is a Betawi beef rib soup from Jakarta, prepared by simmering beef ribs with coconut milk and a spice paste that includes turmeric, galangal, lemongrass, candlenuts, and chilies, resulting in a rich, yellowish-orange broth. It is typically served with steamed white rice, sliced tomatoes, fried shallots, and sambal, and is a traditional dish associated with the Betawi people of Jakarta.

Seblak is an Indonesian street food made by stir-frying crackers (krupuk) with a spicy broth containing ingredients such as vegetables, eggs, and optional protein like seafood or chicken. The dish is typically served hot in a bowl and eaten as a casual snack or light meal, popular in West Java and now found throughout Indonesia.

Sweetened lotus root is prepared by slicing fresh lotus root and cooking it with sugar, typically through simmering or candying until tender and glazed. The dish is commonly served as a dessert or sweet side dish in Asian cuisine, particularly in Chinese culinary traditions.

Arroz negro is a Spanish rice dish made by cooking short-grain rice with squid ink, which gives it a distinctive black color, along with seafood such as squid or cuttlefish, and aromatics like garlic and onion. The dish is typically served as a main course, often accompanied by aioli or lemon wedges, and is particularly popular in coastal regions of Spain.

Kue ape is an Indonesian thin, crispy wafer cookie made from a simple batter of flour, eggs, sugar, and coconut milk, cooked in a special patterned mold or press over heat until golden and crispy. The cookies are typically served as a sweet snack or treat, often given as gifts or sold in markets, and are characterized by their delicate, lace-like texture and slightly sweet coconut flavor.

Sambal pepaya is an Indonesian condiment made from unripe papaya that is grated or finely chopped and mixed with red chilies, garlic, lime juice, and salt to create a spicy, tangy paste. This sambal is typically served as an accompaniment to rice dishes, grilled meats, or seafood to add heat and acidity to meals.

Opor kambing is a traditional Indonesian stew prepared by simmering goat meat with coconut milk, spices such as garlic, shallots, galangal, and turmeric, and aromatics like bay leaves and lemongrass until tender. The dish is typically served during festive occasions, particularly around Eid celebrations, and is commonly accompanied by rice or traditional Indonesian bread.

Pindang is an Indonesian fish dish where fish is poached or simmered in a seasoned broth, typically containing turmeric, garlic, and other spices that give it a distinctive yellow color. The dish is traditionally served with rice and is common in Indonesian home cooking and regional cuisine.

Pu phat phong kari is a Thai stir-fried crab dish prepared with curry powder, turmeric, and eggs, creating a golden-hued mixture that coats the crab pieces. The dish is typically served over rice and garnished with fresh herbs and scallions, commonly enjoyed as a main course in Thai cuisine.

Aam sorse pomfret is a Bengali preparation in which pomfret fish is cooked in a sauce made from mustard paste and raw mango (aam), typically seasoned with green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. It is commonly served with steamed white rice as part of a main meal.

A goodie bag is assembled by filling a rolled, printed paper bag or cone with small items such as toys, trinkets, or confections like candy and chocolate. It is typically distributed to guests at parties, celebrations, or promotional events as a parting gift.

Doi tomato bhetki is a Bengali preparation in which bhetki (barramundi or Asian sea bass) fillets are marinated and cooked in a sauce made from yogurt (doi) and tomatoes, typically seasoned with spices such as turmeric, cumin, and chili. It is commonly served as a main course alongside steamed rice in both home cooking and restaurant settings in Bengal.

Ngiu vun sui is typically a beef-based noodle or broth dish, with the name suggesting slow-cooked beef combined with noodles or rice in a savory, aromatic soup. It is commonly served hot in a bowl, often garnished with fresh herbs, scallions, and chili condiments on the side for seasoning to taste.

Bele machher jhal is a Bengali fish curry prepared with bele fish (gobies, Glossogobius species) cooked in a spiced mustard-based gravy, typically incorporating mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. It is a traditional dish from the Bengal region, commonly served with steamed white rice as a main course meal.

Çoban kavurma is a Turkish dish made with diced meat that is pan-fried or sautéed with onions, peppers, and tomatoes until the ingredients are well-browned and the meat is tender. The dish is typically served as a main course, often accompanied by rice or bread, and is common in Turkish home cooking and casual dining establishments.

Kakrar jhal is a Bengali crab curry in which crabs are cooked with mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil, producing a pungent, spiced gravy. It is typically served with steamed rice as a main course in Bengali households and restaurants across West Bengal and Bangladesh.

Machher pur vora potoler dolma is a Bengali dish in which pointed gourd (parwal/potoler) is hollowed out and stuffed with a spiced fish filling, then fried or cooked in a gravy. It is a traditional preparation from the cuisine of Bengal, typically served as a main course alongside rice.

Laytha machh bhapa is a Bengali steamed fish preparation in which fish — typically hilsa (ilish) or another freshwater variety — is marinated in a paste of mustard seeds, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil, then wrapped and steamed until cooked through. It is commonly served as a main course alongside steamed rice.

Nesselrode pudding is a chilled dessert made by folding whipped cream into a custard base of egg yolk and powdered sugar, then layering or combining it with sponge cake, chestnut puree, candied fruits (Zante currants and succade), and Maraschino liqueur. The mixture is typically set and served cold as an elegant dessert course in Russian cuisine.

Magur machher rosha is a Bengali curry made with catfish (magur) cooked in a spiced gravy base that typically includes onions, tomatoes, and aromatic spices. The dish is traditionally served with rice or bread as a main course in Bengali cuisine.

Achari tel ilish is a Bengali preparation of hilsa fish cooked in mustard oil with pickling spices such as fenugreek, nigella seeds, and dried red chilies, creating a tangy and aromatic sauce. The dish is traditionally served with steamed rice as a main course in Bengali cuisine.

Kasundi ilish is a Bengali fish curry made with hilsa fish cooked in a spiced mustard-based sauce, traditionally prepared with mustard paste, turmeric, and green chilies. The dish is typically served with steamed rice as a main course and is particularly popular during the Bengali fish season.

Rui machher bhuna is a Bengali preparation of rohu fish that is pan-fried or sautéed with onions, garlic, ginger, and spices until the fish develops a golden, caramelized exterior. The dish is typically served as a main course with rice or bread and is common in Bengali cuisine.

Puti machher tok is a Bengali fish curry made with white fish cooked in a tangy broth flavored with mustard oil, turmeric, and often featuring tomatoes or tamarind for acidity. The dish is traditionally served with rice as part of Bengali meals, with the sauce meant to be consumed alongside the flaked fish.

Ilish machher dim vaja is a Bengali preparation featuring hilsa fish eggs (roe) that are seasoned and shallow-fried until golden. The dish is typically served as an appetizer or side dish, often accompanied by rice or flatbread in Bengali cuisine.

Krembanan is a confection consisting of a banana-shaped piece of marshmallow or soft meringue-like cream coated in chocolate, typically produced industrially and sold pre-packaged. It is commonly eaten as a sweet snack or dessert, particularly in Nordic countries where it is a well-known retail confectionery item.

Swoops are a chocolate candy made by coating potato chip-shaped pieces of solid chocolate, produced by The Hershey Company in varieties such as milk chocolate, dark chocolate, white chocolate, and peanut butter. They are sold as a packaged confection intended to be eaten directly from the container as a snack.

Koi machher tel jhal is a Bengali preparation of climbing perch (koi fish) cooked in a pungent, oil-based sauce made with mustard paste, green chilies, and nigella seeds (kalonji), resulting in a sharp, spicy gravy.
