Ofada rice is a Nigerian dish made with locally grown parboiled rice cooked with vegetable oil, seasoning, salt, and iru (fermented locust beans), which impart a distinctive savory flavor. The rice is typically served as a main course, often accompanied by a rich sauce or stew made with meat or fish.

Kapurung is an Indonesian dish from South Kalimantan made from sago flour mixed with coconut milk and formed into small balls or lumps, then cooked in broth with various proteins such as fish or shrimp and vegetables. The dish is traditionally served as a main course or soup, commonly prepared during special occasions and family gatherings in Banjarese cuisine.

Kue lumpur is an Indonesian cake made from a batter of rice flour, coconut milk, and palm sugar, baked or steamed until set. Typically served as a sweet snack or dessert, the cake has a moist, dense crumb and is often cut into squares for serving.

Laksan is an Indonesian noodle soup dish typically prepared by cooking thin noodles in a rich broth made from coconut milk, turmeric, and spices, often with added proteins such as fish or shrimp and vegetables like bean sprouts and green onions. The dish is commonly served hot in a bowl and is particularly popular in Indonesian coastal regions as a breakfast or light meal.
Nakládaný hermelín is a Czech appetizer consisting of soft cheese (hermelín) marinated in oil with garlic, herbs, and spices. It is typically served chilled as a starter with bread or crackers.

Stuffed dates are whole dates with the pit removed and replaced with a filling such as nuts, cheese, marzipan, or spiced paste. This confection is commonly served as a dessert or sweet snack in Middle Eastern and North African cuisines, often presented on platters for festive occasions and celebrations.

Kacchi biryani is a rice dish prepared by layering raw marinated meat with partially cooked rice and aromatics, then cooking together in a sealed pot using the dum pukht (slow-cooking) method until both rice and meat are fully cooked. The dish is traditionally served as a complete meal, often accompanied by raita, pickles, and shorba (broth).

Crab curry is prepared by cooking crab meat in a spiced sauce base typically made with onions, garlic, ginger, and curry spices such as turmeric, cumin, and coriander, often finished with coconut milk or cream. The dish is traditionally served over rice or with flatbread as an accompaniment.

Doces do Brasil is an assortment of traditional Brazilian sweets, typically including confections such as brigadeiros (chocolate and condensed milk truffles rolled in sprinkles), beijinhos (coconut-based bonbons), and quindim (a baked egg yolk and coconut custard). It is commonly served as a dessert platter or at celebrations, offering a selection of bite-sized treats often accompanied by coffee.

Spinach soup is prepared by cooking fresh or frozen spinach with stock or water, typically blended until smooth and creamy, often with the addition of onions, garlic, and sometimes potatoes or cream. It is commonly served hot as a starter or light main course in international cuisine, garnished with croutons, sour cream, or fresh herbs.

Galayet bandora is a Middle Eastern stewed dish made with tomatoes as the primary ingredient, typically cooked with onions, garlic, and spices until softened into a sauce-like consistency. It is commonly served as a side dish or mezze, often accompanied by bread for scooping, and may include additions such as peppers or herbs depending on regional variations.

Zatiruha is a Belarusian dish made from a dough prepared with wheat flour, rye flour, or buckwheat flour mixed with eggs and starch, which is then torn or grated into small pieces and cooked in boiling salted water or broth. It is traditionally served as a side dish or in soup, often accompanied by butter, sour cream, or gravy.

Kiluvõileib is an Estonian open-faced sandwich traditionally made with a slice of rye bread topped with smoked sprat or kiluvõid (sprat spread), typically garnished with sliced onion and sometimes butter. It is commonly served as a light meal, appetizer, or casual lunch item in Estonian cuisine.

Dawet is a sweet Indonesian beverage made from rice flour or green rice flour mixed with coconut milk, brown sugar syrup (gula Jawa), and ice. It is typically served cold as a refreshing drink, often accompanied by small sticky rice balls or eaten as a dessert, particularly popular during hot weather and Ramadan.

Garang asem is an Indonesian soup made by simmering meat (typically beef or chicken) with tamarind, turmeric, galangal, garlic, and chilies to create a sour and spicy broth. The dish is traditionally served hot as a main course, often accompanied by rice and vegetable additions such as cabbage or long beans.

Balado is a Padang cuisine dish in which meat is cooked in a spice paste made from red chilies, garlic, and shallots, creating a distinctly spicy and aromatic preparation. The dish is typically served as a main course with rice and is characterized by its rich, chile-forward flavor profile.

Kuwah itek is an Indonesian duck soup where duck meat is simmered in a savory broth typically seasoned with turmeric, garlic, shallots, and aromatic spices such as galangal and bay leaves. The dish is traditionally served in bowls as a main course, often accompanied by rice, and is popular in Indonesian cuisine as a warming, flavorful meal.

Banjar satay is an Indonesian grilled skewer dish from South Kalimantan featuring marinated chicken meat threaded onto skewers and cooked over charcoal or open flame. The dish is typically served with peanut sauce and rice, often as a main course or appetizer in Indonesian cuisine.

Chilly Gobi is a Indo-Chinese appetizer made from cauliflower florets that are battered, deep-fried until crispy, and then tossed with a spiced sauce typically containing soy sauce, vinegar, and chili peppers. The dish is commonly served as a starter or side dish in Indian restaurants and is known for its combination of crispy texture and spicy-tangy flavor profile.

Ukazi soup is a Nigerian dish made with ukazi leaves (also called African spinach), palm oil, and various proteins such as fish, meat, or seafood, simmered together with stock and seasonings. The soup is typically served hot with fufu, cassava, or other starchy accompaniments as a main course in West African cuisine.

Pav gathiya is a Gujarati snack consisting of fried spiral-shaped pieces of gram flour batter served alongside pav (soft bread rolls). The gathiya is made by piping seasoned gram flour dough into hot oil and frying until crispy, then typically eaten with pav as a breakfast or tea-time accompaniment.

Saag aloo is a Punjabi vegetable dish made by cooking potatoes with saag, a leafy green preparation typically made from spinach or mustard greens that has been wilted and blended into a creamy base. The dish is traditionally seasoned with spices and served as a curry accompaniment to breads such as roti or paratha.

Mapo nasu is a Japanese-Chinese dish featuring eggplant cooked in a spiced meat sauce, typically made with ground pork or beef combined with doubanjiang (spicy bean paste), garlic, ginger, and Sichuan peppercorns. The dish is served over steamed rice, with the tender eggplant absorbing the savory and numbing spiced sauce.

Macha is typically a finely ground green tea powder of Japanese origin, commonly whisked with hot water to produce a frothy, vivid green beverage, though in international contexts the name may also refer to dishes or drinks flavored with this ingredient. It is often served on its own as a traditional tea, blended into lattes with steamed milk, or incorporated into desserts such as ice cream, cakes, and pastries.

Ukwa is a traditional West African dish made with breadfruit that is boiled and mashed, then combined with fish, palm oil, potash, and bitter leaf to create a stew-like preparation seasoned with salt and capsicum. The dish is typically served as a main course and is common in Nigerian and other West African cuisines.

Pepperoni pizza consists of a yeasted dough base topped with tomato sauce, cheese, and sliced pepperoni, then baked in an oven until the crust is golden and the cheese melts. It is typically served hot, cut into slices, and consumed as a main course or casual meal.

A cheese omelet is prepared by beating eggs, cooking them in a pan with butter or oil until partially set, then folding in grated or sliced cheese before folding the omelet in half. It is typically served hot as a breakfast or brunch dish, often accompanied by bread, salad, or potatoes.

Golonka is a Polish braised pork knuckle or ham hock, typically simmered for several hours with vegetables such as onions, carrots, and celery, along with broth and spices until the meat becomes tender and falls from the bone. The dish is traditionally served hot with the braising liquid as a gravy, accompanied by potatoes, sauerkraut, or rye bread, and is common in Polish home cooking and regional cuisine.

A butter board consists of softened butter spread on a wooden board or plate and topped with a variety of ingredients such as herbs, spices, fleur de sel, and sometimes honey or jam. It is typically served at room temperature as an accompaniment to bread, crackers, or toasted bread slices for spreading.

A mushroom omelette is prepared by whisking eggs with milk or cream, pouring the mixture into a buttered pan, and folding in sautéed mushrooms before the eggs set completely. It is typically served hot as a breakfast or light lunch dish, often accompanied by bread, salad, or potatoes.

Grilled pig's foot is prepared by cleaning and seasoning the foot, then grilling it over direct heat until the skin is charred and crispy while the meat becomes tender. The dish is typically served as an appetizer or main course, often accompanied by sauce and vegetables, and is common in French and other European cuisines.

Katsu sando is a Japanese sandwich made with breaded and deep-fried cutlet, typically pork or chicken, placed between slices of soft white bread with tonkatsu sauce and cabbage slaw. The dish is commonly served as a casual meal or street food, often cut diagonally and wrapped in paper for convenient eating.

Gilgeori toast is a Korean street food consisting of sliced bread filled with seasoned vegetables, egg, and meat (typically ham or spam), then pan-fried until the exterior is golden and crispy. It is commonly served as a quick, affordable snack or light meal, often wrapped in paper for consumption on the go.

Babi guling is an Indonesian dish of suckling pig that is marinated in a paste of garlic, turmeric, galangal, and other spices before being roasted whole over charcoal until the skin is crispy. The roasted pig is typically chopped into pieces and served with rice, accompanied by sambal and other condiments, commonly found at celebrations and special occasions in Balinese cuisine.

Pickle relish is made by finely chopping pickled cucumbers and combining them with vinegar, spices, and sometimes other vegetables or sweeteners to create a chunky condiment. It is typically served as a topping for hot dogs, hamburgers, sandwiches, and other dishes to add tangy flavor and texture.

Vacherin glacé is a frozen dessert composed of layers of meringue alternating with ice cream or sorbet, typically assembled in a round mold and topped with whipped cream and garnishes such as fresh fruit or nuts. The dish is served frozen as an elegant plated dessert, often unmolded and sliced into portions at the table or in the kitchen.

A kebab sandwich consists of grilled or roasted meat—typically lamb, chicken, or beef—sliced and served in or with bread, often accompanied by vegetables such as tomatoes, onions, and lettuce, plus sauces like yogurt or tahini-based dressings. It is a popular street food and casual meal in Turkish cuisine and throughout the Middle East and Mediterranean regions.

Pigs in blankets are sausages wrapped in bacon and typically roasted or grilled until the bacon is crispy. This dish is commonly served as an appetizer, side dish, or party food in American and British cuisine.

Geki is a traditional Japanese confectionery (wagashi) typically prepared using ingredients such as sweet rice flour, sugar, and sometimes sweetened bean paste, resulting in a delicate, subtly sweet treat. It is commonly served in small portions alongside green tea, often enjoyed as a light dessert or during tea ceremonies and seasonal celebrations.

A lemon bar is a baked dessert consisting of a shortcrust pastry base topped with a tangy lemon custard or curd filling, typically finished with a dusting of powdered sugar. It is cut into squares and served as a portable sweet treat or dessert at room temperature or chilled.

Pad kaphrao is a stir-fried dish prepared by quickly cooking meat or seafood with fresh holy basil (Ocimum tenuiflorum), garlic, and chilies in a wok over high heat. The dish is typically served over steamed rice with a fried egg on top, commonly eaten as a quick lunch or dinner in Thailand.

Gaetteok is a Korean dish made from small, cylindrical rice cakes that are typically boiled or stir-fried with vegetables, meat, and a savory sauce. It is commonly served as a casual street food or side dish, often prepared in a spicy gochujang-based sauce or in clear broth.

Potato biryani is a rice dish prepared by layering parboiled basmati rice with potatoes that have been fried or roasted with spices, then cooking together using the dum pukht (sealed pot) method to allow flavors to meld. The dish is traditionally served as a complete meal, often accompanied by raita, pickles, and shorba (broth).

Yokh is a traditional Chechen dish made by stuffing cleaned intestines with a mixture of cornmeal, diced tail fat, and sautéed onions, then boiling or simmering until cooked through. The dish is typically served as a main course, often accompanied by broth or additional fat for richness.

Chicken biryani is a rice dish prepared by layering marinated chicken with partially cooked basmati rice, then cooking together in a sealed pot with aromatic spices such as cardamom, cinnamon, and bay leaves until both ingredients are fully cooked. The dish is traditionally served as a complete meal, often accompanied by raita (yogurt sauce) and pickled vegetables.

Rice soup is prepared by simmering rice in broth or water with vegetables, meat, or seafood until the rice softens and releases its starches, creating a creamy consistency. It is typically served hot as a light meal or comfort dish across various cuisines worldwide, often seasoned with salt, pepper, and herbs.

Ewa agoyin is a Nigerian street food made from mashed or pureed beans (typically black-eyed peas) cooked with onions, peppers, and spices, then topped with a spicy pepper sauce made from scotch bonnet peppers, tomatoes, and palm oil. The dish is typically served hot as a breakfast or light meal, often accompanied by bread or fried plantains.

Asinan Bogor is a traditional Indonesian vegetable pickle made by fermenting or soaking mixed vegetables such as cabbage, bean sprouts, and green papaya in a tangy solution of vinegar, salt, and spices, often including garlic and chilies. The dish is typically served as a cold side dish or condiment accompanying main meals, particularly common in the Bogor region of West Java.

Cilok Goreng is an Indonesian fried snack made from tapioca flour dough mixed with garlic and seasonings, formed into small balls or cylinders, and deep-fried until golden. It is typically served hot as a street food or casual appetizer, often accompanied by a peanut or chili dipping sauce.

Jenang grendul is an Indonesian sweet treat made from glutinous rice flour mixed with grated coconut and palm sugar, then cooked together until it reaches a dense, fudgy consistency. The mixture is typically rolled into small balls or formed into a log shape and served as a traditional dessert or snack, often enjoyed during special occasions and celebrations.

Deopbap is a Korean rice bowl topped with a fried egg and various vegetables such as spinach, carrots, and bean sprouts, which are seasoned individually and arranged over the rice. The dish is typically served with gochujang (red chili paste) on the side and mixed together before eating.

Lotus root patties are made by slicing or grating fresh lotus root, mixing it with seasonings and a binding agent, then pan-frying or deep-frying the mixture into patties until golden and crispy. These patties are typically served as a side dish or appetizer in Asian cuisine, often accompanied by dipping sauces.

Mutton biryani is a rice dish prepared by layering marinated mutton with partially cooked basmati rice, aromatics, and spices, then cooking together in a sealed pot using the dum pukht method until the meat and rice are fully cooked. The dish is traditionally served as a complete meal, often accompanied by raita, pickles, and shorba (broth).

Fishcake is a patty made from minced or flaked fish mixed with mashed potato and seasonings, then breaded and either fried or baked until golden. It is commonly served as a main course with chips and peas, or as a standalone item in British cuisine.

Hotchare is a regional confectionery from Kitami in Hokkaido, Japan, typically prepared as a sweet treat using local ingredients and traditional Japanese wagashi-making techniques. It is commonly enjoyed as a small dessert or souvenir snack, often served alongside green tea or offered as a gift representing the Kitami area.

Chahda is typically prepared as a savory dish that may feature a combination of seasoned meats or vegetables, often cooked with aromatic spices common to international fusion cuisine. It is commonly served warm, frequently accompanied by rice, flatbread, or a fresh side salad depending on regional interpretation.

Delhi biryani is prepared by layering partially cooked basmati rice with marinated meat (typically chicken, mutton, or beef), caramelized onions, yogurt, and spices including saffron, cardamom, and cinnamon, then cooking together in a sealed pot using the dum pukht method until fully cooked. The dish is traditionally served as a complete meal, often accompanied by raita, pickle, and shorba (broth).

Sapo tahu is an Indonesian dish made from tofu that is typically fried until golden and then served in a savory sauce made with soy sauce, garlic, and other seasonings. The dish is commonly served as a main course or side dish with steamed rice and is popular in Indonesian cuisine.

Pannfisch is a Northern German fish dish made by pan-frying a mixture of diced fish, potatoes, and onions together in a skillet until golden and crispy. It is typically served with a fried egg on top and accompanied by mustard or pickled beetroot as condiments.

Salmon pizza is prepared by spreading tomato sauce or crème fraîche on a pizza base, topping it with smoked or fresh salmon, and baking until the crust is crispy. It is typically finished with capers, red onions, dill, and a drizzle of olive oil, and served as a main course or appetizer.

Chả trứng is a Vietnamese egg cake made by mixing eggs with ground meat (typically pork), aromatics, and spices, then steaming the mixture in a loaf pan until set. The resulting savory cake is sliced and served as an appetizer or side dish, often accompanied by dipping sauce and fresh herbs.

Tava is a dish cooked on a flat griddle or cast-iron plate, typically featuring lamb meat combined with zucchini, onions, and tomatoes that are seared or griddled together. The ingredients are often cooked until lightly charred and served hot as a main course, commonly found in Middle Eastern and South Asian cuisines.

Rummaneyye is a Turkish dish made by cooking lentils with pomegranate juice, creating a sweet and tangy sauce that balances the earthiness of the legumes. It is typically served as a vegetable side dish or main course, often accompanied by rice or bread.

Bánh đậu xanh is a Vietnamese cake made from mung bean flour mixed with sugar and coconut milk, then steamed until set and typically cut into squares or rectangular pieces. The dessert is commonly served as a sweet snack or treat, often at room temperature or chilled, and features a pale green color with a dense, slightly chewy texture.

Egg fried rice is prepared by stir-frying cooked rice with beaten eggs, typically combined with vegetables, aromatics like garlic and onions, and soy sauce or other seasonings. This dish is commonly served as a main course or side dish in Asian cuisines and can be customized with proteins such as meat, seafood, or tofu.

Arroz con dulce is a sweet rice pudding made by cooking rice with coconut milk, spices such as cinnamon and cloves, and dried fruits like raisins and prunes until creamy. It is traditionally served as a dessert or special occasion dish in Puerto Rican and Caribbean cuisine, often garnished with cinnamon or additional dried fruits.

Luwombo is a traditional Ugandan dish prepared by wrapping meat, offal, or vegetables in banana leaves and steaming them in a pot, often with added spices and sometimes groundnut sauce. The dish is typically served as a main course with staple accompaniments such as matoke (cooked plantains) or rice.

Tagarninas are the edible thistle-like flower buds of Scolymus hispanicus, typically boiled or steamed until tender and served as a vegetable side dish or appetizer. This Spanish delicacy is commonly dressed with olive oil, garlic, and lemon, or incorporated into traditional regional preparations.

Kulesha is a Ukrainian dish made by cooking maize flour with boiling water and fat to form a porridge-like base, typically enriched with bacon, ham, and pork skin for flavor and texture. The dish is traditionally served hot, often topped with fried onions and smetana (sour cream), and seasoned with salt.

Peynirli yumurta is a Turkish dish of eggs cooked with cheese, typically prepared by scrambling or frying eggs and mixing them with crumbled white cheese such as feta or beyaz peynir. It is commonly served for breakfast or brunch, often accompanied by bread and fresh vegetables.

Mingau de tapioca is a creamy porridge made by cooking tapioca starch with milk, water, and sugar until thickened, often flavored with cinnamon or vanilla. It is typically served warm as a breakfast dish or dessert, with a smooth, pudding-like consistency.

Tonya is a traditional Valencian pastry, typically prepared as a sweet baked good using simple ingredients such as flour, sugar, eggs, and olive oil or butter. It is commonly enjoyed as a snack or dessert, often served alongside coffee or as part of a light breakfast.

Conch fritter is a Bahamian appetizer made by mixing minced conch meat with a seasoned batter and deep-frying until golden brown. These savory fritters are typically served hot as a starter or snack, often accompanied by a spicy dipping sauce.

Chuối nếp nướng is a Vietnamese dessert made from glutinous rice and banana that is grilled or roasted until the exterior caramelizes. The dish is typically served warm as a sweet snack or dessert, often enjoyed at street stalls or as part of traditional Vietnamese meals.

Smothered pork chops are prepared by searing seasoned pork chops and then braising them slowly in a savory sauce, typically made with onions, garlic, and broth until tender. The dish is traditionally served with the pork chops covered in the reduced gravy or sauce, often accompanied by rice, mashed potatoes, or vegetables.

Peanuts and Coke is a simple pairing combining roasted peanuts with Coca-Cola, typically served as a casual snack or beverage combination. The peanuts are eaten alongside or dunked into the cola, popular as an informal refreshment in various cultures.

Dhakai Biryani is a rice dish from Dhaka, Bangladesh, made by cooking basmati rice with marinated meat (typically mutton or chicken), potatoes, and aromatics including bay leaves, cinnamon, and cardamom using the dum pukht (slow-cooking) method. The dish is traditionally served with raita, salad, and pickles as an accompaniment.

aliases: ragout de porc a la szekely
description: Székely cabbage stew is a Hungarian-Slovak dish made by braising sauerkraut with pork (typically prepared as pörkölt), then finishing with sour cream for a rich, tangy sauce. The stew is traditionally served as a hearty main course, often accompanied by bread or potatoes.

Water buffalo pindang is an Indonesian dish made by simmering buffalo meat in a aromatic broth infused with turmeric, garlic, shallots, ginger, and chilies, creating a yellow-hued curry-like preparation. The dish is typically served with rice and is common in Indonesian cuisine, particularly in regions where water buffalo is raised.

Chitol machher peti kalia is a Bengali fish curry made with chitol fish (an Indian freshwater fish), typically prepared by cooking fish pieces with spices, onions, and a gravy base until the fish is tender and flavored throughout. The dish is traditionally served with steamed rice as part of Bengali meals, featuring a savory and mildly spiced sauce characteristic of Eastern Indian cuisine.

Eomuk is a Korean fish cake made from ground fish meat mixed with seasonings and binders, then shaped and either steamed, boiled, or fried. It is commonly served as a street food on skewers, in soups, or as a banchan (side dish) at meals.

Fish Kabiraji is a Bengali-origin dish consisting of fish fillets coated in seasoned batter and deep-fried until golden, typically served with a creamy, mildly spiced sauce made from yogurt and spices. The dish is commonly served as a main course with rice or bread and reflects the culinary traditions of Eastern India and Bangladesh.

Caldo galego is a traditional Galician soup made by simmering potatoes, beans, and leafy greens such as cabbage or turnip greens with broth and often including cured meats like chorizo or pancetta. The dish is typically served as a hearty first course or complete meal, particularly during colder months in the Galicia region of northwestern Spain.

Chakalaka is a South African vegetable relish made by cooking diced vegetables—primarily carrots, cabbage, onions, and tomatoes—together with beans and spices until softened and well-combined. The dish is typically served as a side accompaniment to grilled meats, maize porridge, or bread at informal meals and gatherings.

Ölsupa is a traditional Swedish beer soup made by simmering beer with sugar, spices such as cinnamon and cloves, and sometimes bread or rye bread, creating a warm, slightly sweet broth. The dish is typically served hot as a dessert or light meal, often during winter months and festive occasions.

Prażonki are small fried or roasted potato cubes, typically seasoned with salt and sometimes served with caramelized onions or mushrooms. This traditional Polish side dish is commonly served alongside meat mains or used as a component in larger composed dishes.

Ça va se savoir is typically a playfully named international dish whose exact composition varies by establishment, often featuring a chef's selection of seasonal ingredients prepared with techniques drawn from French and broader European cooking traditions. It is commonly served as a main course, plated with complementary sides or sauces, and is generally intended to surprise the diner with unexpected flavor combinations.

Botvinia is a Russian cold soup made from a kvass base combined with finely chopped vegetables such as sorrel, spinach, beets, and cucumbers, traditionally seasoned with mustard and served chilled. The dish is typically accompanied by boiled potatoes and fish, with the components mixed together or served separately for individual assembly at the table.

Curry udon consists of thick wheat noodles served in a curry-flavored broth made with onions, potatoes, and meat or vegetables, seasoned with curry powder or paste. The dish is typically served hot in a bowl and commonly found in Japanese casual dining establishments and curry restaurants.

Thin Mints are chocolate wafer cookies coated with a thin layer of mint-flavored chocolate. These cookies are typically sold by Girl Scouts as part of their annual cookie fundraising program and are served as a packaged snack or dessert.

Chelow kabab consists of grilled meat kebab served with steamed saffron-infused rice, typically accompanied by grilled tomatoes and onions. The dish is a staple of Iranian cuisine, with the meat marinated in onions and spices before being skewered and cooked over charcoal or open flame.

Pizokel is a traditional Occitan pasta dish made from flour dough that is rolled thin, cut into irregular pieces, and typically cooked by boiling or pan-frying. The dish is commonly served as a main course in southern France and neighboring regions, often accompanied by vegetables, cheese, or meat-based sauces.

Zarajo is a traditional Spanish dish consisting of lamb or beef intestines that are cleaned, seasoned, and typically grilled or fried until crispy on the outside. It is commonly served as a tapa or appetizer in Spanish cuisine, often accompanied by lemon wedges or a simple vinaigrette.

Maror is a bitter herb dish traditionally prepared by grating or chopping raw horseradish root, sometimes mixed with vinegar, apples, and beets to create a paste. It is served as part of the Passover Seder meal, where it symbolizes the bitterness of slavery and is typically consumed with matzo bread.

Lontong kupang is an Indonesian dish consisting of glutinous rice compressed and cooked in banana leaves, served with a broth made from clams (kupang) and aromatic spices including garlic, shallots, and chilies. The dish is typically enjoyed as a breakfast or light meal, with the soft rice cakes paired with the flavorful clam broth and garnished with fresh herbs.

Bolo-rainha is a Portuguese sweet bread made with flour, eggs, sugar, and yeast, studded with dried fruits throughout the dough. The bread is baked until golden and is typically served as a breakfast or afternoon snack, often accompanied by tea or coffee.

Gnocchi alla romana is made from a semolina and milk paste that is cooked, cooled, cut into rounds, and baked in layers with butter, cheese, and sometimes tomato sauce. It is a traditional Roman dish typically served as a primo (first course) and differs from potato-based gnocchi in both texture and preparation method.

Dandelion salad is prepared by collecting young dandelion greens, which are typically blanched or used raw, then dressed with vinegar, oil, and seasonings such as garlic and mustard. The dish is commonly served as a side salad or light starter course in European and Mediterranean cuisines, often accompanied by bacon, hard-boiled eggs, or croutons.

Gabus pucung is an Indonesian dish made from snakehead fish (gabus) that is typically prepared by frying or stewing the fish with a paste of shallots, garlic, chilies, and spices until the fish is tender and the flavors are absorbed. The dish is commonly served as a main course with steamed rice and is particularly popular in Central Java cuisine.

Yam kai yang is a spicy Thai salad made with roasted or grilled chicken that is shredded and mixed with lime juice, fish sauce, chilies, and fresh herbs including mint and cilantro. The dish is typically served at room temperature as an appetizer or light main course, often accompanied by sticky rice and raw vegetables.

Nopia is an Indonesian sweet snack made from a batter of flour and eggs that is fried in a waffle-like mold or pan, creating a crispy, golden exterior. The dish is typically served as a dessert or teatime treat, often accompanied by sweet dipping sauces or enjoyed plain.

Flemish-style asparagus consists of fresh asparagus spears typically blanched and served with a sauce made from hard-boiled eggs, melted butter, and sometimes breadcrumbs or parsley. The dish is traditionally served as a vegetable side course or light main course in Belgian and Dutch cuisine, often accompanied by boiled potatoes.

Tabernero is a Spanish-style tavern dish, typically featuring slow-cooked or braised meats such as pork or beef in a rich sauce made with wine, garlic, and aromatic herbs. It is commonly served with crusty bread or rustic potatoes, often enjoyed as hearty fare in casual dining settings alongside a glass of red wine.

Pfefferpotthast is a German beef stew made by braising chunks of beef with onions, vinegar, and spices including pepper and bay leaf, creating a rich, tangy sauce. The dish is traditionally served with boiled potatoes or bread and originates from the Rhineland region of Germany.

Tempe gembus is an Indonesian dish made from leftover soybean pulp (okara) that is pressed, fermented, and then typically fried until crispy or cooked with spices and aromatics. It is commonly served as a side dish or main component of rice meals, valued for its protein content and distinctive nutty flavor from the fermentation process.

Arròs al forn is a baked rice dish from Valencia, Spain, prepared by layering rice with meat (typically pork ribs, chicken, or rabbit), beans, and tomato sauce, then baking in the oven until the rice absorbs the liquid and forms a golden crust on top. The dish is traditionally served as a main course, often accompanied by aioli or a simple tomato garnish.

Mi ongklok is an Indonesian noodle dish from the Palembang region made by stir-frying wheat noodles with vegetables, eggs, and meat or seafood, then folding the mixture into a crispy fried crepe or wrapper. The dish is typically served as a complete meal, often accompanied by sambal and fresh lime wedges for flavor adjustment.

Nasi jagung is an Indonesian rice dish where corn kernels are mixed with or partially substitute for rice, typically cooked together with aromatics like garlic, shallots, and spices. The dish is commonly served as a main course, often accompanied by proteins, vegetables, and sambal, and represents a regional variation that makes use of locally available corn as a staple ingredient.

Gatot is an Indonesian dish made from grated unripe papaya that is mixed with grated coconut, chilies, and spices, then typically served as a fresh salad or side dish. It is commonly prepared in West Java and served alongside rice and main dishes, often eaten fresh without cooking.

Lontong kikil is an Indonesian dish consisting of compressed rice cake (lontong) served with beef tendon (kikil) cooked in a rich, savory broth made from spices and coconut milk. The dish is typically eaten as a main course or breakfast item, often served with sambal, vegetables, and broth spooned over the rice cake.

Kuru fasulye is a Turkish stew made from dried white beans that are soaked and then simmered with tomatoes, onions, and spices until tender, often finished with a tomato-based sauce and sometimes meat. The dish is traditionally served as a main course with rice or bread, and is a staple of Turkish home cooking and regional cuisine.

Kagoshima ramen features thin, straight noodles served in a broth typically made from pork or chicken stock, often with tonkotsu (pork bone) as a base, and is garnished with chashu pork, green onions, and occasionally sesame seeds. The dish is traditionally eaten as a hot noodle soup and is characteristic of the Kyushu region's ramen culture, particularly associated with Kagoshima Prefecture in Japan.

Cachupa is a traditional Cape Verdean stew made by slow-cooking corn (maize) with beans, fish or meat, and vegetables such as potatoes and onions until the ingredients break down into a thick, porridge-like consistency. The dish is typically served as a main course, either in a creamy version called cachupa doce or a brothier version called cachupa à moda, and is a staple comfort food in Cape Verdean cuisine.

Basbousa is a sweet semolina cake made from semolina flour, shredded coconut, and sugar, formed into diamond or square shapes and topped with a whole almond or pine nut before baking. The baked cakes are then soaked in simple sugar syrup or honey, resulting in a moist, dense pastry commonly served as a dessert or snack in Egyptian cuisine.

Rump steak is a cut of beef from the hindquarters that is typically grilled or pan-seared at high heat until browned on the outside. The meat is seasoned with salt and peppercorn, then served as a main course, often accompanied by vegetables or potatoes.

A s'more is assembled by toasting a marshmallow over heat, typically a campfire or flame, then sandwiching it with a piece of chocolate between two graham crackers. The heat from the marshmallow softens the chocolate, creating a melted filling, and this dessert is traditionally eaten as a casual outdoor treat.

Bssisa is a North African (particularly Moroccan and Tunisian) mixture of toasted grains, legumes, and nuts that are ground together into a powder, often including barley, chickpeas, and almonds. It is typically prepared by mixing the powder with butter, honey, and sometimes eggs to form a paste, then consumed as a breakfast dish or energy food, often eaten by hand or with bread.

Kidyo is a traditional candy from the southwest coast of India, typically prepared by slow-cooking ingredients such as coconut, jaggery or sugar, and sometimes rice flour or ghee until the mixture thickens and can be shaped into small pieces. It is commonly served as a sweet treat during festivals, family gatherings, or as an after-meal confection.

Lekkerbekje is a Dutch fried fish snack consisting of small whole fish, typically smelt or other white fish, that are cleaned, coated in seasoned batter, and deep-fried until golden. It is traditionally served hot as a casual street food or appetizer, often accompanied by lemon wedges and eaten with the hands.

Powidltascherl is an Austrian pastry consisting of a thin dough wrapper filled with plum butter (powidl) and typically rolled or folded into a cylindrical or rectangular shape before baking until golden. It is commonly served as a dessert or sweet snack, often dusted with powdered sugar and traditionally accompanied by coffee or tea.

Pig head is prepared by cleaning and cooking the entire head or portions of it, typically through boiling, roasting, or braising until the meat becomes tender and can be easily separated from the bone. The dish is served in various forms across cuisines—as sliced meat with aspic, shredded in salads, in soups, or as part of traditional charcuterie—and utilizes the meat, skin, and offal for maximum use of the animal.

Hyderabadi biryani is a rice dish prepared by layering parboiled basmati rice with marinated meat (traditionally goat or chicken), caramelized onions, yogurt, and spices, then slow-cooked in a sealed pot using the dum pukht method to allow flavors to infuse. The dish is typically served with raita (yogurt-based condiment) and pickle as accompaniments.

Ipoh white coffee is prepared by roasting coffee beans with margarine and condensed milk, then ground and brewed with hot water. The drink is traditionally served hot or iced with sweetened condensed milk and evaporated milk, creating a creamy beverage characteristic of Ipoh, Malaysia.

Dal baati is a traditional Indian dish consisting of baked wheat flour balls (baati) served with a spiced lentil curry (dal), typically prepared with chana dal, moong dal, or arhar dal seasoned with cumin, coriander, and turmeric. The dish originates from Rajasthan and is traditionally served with ghee, often accompanied by churma (a sweet crumbly preparation made from wheat flour and jaggery).

Sayur asem is an Indonesian sour vegetable soup made by simmering vegetables such as cabbage, green beans, and unripe papaya in a broth flavored with tamarind, garlic, shallots, and chilies. The dish is typically served as an everyday accompaniment to rice and protein dishes in Indonesian cuisine.

Dhansak is a Parsi curry prepared by cooking lentils with meat (traditionally mutton or chicken) and spices, then finishing with caramelized onions and a sweet element such as jaggery or lentil puree. The dish is typically served with rice or bread and combines savory, sweet, and lightly sour flavors characteristic of Parsi cuisine.

A mitraillette is a Belgian sandwich made from a split baguette or crusty roll filled with sliced meats (typically salami or ham), cheese, and vegetables such as lettuce and tomatoes, often finished with mayonnaise or other condiments. It is a popular street food and casual meal in Belgium and neighboring regions, typically served as a hand-held sandwich.

Khichri is a one-pot dish made by cooking rice and lentils together with water or broth until both components are soft and blended into a cohesive mixture. It is typically seasoned with ghee, cumin, and salt, and is commonly served as comfort food or a mild, easily digestible meal in Indian cuisine.

Sorrel soup is a Russian dish made by simmering fresh sorrel leaves with potatoes, onions, and broth until tender, then finished with sour cream or egg yolk to create a tangy, creamy consistency. It is traditionally served hot as a first course, often accompanied by rye bread and a dollop of sour cream.

Vine leaf rolls are prepared by wrapping seasoned filling, typically containing rice, herbs, and garlic, in preserved or fresh grape leaves, then rolling them tightly and cooking by steaming or boiling. This dish is commonly served as an appetizer or part of meze platters in Palestinian cuisine, often at room temperature or chilled.

Sofrito is a Greek stew prepared by slowly simmering meat, typically beef or lamb, with tomatoes, onions, and garlic until tender and deeply flavored. The dish is traditionally served with crusty bread or over rice, and is common in Greek cuisine as a hearty main course.

Chewz is typically a casual, snack-style dish whose name suggests bite-sized, chewy morsels, often prepared by frying, baking, or grilling a base of dough, meat, or plant-based ingredients until they reach a satisfying texture. It is commonly served as a shareable appetizer or street-food-style portion, frequently accompanied by dipping sauces such as sweet chili, garlic mayo, or soy-based condiments.

Curd rice is prepared by mixing cooked rice with yogurt and tempering it with mustard seeds, curry leaves, and green chilies in ghee or oil. It is typically served as a cooling side dish or light meal in South Indian cuisine, often accompanied by pickle or pappadum.

Huevos rancheros consists of fried eggs served on warm corn or flour tortillas and topped with a savory tomato-based salsa, typically containing onions, cilantro, and chiles. The dish is traditionally served for breakfast or brunch, often accompanied by refried beans, rice, and additional tortillas on the side.

Pâté de campagne is a coarse country-style pâté made from pork liver, meat, and fat layered with whole pieces of meat and seasoned with herbs and spices, then cooked in a terrine mold. It is typically served chilled or at room temperature as an appetizer, sliced and accompanied by bread, cornichons, and mustard.

Polbo á feira is a traditional Galician dish of boiled octopus served at room temperature, typically seasoned with paprika, sea salt, and olive oil. The octopus is usually cut into pieces and presented on a wooden plate, commonly served as a tapa or appetizer in Galician cuisine.

Ayam Taliwang is an Indonesian dish from West Nusa Tenggara featuring chicken that is crushed or flattened, then fried until crispy and coated in a spiced paste made from shallots, garlic, chilies, shrimp paste, and turmeric. The dish is traditionally served hot as a main course, often accompanied by rice and sambal.

Salt potatoes are small whole potatoes boiled in heavily salted water, a traditional preparation from Syracuse, New York. They are typically served hot with melted butter and are commonly eaten as a side dish or snack, particularly in upstate New York cuisine.

Weckewerk is a Hessian preservation technique where vegetables, particularly green beans, are packed into glass jars with a vinegar-based brine and then heat-processed to create shelf-stable canned goods. The dish is typically served cold as a side dish or appetizer, maintaining its crisp texture and tangy flavor from the pickling process.

canonicalName: judias de el barco de avila
displayName: Judías de El Barco de Ávila
aliases: judias de el barco de avila
description: Judías de El Barco de Ávila are a traditional Spanish dish featuring beans from the El Barco de Ávila region in Castile and León, typically prepared by slow-cooking the beans with aromatic vegetables and cured meats such as chorizo or jamón. The dish is commonly served as a hearty main course or substantial starter, often accompanied by crusty bread to soak up the flavorful cooking liquid.

Alouettes sans tête are thin slices of meat, typically beef or veal, rolled with a filling of breadcrumbs, herbs, and sometimes anchovy or bacon, then browned and braised in wine or broth until tender. The dish is traditionally served hot with the cooking liquid or gravy, often accompanied by vegetables such as root vegetables or mushrooms.

Lemper is an Indonesian rice cake made by wrapping glutinous rice around a filling of seasoned chicken or other protein, then encasing the entire roll in a banana leaf before steaming. It is commonly served as a snack or appetizer, often at celebrations and social gatherings.

aliases: austrian giant bread dumpling prepared in a napkin
description: Serviettenknödel is an Austrian and Bavarian dumpling made from a mixture of bread, eggs, milk, and finely chopped parsley or other herbs, typically wrapped in a cloth napkin (serviette) and poached in broth. The cooked dumpling is sliced and served as a side dish or in soup, often accompanying meat dishes or stews.

Dezhou braised chicken is a Chinese dish from Shandong province made by braising whole or halved chicken in a soy-based sauce with spices, typically including star anise, bay leaves, and other aromatics until the meat becomes tender and deeply browned. The dish is traditionally served at room temperature or chilled, sliced into pieces, and is commonly eaten as a cold appetizer or main course in Chinese cuisine.

Beggar's chicken is a Chinese dish where chicken is wrapped in lotus leaves and a paste of clay or dough, then baked until the exterior hardens into a shell that is cracked open at the table. The chicken is traditionally seasoned with soy sauce, ginger, and other aromatics before wrapping, and the cooking method traps steam to keep the meat moist and infuse it with the flavors of the herbs and leaves.

Nyanya is an ancient Russian dish traditionally prepared by stuffing a sheep's stomach with buckwheat porridge, pieces of mutton, brains, and offal, then slowly baking it in a Russian oven until the filling is tender and richly flavored. It is typically served hot as a hearty main course, often accompanied by rye bread and commonly associated with festive rural meals in historical Russian cuisine.

Adjarian khachapuri is a boat-shaped cheese bread from the Adjara region of Georgia, made with a yeasted dough filled with melted cheese (typically suluguni) and topped with a raw egg and butter that are stirred into the cheese while still hot. It is typically served as a warm appetizer or main course, often accompanied by fresh vegetables and dairy products.

Schwedenbombe is a frozen dessert consisting of layers of ice cream and sponge cake, typically topped with meringue and baked briefly until golden. This Swedish-inspired bomb-shaped dessert is traditionally served as a cold sweet course, often accompanied by berry compote or fruit sauce.

Pollo a la Villeroy is a breaded and deep-fried chicken dish where chicken pieces are coated in a béchamel-based sauce mixed with finely minced chicken or ham, then breaded and fried until golden. It is typically served as a main course, often accompanied by fried potatoes or vegetables.

Chicken à la King is a creamed chicken dish typically made with diced or shredded cooked chicken combined with a béchamel or cream sauce, often enriched with egg yolks and containing vegetables such as mushrooms, pimentos, and peas. The dish is traditionally served over toast points, rice, or in a pastry shell as an entrée.

Tanghulu is a Chinese street snack made by threading fresh fruit, typically hawthorn berries or other small fruits, onto bamboo skewers and coating them with a crispy sugar syrup that hardens into a glossy candy shell. The dish is traditionally served at room temperature as a sweet treat or dessert, with the contrast between the crunchy caramelized exterior and soft fruit interior.

Kanchakalar kopta is a Bengali curry made from banana flower buds that are boiled, mashed, and cooked with mustard oil, onions, and spices including turmeric and cumin. The dish is traditionally served with rice or flatbread as part of Bengali cuisine and is common during monsoon season when banana flowers are in season.

Amrar ombol is a Bengali sour dish made with hog plum (amra), cooked with mustard, green chilies, and spices to produce a tangy, slightly pungent condiment or side dish. It is typically served alongside rice as part of a multi-course Bengali meal, functioning as a palate-cleansing sour accompaniment.

Fulkopir dalna is a Bengali vegetarian curry made by cooking cauliflower florets with potatoes in a spiced sauce typically containing mustard oil, cumin, ginger, turmeric, and tomatoes. It is commonly served as a main course dish alongside steamed rice or flatbreads in Bengali households and is a staple preparation during the winter months when cauliflower is in season.

Khesarir teto dal is a Bengali preparation made from khesari lentils (Lathyrus sativus) cooked with bitter ingredients, typically including neem leaves or other bitter greens, which give the dish its characteristic bitter flavor profile. It is traditionally served as the first course of a Bengali meal, consumed before other dishes as the bitter taste is believed to stimulate digestion and appetite.

Chirer payesh is a Bengali dessert made by simmering flattened rice (chira or poha) in sweetened milk, typically flavored with cardamom, and often garnished with nuts or raisins. It is commonly served chilled or at room temperature as a festive or ceremonial dish during religious occasions and celebrations in Bengali households.

Gota muger dal is a Bengali dish made with whole mung beans (moong dal) that are slow-cooked, typically with mustard oil, ginger, bay leaves, and warming spices such as cumin and turmeric, resulting in a thicker texture than split-lentil preparations. It is commonly served as part of a multi-course meal alongside rice, and is particularly associated with traditional Bengali households and festive or ritual dining contexts.

Koraishunti vate is a Bengali dish made by cooking fresh green peas with rice, typically prepared by boiling or steaming the two together until soft. It is a simple, starchy preparation commonly eaten as a light meal or side dish, seasoned minimally with salt and sometimes a small amount of ghee or oil.

Kumro vate is a Bengali preparation made by boiling or steaming pieces of pumpkin (kumro) until soft, then mashing them and typically seasoning with mustard oil, salt, and green chilies. It is commonly served as a simple side dish alongside rice and lentils as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Lau Chhenchki is a Bengali dry stir-fry dish made from thinly sliced bottle gourd (lau) cooked with mustard seeds, dried red chilies, turmeric, and a small amount of oil until tender. It is typically served as a light side dish alongside rice and dal in everyday Bengali meals.

Tsupukka is a traditional Karelian pie dish, typically prepared with a thin rye or wheat-based crust that is filled and baked until golden. It commonly includes hearty fillings such as potato, rice porridge, or fish, and is often served warm as a savory snack or accompaniment to meals, sometimes topped with butter or egg butter.

Chbâh es' Safra Khobza is typically a North African-inspired dish featuring a saffron-scented stew or tagine, commonly prepared with slow-cooked meat such as lamb or chicken, onions, cinnamon, and dried fruits like prunes or apricots. It is often served alongside khobza, a traditional round flatbread used to scoop up the sauce, making it a shared centerpiece at communal meals.

canonicalName: muger dal o kanthal bichi vate
displayName: Muger dal o kanthal bichi vate
aliases: muger dal o kanthal bichi vate
description: Muger dal o kanthal bichi vate is a Bengali dish combining cooked moong dal (split mung lentils) with jackfruit seeds (kanthal bichi), which are boiled or simmered together with spices such as turmeric and often tempered with mustard seeds or dried chilies. It is typically served as a vegetarian side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Mulor shukto is a Bengali bitter-tasting vegetable dish made primarily with white radish (mulo) cooked in a mildly bitter gravy that typically includes mustard paste, ginger, and sometimes radhuni or other spices, with a characteristic touch of bitterness balanced by a small amount of milk or ghee. It is commonly served as a first course at the beginning of a traditional Bengali meal, eaten with plain steamed rice.

Mug daler papad is a thin, crisp wafer made from ground mung dal (split mung beans) mixed with spices such as black pepper and salt, then shaped into rounds and sun-dried before being stored. It is typically prepared by dry-roasting or deep-frying just before serving and is commonly eaten as a snack or accompaniment to meals in South Asian cuisine.

Panchmishali shukto is a Bengali bitter vegetable dish prepared by cooking a mixture of five vegetables — typically bitter gourd, drumstick, raw banana, eggplant, and white radish — in a mustard and poppy seed paste with a tempering of radhuni or mustard seeds and dried red chilies. It is a traditional dish from West Bengal and Bangladesh, typically served as the first course of a Bengali meal alongside rice, as the bitterness is believed to stimulate digestion.

Motor dal er bori are dried lentil dumplings made from ground yellow split peas (motor dal) that are shaped into small rounds or cylinders and sun-dried until firm. They are a traditional Bengali ingredient used in vegetable dishes and curries, where they are typically fried before being added to the preparation, contributing a chewy texture and nutty flavor.

Palong o methi dal is a Bengali lentil dish prepared by cooking dal (typically masoor or other split lentils) with palong shak (spinach) and methi (fenugreek leaves), seasoned with mustard oil, turmeric, and a tempering of garlic or dried red chilies. It is commonly served as part of a multi-course Bengali meal, accompanied by steamed rice.

Sojne fuler sukto is a Bengali dish made with drumstick flowers (Moringa oleifera blossoms) cooked in a mildly bitter mixed vegetable preparation, typically seasoned with radhuni or mustard seeds, dried ginger, and often finished with milk to balance the bitterness. It is served as a starter course with steamed rice in traditional Bengali meals, following the cultural practice of beginning a meal with a bitter dish.

Tomato diye muger dal is a Bengali lentil dish made by cooking split yellow mung dal (moong dal) with ripe tomatoes, turmeric, and a tempering of mustard seeds, dried red chilies, and sometimes ginger or garlic in oil or ghee. It is typically served as part of a multi-course meal alongside steamed rice, often accompanied by other vegetable dishes or fried items in Bengali household cuisine.

Deshi kakrar tok is a Bengali sour crab dish in which freshwater or mud crabs are cooked with tamarind or sour ingredients, mustard, turmeric, and green chilies to produce a tangy, spiced gravy. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice in Bengali households.

Bread salad is prepared by combining torn or cubed pieces of bread—often stale or toasted—with fresh vegetables such as tomatoes, cucumbers, and onions, dressed with olive oil and vinegar or similar acidic dressings. It is served as a side dish or light main course and appears in various regional forms across multiple cuisines, with well-known versions including the Italian panzanella and Middle Eastern fattoush.

Tiborna is a traditional Portuguese bread-based dish made by soaking or layering thick slices of rustic bread with olive oil, garlic, and toppings such as tomatoes, sardines, or other ingredients depending on the regional variation. It is typically served as a simple meal or snack, often prepared in a clay dish or earthenware vessel, and is common in rural and home-cooking contexts throughout Portugal.

Boulaghmane is a traditional Western Saharan dish made from camel meat slow-cooked with vegetables and spices, typically prepared in a communal pot over an open fire. It is commonly served at gatherings and festive occasions among Sahrawi communities, often accompanied by bread or eaten directly from a shared vessel.

Dumurer Chhenchki is a Bengali dry stir-fry preparation made with unripe fig (dumur) cut into small pieces and cooked with mustard seeds, dried red chilies, turmeric, and oil until tender. It is typically served as a side dish alongside rice and dal in a traditional Bengali meal.

Brenda Bunson is typically presented as a soft, oblong hot dog bun, commonly made from enriched wheat dough that is lightly sweetened and baked until golden. It is usually served split down the center to cradle a grilled sausage, often accompanied by condiments such as mustard, ketchup, or relish in a casual, handheld format.

Kochushaker ghonto is a Bengali vegetarian dish made by finely chopping or shredding colocasia (taro) leaves and stems, which are then cooked with mustard oil, spices such as turmeric and cumin, and often combined with grated coconut or lentils. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Mulo Chhenchki is a Bengali dry stir-fry dish made primarily from thinly sliced white radish (mulo/mooli) cooked with mustard oil, panch phoron (a five-spice blend), dried red chilies, and turmeric. It is typically prepared as a simple side dish and served alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Palong saker ghonto is a Bengali dry-style vegetable dish made by cooking spinach (palong shak) with ingredients such as grated coconut, green chilies, mustard, and sometimes small pieces of fried lentil dumplings or potatoes, all sautéed together until the moisture evaporates. It is typically served as a side dish alongside rice in a traditional Bengali meal.

Panchmishali ghonto is a Bengali vegetarian dish prepared by cooking a mixture of five or more vegetables together—commonly including raw banana, drumstick, yam, eggplant, and legumes—along with spices such as turmeric, cumin, and bay leaf, often finished with a tempering of panch phoron (five-spice blend). The dish is typically served as a side with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal, and it is particularly associated with festive or ritual dining occasions.

Thor Chhenchki is a Bengali dry stir-fry dish made from finely chopped banana blossom (the inner core of the banana flower stem, known as thor), cooked with mustard oil, panch phoron (a five-spice blend), green chilies, turmeric, and grated coconut. It is typically served as a side dish alongside rice and dal in traditional Bengali meals.

Sojne dantar chhechki is a Bengali dry stir-fry dish made from drumstick stems (the fibrous stalk portions of the Moringa tree), cooked with mustard oil, panch phoron (five-spice blend), dried red chilies, and turmeric. It is typically served as a side dish alongside rice and dal in a traditional Bengali meal.

Sojne fuler chhechki is a dry stir-fry dish from Bengali cuisine made with drumstick flowers (Moringa oleifera blossoms), typically cooked with mustard oil, nigella seeds, green chilies, and basic spices. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal, particularly during the spring season when drumstick flowers are available.

Lau-Bhadale Chhenchki is a Bengali dry stir-fry dish made from bottle gourd (lau) cut into small pieces and cooked with mustard seeds, dried red chilies, turmeric, and sometimes nigella seeds in oil until tender. It is typically served as a simple side dish alongside steamed rice and dal in everyday Bengali meals.

Peyanj-koli Chhenchki is a Bengali dry stir-fry dish made with onion stalks (peyanj-koli) cooked with mustard oil, dried red chilies, nigella seeds (kalonji), turmeric, and salt using a quick sauté technique. It is typically served as a simple vegetable side dish alongside rice and dal in everyday Bengali meals.

Shasha Chhenchki is a Bengali dry stir-fry dish made from diced cucumber (shasha) cooked with mustard seeds, dried red chilies, turmeric, and often panch phoron (a five-spice blend), finished with a small amount of oil. It is typically served as a side dish alongside rice and dal in a traditional Bengali meal.

Mulo diye Bhetki machher chhenchki is a Bengali dry-style stir-fry dish made with pieces of Bhetki (Barramundi or Asian sea bass) and radish (mulo), cooked together with mustard oil, turmeric, dried red chilies, and other spices until the moisture is largely reduced. It is typically prepared as a home-style side dish served alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Alu kumror chenchki is a Bengali dry stir-fry made with diced potato (alu) and pumpkin (kumro) cooked together with mustard seeds, dried red chilies, turmeric, and sometimes panch phoron (a five-spice blend). The vegetables are cooked until tender and lightly browned, and the dish is typically served as a side accompaniment with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Chal kumror chenchki is a Bengali dry stir-fry dish made from unripe or semi-ripe ash gourd (white pumpkin) cut into small pieces and cooked with mustard oil, panch phoron (a five-spice blend), dried red chilies, turmeric, and salt. The vegetable pieces are sautéed until tender and lightly browned, and the dish is typically served as a side accompaniment with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Shaplar ghonto is a Bengali dry-style vegetable dish prepared by cooking water lily stems (shapla) with grated coconut, mustard paste, and spices such as turmeric and green chilies, often finished with a tempering of panch phoron. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Roncadin is an Italian-style pizza associated with a producer based in Meduno, Italy, typically prepared with a leavened wheat-flour base topped with tomato sauce, mozzarella, and a variety of regional ingredients such as cured meats, vegetables, or herbs. It is commonly baked at high temperatures and often served as a ready-made or frozen product, making it a convenient option enjoyed as a main course in casual dining settings.

Beet gajor chechki is a Bengali stir-fried vegetable dish made with diced or grated beetroot and carrots cooked together with mustard seeds, dried red chilies, turmeric, and oil until tender. It is typically served as a side dish alongside rice and dal as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Dim er chorchori is a Bengali dry-style stir-fry dish made with boiled or fried eggs cooked together with potatoes, onions, green chilies, and spices such as turmeric and cumin in a small amount of oil until the ingredients are well-coated and semi-dry. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice or flatbreads in everyday Bengali home cooking.

Dim sorshe is a Bengali dish in which eggs are cooked in a mustard-based sauce made from ground mustard seeds, green chilies, turmeric, and oil, typically prepared by simmering hard-boiled or lightly fried eggs in the pungent mustard gravy. It is commonly served with steamed rice as part of a multi-course meal in Bengali households.

Chapor ghonto is a Bengali vegetarian dish made by cooking lentil patties (chapor), typically prepared from ground masoor dal seasoned with spices and pan-fried or dried, together with vegetables such as radish, eggplant, or raw banana in a spiced mustard-based gravy. It is traditionally served as part of a multi-course Bengali meal, accompanied by steamed rice.

Hanser dim begun diye jhol is a Bengali-style light curry (jhol) prepared by simmering duck eggs and eggplant (begun) in a thin, spiced broth typically seasoned with turmeric, cumin, and green chilies. It is commonly served as a main dish alongside steamed rice in Bengali household cooking.

Dim er mamlet dalna is a Bengali dish in which eggs are first fried to form an omelette, then cut into pieces and simmered in a spiced gravy made with tomatoes, onions, ginger, garlic, and a blend of cumin, coriander, turmeric, and garam masala. It is typically served as a main course alongside steamed rice or flatbreads such as roti.

Ol dantar ghonto is a Bengali dish made from taro stems (ol danta), cooked with mustard paste, green chilies, and spices in a dry or semi-dry preparation. It is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Kolmi shaaker ghonto is a Bengali dry stir-fry preparation made with water spinach (kolmi shak), typically cooked with mustard oil, garlic, green chilies, and often combined with small shrimp or fish, seasoned with turmeric and salt. It is served as a side dish alongside steamed rice in traditional Bengali meals.

Shaag Ghonto is a Bengali vegetarian dish prepared by finely chopping leafy greens—commonly spinach or other seasonal greens—and cooking them down with mustard oil, panch phoron (a five-spice blend), and often grated coconut, dried lentil dumplings (bori), or other vegetables until the mixture reaches a thick, semi-dry consistency. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Kola patai dim paturi is a Bengali preparation in which eggs are wrapped in banana leaves along with a spiced mixture and then steamed or cooked over low heat, allowing the flavors to infuse into the egg. It is typically served as a starter or side dish, drawing on the paturi technique common in Bengali cuisine where ingredients are enclosed in banana leaf parcels for cooking.

Pitcher plant pulut is a traditional dish from Malaysia, particularly associated with Sabah and Sarawak, in which glutinous rice (pulut) is packed inside the hollow tubes of pitcher plants (Nepenthes species) and cooked, often by boiling or steaming, until the rice is tender. The natural vessel imparts a subtle flavour to the rice, and the dish is typically served as a snack or side dish, sometimes accompanied by coconut milk or other seasonings.

Penne Siciliano is a pasta dish made with penne pasta tossed in a sauce combining tomatoes, eggplant, olives, capers, and often anchovies or tuna, drawing on traditional Sicilian flavors. It is typically served as a main course or first course and reflects the ingredient profile of the caponata-style preparations common to Sicilian cuisine.

Baked ziti is an Italian-American pasta dish made with ziti (or a similar tubular pasta such as bucatini), tomato sauce, and cheese—commonly ricotta, mozzarella, and Parmesan—layered in a baking dish and cooked in an oven until the top is browned and bubbling. It is typically served as a main course and is a common dish at family gatherings and casual dining settings.

Hyugameshi is a traditional Japanese rice dish from Miyazaki Prefecture in which cooked rice is mixed with ingredients such as chicken, burdock root, carrots, and seasoned with soy sauce and mirin. It is commonly served as a home-style or regional meal, often prepared by simmering the ingredients together with the rice or combining pre-cooked components before serving.

Godiya Special Bread is typically a bakery-style loaf prepared by kneading wheat flour with yeast, water, and a touch of sugar and salt, then baked until golden and soft on the inside. It is commonly served sliced as an accompaniment to meals, used for sandwiches, or enjoyed with butter, jam, or spreads at breakfast.

Amra makha is a Bengali spiced preparation made from amra (hog plum), which is mashed or roughly crushed and mixed with mustard oil, salt, green chili, and sometimes onion or dried chili. It is typically served as a side condiment or light snack, eaten alongside rice or as a palate-stimulating accompaniment in Bengali households.

Chunga Pitha is a traditional rice cake from Bangladesh and Northeast India, prepared by filling seasoned rice paste or glutinous rice into hollow bamboo tubes, which are then roasted over an open fire until cooked. The dish is commonly served during festivals and cultural celebrations, and the bamboo imparts a distinctive smoky flavor to the cooked rice interior.

aliases: tōo sio gue h ta a noodles
tu hsiao yue tan tsai noodles
description: Ta-a noodles (擔仔麵) are a Taiwanese noodle dish originating from Tainan, consisting of thin wheat noodles served in a small portion of pork broth seasoned with shrimp paste, topped with minced pork, a shrimp, bean sprouts, and a half hard-boiled egg. The dish is traditionally associated with street vendors and is commonly served as a light snack or between-meal dish rather than a full entrée.

Gobindo bhoger bhaat is a Bengali rice dish prepared using Gobindobhog, a short-grain aromatic rice variety native to West Bengal, India, cooked with ghee, bay leaves, and sometimes mild spices such as cardamom and cloves. It is traditionally served as a prasad (religious offering) in Hindu temples and during festivals, particularly associated with the worship of Lord Vishnu (Gobindo), and is also prepared for auspicious household occasions.

Takana meshi is a Japanese rice dish prepared by mixing cooked white rice with pickled or sautéed takana, a pungent mustard green (Brassica juncea var. integrifolia) that is typically salted and fermented or stir-fried with sesame oil and soy sauce. It is commonly served as a staple meal or side dish in Kyushu, Japan, where takana pickles are a regional specialty.

Pundi are steamed rice dumplings originating from the coastal Karnataka region of India, made by shaping a cooked rice flour dough into round balls. They are typically served for breakfast or as a snack, accompanied by chutneys, sambar, or coconut-based gravies.

Bandhakopir payesh is a Bengali dessert pudding made by cooking finely shredded or grated cabbage (bandhakopi) in milk along with sugar and often flavored with cardamom, bay leaf, or other aromatics until the mixture thickens to a creamy consistency. It is typically served chilled or at room temperature as a sweet course during meals or on ceremonial occasions.

Anarosher payesh is a Bengali rice pudding prepared by simmering rice in milk with sugar until thickened, then incorporating fresh pineapple (anaros) pieces or juice to add a fruity flavor. It is typically served chilled or at room temperature as a dessert during festive occasions and celebrations in Bengali households.

Bonder payesh is a Bengali rice pudding made by simmering rice in full-fat milk until the mixture thickens, then sweetened with sugar or condensed milk and flavored with cardamom, bay leaves, and sometimes saffron or rose water. It is traditionally served chilled or at room temperature as a dessert during festivals, religious occasions, and celebratory meals in Bengali-speaking regions.

Kaon payesh is a traditional rice pudding from Assam, India, prepared by slowly simmering glutinous rice (bora saul) in milk with sugar until the mixture thickens to a creamy consistency. It is commonly made using fragrant indigenous rice varieties and is typically served during festivals, religious ceremonies, and special occasions in Assamese culture.

Lau er payesh is a Bengali dessert pudding made by cooking bottle gourd (lau) with milk, sugar, and typically flavored with cardamom and bay leaves until the mixture thickens to a creamy consistency. It is served chilled or at room temperature, often as a sweet course during festive meals or special occasions in Bengali households.

Luchir payesh is a Bengali dish combining luchi, which are deep-fried unleavened flatbreads made from refined wheat flour, served with payesh, a sweetened rice pudding cooked in milk and flavored with cardamom and sometimes garnished with nuts or raisins. This combination is traditionally served at festive occasions, religious ceremonies, and celebrations in Bengali households.

Sujir payesh is a Bengali rice pudding variant made by cooking semolina (suji) in milk with sugar, often flavored with cardamom, bay leaves, and sometimes garnished with raisins or nuts. It is typically served as a dessert or sweet dish during festivals, religious occasions, and celebratory meals in Bengali households.

Mefun is a Japanese dish consisting of the kidneys of salmon or other fish, which are cleaned, salted, and typically fermented or seasoned with soy sauce and sake. It is commonly served as a small side dish or accompaniment in Japanese cuisine, often alongside rice or as part of an izakaya spread.

Fabiola is a traditional bread from the Castile region of Spain, typically prepared with wheat flour, water, yeast, and salt, and baked to achieve a firm crust with a soft interior. It is commonly served as an accompaniment to meals, often alongside stews, cured meats, or cheeses characteristic of Spanish cuisine.

Dhone pata bata is a Bengali paste preparation made by grinding fresh coriander leaves (dhone pata) with green chilies, garlic, and sometimes mustard or other aromatics using a stone grinder or similar method. It is typically served as a condiment or side accompaniment alongside rice, dal, or fish dishes in Bengali cuisine.

Mochar chop is a Bengali croquette made from shredded banana blossom (mocha) mixed with spices such as cumin, garam masala, and green chili, shaped into patties or oval forms, coated in breadcrumbs, and deep-fried until golden. It is commonly served as a snack or appetizer, often accompanied by kasundi (Bengali mustard sauce) or chutney.

Posto bata is a Bengali preparation made by grinding white poppy seeds (posto) into a smooth paste, typically combined with mustard oil, green chilies, and salt. It is commonly served as a side dish with steamed rice, often accompanied by lightly fried vegetables or as a standalone condiment in traditional Bengali meals.

Sojne data alu posto is a Bengali vegetarian dish made by cooking drumstick pieces (sojne data) and potato cubes (alu) in a paste of ground poppy seeds (posto), typically seasoned with mustard oil, green chilies, and salt. It is prepared by sautéing the vegetables with the poppy seed paste until cooked through, and is traditionally served as a side dish alongside steamed rice in Bengali households.

Bel er sarbat is a Bengali cold beverage made by blending the pulp of the bael fruit (Aegle marmelos) with water, sugar, and sometimes black salt or roasted cumin powder. It is commonly consumed as a cooling drink during hot weather and is served chilled, often as a street drink or homemade refreshment in South Asia.

Aam tel is a Bengali condiment made by preserving raw mangoes in mustard oil along with spices such as nigella seeds, fenugreek, dried chilies, and turmeric, allowing the mixture to mature over time. It is typically served as a pickle-style accompaniment alongside rice and dal in South Asian meals.

Badhakopir bora is a Bengali fritter made by combining shredded or finely chopped cabbage (badhakopi) with gram flour, spices such as cumin, green chili, and salt, then shallow- or deep-frying the mixture in oil until golden and crisp. It is commonly served as a snack or side dish, often accompanied by mustard sauce or chutney.

Aamsottwo khenjur er achar is an Indian pickle made from amsotto (dried mango leather) and khejur (dates), combined with spices such as mustard, chili, and fenugreek in a vinegar or oil base. It is typically served as a condiment alongside rice, flatbreads, or snacks in Bengali cuisine.

Alu motor is a South Asian dish made by cooking potatoes (alu) and green peas (motor) together in a spiced sauce typically containing onion, tomato, ginger, garlic, and spices such as cumin, turmeric, and garam masala. It is commonly served as a vegetarian side dish accompanied by roti, paratha, or rice in everyday home meals and casual dining settings.

Bori tomator torkari is a Bengali dish made by simmering sun-dried lentil dumplings (bori) with tomatoes, mustard oil, and spices such as turmeric, cumin, and dried red chili. It is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice as part of an everyday Bengali meal.

Chaltar achar is a Bengali pickle made from elephant apple (Dillenia indica, known as 'chalta'), which is cut into pieces and preserved with mustard oil, mustard seeds, dried red chilies, turmeric, and salt. It is commonly served as a condiment or side accompaniment to rice-based meals in Bengali households.

Chal kumro narkel is a Bengali dish made by cooking white ash gourd (chal kumro) with grated or ground coconut in a spiced preparation that typically includes mustard seeds, dried red chilies, and turmeric. It is commonly served as a vegetarian side dish alongside rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Jolpai er achar is a Bengali pickle made from jolpai (Indian olive, Elaeocarpus floribundus), prepared by combining the tart, fibrous fruits with mustard oil, mustard seeds, dried red chilies, nigella seeds, and salt, then allowing the mixture to mature. It is typically served as a condiment alongside rice-based meals or snacks.

Gajor o ranga alur bahar is a Bengali vegetable dish made with carrots (gajor) and sweet potatoes (ranga alu), typically cooked with mustard oil, spices such as turmeric, cumin, and green chilies, and finished as a dry or semi-dry stir-fry. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside rice and dal in a traditional Bengali meal.

Kuler achar is a Nepali pickle made from jujube fruits (kul) preserved with spices such as mustard seeds, fenugreek, chili, turmeric, and salt, often combined with oil and dried seasonings. It is typically prepared by sun-drying or fermenting the fruit with the spice mixture and served as a condiment or side accompaniment to main meals.

Drusenzelte are traditionally small baked pastries or cakes, typically prepared using wine lees (Drusen) left over from winemaking, which impart a distinctive tangy, yeasty flavor to the dough. They are commonly enjoyed as a rustic snack or dessert, often served alongside coffee or a glass of wine in wine-growing regions.

Caldo moreno is typically a dark, hearty broth-based soup, with its name suggesting a rich color likely derived from slow-simmered meats, legumes, or toasted ingredients common in Spanish cooking. It is commonly served hot as a starter or a warming main course, often accompanied by bread and sometimes garnished with fresh herbs or a drizzle of olive oil.

Biuli dal methi kopta is a Bengali preparation in which koftas (dumplings or fritters) are made from biuli dal (urad dal / black gram lentils) combined with methi (fenugreek leaves), shaped and fried, then simmered in a spiced gravy or served alongside a dal-based sauce. The dish is typically served as a vegetarian main course with steamed rice or flatbreads.
