Biulir dal is a Bengali lentil dish prepared by cooking split black-eyed peas (biuli or chawli dal) with aromatics such as ginger, turmeric, and sometimes dried red chilies, often finished with a mustard oil tempering. It is commonly served as part of a multi-course meal alongside rice or flatbreads, particularly in West Bengal and Bangladesh.

Chhirer kopta is a Bengali preparation made from flattened rice (chira/poha) that is soaked, mashed, and shaped into small balls or patties, typically mixed with ingredients such as mashed potato, green chili, and spices before being shallow-fried or pan-cooked. It is commonly served as a snack or light meal, often accompanied by chutneys or yogurt.

Chhatu beguner dolma is a Bengali stuffed eggplant dish in which hollowed brinjal (eggplant) is filled with a spiced mixture of chhatu (roasted gram flour or sattu) along with seasonings such as mustard, chili, and herbs, then typically pan-fried or cooked in oil until tender. It is commonly served as a side dish or snack alongside rice or flatbreads in Bengali home cooking.

Doi kochur dolma is a Bengali dish in which taro (kochur mukhi or arbi) is stuffed with a spiced filling and cooked in a yogurt-based (doi) gravy, typically seasoned with mustard, green chili, and aromatic spices. It is served as a vegetarian side dish, often accompanied by rice in traditional Bengali meals.

Panchmishali achar is a South Asian mixed pickle made by combining five different vegetables or fruits—commonly including raw mango, carrot, cauliflower, green chili, and turnip—preserved in a spiced oil or vinegar base with mustard seeds, turmeric, fenugreek, and other spices. It is typically served as a condiment alongside rice, flatbreads, or lentil dishes.

Lau beson kopta is a Bengali preparation of bottle gourd (lau) dumplings made by mixing grated gourd with chickpea flour (beson) and spices, then shaping the mixture into small balls and frying or simmering them in a spiced gravy. It is typically served as a vegetarian main dish alongside steamed rice, often prepared in home-style Bengali cooking.

Kachkola chal guror dolma is a Bengali stuffed dish in which raw banana (kachkola) is hollowed out and filled with spiced minced meat (typically beef or mutton), then cooked in a seasoned gravy. It is a traditional preparation from the Bengal region, commonly served as a main course alongside rice or flatbread.

Kankurer dalna is a Bengali vegetarian curry made with unripe jackfruit (kanka or kankar) cooked in a spiced gravy typically containing mustard oil, cumin, turmeric, ginger, and garam masala. It is a traditional dish from West Bengal and Bangladesh, commonly served with steamed rice as part of a multi-course Bengali meal.

Lau diye koraisuntir dalna is a Bengali vegetarian dish made by cooking green peas (koraisuti) with bottle gourd (lau) in a spiced gravy, typically prepared with mustard oil, cumin, turmeric, and garam masala. It is commonly served as a side dish with steamed rice or flatbreads in everyday Bengali meals.

Tentuler achar is a Bengali sour pickle or chutney made from tamarind (tentul), typically prepared by cooking tamarind pulp with spices such as mustard seeds, dried red chilies, and salt, sometimes combined with jaggery for a sweet-sour balance. It is commonly served as a condiment alongside rice-based meals or snacks in Bengali cuisine.

Soyabean er korma is a Bengali vegetarian dish in which soybean chunks are cooked in a rich, spiced sauce made with onions, ginger, garlic, yogurt, and warming spices such as cardamom, cloves, and cinnamon. It is typically served with rice or flatbreads and follows the korma style of slow-cooking in a mildly spiced, creamy gravy.

Kancha amer shorbot is a chilled beverage made from raw green mango pulp blended with water, sugar, salt, and spices such as roasted cumin and black pepper. It is commonly served as a cooling drink during the hot summer months in Bengali cuisine, often alongside meals or as a standalone refreshment.

Karupuak lento is a traditional Minangkabau snack from West Sumatra, Indonesia, made from a mixture of black-eyed peas or fermented cassava combined with spices, shaped into small portions, and deep-fried until crispy. It is commonly sold as a street food or market snack and is often eaten as a side accompaniment to rice dishes or consumed on its own as a light snack.

Schläscher Mohstriezel is a Silesian-style braided yeast pastry that is typically prepared with a sweet enriched dough rolled around a generous filling of ground poppy seeds, sugar, and often raisins or candied citrus peel. It is commonly baked until golden, finished with a light glaze or dusting of powdered sugar, and usually served in slices alongside coffee or tea, particularly during holiday seasons.

NY Dosas are a street food preparation created by Tamil chef Thiru Kumar, sold from a cart in Washington Square Park, New York City, consisting of crispy South Indian-style rice and lentil crepes filled with various combinations of ingredients such as spiced potatoes, chutneys, and other fillings adapted for an urban American audience. The dosas are cooked to order on a flat griddle and served as a handheld street food, drawing on traditional South Indian dosa techniques while incorporating locally sourced and eclectic ingredients.

Carang gesing is a Javanese dessert made from bananas cooked in a mixture of coconut milk, palm sugar, and salt, typically wrapped in banana leaves and steamed or simmered until set. It is commonly served as a snack or sweet dish in Indonesian culinary tradition, particularly in Central Java.

Tyangra machher jhol is a Bengali fish curry prepared by simmering tyangra fish (a small freshwater catfish, Mystus vittatus) in a light, spiced broth typically made with mustard oil, turmeric, cumin, green chilies, and sometimes tomato or potato. It is a traditional everyday dish from Bengal, commonly served with steamed white rice.

Parse begun diye jhol is a Bengali fish curry prepared by simmering parshe fish (Mugil parsia) and eggplant (begun) in a light, turmeric- and mustard-spiced broth with green chilies and oil. It is a common everyday dish in Bengali households, typically served with steamed rice.

Boyal Machher Jhol is a Bengali fish curry prepared by simmering boyal fish (Eutropiichthys vacha, a freshwater catfish) in a light, spiced broth typically made with mustard oil, turmeric, cumin, ginger, green chilies, and tomatoes. It is a traditional everyday dish from the Bengal region, commonly served with steamed white rice.

canonicalName: dudh bori diye jhinge beguner jhol
displayName: Dudh bori diye jhinge beguner jhol
aliases: dudh bori diye jhinge beguner jhol
description: Dudh bori diye jhinge beguner jhol is a Bengali vegetable stew made with ridge gourd (jhinge), eggplant (begun), and dried lentil dumplings (bori) cooked in a light, milk-enriched (dudh) broth seasoned with mustard, turmeric, and green chilies. It is typically prepared as a mild, everyday dish and served with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

canonicalName: peyajkoli ar alu diye tyangra machher jhol
displayName: Peyajkoli ar alu diye tyangra machher jhol
aliases: peyajkoli ar alu diye tyangra machher jhol
description: Peyajkoli ar alu diye tyangra machher jhol is a Bengali fish curry prepared by simmering tyangra (Mystus tengara, a small freshwater catfish) with potato chunks and onion stalks (peyajkoli) in a lightly spiced broth seasoned with turmeric, cumin, and green chilies. The dish is a thin, soupy jhol typical of everyday Bengali home cooking and is traditionally served over steamed white rice.

A hot bar is a self-service food station found in grocery stores, cafeterias, and food halls where a rotating selection of pre-cooked hot dishes—such as roasted meats, vegetables, grains, soups, and international entrees—are kept warm in heated trays or chafing dishes. Customers select individual portions from the available items, which vary by location and time of day, and dishes are typically priced by weight or as individual servings.

Boiroli machher jhol is a Bengali fish curry prepared by simmering boiroli (a small freshwater fish, also known as bele or sand goby) in a light, spiced broth typically made with mustard oil, turmeric, green chilies, and whole spices. It is a traditional everyday dish from West Bengal and Bangladesh, commonly served with steamed white rice.

canonicalName: the last mcdonald s burger in iceland
displayName: the last McDonald's burger in Iceland
aliases: the last mcdonald s burger in iceland
ultima hamburguesa de mcdonald s en islandia
the last mcdonalds meal in iceland
description: This is the last McDonald's burger sold in Iceland before the chain closed all its locations there in October 2009, consisting of a standard McDonald's hamburger with a beef patty, bun, ketchup, mustard, onions, and pickles. The item is preserved and displayed at the Bus Hostel in Reykjavik, where it has been kept as a cultural artifact and tourist exhibit rather than served as food.

Masak lemak mumbang is a Malaysian dish in which young, unripe coconut (mumbang) is cooked in a spiced coconut milk gravy typically seasoned with turmeric, lemongrass, galangal, and chili. It is served as a main dish alongside steamed rice, and is associated with Negeri Sembilan and other Malay culinary traditions that make heavy use of coconut-based sauces.

Masak lemak kulat sisir is a Malaysian dish in which oyster mushrooms (kulat sisir) are simmered in a rich, turmeric-spiced coconut milk gravy, typically seasoned with lemongrass, bird's eye chillies, and galangal. It is commonly served with steamed white rice and forms part of a traditional Malay meal, particularly associated with the Negeri Sembilan culinary tradition known for its lemak (coconut milk-based) preparations.

Sayur midin goreng is a Malaysian stir-fried dish made from midin ferns (Stenochlaena palustris), a wild edible fern commonly harvested in Borneo, cooked in a hot wok with garlic, chili, and often belacan (shrimp paste) or oyster sauce. It is typically served as a vegetable side dish accompanying steamed rice in everyday Malaysian meals, particularly in Sarawak.

Bartolo is a traditional pastry from Asturias in northern Spain, typically prepared with a sweet dough that is baked until golden and often filled or topped with ingredients such as sugar, almonds, or local preserves. It is commonly served as a dessert or enjoyed with coffee during breakfast or afternoon merienda, reflecting the region's rich baking heritage.

canonicalName: alu kanchakola diye foli machher jhol
displayName: Alu kanchakola diye foli machher jhol
aliases: alu kanchakola diye foli machher jhol
description: Alu kanchakola diye foli machher jhol is a Bengali fish curry prepared by simmering foli fish (Nandus nandus, also known as gangetic leaffish) with raw banana (kanchakola) and potato (alu) in a light, spiced broth typically seasoned with turmeric, cumin, and green chilies. It is a traditional home-style dish from the Bengal region, commonly served with plain steamed rice as a everyday meal.

canonicalName: alu bori tomato diye telapia machher jhol
displayName: Alu bori tomato diye telapia machher jhol
aliases: alu bori tomato diye telapia machher jhol
description: Alu bori tomato diye telapia machher jhol is a Bengali fish curry prepared by simmering tilapia pieces with potatoes, sun-dried lentil dumplings (bori), and tomatoes in a spiced broth typically seasoned with turmeric, cumin, and green chilies. It is a light, soupy dish commonly served over steamed rice as a everyday meal in Bengali households.

canonicalName: alu begun diye telapia machher jhol
displayName: Alu begun diye telapia machher jhol
aliases: alu begun diye telapia machher jhol
description: Alu begun diye telapia machher jhol is a Bengali fish curry prepared by simmering tilapia pieces with potatoes (alu) and eggplant (begun) in a spiced broth typically seasoned with turmeric, cumin, coriander, and green chilies. The dish is cooked in a thin, light gravy style characteristic of Bengali jhol preparations and is traditionally served with steamed white rice as a everyday meal.

Kalojeera diye kajoli machher jhol is a Bengali fish curry prepared by simmering kajoli (gangetic hairfin anchovy) with nigella seeds (kalojeera), green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil in a light, thin gravy. It is typically served as a main course alongside steamed rice in Bengali households.

canonicalName: alu bori diye topse machher jhol
displayName: Alu bori diye topse machher jhol
aliases: alu bori diye topse machher jhol
description: Alu bori diye topse machher jhol is a Bengali fish curry prepared by simmering topse (mango fish or Polynemus paradiseus) with potatoes (alu) and sun-dried lentil dumplings (bori) in a light, spiced broth typically seasoned with turmeric, cumin, and green chilies. The dish is commonly served with steamed rice as a everyday meal in Bengali households across West Bengal and Bangladesh.

Peyajkoli diye chapila machher jhol is a Bengali fish curry prepared by simmering chapila (Gudusia chapra), a small freshwater fish, with onion stalks (peyajkoli) in a light, spiced broth typically seasoned with mustard oil, turmeric, green chilies, and salt. The dish is commonly served with steamed rice as an everyday meal in Bengali households, particularly in Bangladesh and West Bengal.

canonicalName: kanchkola diye parse machher patla jhol
displayName: Kanchkola diye parse machher patla jhol
aliases: kanchkola diye parse machher patla jhol
description: Kanchkola diye parse machher patla jhol is a light Bengali fish curry made with parse fish (a small freshwater fish) and raw green banana (kanchkola), cooked in a thin, watery broth seasoned with mustard oil, turmeric, cumin, and green chilies. The dish is typically prepared by simmering the fish and unripe banana pieces together in the spiced broth, and it is commonly served with steamed white rice as a everyday Bengali meal.

canonicalName: ganti kochu diye latha machher jhol
displayName: Ganti kochu diye latha machher jhol
aliases: ganti kochu diye latha machher jhol
description: Ganti kochu diye latha machher jhol is a Bengali fish curry prepared by simmering latha fish (a type of long, thin freshwater fish) with taro stems (ganti kochu) in a light, spiced broth typically seasoned with turmeric, green chilies, and mustard oil. It is a traditional home-style dish commonly served with steamed rice as part of an everyday Bengali meal.

Olkopi diye bata machher jhol is a Bengali fish curry prepared by simmering bata fish (Labeo bata) with kohlrabi (olkopi) in a spiced broth typically seasoned with turmeric, cumin, coriander, and mustard oil. It is a everyday home-style dish commonly served with steamed white rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Mulo diye shol machher jhol is a Bengali fish curry prepared by simmering pieces of snakehead fish (shol maach) with white radish (mulo) in a light, spiced broth typically seasoned with turmeric, cumin, green chilies, and mustard oil. It is a common everyday home-cooked dish in Bengal, traditionally served with steamed white rice.

Kucho chingrir borar jhol is a Bengali dish consisting of small shrimp fritters (bora) simmered in a thin, spiced broth (jhol) typically seasoned with turmeric, green chilies, and mustard or cumin. The fritters are made by mixing tiny dried or fresh shrimp with gram flour and spices before frying, then cooked in the light gravy and served with steamed rice.

A spice burger is a seasoned ground meat patty, typically blended with spices such as chili, cumin, and garlic, then grilled or pan-fried and served in a bun. It is commonly accompanied by toppings such as lettuce, tomato, and condiments, and is a standard item in casual dining and fast-food contexts in Ireland and internationally.

Thakkalikkary is a South Indian tomato-based curry made by cooking ripe tomatoes with mustard seeds, curry leaves, dried red chilies, onions, and spices such as turmeric and coriander in oil. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside rice or flatbreads like chapati in Kerala and Tamil Nadu households.

Mukisoba is typically a noodle-based dish, likely a variation of yakisoba, prepared by stir-frying wheat noodles with a savory sauce, vegetables such as cabbage, carrots, and onions, and often a protein like pork, chicken, or seafood. It is commonly served hot as a main course, sometimes garnished with pickled ginger, dried seaweed flakes, or bonito shavings.

Jhinger khosa bata is a Bengali dish made by grinding or mashing the outer peel (khosa) of ridge gourd (jhinge) with mustard, green chilies, and mustard oil, sometimes combined with fried onions or garlic. It is typically served as a side dish or accompaniment with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

canonicalName: musur dal kathal er bij diye
displayName: Musur dal kathal er bij diye
aliases: musur dal kathal er bij diye
description: Musur dal kathal er bij diye is a Bengali lentil dish prepared by cooking red lentils (musur dal) together with jackfruit seeds (kathal er bij), typically seasoned with turmeric, cumin, and other spices. It is commonly served as part of a multi-course meal alongside rice, making use of the starchy, nutty jackfruit seeds as a protein and texture complement to the lentils.

Begun borir tok is a Bengali sour dish made by frying eggplant (begun) and lentil dumplings (bori) and simmering them in a tangy tamarind-based sauce, often seasoned with mustard, dried red chilies, and spices. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice in Bengali households, particularly during the warmer months when sour preparations are traditional.

Pui shaak kumro diye is a Bengali vegetable dish prepared by cooking malabar spinach (pui shaak) together with pieces of pumpkin or gourd (kumro) in a lightly spiced preparation that typically includes mustard oil, dried red chilies, and panch phoron (a five-spice blend). It is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Aam begun is a Bengali dish made by cooking raw mango (aam) with eggplant (begun/brinjal), typically prepared by frying or sautéing the vegetables together with mustard oil, green chilies, turmeric, and other spices. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Chhana koraishuntir chop is a Bengali fried croquette made from chhana (fresh curd cheese) combined with koraishuti (green peas), shaped into patties or cylindrical portions, coated in breadcrumbs or batter, and deep-fried until golden. It is typically served as a snack or appetizer, often accompanied by kasundi (mustard sauce) or tamarind chutney.

Chal potol is a Bengali dish made by cooking pointed gourd (parwal/potol) with rice (chal) in a spiced gravy, typically incorporating mustard paste, turmeric, and sometimes coconut or poppy seeds. It is commonly served as a vegetarian side dish alongside steamed rice in Bengali household meals.

Natun alur dum is a Bengali dish made with small new potatoes (natun alu) cooked in a spiced gravy typically containing mustard oil, turmeric, cumin, bay leaves, dried red chilies, and sometimes tomato or yogurt. The potatoes are first parboiled or fried and then slow-cooked (dum) in the sealed vessel to absorb the spices, and the dish is commonly served with luchi (fried flatbread) or rice during festive occasions and everyday meals.

Doi korola is a Bengali dish made by cooking bitter melon (karela) with yogurt (doi), along with spices such as turmeric, cumin, and mustard, which temper the bitterness of the gourd. It is typically served as a side dish alongside rice as part of a larger meal.

Peyajkoli posto is a Bengali vegetarian dish made from the green stalks and leaves of spring onions (peyajkoli) cooked with poppy seed paste (posto), typically seasoned with green chilies, mustard oil, and salt. It is prepared by sautéing the chopped greens with the ground poppy seed paste until the mixture is cooked through, and is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice in Bengali households.

Dudh paneer is an Indian dish in which cubes of fresh paneer (unsalted white cheese) are cooked in a mild, creamy sauce made primarily from whole milk (dudh) along with spices such as cardamom, black pepper, and sometimes saffron. It is typically served as a main course alongside flatbreads such as naan or roti, or with rice.

Pui shaak diye masoor dal is a Bengali lentil dish prepared by cooking red lentils (masoor dal) with Malabar spinach (pui shaak) leaves and stems, typically seasoned with mustard seeds, dry red chilies, turmeric, and sometimes garlic. It is commonly served as part of a multi-course Bengali meal alongside steamed rice.

Lau diye tetor dal is a Bengali lentil dish prepared by cooking bitter gourd (teto) with lau (bottle gourd) and split lentils, typically seasoned with mustard seeds, dried red chilies, and turmeric. It is a traditional bitter-starter dish commonly served at the beginning of a Bengali meal alongside steamed rice.

Natorer luchir payesh is a Bengali dessert originating from Natore, Bangladesh, made by cooking broken or crumbled luchi (deep-fried unleavened wheat bread) in sweetened milk along with sugar and cardamom until the mixture thickens into a pudding-like consistency. It is typically served as a sweet dish during festivals, special occasions, and traditional Bengali celebrations.

Bhindi sorshe is a Bengali dish made by cooking okra (bhindi) in a mustard-based sauce, typically prepared with ground mustard seeds, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. It is commonly served as a side dish with steamed rice as part of a Bengali meal.

Panela de San Joaquín is a traditional Venezuelan pastry, typically prepared with wheat flour, sugar, and butter, and often flavored with anise or other local spices characteristic of the San Joaquín region in Carabobo. It is commonly enjoyed as a sweet snack or accompaniment to coffee, and is frequently sold by street vendors and bakeries as a regional specialty.

Kancha lau pata bata is a Bengali preparation made by grinding or pounding raw bottle gourd leaves (kancha lau pata) into a paste, typically combined with mustard, green chilies, and mustard oil to create a pungent, coarse condiment. It is commonly served as an accompaniment to plain rice, often eaten at the start of a meal.

Lau patar bora are fritters made from bottle gourd leaves (lau pata), which are coated in a spiced chickpea flour or rice flour batter and deep-fried until crisp. They are a traditional Bengali preparation typically served as a snack or side dish, often accompanied by rice or consumed during informal meals.

Posto bhapa is a Bengali dish made by steaming a paste of poppy seeds (posto) combined with mustard paste, green chilies, and mustard oil, typically wrapped in banana leaf or cooked in a covered vessel. It is served as part of a multi-course meal alongside rice, with the steaming method producing a firm, dense cake with a mildly pungent, nutty flavor.

Tetul pata makha is a Bengali preparation made by mashing tender tamarind leaves with mustard oil, green chilies, salt, and sometimes dried red chilies or onion until combined into a coarse, tangy mixture. It is typically served as a simple side or accompaniment with rice, particularly during the season when young tamarind leaves are available.

Sorshe diye ol danta is a Bengali preparation in which ol danta (the stem or stalk of elephant foot yam or arum) is cooked with a mustard seed paste (sorshe) along with green chilies, mustard oil, and spices. The dish is typically served as a vegetable side with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

canonicalName: ol danta o kathal bij diye sorse bata
displayName: Ol danta o kathal bij diye sorse bata
aliases: ol danta o kathal bij diye sorse bata
description: Ol danta o kathal bij diye sorse bata is a Bengali dish made by cooking taro stems (ol danta) and jackfruit seeds (kathal bij) in a mustard paste (sorse bata) along with green chilies, mustard oil, and spices. It is typically prepared by simmering the vegetables in the pungent mustard-based sauce and is served as a side dish alongside steamed rice in traditional Bengali households.

Misti alu vaja is a Bengali preparation of sweet potatoes that are sliced and shallow-fried or pan-fried in oil, often seasoned with salt and sometimes turmeric or spices until the exterior is lightly caramelized and tender. It is commonly served as a side dish or snack, accompanying rice-based meals in Bengali households.

Begun bori diye betho saag is a Bengali vegetable dish made by cooking betho saag (Chenopodium album, also known as bathua or lamb's quarters) with fried eggplant (begun) and sun-dried lentil dumplings (bori), typically seasoned with mustard oil, dried red chilies, and spices. It is commonly served as a side dish with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Dumurer dalna is a Bengali vegetarian curry made from unripe figs (dumur) cooked with spices such as turmeric, cumin, and bay leaf in a mustard oil base, often combined with potatoes and tomatoes. It is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

canonicalName: pepe alu diye gadal patar torkari
displayName: Pepe alu diye gadal patar torkari
aliases: pepe alu diye gadal patar torkari
description: Pepe alu diye gadal patar torkari is a Bengali vegetable curry made with raw papaya (pepe), potato (alu), and colocasia or taro leaves (gadal pata), cooked together with mustard oil, turmeric, and spices. It is typically prepared as a dry or semi-dry stir-fry and served as a side dish alongside rice in everyday Bengali meals.

Lau shaager posto is a Bengali preparation made by cooking bottle gourd leaves (lau shaag) with poppy seed paste (posto), typically sautéed with mustard oil, green chilies, and spices. It is commonly served as a vegetarian side dish alongside steamed rice in Bengali household meals.

Tal er khir is a Bengali pudding made by cooking the pulp or juice extracted from the ripe tal fruit (palmyra palm, Borassus flabellifer) with milk and sugar until thickened to a creamy consistency. It is typically served chilled as a dessert, often during the summer months when the fruit is in season across Bangladesh and West Bengal.

Tal er luchi is a deep-fried flatbread from Bengali cuisine made with palm fruit (tal) pulp mixed into refined wheat flour (maida) dough, which is rolled thin and fried in oil until puffed. It is typically served as a seasonal treat during the period when ripe palm fruits are available, often accompanied by savory or sweet accompaniments.

canonicalName: alu o chhola diye dumurer torkari
displayName: Alu o chhola diye dumurer torkari
aliases: alu o chhola diye dumurer torkari
description: Alu o chhola diye dumurer torkari is a Bengali vegetable dish made by cooking raw fig (dumur) pieces together with potatoes (alu) and chickpeas (chhola) in a spiced preparation typically involving mustard oil, turmeric, cumin, and green chilies. It is a dry or semi-dry curry served as a side dish alongside rice or flatbread in traditional Bengali household meals.

Kochra bhaja is a Bengali preparation made by slicing kohlrabi (kochra) and shallow-frying or pan-frying the pieces in oil with spices such as turmeric, chili, and salt until golden and tender. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside rice and dal in everyday Bengali meals.

Bagharj is a traditional Armenian sweet bread, typically prepared from an enriched yeast dough that commonly includes flour, butter, eggs, sugar, and milk, sometimes flavored with mahleb or vanilla. It is often baked until golden and served as a treat with tea or coffee, particularly during holidays and family gatherings.

Pur vora niramish alur dom is a Bengali vegetarian dish in which potatoes are stuffed with a spiced filling—typically made from paneer or coconut, nuts, raisins, and aromatic spices—then cooked in a rich, onion- and tomato-based gravy without meat or fish. It is commonly served as a main course during religious occasions, festive meals, or anytime a niramish (meatless) preparation is required, often accompanied by luchi or rice.

Narkeler noboratna is a Bengali sweet dish prepared by cooking nine varieties of mixed nuts, dried fruits, and spices in a coconut-based mixture, often incorporating ingredients such as cashews, raisins, pistachios, and cardamom. It is typically served as a festive or celebratory dessert during special occasions and religious gatherings in Bengali culinary tradition.

Ghondhoraj lebu'r ghol is a Bengali spiced buttermilk or yogurt-based drink prepared by blending yogurt or whey with water, salt, roasted cumin, and the juice or zest of Gondhoraj lime (Citrus x limon 'Gondhoraj'), a fragrant variety native to West Bengal known for its distinctive floral aroma. It is typically served chilled as a refreshing beverage, often accompanying meals or consumed on its own, and the Gondhoraj lime distinguishes it from standard masala chaas by imparting a uniquely aromatic citrus character.

Niramish Doi Olkopi is a Bengali vegetarian dish made by cooking cauliflower (olkopi) in a sauce of yogurt (doi) along with spices such as turmeric, cumin, ginger, and green chilies, without the use of onion or garlic, making it suitable for religious or fasting occasions. It is typically served as a side dish alongside rice or flatbreads in Bengali Hindu households, particularly during religious observances where alliums are avoided.

Kalojira bhorta is a Bangladeshi mashed preparation made by dry-roasting nigella seeds (kalojira) and then crushing them with mustard oil, green chilies, onion, and salt to form a coarse paste. It is typically served as a side dish alongside plain steamed rice as part of a traditional Bangladeshi meal.

Mulo diye musur dal is a Bengali lentil dish prepared by cooking red lentils (musur dal) with daikon radish (mulo) and seasoned with a tempering of mustard seeds, dried red chilies, and turmeric. It is typically served as part of a multi-course meal alongside steamed rice in Bengali households across Bangladesh and West Bengal.

Konkanastha matarchi usal is a dry or semi-dry spiced green peas dish from the Konkanastha (Chitpavan) Brahmin culinary tradition of Maharashtra, prepared by tempering mustard seeds, asafoetida, and curry leaves in oil before cooking shelled green peas with grated coconut, turmeric, and green chilies. It is typically served as a side dish alongside chapati or rice as part of a traditional Maharashtrian meal.

Seco de chabelo is a traditional dish from the Piura region of northern Peru, made by combining dried or salted beef (charki or cecina) with a thick stew base of chicha de jora (fermented corn beer), cilantro, and spices, then mixed with mashed or fried plantains. It is typically served as a hearty main course, often accompanied by bean stew (menestras) and rice, and is closely associated with festive and family gatherings in the region.

Danta chorchori is a Bengali dry stir-fry dish made from the stalks and stems of leafy vegetables, typically cooked with mustard oil, green chilies, turmeric, and sometimes small fish or shrimp. It is a common everyday preparation in Bengali households, typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice and dal.

Bokfuler bora are fritters made from bokful (a leafy green vegetable, also known as Malabar spinach or basella) leaves coated in a spiced gram flour batter and deep-fried until crisp. They are commonly served as a snack or appetizer, often accompanied by chutneys or sauces, and are a traditional preparation found in Bengali and South Asian cuisine.

Postor bora is a Bengali fritter made by coating poppy seeds (posto) with a batter and deep-frying them until crisp, often combined with chopped onions, green chilies, and sometimes potatoes or other vegetables. It is commonly served as a snack or side dish, particularly in West Bengal and Bangladesh.

Sojne dantar chorchori is a Bengali dry vegetable dish made primarily from drumstick stems (sojne danta), cooked with mustard oil, panch phoron (a five-spice blend), green chilies, and turmeric using a stir-fry and slow-cook method until the vegetables are tender and lightly caramelized. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Mourola macher bati chorchori is a Bengali dry preparation made with mourola fish (Amblypharyngodon mola, a small freshwater fish), cooked in a clay or metal bowl with mustard oil, turmeric, dried red chilies, and minimal water until the liquid evaporates and the ingredients meld together. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice in traditional Bengali households.

Lücha fobing is a Hunan-style dish that typically features green tea and sesame seeds as central flavoring elements, likely prepared by combining ground or brewed tea with toasted sesame and other regional ingredients into a savory preparation. It is commonly served as a light course or accompaniment, reflecting the Hunan tradition of incorporating tea into everyday cooking for its aromatic and slightly bitter notes.

Polta patar bora is a Bengali fritter made by coating leaves of the polta or tilapia plant (Enhydra fluctuans) in a spiced batter of gram flour (besan) and then deep-frying them until crisp. It is typically served as a snack or side dish, often accompanied by rice or eaten on its own as a tea-time accompaniment.

Pui shaker chorchori is a Bengali dry stir-fry dish made with Malabar spinach (pui shak) cooked with mustard oil, nigella seeds, green chilies, and often potato or other vegetables in a minimal-liquid technique. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice in everyday Bengali meals.

Mourala machher charchari is a Bengali dry-style preparation made with mourala fish (Amblypharyngodon mola), a small freshwater fish, cooked with mustard oil, green chilies, turmeric, and spices in a technique that reduces liquid to create a thick, semi-dry coating around the fish. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Alu potol bori-bati chorchori is a Bengali dry vegetable dish made by cooking potatoes (alu) and pointed gourd (potol) together with sun-dried lentil dumplings (bori) and hard-boiled eggs (bati), seasoned with mustard oil, turmeric, and other spices. The dish is prepared by lightly frying the bori and eggs before combining them with the vegetables in a semi-dry, stir-fried preparation, and it is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice in a traditional Bengali meal.

Mete chorchori is a Bengali dry stir-fry preparation made with animal liver (mete) and a mixture of vegetables, cooked with mustard oil, turmeric, cumin, green chilies, and other spices until the liquid is reduced. It is typically served as a side dish alongside rice or flatbreads as part of a larger meal.

Fulkopir dantar chorchori is a Bengali dry stir-fry dish made from cauliflower stalks and stems, which are typically cut into small pieces and cooked with mustard oil, panch phoron (a five-spice blend), green chilies, and turmeric. It is a simple, economical preparation that utilizes the often-discarded parts of the cauliflower and is commonly served as a side dish with rice in everyday Bengali meals.

Chhoto bele machher chorchori is a Bengali dry-style preparation made with small bele fish (gobies/mudskippers), cooked with mustard oil, turmeric, green chilies, onions, and spices using a stir-fry method that allows the ingredients to cook together until the moisture reduces significantly. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Kolmi shaaker chorchori is a Bengali stir-fried dish made with water spinach (kolmi shak) cooked together with mustard oil, green chilies, garlic, and often small dried shrimp or fish, using a dry sauté technique that allows the greens to wilt and absorb the seasonings. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a everyday Bengali meal.

canonicalName: rui machher matha diye note dantar chorchori
displayName: Rui machher matha diye note dantar chorchori
aliases: rui machher matha diye note dantar chorchori
description: Rui machher matha diye note dantar chorchori is a Bengali dry-style vegetable dish (chorchori) prepared by cooking note danta (the stems of colocasia/taro leaves) with the head of rui (rohu) fish, mustard oil, and whole spices such as kalonji and dried red chili. The dish is cooked by a stir-frying and slow-cooking method until the vegetables absorb the flavors of the fish head, and it is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice in a traditional Bengali meal.

Amudi machher chorchori is a Bengali dry-style fish dish prepared by cooking amudi fish (rohu or a similar freshwater species) with mustard oil, turmeric, green chilies, and seasonal vegetables such as potatoes and eggplant, allowing the ingredients to cook together until the moisture reduces. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Darkina machher chorchori is a Bengali dry-style fish preparation made with darkina (a small freshwater fish), cooked with mustard oil, turmeric, green chilies, and an assortment of vegetables such as potato and eggplant in a minimal-liquid stir-fry technique. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Chanda machher chorchori is a Bengali dry-style stir-fry made with chanda fish (moonfish or Parambassis species), cooked together with mustard oil, turmeric, green chilies, and assorted vegetables such as potatoes and eggplant in a minimal-liquid technique that allows the ingredients to cook in their own moisture. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Gule machher jhal chorchori is a Bengali dry-style fish preparation made with small round fish (gule mach) cooked with mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and a small amount of oil until the liquid reduces to a thick, clinging coating. It is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Pächter's Healthcake is typically a wholesome baked good, often prepared with wholegrain flours, oats, seeds, nuts, and dried fruits, sometimes sweetened naturally with honey or fruit purees. It is commonly served as a nutritious snack or light breakfast option, either on its own or accompanied by yogurt, fresh fruit, or a hot beverage.

Longsheng Pastry is typically a baked or steamed pastry named after the Longsheng region, commonly prepared with a wheat-based dough and filled with sweet ingredients such as red bean paste, lotus seed, or nuts, though savory versions with meat or vegetables may also exist. It is often served as a snack or accompaniment to tea, and is usually enjoyed warm or at room temperature.

Punti machher bati chorchori is a Bengali dish made by cooking small punti fish (Puntius species) with mustard oil, turmeric, green chilies, and other spices in a covered pot, allowing the ingredients to steam and slow-cook together until the fish is tender and the flavors meld. It is traditionally served with steamed rice as part of a multi-course Bengali meal, and the preparation is characterized by minimal water use, relying on the natural moisture of the fish and aromatics.

Mamuang kuan is a Thai preserved mango preparation made by cooking unripe or semi-ripe mango with sugar and salt over low heat until the mixture thickens and the fruit becomes candied or jam-like in consistency. It is typically served as a sweet-salty condiment or snack, and is commonly found in Thai markets and households as a way of preserving seasonal mango.

Topse begun chorchori is a Bengali dry stir-fry dish made with topse fish (Mango fish / Polynemus paradiseus) and eggplant (begun), cooked together with mustard oil, turmeric, green chilies, and spices until the ingredients are well-integrated and lightly caramelized. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice in a traditional Bengali meal.

Gang mourala machher chorchori is a Bengali dry-style fish preparation made with gang mourala, a small freshwater fish, cooked together with mustard oil, turmeric, green chilies, and seasonal vegetables in a technique that allows the ingredients to cook in their own moisture with minimal added liquid. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice in traditional Bengali household meals.

Imonamasu is a Japanese vinegared salad made from thinly sliced or shredded taro (satoimo) that has been briefly cooked and then dressed with rice vinegar, sugar, and salt. It is a traditional dish associated with Japanese New Year cuisine (osechi ryori), typically served chilled as part of a multi-dish celebratory meal.

Jaga butter is a Japanese dish consisting of boiled or steamed whole potatoes served with a pat of butter placed on top, allowing it to melt into the potato. It is a common casual food found at festivals, street stalls, and izakayas in Japan, typically seasoned simply with salt.

Dekomawashi is a traditional Japanese dish from the Tōhoku region, particularly associated with Akita and surrounding areas, in which ingredients such as tofu, konnyaku, potatoes, and fish paste are skewered on wooden sticks and grilled or simmered in miso sauce. It is commonly served as a street food or at festivals, with each skewered item coated in a savory miso-based glaze before being eaten directly off the stick.

Alu uchchher kopta is a Bengali dish consisting of koftas (spiced dumplings or balls) made from a mixture of potato (alu) and bitter melon (uchche/karela), typically shaped and fried before being simmered in a spiced gravy. It is commonly served as part of a multi-course Bengali meal alongside rice, with the bitter melon contributing a characteristic bitter flavor balanced by the starchy potato and aromatic spices.

Shio pan is a Japanese salt bread roll made from an enriched dough that is rolled around a piece of butter, then shaped into a crescent, and baked until the butter melts and crisps the base while the top remains soft. It is seasoned with salt on the surface and is commonly served as a bakery item eaten on its own or alongside meals.

The Herculaneum loaf is a round, scored bread baked in ancient Roman style, based on a carbonized loaf discovered at the archaeological site of Herculaneum, preserved by the eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD. It is made from wheat flour and formed with characteristic string-tied segments dividing the loaf into equal portions, replicating the appearance and construction of bread found in Roman bakeries.

Wonder Bread is a mass-produced American white sandwich bread made from enriched wheat flour, water, yeast, sugar, and added vitamins and minerals, produced through a mechanized industrial baking process. It is sold pre-sliced in a sealed plastic bag and is commonly used for sandwiches, toast, and similar everyday applications.

Nutribun is a dense, fortified bread roll developed in the 1970s as a nutritional supplement, originally formulated with wheat flour, soy flour, non-fat dry milk, and added vitamins and minerals to address malnutrition. It was distributed primarily through school feeding programs, particularly in the Philippines, and is consumed as a standalone baked good intended to provide a concentrated source of protein and micronutrients.

Upplandskubb is a traditional Swedish bread originating from the Uppland region, made from rye flour and typically baked in a cylindrical shape, resulting in a dense, dark loaf. It is commonly sliced and served with butter, cheese, or cured fish, and is associated with everyday Scandinavian meals and open-faced sandwiches.

In the context of ancient Roman cuisine, a muffin would likely refer to a small, individually baked bread or cake, typically prepared from wheat or spelt flour, enriched with honey, eggs, and occasionally cheese or dried fruits such as raisins. It would commonly be baked in small molds or on a hearth and often served as part of a light meal, an accompaniment to wine, or an offering during religious and celebratory occasions.

Bolivian bread, known as "pan boliviano," is a traditional yeast-leavened wheat bread typically shaped into round or oval rolls with a soft interior and lightly browned crust, often baked in clay or conventional ovens. It is commonly served alongside meals or at breakfast, and regional variations may incorporate ingredients such as anise seeds or local fats to vary the flavor and texture.

A telera is a Mexican bread roll with a soft, slightly chewy crumb and a lightly crisp crust, characterized by its oval shape and two parallel score lines pressed into the top surface. It is commonly used as the bread for tortas, the Mexican sandwich, and is filled with ingredients such as refried beans, avocado, various meats, and cheese.

Cinta bread is a Spanish braided or ribboned bread, typically made from a simple dough of wheat flour, water, yeast, and salt, shaped into a flat, elongated loaf with a distinctive scored or layered surface. It is commonly served as a table bread alongside meals or used as a base for sandwiches and tapas in Spanish culinary tradition.

Galletes d'oli are traditional Mallorcan flatbread crackers made from wheat flour, olive oil, water, and salt, shaped into thin rounds and baked until crisp. They are commonly served as a base for toppings such as tomato, cured meats, or cheese, and are a staple of Balearic cuisine often found at breakfast or as a snack.

Putok is a Filipino bread roll characterized by its dense, slightly sweet dough that cracks or bursts open on top during baking, giving it its name, which means 'explode' in Filipino. It is commonly sold as a street food or in local bakeries and is typically eaten plain or paired with margarine, coffee, or hot chocolate as a breakfast or snack item.

Gurasa is a leavened flatbread originating from northern Nigeria, made from flour, eggs, baking powder, and yeast, which are combined into a batter and cooked on a griddle or pan until set. It is commonly served as a breakfast item or snack, often accompanied by soup, stew, or tea.

Libreta is a traditional Spanish bread roll characterized by its elongated shape and a distinctive central score along the top crust, made from wheat flour, water, yeast, and salt. It is typically baked in a deck oven to produce a crisp crust and soft interior, and is commonly served as a table bread or used for sandwiches in everyday Spanish meals.

Pada de Vale de Ílhavo is a traditional Portuguese bread from the Vale de Ílhavo region, made with wheat flour, water, salt, and a poolish pre-ferment that contributes to its characteristic open crumb and slightly tangy flavor. The use of poolish, a wet preferment of flour, water, and a small amount of leavening, requires an extended fermentation period before the dough is shaped and baked, and the bread is typically served as a table bread alongside meals.

Mennonite bread is a dense, white wheat bread traditionally baked by Mennonite communities in Mexico, particularly in the states of Chihuahua and Durango, using flour, water, salt, and leavening. It is commonly sold in rounds or loaves at local markets and is typically consumed as an everyday table bread alongside meals or used to make sandwiches.

A triangular loaf of bread is a yeast- or chemical-leavened bread shaped into a triangular prism form before baking, typically made from wheat flour, water, salt, and a leavening agent. It is sliced and served in the same contexts as conventional rectangular sandwich bread, including as a base for sandwiches or toast.

Sakhnovshchyna korovai is a traditional Ukrainian ceremonial bread originating from the Sakhnovshchyna region of Kharkiv Oblast, prepared from enriched wheat dough and decorated with ornamental dough sculptures depicting birds, flowers, and braided motifs before baking. It is typically presented at weddings and other significant life events as a ritual bread, often carried on an embroidered cloth and offered to honored guests or the wedding couple.

canonicalName: bread loaf bound with papyrus strips
displayName: Bread loaf bound with papyrus strips
aliases: bread loaf bound with papyrus strips
forma di pane legata con strisce di papiro
description: This preparation consists of a bread loaf that is physically bound or wrapped with strips of papyrus, a technique documented in ancient Egyptian contexts where papyrus was used as a binding or packaging material during baking or transport. The dish reflects ancient bread-making practices in which dough was shaped and secured with plant-based materials before or during the baking process.

Griouech is a traditional Algerian and Moroccan fried pastry made from a dough of flour, eggs, butter, and orange blossom water, shaped into a latticed or folded form before being deep-fried until golden. The fried pastry is then dipped in honey and sometimes sprinkled with sesame seeds, and it is commonly served during Ramadan and festive occasions such as weddings.

Jeljlania is a traditional Tunisian confection, typically prepared as a sweet jelly-like treat made from ingredients such as sugar, starch, and natural flavorings, often scented with rose water or citrus. It is commonly served in small portions as a dessert or festive sweet, particularly during holidays and family celebrations, and may be dusted with powdered sugar or garnished with nuts.

Unagi pie is a crisp, layered butter pastry (pâte feuilletée-style cookie) produced by the confectionery company Shunkado in Hamamatsu, Japan, incorporating powdered eel (unagi) extract along with sugar, wheat flour, and lard to give it a faintly savory, slightly sweet flavor. It is widely sold as a regional souvenir (omiyage) of Shizuoka Prefecture and is typically eaten as a snack or tea accompaniment rather than as part of a main meal.

Hualien sweet potato refers to the sweet potatoes cultivated in Hualien County on Taiwan's east coast, known for their dense, starchy flesh and naturally sweet flavor. They are commonly baked, steamed, or boiled and served as a standalone snack or side dish, and are a prominent agricultural product frequently sold at local markets and roadside stalls throughout the region.

Rakvička is a Czech and Slovak pastry consisting of a crisp, oblong choux or shortcrust shell filled with whipped cream, named after a small coffin due to its rectangular shape. It is typically served as a dessert or café pastry, often dusted with powdered sugar or topped with chocolate glaze.

Darbesh is a traditional Bengali sweet made by deep-frying small balls of chickpea flour (besan) and then binding them together with sugar syrup or jaggery, often flavored with cardamom and garnished with nuts or dried fruits. It is typically served as a confection during festive occasions and celebrations in the Indian subcontinent.

Alfort is a Japanese chocolate biscuit produced by Bourbon, consisting of a rectangular butter cookie base topped with a layer of chocolate printed with a raised relief design depicting a sailing ship. It is typically served as a packaged snack and eaten at room temperature, often accompanied by tea or coffee.

Batasa is a hard, dry sugar candy traditionally made by boiling and setting sugar or jaggery into small, drop-shaped confections. It is commonly served as a sweet accompaniment to tea or offered as a prasad (religious offering) in South Asian contexts.

Tameletjie is a traditional South African hard candy made by boiling sugar syrup, often combined with aniseed or other flavourings, until it reaches a brittle, glass-like consistency. It is typically formed into small hard sweets or pulled into sticks and is commonly sold as a street food or confectionery snack.

Love Hearts are small, hard sugar confectionery tablets made primarily from sugar, compressed into a flat oval shape and stamped with short romantic phrases or messages. They are typically consumed as a sweet snack or novelty candy and are commonly associated with Valentine's Day and other occasions, sold in rolls or bags.

Neru Neru Nerune is a Japanese DIY candy kit produced by Kracie (formerly Kanebo), in which children mix flavored powders with water in a tray to create a stretchy, foamy, color-changing paste. The kit typically includes sour powder coatings and small candy toppings, and is consumed as a snack by dipping the toppings into the prepared paste.

Marble chocolate is a confection made by swirling or blending dark and white chocolate together to create a marbled visual pattern throughout the piece. It is typically molded into bars or individual portions and served as a standalone sweet or dessert accompaniment.

Turkish honey (Türk balı) is raw honey produced by bees from nectar collected across various regions of Turkey, including floral varieties such as chestnut, pine, and thyme, with pine honey from the Aegean region being particularly notable. It is commonly served at breakfast alongside bread, butter, and clotted cream (kaymak), and is also used as a sweetener in traditional Turkish desserts and beverages.

Bí-láu is a Taiwanese braised dish made by slow-cooking pork (typically belly or other cuts) with soy sauce, rice wine, sugar, and aromatics such as garlic and five-spice until the meat is tender and the sauce is reduced to a rich glaze. It is commonly served over steamed white rice and is a staple of Taiwanese home cooking and casual dining.

Yemas de San Leandro are a traditional Spanish confection originating from the Monastery of San Leandro in Seville, made primarily from egg yolks and sugar cooked together and shaped into small rounded sweets. They are typically coated in a thin layer of powdered sugar and served as a dessert or sweet treat, often packaged as a regional specialty.

Mame shitogi is a traditional Japanese food from Iwate Prefecture made by grinding raw soybeans or green soybeans into a paste, which is then shaped into flat round cakes and sometimes grilled or toasted. It is typically offered as a ritual food during festivals and ceremonies, and can also be eaten as a snack, often with a slightly chewy texture from the uncooked or lightly cooked bean paste.

Doi parse is a traditional Bengali dish that typically features a main ingredient, such as fish or vegetables, cooked in a seasoned yogurt-based sauce ('doi' meaning yogurt in Bengali), often flavored with mustard, green chilies, and warming spices like turmeric and cumin. It is commonly served hot alongside steamed rice as part of a larger meal, allowing the tangy, creamy gravy to complement the rice.

Chinois is a spiral-shaped sweet pastry made from laminated brioche or enriched yeast dough that is rolled out, spread with pastry cream (crème pâtissière) and sometimes raisins or other fillings, then tightly rolled into a log and sliced into rounds before baking. The individual swirled portions are arranged closely together in a round cake tin, allowed to rise, and baked until golden, resulting in a pull-apart pastry commonly served as a breakfast or dessert item in French and German-speaking bakeries.

Pain de Sainte-Agathe is a traditional bread made from wheat flour, water, table salt, and baker's yeast, shaped and baked according to a regional formula. It is associated with the feast of Saint Agatha on February 5th, when loaves are customarily blessed in church before being distributed or kept in homes as a protective symbol.

Torta Bertolina is a traditional cake from Crema, in the Lombardy region of Italy, made primarily from concord grapes (uva fragola) folded into a simple batter of flour, eggs, butter, sugar, and leavening. It is typically baked in a round pan and served during the autumn grape harvest season, when the fragrant black grapes are at peak ripeness.

Mirliton de Pont-Audemer is a small Norman pastry from Pont-Audemer in Normandy, France, consisting of a short pastry shell filled with an almond cream mixture made from ground almonds, sugar, eggs, and sometimes flavored with rum or vanilla. It is typically served as an individual tartlet and is a regional specialty found in pastry shops in the Pont-Audemer area.

Aravana is a sweet prasadam (ritual offering) from the Sabarimala temple in Kerala, India, made by cooking raw rice with jaggery, ghee, cardamom, dry ginger, and coconut pieces until it forms a thick, semi-solid pudding. It is distributed to pilgrims visiting the Sabarimala shrine as a sacred offering, served warm in a sealed container.

Zézette de Sète is a dry, elongated shortbread-style biscuit originating from Sète in southern France, made with flour, sugar, vanilla, and white wine, which gives the cookie its characteristic crumbly yet slightly crisp texture. It is typically served as a snack or accompaniment to coffee or dessert wine and is closely associated with the culinary traditions of the Hérault region.

Klaushähnchen is a German roasted chicken dish traditionally prepared for the feast of Saint Nicholas (December 6), in which a whole chicken is seasoned and oven-roasted until the skin is crisp. It is typically served as a festive family meal, often accompanied by side dishes such as potatoes and vegetables, as part of the Saint Nicholas Day celebration in German-speaking regions.

Patoleo is a traditional Goan dish made by spreading a paste of soaked rice mixed with jaggery and grated coconut onto turmeric leaves, which are then folded and steamed until cooked. It is commonly prepared as a festive food in Goa, particularly during the Feast of the Assumption and the Ganesh Chaturthi festival, and is served wrapped in the turmeric leaves that impart a subtle aroma to the filling.

Zuckerrenkerl is a traditional Bavarian freshwater fish, specifically a whitefish (Coregonus) from Alpine lakes such as the Ammersee and Starnberger See, typically prepared by smoking, pan-frying, or poaching with simple seasonings. It is commonly served as a main course or starter in southern German and Austrian cuisine, often accompanied by lemon, bread, and salad.

Thuvarappayasam is a South Indian sweet pudding made by cooking split pigeon peas (toor dal) with jaggery, coconut milk, and spices such as cardamom and dry ginger until thick and creamy. It is traditionally prepared in Kerala and Tamil Nadu, often served as a dessert during festivals, temple offerings, and celebratory meals.

Bepang is a Malaysian confection made by binding roasted peanuts and sesame seeds together with cooked sugar syrup, which is then pressed into a flat slab and cut into rectangular or square pieces once cooled and hardened. It is commonly sold as a snack or street food and is associated with traditional Malaysian and Chinese-Malaysian festive and everyday snacking contexts.

Phu Thê Cake is a traditional Vietnamese steamed cake made from mung bean paste and glutinous rice flour or tapioca starch, often incorporating shredded coconut, pandan juice for color, and a sweet filling of mung bean and sugar. The cake is typically encased in a translucent, jelly-like outer layer and packaged in small woven palm-leaf or paper boxes, commonly served at weddings and festive occasions as a symbol of marital fidelity.

Abbamele is a Sardinian concentrated syrup made by slowly cooking honeycomb, including honey, beeswax, and pollen, until it reduces to a thick, dark paste with a bitter-sweet flavor. It is traditionally used as a spread on bread or paired with cheeses and is a typical product of Sardinian artisanal food production.

Frustingo is a traditional baked fig cake from the Marche region of Italy, made primarily from dried Ficus carica (figs) combined with ingredients such as nuts, cocoa, spices, and sometimes dried fruit or honey. It is typically prepared by mixing the ingredients into a dense dough that is shaped and baked, and is commonly served during the winter holiday season as a festive dessert.

Bossolà bresciano is a traditional ring-shaped leavened cake from Brescia in Lombardy, Italy, made with flour, eggs, butter, sugar, and yeast, giving it a soft, brioche-like texture. It is a characteristic Easter bread, typically served as a festive breakfast or dessert during the spring holiday period.

Vlčnovský vdolek is a traditional Czech sweet pastry originating from the village of Vlčnov in Moravia, made from a leavened yeast dough that is shaped into a round, flat form and topped with ingredients such as quark (tvaroh), poppy seeds, or plum jam. It is typically served as a festive food, closely associated with local folk celebrations and the Vlčnov Ride of the Kings festival.

Arippayasam is a South Indian rice pudding made by simmering raw rice in milk with sugar, and flavored with cardamom, cashews, and raisins. It is a traditional Kerala dessert typically served during festivals, religious ceremonies, and special occasions as part of a sadya (feast).

Ceglie biscuit (biscotto di Ceglie) is a traditional dry pastry from Ceglie Messapica in Apulia, made from a short-crust dough filled with a mixture of almonds, bitter almonds, cocoa, and grape must or fig jam, then baked until firm. These small, dome-shaped cookies are a staple of local celebrations and festivals, and are commonly served as a dessert or sweet snack alongside coffee or dessert wines.

Vermicelles is a Swiss dessert made from sweetened chestnut purée that is pressed through a potato ricer or special mill to create thin, worm-like strands resembling vermicelli pasta. It is typically served as a mound of chestnut strands, often accompanied by whipped cream and meringue, and is especially popular in autumn and winter in the French- and German-speaking regions of Switzerland.

Sugolo is a traditional Italian pudding made by thickening fresh grape must with flour (typically chestnut or wheat flour) and simmering it until it reaches a dense, gel-like consistency. It is flavored with spices such as cinnamon and anise, poured into molds or dishes to set, and served as a dessert, particularly during the autumn grape harvest season in regions such as Veneto and Friuli.

Papassinos are traditional Sardinian cookies made from a dough of flour, lard, sugar, eggs, and dried grapes or raisins, often incorporating walnuts, almonds, and spices such as cinnamon and anise. They are typically glazed with a sugar and egg white icing and served during festive occasions, particularly around All Saints' Day and All Souls' Day.

Rame di Napoli are soft, dark Sicilian cookies made from a dough of flour, honey, cocoa, spices such as cinnamon and cloves, and sometimes candied fruit or nuts, which is shaped into oval portions and baked. They are traditionally topped with a chocolate glaze and are commonly served during the autumn and winter holiday season, particularly around All Saints' Day in Sicily.

Cassatella di Sant'Agata is a Sicilian pastry from Catania traditionally prepared for the feast of Saint Agatha, consisting of a rounded shell of marzipan or sponge cake filled with sweetened ricotta and candied citrus peel (succade), then coated in a white ceramic-style sugar glaze and topped with a red cherry to resemble a stylized breast. The confection is served as a festive dessert during the February celebrations honoring the patron saint of Catania, and is produced by local pastry shops throughout Sicily.

Hergma is a Maghrebi dish made by slow-cooking cow or calf feet with wheatberries and chickpeas until the collagen from the feet produces a thick, gelatinous broth. It is typically served as a hearty stew, often enjoyed as a main meal, particularly in North African countries such as Tunisia and Algeria.

Çudu is a baked flatbread pie from the Caucasus region, made from a thin yeast dough filled with a mixture of minced beef and lamb seasoned with herbs. It is typically cooked on a dry griddle or in an oven and served hot, often cut into portions as a main dish or snack.

This dish typically features ingredients sourced from lands reclaimed from mafia control in Italy, often including products such as organic pasta, legumes, olive oil, or tomato-based sauces produced by social cooperatives on confiscated properties. It is commonly served as a pasta or mixed plate highlighting traceable, ethically grown Italian staples, presented as part of a menu that emphasizes the social and civic significance of the ingredients' origin.

Math is a traditional sweet from the Bengal region, typically prepared using ingredients such as sugar, milk solids, or jaggery shaped into small confections. It is commonly served as a dessert or festive treat, often accompanying tea or offered during celebrations and religious occasions.

C. Howard's Violet candies are small, compressed sugar tablets flavored with violet flower extract, first produced by the C. Howard Company in New York in the early 20th century. They are typically sold in flat paper packages and consumed as a breath-freshening confection, notable for their distinctive floral violet flavor derived from orris root and violet oil.

Cadbury Shots are small, bite-sized chocolate confections consisting of a thin chocolate shell filled with a liquid or soft chocolate center, manufactured by Cadbury. They are typically served as a snack or dessert treat, packaged in small portions and consumed at room temperature.

Fruit Gushers are a commercially produced fruit snack manufactured by General Mills, consisting of small hexagonal pouches made from a chewy fruit-flavored outer shell encasing a liquid fruit-flavored gel center. They are sold pre-packaged and consumed as a ready-to-eat snack, commonly found in school lunches and as a children's snack in North America and other markets.

Sankaku Choco Pie is a triangular Japanese snack consisting of a soft, biscuit-like pastry shell filled with chocolate cream, produced by Furuta Confectionery. It is typically sold as a packaged convenience snack and eaten at room temperature as a sweet treat.

canonicalName: sankaku choco pie sweet potato and caramel
displayName: Sankaku Choco Pie Sweet Potato and Caramel
aliases: sankaku choco pie sweet potato and caramel
description: Sankaku Choco Pie Sweet Potato and Caramel is a triangular Japanese snack cake consisting of a soft chocolate-coated pastry shell filled with sweet potato-flavored cream and caramel. It is a seasonal or limited-edition variation of the Sankaku Choco Pie line, typically sold individually as a packaged convenience store or supermarket snack in Japan.

Toppo is a Japanese snack made by Lotte, consisting of a thin, hollow pretzel-like stick filled with a smooth chocolate cream running through its center. It is typically sold in small boxes as a ready-to-eat snack and is commonly consumed as a casual treat or shared snack in Japan and other Asian markets.

Violet candy is a confection flavored with violet flower extract or violet essence, typically produced in the form of small, hard sugar lozenges or pastilles with a distinctive floral taste and purple hue. It is commonly associated with the French city of Toulouse, where it has been made since the 19th century, and is often consumed as a sweet treat or used as a decorative garnish on desserts.

Atsugi sirokoro horumon is a regional specialty from Atsugi City in Kanagawa Prefecture, Japan, consisting of pork large intestine (horumon) cut into thick, round pieces and grilled or stewed until tender. It is typically seasoned with miso or soy-based sauces and served as a main dish or accompaniment to rice and alcoholic beverages, and is recognized as a local B-grade gourmet food of the area.

Pasta al fumè is an Italian pasta dish prepared with a smoked flavor profile, typically achieved through smoked ingredients such as smoked salmon, smoked bacon, or smoked cheese combined with a cream-based sauce. It is commonly served as a first course, with the pasta cooked al dente and tossed in the sauce until evenly coated.

Crystal bread (pan de cristal) is a Spanish bread characterized by its extremely thin, crisp crust and large, open-crumb interior, achieved through a very high-hydration dough (often above 100% water-to-flour ratio) that is baked at high temperature. It is typically served sliced and topped with olive oil, tomato, or charcuterie, and is commonly found in Catalan cuisine as a base for dishes like pa amb tomàquet.

Djote is a traditional dish from the Liège region of Belgium made with potatoes, vegetables, and lardons cooked together, typically in a single pot. It is a rustic, hearty preparation commonly served as a main course, reflecting the peasant cooking traditions of the Walloon countryside.

Lontar cake is an Indonesian custard tart originating from Eastern Indonesia, made from a mixture of eggs, sugar, coconut milk, and vanilla baked in a pastry shell derived from the sap or fruit of the lontar palm. It is commonly served as a dessert or snack at celebrations and gatherings, particularly in regions such as East Nusa Tenggara and Maluku.

Ash-e Abbasali is a thick Iranian soup made with chickpeas, lentils, spinach, dried herbs, and kashk (whey), often enriched with fried onions, dried mint, and sometimes lamb or offal. It is a traditional dish associated with religious and communal gatherings in Iran, typically served hot as a hearty main course.

Serinakaker are traditional Norwegian butter cookies made from a dough of flour, butter, sugar, eggs, and often flavored with cardamom or vanilla, rolled out and cut into shapes before baking until lightly golden. They are a common fixture in Scandinavian holiday baking, typically served during the Christmas season alongside other traditional cookies as part of an assorted cookie platter.

Doghramaj is a cold Azerbaijani soup made by combining torn or chopped pieces of flatbread (typically lavash) with shredded boiled chicken, fresh herbs such as basil and cilantro, and diced onion, all mixed with tangy fermented milk (ayran or katyk). It is served cold or at room temperature, commonly as a refreshing dish during warm weather, with the liquid poured over the solid ingredients just before eating.

Nihari Haleem is a slow-cooked dish that combines elements of two traditional South Asian preparations: Nihari, a spiced bone-in meat stew typically made with beef or lamb shank simmered overnight with whole spices, and Haleem, a thick porridge of lentils, cracked wheat, and braised meat cooked until blended into a smooth, dense consistency. It is commonly served as a hearty meal garnished with fried onions, fresh ginger, green chilies, lemon, and coriander, and is associated with both everyday and celebratory eating in South Asian Muslim culinary traditions.

Kumror kopta is a Bengali dish made from grated or mashed pumpkin mixed with spices, chickpea flour, and sometimes coconut or raisins, shaped into balls or patties and deep-fried until golden. It is typically served as a side dish or snack, often accompanied by rice or flatbreads in traditional Bengali meals.

Makhani dal kopta is a dish combining dal makhani—slow-cooked black lentils and kidney beans simmered in a tomato, butter, and cream sauce—with kofta, which are spiced balls typically made from minced meat or vegetables that are fried or baked before being added to or served alongside the sauce. It is generally served as a main course, accompanied by flatbreads such as naan or roti, or with rice.

Enchor er kalia is a Bengali dish made from raw or unripe jackfruit (enchor) cooked in a rich, spiced gravy typically containing onion, ginger, garlic, tomato, and a blend of whole and ground spices such as cumin, coriander, turmeric, and garam masala. The jackfruit pieces are often par-boiled before being simmered in the sauce until they absorb the flavors, and the dish is commonly served with steamed rice or flatbreads as part of a main meal.

Fulkopir doi kalia is a Bengali dish made by cooking cauliflower florets in a spiced yogurt-based gravy, typically seasoned with turmeric, cumin, ginger, and garam masala. It is commonly served as a vegetarian main course alongside rice or flatbreads such as roti.

Dhokar kalia is a Bengali vegetarian dish made from fried lentil cakes (dhoka) prepared from chana dal (split chickpeas) that are shaped, fried until golden, and then simmered in a spiced gravy typically containing ginger, cumin, turmeric, and tomatoes. It is commonly served as a main course dish in Bengali Hindu households, particularly during festivals and special occasions when meat is avoided.

Bizcocho de Moya is a traditional sponge cake originating from the municipality of Moya in Gran Canaria, Spain, made primarily from eggs, sugar, flour, and lemon zest, which gives it a distinctive citrus aroma. It is baked until light and airy in texture and is commonly served as a dessert or snack, often accompanied by coffee or tea, and is considered a protected local specialty of the Canary Islands.

Russe is a classic layered dessert composed of a meringue or dacquoise base topped with a praline or hazelnut buttercream, often finished with crushed caramelized almonds or hazelnuts on the exterior. It is typically served sliced as a patisserie cake in French and European culinary traditions.

Slastions are Ukrainian fried dough pastries made from a yeast-leavened dough that is shaped into small rounds or ovals and deep-fried until golden. They are traditionally dusted with powdered sugar and served as a dessert or sweet snack, similar in character to doughnuts.

Torta di fioretto is an Italian baked cake made with fioretto, a finely ground cornmeal (a softer, finer grade than standard polenta flour), combined with eggs, butter, sugar, and flour to produce a tender, crumbly texture. It is typically served as a dessert or afternoon snack, often dusted with powdered sugar, and is associated with northern Italian baking traditions.

Bougatsa (φογάτσα/phogatsa) is a Greek pastry made from thin phyllo dough wrapped around a filling of semolina custard cream, though savory versions filled with cheese or minced meat also exist. It is typically baked until golden and crisp, then sliced and served warm, dusted with powdered sugar and cinnamon in the sweet version, and is commonly eaten as a breakfast or snack item in Greece.

aliases: torat de aceite de alcala de los gazules
description: Torta de pellizco is a traditional Spanish sponge cake made with flour, eggs, sugar, oil, and leavening agents, characterized by a light, airy crumb achieved by pinching or pulling apart the dough before baking, which gives the cake its name ('pellizco' meaning 'pinch'). It is typically served as a snack or dessert, often dusted with powdered sugar, and is commonly found in home baking and regional pastry traditions across Spain.

Estofinade is a traditional dish from the Rouergue region of southern France, made primarily from dried salt cod (stockfish) that is rehydrated and then combined with eggs, cream or milk, garlic, parsley, and often walnut oil or potatoes. It is typically served as a main course, with the cod broken down and cooked into a thick, creamy mixture resembling a brandade-style preparation.

Dagang rice is a Malaysian dish consisting of glutinous rice cooked in coconut milk, traditionally steamed and served with a curry made from tuna fish (typically aya or skipjack), along with a side of pickled vegetables and hard-boiled egg. It is commonly associated with the East Coast states of Malaysia, particularly Terengganu and Kelantan, where it is a customary breakfast dish eaten during festive occasions such as Eid al-Fitr.

Nkui is a thick, viscous soup originating from the Western Region of Cameroon, prepared by simmering the bark or leaves of the nkui plant (Triumfetta cordifolia) until the liquid develops a characteristic slimy, mucilaginous texture, often combined with palm oil, crayfish, pepper, and various proteins such as smoked fish or meat. It is traditionally served to nursing mothers in the postpartum period, as it is believed to promote breast milk production, and is typically eaten with fufu or other starchy accompaniments.

Koki is a Cameroonian steamed dish made from black-eyed peas that are ground into a paste, mixed with red palm oil, salt, and sometimes hot peppers or leafy greens, then wrapped in banana leaves and steamed until firm. It is typically served as a side dish or snack and is a staple preparation found across Central and West African cuisines.

Msoki is a Tunisian dish traditionally prepared during Passover by Sephardic Jewish communities, consisting of a stew made with spring vegetables such as artichokes, fava beans, peas, and fennel, often combined with lamb or meat. It is typically slow-cooked with herbs and spices and served as a festive main course during the holiday meal.

Katemak is a traditional Timorese dish from East Nusa Tenggara, Indonesia, made by boiling corn kernels together with green beans and sometimes other legumes until soft. It is commonly served as a staple meal or side dish, reflecting the corn-based food traditions of the region.

Sahlab (also spelled sohlob) is a warm beverage made from a powder derived from the dried tubers of orchid plants (genus Orchis), mixed with hot milk and typically sweetened with sugar. It is commonly served topped with cinnamon, coconut flakes, and chopped nuts, and is traditional in Middle Eastern and Eastern Mediterranean countries, particularly during cooler months.

Sukalki is a traditional Basque meat stew prepared by slow-cooking pieces of beef with potatoes, onions, peppers, tomatoes, and wine or broth until the ingredients are tender and the flavors are well integrated. It is a hearty dish commonly served as a main course in Basque households and traditional restaurants, particularly in the inland regions of the Basque Country.

Kaddo boddong is an Afghan dish consisting of pan-fried or baked pumpkin or butternut squash that is first sautéed in oil and sugar to caramelize the exterior, then slow-cooked until tender. It is typically served topped with a seasoned yogurt sauce and a spiced ground meat (qorma) sauce, and is commonly eaten as an appetizer or side dish in Afghan cuisine.

Haagsche Hopjes are small, hard coffee-and-caramel candies originating from The Hague, Netherlands, traditionally made by boiling together strong coffee, sugar, butter, and cream until the mixture reaches a hard-crack stage, then pouring it into molds to set. Rademaker is the commercial producer most closely associated with this confection, which is typically served as an after-dinner sweet or alongside coffee.

Cadbury Favourites is an assorted collection of individually wrapped miniature Cadbury chocolate confections, including varieties such as Crunchie, Caramello, Roses, and Dream bars. The selection is typically served as a sharing box at casual gatherings, parties, or as a gift, and is produced by Cadbury primarily for the Australian and New Zealand markets.

Bombay halwa, also known as corn flour halwa or Karachi halwa, is a dense, chewy confection made by cooking corn starch (cornflour) with sugar, ghee, water, and food coloring until it reaches a firm, translucent consistency, often studded with cashews or other nuts. It is typically cut into squares or diamond shapes and served as a sweet snack or dessert, commonly found in Indian sweet shops and during festive occasions.
