Aquafaba meringue is prepared by whipping the liquid drained from cooked chickpeas (aquafaba) with sugar until stiff, glossy peaks form, replacing the egg whites used in traditional meringue. It is a staple of vegan French-inspired cuisine and can be served as individual baked shells, used as a topping for tarts and pies, or incorporated into mousses and other confections.

Costrada is a Spanish layered pastry dish made with puff pastry or flaky dough filled with a sweet or savory mixture, typically incorporating eggs, cheese, or sweet fillings such as marzipan and jam, baked until golden. It is traditionally served sliced as a tart or pie, and appears in both dessert and savory contexts depending on regional variation.

Glorias de Linares are a traditional Mexican candy from Linares, Nuevo León, made by cooking goat's milk with sugar, cinnamon, and ground pecans (Carya illinoinensis) until a soft caramel-like paste forms, with baking soda added to influence texture during the cooking process. They are typically shaped into small rounds or cylinders, wrapped in wax paper or cellophane, and served as a confection or regional sweet sold in markets and gift shops throughout northeastern Mexico.

Nata de piña is a chewy, translucent gel produced through bacterial fermentation of pineapple juice or pineapple water using Komagataeibacter xylinus (formerly Acetobacter xylinum), which generates a thick cellulose pellicle that is harvested, washed, cut into cubes, and cooked in sugar syrup. It is commonly served as a dessert ingredient in the Philippines, appearing in dishes such as halo-halo, fruit salads, and sweetened drinks alongside other jellied or preserved components.

Niflettes are small, round pastries from the Provins region of France, made with a shortcrust or puff pastry shell filled with a custard cream flavored with orange blossom water. They are traditionally associated with the Feast of All Saints (Toussaint) in November and are served as a dessert or sweet snack.

Kamalabhog is a Bengali sweet from West Bengal, India, made from chhena (fresh cheese curds) shaped to resemble a lotus flower and soaked in sugar syrup, often flavored with saffron or cardamom and tinted orange or pink. It is typically served as a festive or ceremonial dessert and is associated with religious offerings as well as special occasions.

Kheer Kamala is an Indian dessert made by combining kheer (a rice pudding prepared by simmering rice in milk with sugar) with segments or juice of oranges (kamala), which lend the dish a citrus flavor and aroma. It is typically served chilled as a sweet course during festivals, special occasions, or as a traditional dessert.

A dutchie is a sweet, yeasted bun of Caribbean origin, traditionally made with raisins, brown sugar, and coconut milk, and baked until soft and golden. It is commonly served as a breakfast item or snack and is closely associated with Jamaican baking traditions.

Perpetual stew, also known as hunter's pot or forever soup, is a continuously simmered broth-based dish in which new ingredients such as vegetables, legumes, grains, and meats are added regularly to replenish the pot without ever fully emptying it. It is associated with medieval cooking traditions and is served as a hearty, variable main course whose flavor deepens over time due to the accumulation of ingredients.

Imperial ruso is a layered salad of Argentine origin made with diced boiled potatoes, carrots, peas, and hard-boiled eggs, bound together with mayonnaise, often incorporating cold cuts such as ham or mortadella. It is typically served chilled as a side dish or appetizer and is a common feature at gatherings and buffets throughout Argentina.

Fatias de Tomar is a traditional Portuguese egg-based sweet from the town of Tomar, made by soaking slices of a firm egg yolk and sugar sponge in a light sugar syrup. It is typically served as a dessert or pastry and is associated with the convent sweets tradition of Portugal, sharing origins with other regional egg-yolk confections.

Sbatudin is a traditional Italian preparation made by vigorously whisking egg yolk with sugar until pale and frothy, then incorporating strong coffee and sometimes a small amount of cold milk. It is typically served as a light breakfast or snack drink, particularly in the Veneto region, and is consumed immediately after preparation to preserve its foamy texture.

Hesîde is a traditional Kurdish sweet dish made by cooking flour in butter or fat until browned, then mixing it with sugar or honey and sometimes water to form a thick, paste-like consistency. It is typically served warm as a dessert or festive food, often prepared for special occasions and celebrations.

Unnakai is a Kerala snack made by mashing steamed ripe cooking banana (plantain) and shaping it around a sweet filling typically consisting of grated coconut, sugar, and cardamom, then sealing and rolling into an oval or ball shape. It is commonly served as an evening snack or at festive occasions in the Malabar region of Kerala, India.

Merengon is a Colombian layered dessert assembled from crushed or whole meringue pieces, whipped cream, and fresh fruit such as strawberries or bananas, built up in alternating strata. It is typically served chilled in individual cups or a large trifle-style dish and is a common feature at celebrations and family gatherings throughout Colombia.

Shemai is a sweet vermicelli dish made by toasting thin wheat noodles and cooking them in milk with sugar, ghee, and aromatic spices such as cardamom, often garnished with raisins and nuts. It is commonly prepared during festive occasions, particularly Eid celebrations, and is served warm or at room temperature as a dessert or sweet breakfast dish across South Asia.

Gueffus are traditional Sardinian sweets made from ground almonds, sugar, and lemon zest, shaped into small rounded forms and often coated in powdered sugar or icing. They are typically served at festive occasions and celebrations in Sardinia, and are a common feature of local confectionery traditions alongside other almond-based sweets.

Tomato casserole from Gjakova is a traditional Albanian dish from the city of Gjakova in Kosovo, prepared by slow-cooking ripe tomatoes with onions, garlic, and olive oil, often combined with meat such as lamb or veal, in an oven or on a stovetop until the ingredients meld into a thick, savory mixture. It is typically served as a main course accompanied by bread and is a characteristic example of Albanian home cooking rooted in the culinary traditions of the western Balkans.

Sweet peanut sticky rice is an Indonesian dish made from glutinous rice cooked until tender and combined with roasted ground peanuts and palm sugar or coconut sugar to create a sweet, dense mixture. It is commonly served as a snack or dessert, often wrapped in banana leaves or presented in small portions at traditional markets and celebratory gatherings.

Sate buntel is a Javanese dish consisting of ground or minced meat, typically lamb or goat, wrapped in caul fat and skewered before being grilled over charcoal. It is a specialty of Solo (Surakarta), Central Java, and is commonly served with rice cake (lontong or nasi), sweet soy sauce, and fresh shallots.

Awug is a traditional Indonesian steamed rice flour cake made from a mixture of rice flour, palm sugar, and grated coconut, layered and cooked in a cone-shaped bamboo steamer that gives the dish its distinctive triangular form. It is a common street food and snack in West Java, often sold at traditional markets and consumed as a casual daytime snack.

Piti mi is a Central Asian slow-cooked soup traditionally prepared by simmering lamb, chickpeas, chestnuts or potatoes, and onions in individual clay pots, often with saffron and tail fat for depth of flavor. It is typically served as a two-course meal, with the broth poured over bread eaten first, followed by the solid ingredients mashed together with flatbread.

Ponorogo satay is a grilled meat skewer dish originating from Ponorogo, East Java, Indonesia, made from chicken pieces marinated and coated in a thick peanut sauce blended with coconut milk, palm sugar, and spices before being grilled over charcoal. It is typically served with additional peanut sauce, steamed rice or lontong (compressed rice cake), and accompanied by raw shallots and chili.

Dalan is a traditional Mongolian dish prepared by boiling horse bones and rendering the fat from the marrow and surrounding tissue, producing a rich broth and extracted horse fat. It is typically consumed as a warming, high-calorie meal suited to the nomadic diet of Mongolia, often eaten during cold seasons or after slaughter periods when horse fat is available.

Rujak soto is an Indonesian dish from Banyuwangi, East Java, that combines two distinct dishes: rujak (a spiced fruit and vegetable salad dressed with a peanut-based sauce containing palm sugar, chili, and fermented shrimp paste) and soto (a clear beef broth soup served with meat, rice cakes, and bean sprouts). The two components are served together in a single bowl, with the soto broth poured over or alongside the rujak elements, creating a combination of savory, spicy, and sweet flavors typical of Banyuwangi culinary tradition.

Sambal tuktuk is a Batak condiment from North Sumatra, Indonesia, made by grinding together chili peppers, andaliman (Sichuan-like Batak pepper), shallots, garlic, and sometimes dried or smoked fish such as ikan teri, resulting in a coarse, pungent paste with a numbing, spicy flavor. It is typically served as an accompaniment to grilled or fried fish and other traditional Batak dishes such as arsik.

Zhent is a traditional Central Asian dish prepared by combining cooked millet with quark (fresh curd cheese), clarified butter, honey, white sugar, and raisins, then mixing the ingredients until a thick, cohesive mixture is formed. It is typically served as a sweet, nourishing dish and is associated with Kyrgyz and Kazakh culinary traditions, often consumed during festive occasions or as a substantial everyday food.

Hime is a traditional Buryat boiled dough dish made from a simple mixture of water and flour, filled or served with ground or chopped beef and onion. It is typically prepared by shaping the dough into flat pieces or pockets, cooking them in boiling water, and serving as a staple main course in Buryat households.

Satay kalong is an Indonesian grilled skewer dish traditionally prepared and sold at night, with the name 'kalong' referring to the large fruit bat active after dark. It consists of small portions of meat, often offal or a mix of meats, seasoned and grilled over charcoal, and is typically served as a street food with peanut sauce or soy-based condiments.

Suwar-suwir is an Indonesian chewy candy made primarily from tapioca starch and sugar, cooked together until the mixture reaches a firm, pliable consistency and then cut into small rectangular or strip-shaped pieces. It is commonly found in East Java, particularly associated with the city of Jember, and is typically sold as a packaged snack or regional souvenir in various flavors such as durian, pandan, and strawberry.

Seasoning soup, known in Indonesian as 'sop bumbu,' is a clear broth-based soup prepared by simmering a blend of aromatic spices including shallots, garlic, ginger, and other traditional Indonesian seasonings with meat or vegetables. It is commonly served as a everyday meal or alongside rice in Indonesian households and warung (small food stalls).

Kasav is a flatbread made from grated and pressed cassava (manioc) that is dried and toasted on a griddle until firm and dry. It is a staple food in Haitian and broader Caribbean cuisine, commonly served as an accompaniment to meals or used as a base for toppings.

Hototay is a Chinese-origin soup dish consisting of a clear or lightly seasoned broth containing sliced chicken breast, green peas, and a poached egg. It is typically served as a starter or light main course and is associated with Chinese restaurant menus internationally, particularly in the Philippines and other parts of Southeast Asia.

Squid Tongseng is an Indonesian curry-style dish in which squid is cooked in a spiced coconut milk broth seasoned with kecap manis, palm sugar, galangal, lemongrass, and chili, following the same preparation as the more common goat or beef version of tongseng. It is typically served over steamed rice and accompanied by sliced cabbage and tomato, which are added to the broth during the final stage of cooking.

Dulcia domestica is a Roman sweet dish made by stoning dates and stuffing them with nuts (such as pine nuts or walnuts) and ground pepper, then rolling them in salt and frying them in heated honey. The dish is documented in the ancient Roman cookbook Apicius and was typically served as a dessert or sweet course at Roman meals.

Khanfaroosh is a traditional Iranian sweet made from rice flour, eggs, sugar, and saffron, mixed into a batter and deep-fried until golden. It is commonly served as a dessert or snack, often dusted with powdered sugar or cardamom, and is particularly associated with the cuisine of the Persian Gulf coastal regions of Iran.

Kanjuku Melon Cream is a dessert offered by Sugakiya, a Japanese fast-food chain, consisting of soft-serve ice cream served with a melon-flavored syrup or sauce, typically in a cup or cone format. It is designed to evoke the flavor of ripe (kanjuku, meaning fully ripened) melon and is served as a standalone cold dessert item within the chain's menu.

Baba de camelo is a Portuguese dessert made by folding whipped egg whites into sweetened condensed milk to create a light, mousse-like texture. It is typically served chilled in individual portions and garnished with crushed or slivered almonds.

Rhubarb fool is a cold British dessert made by folding stewed, sweetened rhubarb into whipped cream to create a soft, layered or swirled mixture. It is typically served chilled in glasses or bowls as a simple dessert, often garnished with a biscuit or additional rhubarb compote.

Khailmag is a Mongolian dish made by slowly heating fresh clotted cream (öröm) in a pan until the fat separates and the milk solids caramelize, often with the addition of flour and sugar to form a thick, rich mixture. It is typically served warm as a dessert or substantial snack, sometimes accompanied by bread or rice, and is commonly prepared during festive occasions and family gatherings.

aliases: hot pot de brotes de bambu
description: Bamboo shoot hot pot is a Chinese communal dish prepared by simmering fresh or fermented bamboo shoots in a seasoned broth alongside ingredients such as tofu, mushrooms, leafy vegetables, and various proteins in a shared pot at the table. It is commonly served as a main course in both home and restaurant settings, with diners cooking additional ingredients directly in the simmering broth throughout the meal.

Zlijevka is a traditional Bosnian baked dish made by pouring a liquid batter of eggs, milk, flour, and fat (typically lard or butter) into a greased baking pan and cooking it until set, sometimes incorporating cheese, kajmak, or other dairy ingredients. It is typically served as a savory main dish or side, cut into portions directly from the baking vessel, and is common in rural Bosnian household cooking.

Espineta amb caragolins is a traditional Catalan dish consisting of pork spare ribs (espineta) slow-cooked together with small snails (caragolins), typically prepared in a tomato-based sauce seasoned with garlic, herbs, and sometimes dried peppers. It is commonly served as a main course in Catalan home cooking and rustic restaurants, often accompanied by bread to soak up the sauce.

Yasai Tappuri Tantan-men is a Japanese-style noodle dish based on dan dan mian, featuring wheat noodles served in a sesame and chili-based broth with an abundant topping of stir-fried or blanched vegetables such as bok choy, bean sprouts, and mushrooms. The dish is typically finished with ground meat or a meat-free protein, chili oil, and ground sesame, and is served as a main course in ramen restaurants.

Ilisher doi korma is a Bengali preparation of hilsa fish (ilish) cooked in a korma-style sauce made with yogurt (doi), along with spices such as turmeric, cumin, and sometimes mustard or onion paste. The dish is typically served with steamed rice and represents a richer, yogurt-braised variation of traditional hilsa preparations common in Bengali cuisine.

Koi machh vaja is a Bengali fried fish dish prepared by marinating koi fish (climbing perch) with turmeric, salt, and spices before shallow or deep frying in oil until the exterior is crisp and golden. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice and lentils in Bengali households across Bangladesh and West Bengal.

Bhetki machher moul is a Bengali preparation made from the head (moul) of bhetki fish (Barramundi/Asian sea bass), typically slow-cooked with onions, ginger, garlic, and whole spices such as bay leaves and cardamom in a mustard oil-based gravy. It is commonly served as a standalone dish or alongside steamed rice, and is considered a delicacy in Bengali households where fish heads are prized for their gelatinous texture and concentrated flavor.

Koi macher korma is a Bengali dish made with climbing perch (koi fish) cooked in a rich, spiced korma sauce typically prepared with yogurt, onion paste, ginger, garlic, and whole spices such as cardamom, cloves, and cinnamon. The fish is generally lightly fried before being simmered in the korma gravy, and the dish is commonly served with steamed rice.

Palongshak macher kanta diye torkari is a Bengali vegetable dish in which spinach (palongshak) is cooked with small fish bones or fish scraps (macher kanta), typically sautéed with mustard oil, garlic, dried red chilies, and spices until the greens are tender and the fish bones become soft and edible. It is a home-style preparation commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice in everyday Bengali meals.

Moshola chingri is a Bengali preparation of prawns cooked with a blend of whole and ground spices, typically including mustard, turmeric, chili, cumin, and coriander, often finished with mustard oil. It is commonly served as a main course alongside steamed rice in Bengali households and restaurants.

Shol macher chhain is a Bengali preparation made from snakehead fish (shol mach) that is minced or finely chopped and typically mixed with spices such as turmeric, chili, and mustard before being shaped and cooked, often by shallow or deep frying. It is commonly served as a side dish or snack alongside rice in Bengali households, particularly in Bangladesh and West Bengal.

Tomato diye magur machh is a Bengali preparation in which catfish (magur) is cooked with fresh tomatoes, onions, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil, resulting in a tangy, spiced curry. It is typically served with steamed rice as part of a multi-course Bengali meal.

Parshe machher paturi is a Bengali preparation in which parshe fish (mullet) is coated in a spiced paste typically made with mustard, coconut, green chili, and turmeric, then wrapped in banana leaves and steamed or sometimes pan-cooked until the fish absorbs the aromatics. It is commonly served as part of a multi-course rice-based meal in Bengali households and restaurants.

Kizushi is a traditional Japanese style of sushi in which lightly salted and vinegared fish, typically mackerel or sea bream, is pressed and aged with kombu seaweed for several hours to allow the flavors to meld. It is sliced and served as nigiri or in pressed block form, and represents an older preservation-based approach to sushi preparation distinct from the fresh raw fish style common today.

Tiella di Gaeta is a traditional double-crusted savory pie from Gaeta, in the Lazio region of Italy, made with an olive oil-based dough encasing fillings such as octopus and olives, or escarole with olives, capers, and anchovies. It is baked in a round pan and typically served as a street food or light meal, cut into wedges.

Yin yang fish is a Chinese dish in which a whole live carp is prepared so that the body is deep-fried until crispy while the head remains alive and uncooked, resulting in a contrast between the cooked and uncooked portions. It is typically served immediately at the table, often accompanied by a sweet and sour sauce poured over the fried body.

Rosbora is a traditional Bengali sweet, typically prepared by deep-frying small dumplings made from chhana (fresh cheese curd) or lentil paste until golden, then soaking them in a fragrant sugar syrup often infused with cardamom. It is commonly served chilled or at room temperature as a dessert, particularly during festivals, family gatherings, and special occasions.

Topse machher jhal is a Bengali preparation featuring topse fish (Polynemus paradiseus), which are typically coated in a spiced mustard or turmeric-based paste and shallow-fried or cooked in a pungent, oil-rich gravy made with mustard paste, green chilies, and nigella seeds. It is commonly served as a main course alongside steamed white rice in Bengali households and restaurants.

Pomfret fish (machh) is coated in spices and shallow- or deep-fried (vaja) in oil, typically using a marinade of turmeric, chili, and salt before cooking. It is a common preparation in Bengali and broader South Asian cuisine, served as a side dish or starter alongside rice and lentils.

Parisienne refers to a classic French preparation style in which ingredients—most commonly potatoes—are cut into small uniform spheres using a melon baller or Parisienne scoop, then typically sautéed in butter. The technique is also applied to other vegetables or garnishes and is commonly served as a refined side dish in French cuisine.

Cortadillo norteño is a northern Mexican beef dish made by simmering small cubes of beef with tomatoes, serrano or jalapeño peppers, onion, and garlic until the meat is tender and the sauce is reduced. It is commonly served as a filling for flour tortillas or accompanied by rice and beans, and is a staple of the cuisine of states such as Chihuahua and Nuevo León.

Bol renversé is a dish originating from Réunion in which steamed white rice is packed into a bowl, inverted onto a plate, and topped with a meat-based stew or curry, typically made with chicken, pork, or beef, along with vegetables and sauce. It is a common everyday meal on the island, served as a single composed plate where the domed rice sits at the center surrounded by or covered with the accompanying braise.

Pommé is a French confection made by slowly cooking apple pulp until it reduces to a very firm, dense paste that can be sliced or cut into pieces. It is made primarily from apples and is typically served as a sweet, shelf-stable preserve, often presented as a confectionery product or accompaniment to cheeses and charcuterie.

Mesclun (musculine) is a mixed salad originating from the Nice region of France, composed of a blend of young tender leaves including varieties such as arugula, chervil, dandelion, and oak leaf lettuce. It is typically served as a simple green salad, lightly dressed with olive oil and vinegar, and is commonly presented as a starter or side dish in French and international cuisine.

Nièr beurre is a dark, thick spread made by slowly cooking apple juice or cider down to a concentrated syrup, a traditional preparation from the Liège region of Belgium. It is typically served on bread or used as a condiment alongside cheese, and shares similarities with other fruit-based reductions such as sirop de Liège.

Market jelbana is a North African dish centered on fresh or dried peas (Pisum sativum) cooked with meat, typically lamb or beef, often in a broth or light sauce seasoned with regional spices. It is commonly served as a main course, frequently accompanied by bread or over a starch base.

Tal er bora are fritters made from the pollen or flour derived from the tal (palmyra palm) fruit, mixed with sugar or jaggery and sometimes spices, then deep-fried until golden. They are a traditional Bengali snack, typically prepared during the season when palmyra palm fruit is available and served as a tea-time accompaniment or festive treat.

Palong shak er bora are fritters made by combining chopped spinach (palong shak) with gram flour (besan), onion, green chili, and spices, then deep-frying the mixture in oil until crisp. They are a common snack or side dish in Bengali cuisine, typically served hot with mustard sauce or green chutney.

Khoi er bora are Bengali fritters made from khoi (puffed or popped rice), typically bound with a batter of rice flour or gram flour along with spices such as green chili and ginger, then deep-fried until crisp. They are commonly served as a snack or street food, often accompanied by chutneys or sauces.

Ol shaager bora is a Bengali fritter made by coating leaves of ol shag (taro or elephant foot yam greens) in a spiced chickpea flour batter and deep-frying until crisp. It is typically served as a snack or side dish, often accompanied by rice or dal in everyday South Asian meals.

Pui Shaker Bora are fritters made from pui shak (Malabar spinach) leaves, typically mixed with a spiced chickpea flour batter and deep-fried until crisp. They are commonly served as a snack or side dish in Bengali cuisine, often accompanied by mustard-based condiments or rice meals.

Gadal Patar Bora is a Bengali fritter made by coating taro or colocasia leaves with a spiced batter, typically consisting of chickpea flour (besan) mixed with spices such as turmeric, chili, and salt, then deep-frying until crisp. It is commonly served as a snack or side dish, particularly during monsoon season in West Bengal and Bangladesh.

Ogontoh is typically a grilled or simmered Japanese-style dish, likely featuring seasoned seafood or meat prepared with soy-based aromatics such as mirin, sake, and ginger. It is commonly served warm in a small bowl or on a shared plate, often accompanied by steamed rice and pickled vegetables as part of a traditional meal.

Young donuts are typically small, freshly fried dough pastries inspired by the Western-style donut but adapted within Japanese bakery and café traditions, often made from a light yeasted or cake-style batter and finished with a dusting of sugar, cinnamon, or a thin glaze. They are commonly served warm as a casual snack or dessert, sometimes accompanied by coffee, tea, or matcha, and may appear in bite-sized portions suitable for sharing.

Sojne fuler bora are fritters made from drumstick tree flowers (Moringa oleifera), which are coated in a seasoned chickpea flour batter and deep-fried until crisp. They are a traditional Bengali preparation, typically served as a snack or appetizer, particularly during the spring season when drumstick flowers are in bloom.

Onion casserole from Gjakova is a traditional Albanian dish from the city of Gjakova in Kosovo, prepared by slow-cooking large quantities of onions with meat, typically lamb or veal, along with oil and seasonings until the onions caramelize and form a rich, thick base. It is commonly served as a main course, often accompanied by bread, and is associated with home cooking and regional Albanian culinary traditions.

Rufioi are a type of filled pasta from the Veneto region of Italy, made by enclosing a mixture typically containing cheese, vegetables, or meat within thin sheets of egg-based dough, then sealing and shaping them similarly to ravioli. They are traditionally served with butter and sage or other regional sauces, and are commonly prepared for festive or holiday meals.

Run down is a Caribbean stew made by simmering fish or meat—commonly mackerel, saltfish, or pork—in coconut milk that has been reduced until thick and creamy, combined with vegetables such as plantain, yam, breadfruit, and dumplings, and seasoned with onion, garlic, scotch bonnet pepper, and herbs. It is a traditional dish found across Jamaica and other Caribbean islands, typically served as a main course with ground provisions or rice.

Tunnina is a traditional Sicilian preserved tuna preparation in which tuna from the Thunnini tribe is cleaned, boiled or cooked, and packed in olive oil or salt, often in jars or tins for extended preservation. It is typically served as an antipasto, used in pasta dishes, or eaten with bread and vegetables as part of a simple meal.

Verhuny are traditional Ukrainian fried pastries made from an egg-and-flour dough that is rolled thin, cut into strips or decorative shapes, and deep-fried in oil until golden and crisp. They are typically dusted with powdered sugar and served as a sweet snack or dessert, particularly during festive occasions such as Maslenitsa (Butter Week).

Pea soup with pancakes is a dish consisting of a thick cooked pea soup served alongside thin pancakes or crêpes, which are used to accompany or dip into the soup. It is a traditional combination found in several Northern and Eastern European cuisines, commonly served as a hearty everyday meal.

Traditional Rice of Sri Lanka, known locally as 'rice and curry,' consists of steamed white or red rice served alongside an array of accompanying dishes including dhal (lentil curry), vegetable curries, fish or meat preparations, sambols such as coconut sambol, and pickles. The meal is typically eaten communally, often served on a banana leaf, with each component prepared using spices such as turmeric, cumin, coriander, fenugreek, and pandan leaves, reflecting the island's distinct culinary traditions.

Caragolada is a traditional Catalan dish consisting of snails cooked over an open fire or grill, typically seasoned with salt and sometimes accompanied by aioli, romesco sauce, or bread with tomato. It is commonly served as a communal or festive meal, particularly in rural Catalonia and during spring celebrations.

Lentho is an Indonesian fried snack made from a mixture of mashed cowpeas or black-eyed peas combined with grated coconut, kencur (aromatic ginger), and spices, shaped into small oval or round patties and deep-fried until crispy. It is commonly served as a street food snack or as part of a traditional Javanese breakfast alongside other small dishes such as rice porridge or fried tempeh.

Bibikkan is a traditional Sri Lankan coconut cake made by combining freshly scraped coconut, treacle (kithul palm syrup), semolina or rice flour, cashew nuts, dates, and spices such as cardamom and cinnamon, which are mixed and baked until firm. It is commonly prepared for festive occasions, particularly Christmas, and is typically sliced into squares or wedges and served as a dessert or sweet snack.

Maranggi satay is a grilled meat skewer originating from the Purwakarta and Cianjur regions of West Java, Indonesia, made from beef or goat meat marinated in a mixture of sweet soy sauce, coriander, and palm sugar before being charcoal-grilled. It is typically served with sambal oncom or a chili-based condiment alongside lontong (rice cake) or steamed rice, and is commonly found at roadside stalls and traditional markets.

Yurma is a traditional Russian soup in which chicken, meat, and fish are cooked together in a shared bouillon, a combination that distinguishes it from single-protein broths. It is typically served as a hot first course, with the mixed meats and fish presented in the broth.

Bugis is a traditional snack from Central Java, typically prepared by wrapping a sweet filling such as grated coconut cooked with palm sugar inside a soft dough made from glutinous rice flour. It is commonly steamed in banana leaves and often served as a teatime treat or at ceremonial gatherings.

Wereszczaka is a traditional Polish dish consisting of cooked sausage and salo (cured pork fat) simmered in a sauce made with beer and onions. It is typically served as a hearty main course, with the beer-based onion sauce providing a savory, slightly bitter flavor profile to the meat components.

Rice lengko is an Indonesian dish from Cirebon, West Java, consisting of steamed white rice topped with tofu, tempeh, bean sprouts, cucumber, and fresh herbs, all drizzled with sweet soy sauce and peanut sauce. It is commonly served as a simple, everyday meal at street stalls and warungs, and is notable for being a plant-based dish relying on soy-based proteins and fresh vegetables.

Muzhuzhi is a traditional Georgian dish made from pork extremities, typically trotters, ears, and tail, which are boiled until tender and then preserved in a cold brine of water, vinegar, and garlic. It is served cold as an appetizer or snack, often accompanied by bread or as part of a spread of Georgian cold dishes.

Mie celor is an Indonesian noodle dish originating from Palembang, South Sumatra, prepared with thick wheat noodles served in a rich, coconut milk-based broth flavored with dried shrimp, shrimp paste, and spices. It is typically garnished with bean sprouts, hard-boiled eggs, celery leaves, and fried shallots, and is commonly consumed as a breakfast or lunch dish in the Palembang region.

Brustico is a traditional preparation from the Lake Trasimeno area of Umbria, Italy, in which European perch and other small lake fish are grilled or roasted directly over an open fire, often on skewers or grills placed above burning reeds or wood. The dish is typically served as a main course and is closely associated with the fishing communities surrounding Lake Trasimeno, where it has been a customary way of cooking freshwater catch.

Es doger is an Indonesian shaved ice drink originating from Bandung, West Java, made with a base of coconut milk and rose syrup poured over finely shaved ice and typically mixed with ingredients such as cincau (black grass jelly), tapai (fermented cassava), avocado, and jackfruit. It is commonly sold by street vendors and consumed as a cold refreshment, particularly popular during hot weather throughout Indonesia.

Combro is a Sundanese fried snack made from grated cassava that is shaped into an oval or round form and filled with a spiced mixture of oncom, chili, and seasoning before being deep-fried until golden and crispy. It is commonly sold as a street food snack in West Java, Indonesia, and is often served alongside other cassava-based snacks such as misro.

Bánh rế is a Vietnamese deep-fried flatbread made from a mixture of grated sweet potato (or taro) and rice flour, formed into a thin, lacy round patty and fried in oil until crisp and golden. It is commonly sold as a street food snack, served on its own or alongside other snacks, and is recognized by its distinctive net-like, crispy texture.

Delele is a Malawian dish made from okra, which is cooked until it develops a characteristic slimy or mucilaginous texture, often combined with tomatoes, onions, and sometimes groundnut flour or bicarbonate of soda to adjust the consistency. It is typically served as a side dish alongside nsima, the stiff maize porridge that forms the staple base of meals in Malawi and neighboring countries.

Faroese puffin is a traditional dish from the Faroe Islands prepared using Atlantic Puffin, which is typically hunted by net and then plucked, cleaned, and slow-cooked or roasted, sometimes smoked or served in a broth. It is commonly served as a main course in domestic and festive contexts in the Faroe Islands, where puffin hunting has historically been a customary practice.

Arroz a la zamorana is a traditional Spanish rice dish from the province of Zamora, prepared by slow-cooking short-grain rice with pork-based ingredients including pig's trotters, ears, snout, and chorizo in a paprika-seasoned broth. It is typically served as a hearty main course, particularly during cold months, and is considered a representative dish of Castilian peasant cooking.

Asadurilla is a Spanish offal dish prepared by sautéing chopped lamb or kid liver, lungs, and other viscera with garlic, paprika, olive oil, and sometimes vinegar or white wine. It is typically served as a tapa or main course, particularly associated with rural and traditional cooking in regions such as Castilla-La Mancha and Extremadura.

Pataleh Polo is an Iranian rice dish in which parboiled basmati rice is layered and steamed with a mixture of herbs, typically including fenugreek leaves, alongside lamb or other meat and aromatic spices. It is commonly served as a main course, often accompanied by yogurt or pickled vegetables, and is associated with Persian culinary traditions.

Fricot is an Acadian stew traditionally made with chicken or rabbit, potatoes, onions, and savory, simmered together in a broth until tender. It is a staple dish of Acadian cuisine, commonly served as a hearty main course, and is sometimes prepared with dumplings or other vegetables depending on regional variations.

Bubur anak lebah is a Malaysian porridge whose name literally translates to 'baby bee porridge,' typically prepared by cooking glutinous rice flour or rice into a thick, sweet pudding-like base. It commonly includes coconut milk and palm sugar (gula melaka), with small dough pearls resembling bee larvae giving the dish its name, and is often served warm as a dessert or traditional snack.

Palbochae is a Korean mixed vegetable dish composed of eight ingredients, typically including a variety of stir-fried or blanched vegetables such as mushrooms, bamboo shoots, carrots, and other seasonal produce. It is commonly served as a side dish or banchan in Korean cuisine, often accompanying rice or as part of a larger multi-course meal.

Pan meino is a traditional Lombard cornmeal cake made with finely ground yellow maize flour, eggs, butter, sugar, and elderflower blossoms, which give it a distinctive floral aroma. It is baked into small, flat rounds and is traditionally eaten in the Milan area on the feast of Sant'Ambrogio (December 7), often accompanied by fresh cream.

San Antón rolls are a traditional dish from Cartagena, Spain, prepared using the offal and leftover cuts of pork — including ears, snout, and trotters — that are cooked, seasoned, and formed into compact rolls or cylinders. They are historically associated with the feast of San Antón (January 17th) and are typically served sliced as a cold cut or tapa, reflecting the regional tradition of utilizing the whole pig following the winter matanza (slaughter).

Solozhenyk is a traditional Ukrainian dessert made from layers of unleavened flatbread or thin pastry sheets combined with a filling of sweetened sour cream, sometimes enriched with honey, nuts, or dried fruits, then pressed and chilled until set. It is typically served sliced as a cold dessert or sweet snack, and its name derives from the Ukrainian word for a layered or stacked preparation.

Chocolate salo is a Ukrainian confection made by coating or enrobing cured pork fatback (salo) in a layer of dark or milk chocolate, sometimes with butter incorporated to smooth the chocolate mixture. It is typically served chilled as a novelty sweet or snack, and is commonly found at Ukrainian markets and food festivals as a playful fusion of traditional salo culture with chocolate confectionery.

Dragon's Breath is a novelty snack consisting of cereal puffs, typically cheese puffs or fruit-flavored cereals, that have been submerged in liquid nitrogen until frozen solid. It is served immediately after preparation so that consumers exhale visible vapor clouds resembling smoke when eating the frozen pieces, and is commonly sold as a street food or carnival treat.

Abisinios are Spanish pastry rolls made from a thin dough that is filled with pastry cream and shaped into cylindrical or rolled forms before baking. They are a traditional confection typically found in Spanish pastry shops and served as a sweet snack or dessert.

A stuffed cookie is a baked confection in which a filling—commonly chocolate ganache, caramel, cream cheese, or fruit preserves—is enclosed within cookie dough before baking, resulting in a soft outer layer surrounding a molten or set interior. It is typically served as a dessert or snack and is found across international bakeries and cafés, including those in Iran where variations may incorporate local flavors such as saffron or rose water.

Chhena Tadia is an Odia dish made by deep-frying flattened patties or balls of fresh chhena (Indian cottage cheese), which are then simmered in a spiced gravy typically made with tomatoes, onions, and aromatic spices such as cumin, turmeric, and garam masala. It is commonly served as a main course accompaniment to rice or flatbreads in the cuisine of Odisha, eastern India.

Molotof is a Portuguese dessert made by whipping egg whites with sugar into a stiff meringue, which is then baked in a caramel-lined mold to produce a light, airy pudding with a soft interior and a caramel coating. It is typically unmolded and served at room temperature or chilled, often as a dessert course in traditional Portuguese households and restaurants.

Pan della Marchesa is a traditional Italian sweet bread originating from the Marche region, prepared with flour, eggs, sugar, butter, and typically flavored with anise or other aromatic ingredients. It is commonly served as a festive or celebratory bread, often associated with local holidays and special occasions in central Italy.

Pane coi santi is a traditional Tuscan sweet bread made with walnuts, raisins, black pepper, and olive oil, enriched with red wine and sometimes anise or rosemary, then baked until golden. It is a seasonal bread historically associated with All Saints' Day on November 1st in Tuscany, particularly in and around Siena and Florence.

Koi machher horogouri is a Bengali dish prepared by cooking koi fish (climbing perch) with a spiced paste made from mustard seeds, poppy seeds, and green chilies, typically finished with mustard oil. It is commonly served as a main course alongside steamed rice in Bengali household meals.

Macher moul is a Bengali fish head preparation, where the head of a large freshwater fish such as rohu or katla is typically fried and then simmered in a spiced gravy or cooked together with lentils or vegetables. It is commonly served with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal, often featured during festive occasions or family gatherings.

Bingka Barandam is a traditional Banjar cake from South Kalimantan, Indonesia, made from rice flour, coconut milk, sugar, and eggs, mixed into a batter and baked or steamed in a mold until set. It is commonly served as a snack or dessert during festive occasions and religious celebrations in Banjar communities.

Biscottini di Novara are small, dry Italian biscuits from the Piedmont region, made by mixing flour, sugar, eggs, vanilla, and a small amount of sodium bicarbonate, then shaping the dough into bite-sized pieces and baking them until firm and lightly golden. They are typically served as an accompaniment to dessert wines, coffee, or tea, and are also commonly paired with soft cheeses in traditional northern Italian contexts.

Tounao is a traditional Chinese breakfast dish originating from Taiyuan, Shanxi province, consisting of a broth-based preparation made with mutton, lotus rhizome, Dioscorea polystachya (Chinese yam), grain lees, and wheat flour, which together produce a thick, warming soup. It is typically served in the morning, often accompanied by a fried dough stick for dipping, and is associated with cold-weather consumption.

Owo soup is a Nigerian dish originating from the Urhobo people of Delta State, prepared by cooking palm fruit extract with fresh fish, crayfish, and various seasonings to produce a thick, reddish broth. It is traditionally served alongside starch (made from cassava) or eba, and the soup is characteristically left unsalted or lightly seasoned to complement its accompaniments.

Cigarette russe is a thin, crisp rolled wafer cookie made from a batter of butter, sugar, egg whites, and flour, baked flat and shaped into a cylinder while still warm. In the Netherlands, it is commonly served as an accompaniment to coffee, tea, or ice cream desserts.

Sopa de calducho is a traditional soup from the Canary Islands, particularly associated with La Palma, made from water, garlic, oil, vinegar, and stale bread, sometimes enriched with paprika or other simple pantry ingredients. It is a humble, peasant-origin dish historically prepared as a light meal using leftover bread, and is typically served warm as a simple first course or light supper.

Amaretti di Mombaruzzo are small, soft Italian almond cookies originating from Mombaruzzo in the Piedmont region, made primarily from sweet and bitter almonds, sugar, and egg whites, which are mixed into a paste, shaped into small rounds, and baked until lightly golden with a tender, slightly chewy interior. They are traditionally served as a dessert or confection, often accompanied by dessert wines such as Moscato d'Asti, and are also used as an ingredient in regional Piedmontese dishes like stuffed pasta and baked peaches.

Caldosa is a Cuban street food dish consisting of a thick soup or stew made with chicken, root vegetables such as yuca and plantain, and seasonings, served in a cup or bowl. It is commonly sold by street vendors and is a popular casual dish associated with outdoor festivals and public gatherings in Cuba.

Shakarap is a Tajik fresh salad made from finely chopped tomatoes and onions seasoned with salt, sometimes accompanied by herbs. It is typically served as a side dish or accompaniment to grilled meats and other main courses in Central Asian cuisine.

Editan is a Nigerian soup, particularly associated with the Efik and Ibibio peoples of Cross River and Akwa Ibom states, prepared by cooking leafy vegetables—including waterleaf and the leaves of Catharanthus roseus (periwinkle plant)—with palm oil, meat, stockfish, crayfish, and ground Capsicum peppers. It is typically served as a thick, protein-rich soup alongside starchy accompaniments such as fufu, pounded yam, or eba.

Pleah sach ko is a Cambodian beef salad prepared by briefly cooking thinly sliced beef in lime juice, which partially denatures the meat through acid, then combining it with fresh herbs such as lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, mint, and toasted rice powder, often finished with fish sauce and chili. It is typically served as a starter or light main course, presented at room temperature alongside fresh vegetables or rice.

Port sausage is an Iranian preparation in which sausage is cooked with tomato paste, fresh tomatoes, onion, and spices to produce a sauced dish. It is commonly served as a quick meal, often accompanied by bread or rice.

Pasta al zozzona is a Roman pasta dish that combines elements of several classic sauces, made with rigatoni or mezze maniche tossed in a sauce of guanciale, sausage, eggs, Pecorino Romano, and tomatoes. It is typically served as a primi piatti in Roman trattorias, representing a rich, egg-and-pork-enriched tomato preparation that draws on traditions from cacio e pepe, carbonara, amatriciana, and gricia.

Gondhoraj rui machh is a Bengali preparation of rohu fish (Labeo rohita) marinated and cooked with gondhoraj lime (a fragrant citrus variety native to Bengal), mustard paste, green chilies, and mustard oil, often finished with a squeeze of gondhoraj lime juice to highlight its distinctive floral aroma. It is typically served with steamed white rice as part of a traditional Bengali multi-course meal.

Lote paturi is a Bengali preparation in which lote fish (Bombay duck, Harpadon nehereus) is marinated with mustard paste, green chili, turmeric, and mustard oil, then wrapped in banana leaves and steamed or cooked over low heat. It is typically served as a main course alongside steamed rice.

Lote machher jhuri is a Bengali preparation made by shredding or finely flaking Bombay duck (loitta or lote fish) and cooking it with mustard oil, onions, green chilies, turmeric, and spices until dry and crumbly. It is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Lote machh fry is a Bengali preparation in which Bombay duck (Harpadon nehereus), a soft, highly perishable saltwater fish, is coated in spiced flour or gram flour and shallow- or deep-fried until crisp. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish alongside rice and dal in Bengali households and restaurants.

Pona machh diye bandhakopi is a Bengali dish made by cooking shredded or chopped cabbage (bandhakopi) with pona fish (Labeo bata), typically prepared with mustard oil, turmeric, green chilies, and whole spices such as cumin and bay leaf. It is a common everyday home-cooked dish in Bengali households, served alongside steamed rice as part of a multi-course meal.

Mourala machh vaja is a Bengali fried fish dish prepared by coating small mourala fish (Amblypharyngodon mola, a freshwater minnow) in spiced turmeric and chili paste before shallow- or deep-frying until crisp. It is commonly served as a side dish or starter alongside rice in traditional Bengali meals from the Indian subcontinent.

Pabda sorse is a Bengali fish dish in which pabda (butter catfish, Ompok pabda) is cooked in a mustard-based sauce made from ground mustard seeds, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. It is typically prepared by lightly frying the fish before simmering it in the pungent mustard paste, and is served with steamed rice as a main course.

Pomfret jhal is a Bengali fish preparation in which pomfret is cooked in a pungent mustard-based sauce, typically made with ground mustard seeds, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. It is commonly served with steamed white rice as a main course in Bengali households and restaurants.

Topse machh vaja is a Bengali fried fish dish made from tapsi fish (Polynemus paradiseus), which are coated in a spiced batter—typically incorporating turmeric, red chili, and salt—and deep-fried in oil until crisp. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish in Bengali households and restaurants, often accompanied by rice and dal.

Kosha aar machh is a Bengali preparation of catfish (typically local river catfish known as aar or boal) slow-cooked through a dry-frying technique called 'kosha,' in which the fish is braised with onions, ginger, garlic, and a blend of spices such as turmeric, cumin, coriander, and red chili until the sauce is deeply reduced and clings to the fish. It is commonly served with steamed rice as a main course in Bengali households and restaurants.

Panchforon bati chingri is a Bengali dish in which shrimp are cooked with panchforon, a whole spice blend consisting of fenugreek, nigella, cumin, black mustard, and fennel seeds, along with mustard oil, green chilies, and aromatics. The preparation typically involves marinating or cooking the shrimp with the spice mixture and serving it as a main course accompaniment to steamed rice.

Katla machher korma is a Bengali dish made by cooking katla fish (Catla catla) in a rich, spiced sauce based on yogurt, onion paste, ginger, garlic, and whole spices such as cardamom, cloves, and cinnamon, often finished with ghee. It is typically served with steamed rice as part of a multi-course Bengali meal.

Chitol peti is a Bengali dish made from the belly and chest portion of the chitol fish (Chitala chitala), which is deboned, mixed with spices, and typically shaped into kofta balls or cooked as a curry. It is commonly prepared with mustard paste, onion, green chilies, and aromatic spices, and served as a main course alongside rice in Bengali households and restaurants.

Masala pomfret is a dish in which whole or sliced pomfret fish is coated in a spiced marinade typically made from chili, turmeric, coriander, cumin, ginger, and garlic, then pan-fried or shallow-fried until the exterior is crisp. It is commonly served as a starter or main course alongside rice, bread, or chutney, and is particularly associated with coastal South Asian and Indian seafood cooking.

Sorse pomfret is a Bengali preparation in which pomfret fish is cooked in a mustard-based sauce (sorse) made from ground mustard seeds, green chilies, and mustard oil, often with turmeric and salt. It is typically served with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Févi is typically a savory stew-like preparation, with the name suggesting a dish centered on beans or legumes, often simmered slowly with aromatics, herbs, and sometimes meat or seafood depending on regional interpretation. It is commonly served warm as a main course, frequently accompanied by rice, bread, or a starchy side to complement the hearty base.

Hinchar bora is a traditional Bengali fritter typically prepared by combining finely chopped hincha (a leafy green herb commonly used in Bengali cooking) with a seasoned batter of rice flour or gram flour, then deep-frying small portions until golden and crisp. It is often served as a side dish alongside steamed rice and dal, or enjoyed as a monsoon-season snack with tea.

Lal shak diye bele machh is a Bengali preparation in which bele machh (sand goby fish) is cooked with lal shak (red amaranth leaves), typically combined with mustard oil, green chilies, turmeric, and other spices in a stir-fry or light curry style. The dish is commonly served with steamed rice as part of a traditional multi-course Bengali meal.

Til rui is a Bengali dish in which rohu fish (Labeo rohita) is cooked in a sesame seed (til) based sauce, typically prepared by grinding white sesame seeds into a paste and combining it with mustard oil, green chilies, turmeric, and salt. The fish pieces are usually lightly fried before being simmered in the sesame gravy, and the dish is commonly served with steamed white rice.

Lebu patay katla is a Bengali preparation of katla (rohu-family carp) cooked with lemon juice, where the acidity of the lemon is used to effectively 'kill' or cure the fish, giving the dish its name ('lebu patay' meaning 'killed by lemon'). The fish is typically marinated or simmered with lemon juice, green chillies, and spices, and is served as part of a traditional Bengali meal alongside rice.

Kalio is an Indonesian dry curry made by simmering meat, most commonly beef, in a rich spiced coconut milk sauce with ingredients such as galangal, turmeric, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, and chilies until the liquid is nearly fully reduced and the sauce clings to the meat. It is considered an intermediate stage of rendang, cooked for less time so that the coconut milk does not fully caramelize, resulting in a moist, saucy texture, and it is typically served with steamed rice as a main dish.

Pipareddi are traditional Sicilian fried pastries made from a simple dough that is shaped into small rings or twisted forms and deep-fried until golden. They are typically dusted with powdered sugar or drizzled with honey and served as a festive sweet, commonly associated with carnival celebrations and religious holidays in Sicily.

Patir is a Central Asian flatbread, particularly associated with Uzbek cuisine, made from wheat flour dough that is shaped into thick rounds and baked in a tandoor oven. It is typically served as an accompaniment to meals, often alongside soups, stews, or grilled meats, and is characterized by a crisp outer crust with a soft interior.

Luzhu Huoshao is a traditional Beijing street food dish consisting of braised pig offal—typically intestines, liver, and lungs—simmered in a seasoned broth alongside fried dough sticks (youtiao) and tofu, with the cooking liquid heavily flavored with soy sauce, fermented bean curd, and spices. It is commonly served as a breakfast or casual meal, ladled into bowls with the braised components and broth together, and often garnished with cilantro and chili oil.

Wantuo is a traditional food from the Shanxi province of China, made by cooking buckwheat or sorghum flour into a thick paste that is then cooled and set into a firm, jelly-like cake. It is typically sliced into strips or blocks and served cold with a seasoned dressing of vinegar, garlic, chili oil, and other condiments.

Carnikava lamprey refers to river lampreys (Lampetra fluviatilis) caught in the Carnikava region of Latvia along the Gauja River, traditionally prepared by smoking, marinating, or frying the whole cleaned fish. The dish is commonly served as a cold smoked or marinated appetizer, often accompanied by bread, and holds protected geographical indication status reflecting its regional origin.

Popcorn is made by heating dried maize kernels until the moisture inside causes them to expand and burst, producing a light, puffed snack. It is commonly finished with butter or flavored oils and served as a standalone snack or accompaniment in casual dining and entertainment contexts.

Pecora alla callara is a traditional Abruzzese dish of mature sheep (pecora) slow-cooked in a large copper or terracotta pot called a callara, along with aromatics such as wild celery, onion, garlic, and local herbs, until the meat is fully tender. It is a rustic preparation historically associated with shepherding culture in Abruzzo and is typically served as a communal dish during festivals or seasonal gatherings.

Couvert is a pre-meal table service common in Portuguese restaurants, typically consisting of bread, butter, and various small accompaniments such as olives, cheese, cured meats, or spreads placed on the table before ordering. It is not complimentary and is charged per person, with guests having the option to decline the items to avoid the additional cost.

Korolar bora is a Bengali-style fritter typically prepared by grating or finely chopping bitter gourd (korola), mixing it with a spiced batter of rice flour or gram flour, green chilies, and onions, and then deep-frying until golden and crisp. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish alongside steamed rice and dal as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Ofe Uda is a Nigerian soup originating from the Igbo people, prepared by cooking uda (Negro pepper, Xylopia aethiopica) pods along with palm fruit extract, leafy vegetables, assorted meats or fish, and various traditional spices and seasonings. It is typically served as a thick, flavorful soup accompanied by swallows such as fufu, eba, or pounded yam.

Pawpaw soup is prepared using unripe or ripe pawpaw (papaya) as a primary ingredient, typically combined with palm oil, crayfish, leafy vegetables, and various proteins such as meat or fish, then simmered into a stew-like consistency. It is commonly served as a main course alongside staple foods such as rice, fufu, or plantains, and is found particularly in West African culinary traditions.

Three Sisters Soup is a traditional dish from Indigenous cuisines of the Americas made by simmering the three companion crops—corn, beans, and squash—together in a broth, often with additional aromatics such as onion and sometimes meat or chili peppers depending on the regional variation. It is typically served as a hearty staple meal and reflects the agricultural practice of growing corn, beans, and squash together, a polyculture system used by many Native American peoples.

Ila Alasepo is a Yoruba okra soup prepared by cooking whole or sliced okra with palm oil, locust beans (iru), assorted meats, fish, and seasonings until the okra reaches a characteristic slimy, draw consistency. It is traditionally served with swallows such as eba, amala, or pounded yam as part of West African, particularly Nigerian, meals.

Mustard soup is a warm soup prepared by incorporating mustard seeds or ground mustard into a liquid base, often combined with cream, broth, and aromatics to produce a smooth or lightly textured consistency. It is typically served as a first course and appears in various European culinary traditions, where the pungency of mustard is balanced by dairy or stock components.

Okazi soup is a traditional Nigerian soup prepared by simmering shredded okazi (Gnetum africanum) leaves with palm oil, assorted meats or fish, crayfish, and seasonings such as ogiri or iru, often thickened with cocoyam paste or achi. It is typically served with swallows such as fufu, eba, or pounded yam, and is particularly associated with the Igbo and Efik peoples of southeastern Nigeria.

Horseradish soup is prepared by simmering grated or creamed horseradish root with broth, often enriched with cream, butter, and aromatics such as onion and garlic, then seasoned with salt, pepper, and sometimes lemon juice or vinegar. It is typically served hot as a first course, occasionally garnished with croutons, boiled egg, or fresh herbs, and appears in Central and Eastern European culinary traditions as well as contemporary international menus.

Curry soup is a liquid dish prepared by simmering a broth base with curry spices—typically including turmeric, cumin, coriander, and chili—along with vegetables, legumes, or meat. It is commonly served hot as a starter or main course and appears across various international cuisines, often accompanied by bread or rice.

Miyan zogale is a Nigerian soup made primarily from moringa leaves (zogale), which are cooked with ground ingredients such as groundnut paste, onions, and spices, often with the addition of meat or fish. It is a traditional dish from northern Nigeria, commonly served over tuwo shinkafa (rice flour porridge) or other starchy staples as a main meal.

Storceag la cuptor is a Romanian baked dish made from sturgeon (storceag) that is seasoned and cooked in the oven, typically with onions, tomatoes, peppers, and sometimes white wine or oil. It is commonly served as a main course, often accompanied by polenta or bread, and represents a traditional preparation from the Danube Delta region of Romania.

Tarempa noodles are a dish originating from Tarempa, the capital of Anambas Islands Regency in the Riau Islands province of Indonesia, prepared by stir-frying flat rice noodles with tuna or other fish, egg, chili, and soy-based seasonings. The dish is a common street food and everyday meal in the region, typically served on a plate and known for its savory, slightly spicy flavor profile derived from the combination of fresh fish and chili.

The Freshness Burger from Freshness Burger's Yaba-chō-ten location is a beef hamburger served in a bun with fresh vegetables including lettuce and tomato, along with condiments such as mustard and ketchup. It is typically served as a fast-casual meal alongside sides such as french fries and a beverage at Freshness Burger, a Japanese burger chain known for using relatively fresh, minimally processed ingredients compared to conventional fast food.

Eka soup is a West African soup prepared by combining bitter leaf, crayfish, red pepper, onion, and palm oil in a water base, seasoned with salt and enriched with ground peanut and sesame seed candy to create a thick, savory broth. It is typically served as a main dish accompanied by a starchy staple such as fufu or pounded yam.

Kostar laddu is typically a sweet, round confection in the laddu family, commonly prepared by binding roasted flour, ghee, sugar or jaggery, and occasionally nuts or seeds into bite-sized balls. It is often served as a dessert or festive treat, presented at room temperature alongside tea or as part of a celebratory spread.

Rice pudding cake is prepared by combining cooked rice with milk, eggs, sugar, and flavorings such as vanilla, then baking the mixture in a cake tin until set into a firm, sliceable form. It is typically served as a dessert, either warm or chilled, and bridges the texture of traditional baked rice pudding with that of a molded cake.

Sulu muhallebi is a Turkish milk pudding prepared by cooking milk with rice flour or starch and sugar into a thin, pourable consistency, distinguishing it from the firmer set muhallebi. It is typically served as a light dessert or snack, often at room temperature or chilled, sometimes flavored with rose water.

Pane dell'Assedio is a traditional bread from the Mantua area, historically associated with the siege of Mantua, made with a dense, long-keeping dough that allowed it to be stored and consumed over extended periods. It is a rustic, wheat-based loaf characteristic of the upper Mantuan culinary tradition and is typically served as an accompaniment to local cured meats, cheeses, and other regional foods.

Javanais are small French pastries made from almond sponge cake (génoise or joconde) soaked in kirsch- or rum-flavored syrup and filled with mocha or chocolate buttercream, then coated in green marzipan or fondant icing. They are typically cut into small rectangular or diamond shapes and served as individual petit fours in pâtisseries or at formal gatherings.

Biscuits of Saint Martin (Biscotti di San Martino) are traditional Sicilian dry, hard cookies prepared with flour, anise seeds, and sometimes almonds or candied fruit, baked until firm and crisp. They are typically associated with the feast of Saint Martin on November 11th and are traditionally served dunked in sweet wine such as Moscato or Marsala.

Tigelada is a traditional Portuguese baked custard made by combining eggs, milk, sugar, flour, salt, and lemon zest, then pouring the mixture into individual earthenware bowls and baking until set with a lightly browned surface. It is typically served at room temperature or warm as a dessert, and is closely associated with the Beira Baixa region of Portugal.

Biscuit cake is a no-bake dessert made by combining crushed or layered biscuits with a mixture of butter, cocoa, sugar, and sometimes eggs or condensed milk, then chilling the assembled cake until firm. It is commonly sliced and served cold as a simple everyday dessert or party treat, and variations appear across many European and Middle Eastern countries.

Bananas Foster bread pudding is made by layering or mixing cubed bread—typically day-old French or brioche—with a custard of eggs, cream, sugar, and bananas, then baking until set. It is served with a warm Bananas Foster sauce made from butter, brown sugar, rum, and banana liqueur, combining the classic New Orleans dessert with traditional bread pudding.

Radhaballav is a Bengali dish originating from West Bengal, India, consisting of deep-fried lentil-stuffed kachori (puffed bread) made with urad dal filling seasoned with spices such as asafoetida, ginger, and pepper. It is traditionally served with a spiced chickpea curry (cholar dal) and is a customary breakfast or festival dish, particularly associated with the Radhaballav temple in Krishnanagar.

'Ohana Bread Pudding is a baked dessert served at 'Ohana restaurant in Disney's Polynesian Village Resort, prepared by soaking bread in a sweetened custard mixture and baking until set. It is typically served warm with a banana-caramel sauce and whipped cream, and is a standard dessert offering on the restaurant's family-style dinner menu.

Sili ice cream is a Filipino frozen dessert made by incorporating chili peppers (sili) into a cream-based ice cream mixture, producing a sweet base with a spicy heat from the peppers. It is typically served as a scoop in a cup or cone and is found at specialty ice cream shops and food markets in the Philippines as a novelty flavor.

Piada dei morti is a traditional flatbread from the Romagna region of Italy, prepared for the Day of the Dead (November 2nd) by incorporating dried figs, walnuts, raisins, and anise seeds into a dough made with flour, lard or olive oil, and sugar. It is baked or cooked on a griddle and served as a sweet treat during the autumn commemoration period, distinguishing it from the savory everyday piadina of the same region.

Yogurt Cream is a soft-serve frozen dessert offered by the Japanese fast food chain Sugakiya, made from a yogurt-flavored cream base with a light, tangy taste. It is typically served in a cone or cup as a standalone dessert item at Sugakiya restaurant locations in Japan.

Tàfara is a traditional dish associated with Vulanza in Italy, typically prepared using locally sourced ingredients characteristic of regional Italian cooking, which often include grains, vegetables, and cured or roasted meats. It is commonly served as part of a main course during communal meals or village festivities, reflecting the rustic culinary traditions of the area.

Avanît is a traditional Kurdish dish, typically prepared using regional staples such as grains, dairy, or slow-cooked meats seasoned with herbs and mild spices common to the cuisine. It is commonly served as part of a shared meal, often accompanied by flatbread, yogurt, or fresh vegetables in a home-style setting.

Gondhoraj Bhetki is a Bengali preparation in which bhetki (barramundi or Asian sea bass) fillets are marinated and cooked with gondhoraj lime, a fragrant variety of lime native to Bengal, along with mustard paste, green chilies, and oil, typically steamed or pan-cooked in banana leaf parcels. The dish is served as a main course, often accompanied by steamed rice, and is noted for the distinctive citrus aroma imparted by the gondhoraj lime.

Peyajkoli diye rui machh is a Bengali dish made by cooking rohu fish (Labeo rohita) with the green shoots of onion plants (peyajkoli), typically prepared by lightly frying the fish pieces and then simmering them in a mustard oil-based sauce seasoned with turmeric, green chilies, and salt. It is commonly served as a main course alongside steamed rice in Bengali households, particularly during seasons when onion greens are available.

Es goyobod is a cold Indonesian dessert drink originating from West Java, made with goyobod (a soft, chewy cake prepared from green bean flour or wheat flour), coconut milk, palm sugar syrup, and shaved or crushed ice. It is commonly served as a refreshing street food beverage, often accompanied by additional ingredients such as avocado, kolang-kaling (palm fruit), and cincau (grass jelly).

Stuffed mallow (ebegümeci dolması) is a Turkish dish in which fresh mallow leaves are filled with a mixture of rice, onion, herbs such as mint and dill, and sometimes ground meat, then rolled and slowly cooked in a pot with water, olive oil, and lemon juice. It is typically served as a main course or side dish, often accompanied by yogurt, and is particularly common in the Aegean and Mediterranean regions of Turkey.

Snail satay is an Indonesian dish in which land or freshwater snails are skewered on bamboo sticks, seasoned with a spiced marinade typically including garlic, shallots, chili, and sweet soy sauce, then grilled over charcoal. It is commonly sold as a street food snack and served with sambal or peanut sauce on the side.

Ukodo is a Nigerian peppersoup-style dish originating from the Urhobo people of the Niger Delta region, prepared by simmering yam (Dioscorea species) and unripe plantain (cooking banana) together with meat in a spiced broth seasoned with local peppersoup spices. It is traditionally served as a one-pot meal, particularly at celebratory gatherings such as naming ceremonies and funerals, and is commonly eaten as a warming, substantial dish.

Lontong dekem is an Indonesian dish consisting of compressed rice cake (lontong) submerged or soaked in a rich, spiced coconut milk broth, typically combined with ingredients such as tofu, tempeh, eggs, and vegetables. It is a traditional dish commonly found in Java, served as a filling meal at breakfast or throughout the day.

Ros bratel is a Swiss-German braised pork dish in which a bone-in pork shoulder or neck cut is slow-cooked in a sauce made with onions, wine or vinegar, and spices until tender. It is traditionally served with accompaniments such as rösti, bread dumplings, or boiled potatoes, and is a common feature of hearty Central European home cooking and restaurant menus.

Cactus fries are strips of nopal (prickly pear cactus pads) that are battered and deep-fried until crispy, often seasoned with salt and spices. They are commonly served as a snack or side dish, sometimes accompanied by dipping sauces such as sour cream or salsa.

Zeroa is a roasted lamb shankbone or chicken neck prepared by roasting over an open flame or in an oven until charred on the outside. It is one of the six symbolic items placed on the Passover Seder plate, where it represents the sacrificial lamb offering made by ancient Israelites in Egypt.

Qarta is a traditional dish made from horse rectum, typically cleaned thoroughly and either boiled or served as part of a larger preparation of horse offal. It is consumed in Central Asian culinary traditions, often alongside other parts of the horse, and is served as a component of festive or everyday meals in regions where horse meat is a dietary staple.

Kondowole is a thick porridge made from cassava flour, commonly prepared by cooking the flour with water until it reaches a stiff, dough-like consistency similar to ugali or fufu. It is a staple food in Malawi and parts of central Africa, typically served as an accompaniment to relishes such as vegetables, beans, or meat stews.

Babi panggang karo is a grilled pork dish originating from the Batak Karo people of North Sumatra, prepared by marinating the pork in a spiced mixture that typically includes andaliman (Batak pepper), garlic, shallots, and turmeric before grilling over charcoal. It is commonly served with a rich pork blood-based sauce and steamed rice, and is a central dish at ceremonial gatherings and festive occasions within Karo Batak communities.

Kecampit is a traditional snack originating from Way Kanan in the Lampung province of Indonesia, typically prepared from simple starchy ingredients such as cassava or rice flour that are shaped into small pieces and fried until crisp. It is commonly enjoyed as a light accompaniment to tea or coffee and is often served during gatherings and local celebrations.

Naranjas picadas is a Spanish salad dish in which sliced oranges are combined with salted cod (bacalao) or mackerel, onion, olive oil, vinegar, and salt. It is typically served cold as a starter or side dish, often associated with Andalusian and broader Spanish home cooking traditions.

Kaz tiridi is a traditional Turkish dish made by tearing or shredding cooked goose meat and layering it over pieces of stale or toasted bread soaked in the bird's own cooking broth and fat. It is typically served as a hearty main course, often garnished with the rendered goose fat drizzled on top, and is commonly prepared during winter months when geese are slaughtered.

Pallu butung is a traditional South Sulawesi (Bugis-Makassar) dessert soup made by simmering unripe banana pieces in a sweetened coconut milk broth, typically flavored with pandanus leaves and palm sugar. It is commonly served warm or at room temperature as a street food or home dessert, sometimes accompanied by a drizzle of additional coconut milk on top.

Sour meat is a traditional fermented or acidified meat preparation found across various cultures, in which raw or cooked meat is preserved through lactic acid fermentation, vinegar, or souring agents, often combined with salt, spices, and sometimes vegetables. It is typically served as a cold cut, spread, or cooked dish depending on regional tradition, and appears in contexts ranging from everyday meals to preserved food stores.

Talabaw is a Filipino soup or broth made by simmering fish, shrimp, or other seafood with vegetables such as moringa leaves, squash, or banana blossoms, often seasoned with ginger and salt. It is typically served as a main dish or alongside steamed rice in everyday Filipino household meals.

Binte biluhuta is a traditional Gorontalo soup made from corn kernels cooked with tuna or skipjack fish, shallots, lemongrass, lime leaves, and chili peppers, finished with a squeeze of lime juice. It is a staple dish of the Gorontalo people in Sulawesi, Indonesia, commonly served as a main course accompanied by steamed rice.

Keumamah is a traditional Acehnese dish made from dried and smoked skipjack tuna (tongkol) that has been rehydrated and cooked with spices including coconut, galangal, lemongrass, chili, and turmeric. It is a dry-style preparation commonly served as a side dish with steamed rice and is considered a staple in the cuisine of Aceh province in Indonesia.

Ambal satay is a style of satay originating from the Kebumen region of Central Java, Indonesia, made from chicken or goat meat skewered and grilled over charcoal. It is distinguished by its spicy peanut-based sauce seasoned with chili and local spices, and is typically served as a street food or at local warungs accompanied by lontong or rice.

Sambalingkung is a traditional Sundanese dish made from fermented or dried fish, typically ikan asin, that is ground or pounded together with spices such as chili, shallots, garlic, and coconut, then cooked into a dense, savory paste or condiment. It is commonly served as a side dish or accompaniment to steamed rice in West Java, Indonesia.

Jepara beef satay is a grilled skewered meat dish originating from Jepara, Central Java, Indonesia, in which seasoned beef pieces are threaded onto skewers and cooked over charcoal. It is typically served with a spiced sauce or peanut-based accompaniment and is commonly found as a street food or at local Indonesian eateries.

Mi gomak is a traditional Batak noodle dish from North Sumatra, Indonesia, prepared by cooking thick rice noodles (laksa noodles) with a spiced coconut milk broth seasoned with andaliman (Sichuan pepper native to the Batak region), giving it a distinctive numbing and citrusy flavor. It is commonly served as a main meal and is a staple at Batak ceremonial gatherings and everyday dining in the Lake Toba area.

Meuseukat is a traditional Acehnese sweet confection from the Aceh region of Indonesia, made by cooking a thick paste of pineapple, sugar, and wheat flour until it sets into a dense, chewy block. It is typically cut into rectangular or diamond-shaped pieces and served as a ceremonial or festive treat, commonly prepared for weddings and religious celebrations.

Roti gambang is a traditional Indonesian bread originating from Betawi (Jakarta) culture, made from a dough of wheat flour, brown sugar (gula aren or gula merah), cinnamon, and anise, which gives it a distinctly sweet and spiced flavor. It is baked until firm with a slightly dry, dense texture and a sesame seed-coated surface, and is commonly sold by street vendors or bakeries as a snack or light accompaniment to tea or coffee.

Tasak telu is a traditional Sasak dish from Lombok, Indonesia, made by boiling three main ingredients together: chicken, unripe coconut, and young papaya, typically with spices. The name literally refers to 'three boiled' ingredients, and the dish is commonly prepared and served during ceremonial or communal occasions in Sasak culture.

Buchholzia coriacea, commonly known as wonderful kola, is typically prepared by using the seeds of the plant, which are often consumed raw or incorporated into traditional West African dishes and herbal preparations. It is commonly served as a snack, chewed for its sharp, pungent flavor, or included in medicinal broths and tonics alongside other spices and ingredients.

Katla posto is a Bengali dish made by cooking katla fish (Catla catla) pieces in a paste of poppy seeds (posto), typically combined with mustard oil, green chilies, and turmeric. It is prepared by coating or simmering the fish in the poppy seed mixture and is commonly served as part of a multi-course rice-based meal.

Aam shole machh is a Bengali preparation in which shoal fish (snakehead murrel) is cooked with raw or sour mango, mustard paste, green chilies, and turmeric in a light, tangy gravy. It is typically served with steamed rice as part of a multi-course Bengali meal, particularly during the summer months when raw mangoes are in season.
