Aam diye mourala machh is a Bengali preparation of mourala (small freshwater fish, Amblypharyngodon mola) cooked with raw or semi-ripe mango pieces, typically combined with mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. The dish is simmered until the fish absorbs the tangy flavor of the mango and is commonly served with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Bhetki begum bahar is a Bengali preparation of bhetki (barramundi or Asian sea bass) fillets cooked in a rich sauce typically made with onion, tomato, yogurt, cream, and aromatic spices such as cardamom, cloves, and bay leaves. The dish is commonly served as a main course alongside rice or bread in both restaurant and celebratory dining contexts.

Doi shol machh is a Bengali dish in which snakehead fish (shol) is marinated and cooked in a sauce based on doi (yogurt), typically combined with spices such as turmeric, cumin, and green chilies. It is commonly served with steamed rice as part of a multi-course meal in Bengali households and restaurants.

Hing pabda is a Bengali fish dish prepared by cooking pabda (butter catfish, Ompok bimaculatus) with a seasoning of hing (asafoetida), mustard oil, green chilies, turmeric, and other spices in a light gravy. It is typically served with steamed rice as part of a multi-course meal in Bengali households.

Kajoli machher jhal is a Bengali fish curry prepared with kajoli fish (Ailia coila), a small freshwater catfish, cooked in a pungent mustard-based gravy seasoned with green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. It is typically served with steamed white rice as a everyday meal in Bengali households of West Bengal and Bangladesh.

Lonka pora katla is a Bengali preparation in which katla fish (Catla catla) is cooked with charred or fire-roasted green chilies (lonka pora), along with mustard oil, onion, and spices to produce a smoky, pungent flavor profile. It is typically served as a main course alongside steamed rice in traditional Bengali household meals.

Mourala machher peyaji is a Bengali fritter made by coating small mourala fish (Ambassis ranga) with a spiced batter that typically includes onion, green chili, gram flour, and turmeric, then deep-frying until crisp. It is commonly served as a snack or appetizer, often accompanied by mustard sauce or green chutney.

Lau patay koi machh bhapa is a Bengali steamed dish in which live koi fish (climbing perch) are cooked with bottle gourd (lau) leaves or strips, mustard paste, mustard oil, green chilies, and spices wrapped in a parcel. The dish is traditionally prepared by steaming the seasoned fish and gourd together, allowing the ingredients to cook in their own moisture, and is typically served with steamed rice.

Laytha machh diye palong shaak is a Bengali dish made by cooking fresh spinach (palong shaak) with laytha fish, a small freshwater fish common in Bangladesh and West Bengal, typically prepared by sautéing the fish with spinach, mustard oil, green chilies, garlic, and spices such as turmeric and cumin. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Thakurbarir dudh katla is a Bengali preparation in which katla fish (Catla catla) is cooked in a milk-based sauce, a style associated with the culinary traditions of aristocratic households (thakurbari) of Bengal. The dish typically involves simmering lightly fried fish pieces in whole milk with spices such as bay leaf, cardamom, and cloves, resulting in a pale, mildly spiced gravy served alongside steamed rice.

Boyal machh bhaja is a Bengali fried fish dish prepared by marinating boyal fish (Eutropiichthys vacha, a freshwater catfish) with turmeric, salt, and sometimes red chili, then shallow- or deep-frying in mustard oil until the exterior is crisp. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice and dal in Bengali households across Bangladesh and West Bengal.

Katla vapa is a Bengali preparation in which katla fish (Catla catla) is steamed, typically wrapped in banana leaf or cooked in a sealed vessel, with a paste of mustard, green chili, turmeric, and mustard oil. It is traditionally served as part of a multi-course meal alongside rice, with the steaming method preserving the delicate texture of the fish and the pungency of the mustard marinade.

Thuramanga is typically a South Indian preparation centered on raw mango (maanga), often combined with grated coconut, green chilies, mustard seeds, and curry leaves to create a tangy, lightly spiced relish or side dish. It is commonly served alongside steamed rice, curd rice, or traditional thali meals, where its sharp, sour notes balance richer curries and lentil-based dishes.

Sorputi machher jhal is a Bengali fish curry prepared by cooking sorputi (Puntius sarana, a freshwater carp) in a pungent, spiced gravy made with mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. It is typically served with steamed white rice as a main course in Bengali household meals.

Ilisher muro diye shapla is a Bengali dish prepared by cooking water lily stems (shapla) with hilsa fish heads (ilisher muro), typically simmered with mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. It is served as a side dish with steamed rice and is a traditional preparation commonly found in the cuisine of Bangladesh and West Bengal.

canonicalName: ilisher tel ar matha diye note shaag
displayName: Ilisher tel ar matha diye note shaag
aliases: ilisher tel ar matha diye note shaag
description: Ilisher tel ar matha diye note shaag is a Bengali dish in which note shaag (a leafy green, also known as slender amaranth or fine-leaved amaranth) is cooked with the head and oil of hilsa fish (Tenualosa ilisha), typically through a process of sautéing or simmering that allows the greens to absorb the rich, pungent flavor of the fish. It is traditionally served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a multi-course Bengali meal.

canonicalName: bhola machh begun sorse diye jhal
displayName: Bhola machh begun sorse diye jhal
aliases: bhola machh begun sorse diye jhal
description: Bhola machh begun sorse diye jhal is a Bengali preparation featuring bhola fish (a variety of croaker) and eggplant cooked in a pungent mustard-based sauce, typically seasoned with green chilies, mustard oil, and turmeric. The dish is commonly served with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal, with the mustard paste providing its characteristic sharp, spicy flavor.

Chela machher jhal is a Bengali spiced fish curry prepared using chela fish (a small freshwater fish common in the rivers of Bengal), cooked with mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil to produce a pungent, fiery gravy. It is typically served with steamed white rice as a main course in everyday Bengali household meals.

Dhela machh bhaja is a Bengali fried fish dish prepared by coating chunks of fish—typically a freshwater species—in spiced turmeric and salt before shallow- or deep-frying in mustard oil until crisp. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside rice and dal in traditional Bengali meals.

Khoyra machh bhaja is a Bengali fried fish preparation in which small freshwater fish, typically of the khoyra variety, are marinated with turmeric and salt before being shallow- or deep-fried in mustard oil until crisp. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice and lentils in traditional Bengali meals.

Punti machher jhal is a Bengali preparation made with small punti fish (Puntius species) cooked in a spiced mustard-based gravy, typically seasoned with turmeric, green chilies, and mustard oil. It is commonly served as a side dish with steamed rice in traditional Bengali households.

canonicalName: sorse posto bata diye sorpunti machher jhal
displayName: Sorse posto bata diye sorpunti machher jhal
aliases: sorse posto bata diye sorpunti machher jhal
description: Sorpunti machher jhal is a Bengali fish curry prepared with sorpunti (Puntius sophore, a small freshwater fish) cooked in a spiced mustard-based gravy made from posto (poppy seed) paste and sorse (mustard) paste, along with green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. It is typically served as a main course alongside steamed white rice in traditional Bengali households.

canonicalName: sorse jeera bata diye sorpunti machher jhal
displayName: Sorse jeera bata diye sorpunti machher jhal
aliases: sorse jeera bata diye sorpunti machher jhal
description: Sorpunti machher jhal is a Bengali fish curry prepared by cooking sorpunti (Puntius sophore, a small freshwater fish) in a spiced sauce made with mustard paste (sorse bata) and cumin (jeera), along with green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. The dish is typically simmered until the fish absorbs the pungent, sharp flavors of the mustard-cumin base and is served with steamed white rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Sorse diye khoyra machher jhal is a Bengali fish curry prepared with khoyra fish (a small freshwater species) cooked in a pungent mustard-based sauce, typically incorporating mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. The dish is commonly served with steamed white rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Austrian pancakes, known as Palatschinken, are thin crepes made from a batter of flour, eggs, milk, and a pinch of salt, cooked in a lightly buttered pan until golden on both sides. They are commonly served rolled or folded and filled with apricot jam, sweet quark, or chocolate sauce, and appear as both a dessert and a main course in Austrian cuisine.

Jhor jhore chingrir matha is a Bengali preparation made from dried or roasted prawn heads cooked until they achieve a dry, crispy texture, typically seasoned with mustard oil, turmeric, dried chili, and other spices. It is commonly served as a side dish or accompaniment with rice, utilizing prawn heads that would otherwise be discarded, and is particularly associated with home cooking in Bengal.

Lalak is typically prepared as a bran-based dish, often cooked or toasted to develop a nutty flavor and hearty texture. It is commonly served as a wholesome side or light main course, sometimes accompanied by broths, vegetables, or seasonings depending on regional preferences.

Ahle Wurst is a traditional German dry-cured sausage originating from the Kassel region of Hesse, made primarily from coarsely ground pork that is seasoned, stuffed into natural casings, and air-dried or smoked over an extended period ranging from several weeks to several months. It is typically served sliced thinly as a cold cut, often accompanied by bread, and is a staple of regional charcuterie in northern Hesse.

Chinmi refers to a category of Japanese delicacies prized for their unusual or acquired flavors, typically consisting of preserved, fermented, or otherwise specially prepared ingredients such as sea cucumber innards (konowata), salted mullet roe (karasumi), and fermented sea urchin (uni no shiokara). These items are traditionally served in small portions as accompaniments to sake or as part of a formal Japanese meal.

Clay-fried fish is a Malaysian preparation in which whole fish or fish pieces are coated and cooked in a sealed clay pot or with a clay-based coating, allowing the fish to steam and fry simultaneously in its own juices alongside aromatics such as lemongrass, chili, and turmeric. The dish is commonly served as a main course with steamed rice and is found at hawker stalls and casual dining settings across Malaysia.

Pepper crab gravy is a Malaysian-style sauce made by cooking down the braising liquid used in black pepper or white pepper crab preparations, typically combining cracked peppercorns, butter, garlic, oyster sauce, and soy sauce into a thick, savory gravy. It is commonly served as an accompaniment to steamed rice or bread, allowing diners to enjoy the concentrated flavors of the crab cooking liquid without the shellfish itself.

Lau diye shol machher jhol is a Bengali fish curry prepared by simmering pieces of snakehead fish (shol maach) with bottle gourd (lau) in a light, spiced broth typically seasoned with turmeric, cumin, and green chilies. It is a everyday home-style dish commonly served with steamed white rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

canonicalName: notun alu tomato motorsuti diye rui machher jhol
displayName: Notun alu tomato motorsuti diye rui machher jhol
aliases: notun alu tomato motorsuti diye rui machher jhol
description: Notun alu tomato motorsuti diye rui machher jhol is a Bengali fish curry prepared by simmering rohu (rui) fish pieces with new potatoes (notun alu), tomatoes, and green peas (motorsuti) in a lightly spiced broth typically seasoned with turmeric, cumin, and green chilies. The dish is a staple of everyday Bengali home cooking and is commonly served with steamed white rice.

Cencaru sumbat sambal is a Malaysian dish consisting of whole hardtail scad fish (cencaru) stuffed with a spiced sambal paste made from chillies, shallots, belacan (shrimp paste), and other aromatics, then grilled or pan-fried until cooked through. It is commonly served as part of a rice-based meal alongside other dishes in everyday Malaysian home cooking and hawker settings.

Dried steamed fish is prepared by first drying fish through sun-drying or salting to remove moisture and concentrate flavour, then steaming the preserved fish with aromatics such as ginger, chilli, and soy sauce until heated through and tender. It is commonly served as part of a shared meal alongside steamed rice and other dishes in Malaysian household and casual dining contexts.

Solok ikan is a Malaysian stuffed fish dish in which cleaned fish is filled with a spiced mixture typically containing grated coconut, shallots, chili, turmeric, and other aromatics before being wrapped in banana leaves and grilled or steamed. It is commonly served as a main course alongside steamed rice and is found in traditional Malay home cooking as well as at local eateries.

Chingri machh vate is a Bengali dish consisting of prawns (chingri) cooked and served with plain steamed rice (vate), typically prepared by simmering the prawns with mustard oil, turmeric, green chilies, and salt. It is a simple, traditional preparation commonly eaten as a home-style meal in Bengali households, where the prawns are often mashed or mixed lightly into the rice before eating.

Chingri machher salad is a Bengali-influenced dish featuring prawns (chingri mach) combined with fresh vegetables and a seasoned dressing, drawing on both South Asian and international culinary techniques. It is typically served as a starter or light course, with the prawns prepared by boiling or light sautéing and accompanied by ingredients such as cucumber, tomato, onion, and a tangy dressing.

canonicalName: bansh er thor diye chingri machh
displayName: Bansh er thor diye chingri machh
aliases: bansh er thor diye chingri machh
description: Bansh er thor diye chingri machh is a Bengali dish made by cooking tender bamboo shoot pith (thor) with shrimp (chingri) in a spiced gravy typically containing mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. It is commonly served as a main course alongside steamed rice in Bengali households, particularly during seasons when fresh bamboo shoots are available.

Enchor chingri is a Bengali dish made with raw jackfruit (enchor) and prawns (chingri) cooked together in a spiced gravy typically containing mustard paste, turmeric, green chilies, and mustard oil. The young jackfruit absorbs the flavors of the sauce and mimics a meaty texture, making it a common preparation served with steamed rice in Bengali households.

Holy Spina is likely an Italian-inspired dish featuring spinach (spina) as a central ingredient, typically prepared sautéed with olive oil, garlic, and a touch of seasoning, and often combined with ricotta or other soft cheeses. It is commonly served as a side dish or as a filling for pasta such as ravioli or cannelloni, accompanied by a light tomato or butter-sage sauce.

Moshla chingri is a Bengali dish of prawns cooked in a spiced sauce made with onions, tomatoes, ginger, garlic, and a blend of ground spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander, and red chili. It is typically prepared by sautéing the aromatics and spices before simmering the prawns until cooked through, and is commonly served with steamed rice or flatbread.

Piyajkoli chingri machh is a Bengali dish prepared by cooking prawns (chingri) with spring onion greens (piyajkoli) in a light stir-fry or curry style, typically seasoned with mustard oil, green chilies, turmeric, and salt. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Mocha chingri is a Bengali dish combining mocha (banana blossom) and chingri (prawns or shrimp), cooked together with mustard paste, coconut, green chilies, and whole spices such as bay leaf and cumin. It is typically prepared by sautéing the banana blossom with the shrimp and spice mixture and is served as a main course alongside steamed rice.

Chingri diye kolai dal is a Bengali dish made by cooking split black-eyed peas (kolai dal) with prawns (chingri), typically tempered with mustard seeds, dried red chilies, and ginger, then seasoned with turmeric and salt. It is commonly served as part of a multi-course Bengali meal alongside steamed rice.

Palang shak diye chingri vapa is a Bengali steamed dish made with fresh shrimp (chingri) and spinach (palang shak), combined with mustard paste, green chilies, mustard oil, and spices, then cooked by steaming in a sealed vessel or wrapped container. It is typically served as a main course alongside steamed white rice and is a traditional preparation found in the cuisines of Bengal, including West Bengal and Bangladesh.

canonicalName: dhonepata bata diye mulo sheem alu chingri
displayName: Dhonepata bata diye mulo sheem alu chingri
aliases: dhonepata bata diye mulo sheem alu chingri
description: Dhonepata bata diye mulo sheem alu chingri is a Bengali dish in which shrimp (chingri) are cooked with radish (mulo), flat beans (sheem), and potato (alu) in a paste of ground fresh coriander leaves (dhonepata bata). The preparation typically involves simmering the vegetables and shrimp together with the coriander paste, mustard oil, and spices, and is served as a side dish alongside steamed rice in a traditional Bengali meal.

Dumur Chingri is a Bengali dish combining fig (dumur) with prawns (chingri), typically prepared by cooking the two together with mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and oil in a style common to West Bengal and Bangladesh. It is generally served as a side dish or main course accompanied by steamed rice.

Chingri diye panchmishali torkari is a Bengali mixed vegetable curry prepared with prawns (chingri) and five varieties of vegetables (panchmishali), typically including potatoes, eggplant, drumstick, raw banana, and other seasonal produce, cooked together with mustard oil, turmeric, and spices. It is a traditional dish from Bengali cuisine commonly served as a main course alongside steamed rice.

Dheros chingri diye tentuler tok is a Bengali sour dish made with okra (dheros) and shrimp (chingri) cooked in a tamarind (tentul)-based sauce, seasoned with mustard, dried red chilies, and spices. It is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice and represents a traditional preparation from the Bengali culinary tradition combining seafood with the characteristic tartness of tamarind.

Chingri diye kolmi saag is a Bengali dish made by stir-frying water spinach (kolmi saag) with shrimp (chingri), typically seasoned with mustard seeds, green chilies, garlic, turmeric, and salt. It is commonly prepared as a dry or lightly sauced side dish and served alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Thor chingri narkel diye is a Bengali dish made from banana blossom (thor) cooked with prawns (chingri) and grated coconut (narkel), typically prepared by sautéing the banana blossom with spices such as turmeric, cumin, and green chilies before adding the prawns and coconut. It is commonly served as a main course alongside steamed rice in Bengali households and is a traditional preparation from the Bengal region of the Indian subcontinent.

Franciasaláta is a Hungarian-style potato salad made from diced cooked potatoes, carrots, parsnips, green peas, and pickles, all bound together with mayonnaise. It is commonly served cold as a side dish or appetizer, particularly during holidays and celebrations in Hungary.

Ketan sarikaya is an Indonesian steamed glutinous rice dish topped with a custard layer made from eggs, coconut milk, and palm sugar, which is set through steaming until firm. It is commonly served as a snack or dessert, with the dense, sticky rice base contrasting the sweet, soft custard topping.

Lapek sagan is a traditional Minangkabau steamed cake from West Sumatra, made primarily from glutinous rice flour, coconut milk, and palm sugar, wrapped and cooked in banana leaves. It is typically served as a snack or dessert and is commonly found at traditional markets and ceremonial gatherings in the Minangkabau community.

Leum Kleun is a Thai dish whose name translates roughly to 'forget to swallow,' and it is typically prepared as a savory snack or appetizer featuring bite-sized morsels such as stir-fried or simmered pork, chicken, or seafood combined with aromatic herbs, chilies, shallots, peanuts, and a balance of sweet, salty, and tangy seasonings. It is commonly served at room temperature alongside fresh vegetables or lettuce leaves used for wrapping, often accompanied by steamed rice or enjoyed as finger food with drinks.

Rendang maco is a dry, slow-cooked West Sumatran dish made by simmering dried salted fish (maco) with coconut milk and a spice paste of chili peppers, galangal, lemongrass, turmeric, and other aromatics until the liquid is fully absorbed and the fish is coated in dark, caramelized spices. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice and is a traditional preparation found in Minangkabau cuisine, known for its intensely concentrated flavors from the extended cooking process.

Choesels is a traditional Belgian braised dish made from pancreas and other offal cuts of beef, slow-cooked with onions, kidneys, and sweetbreads in a rich sauce typically made with lambic beer or Madeira wine. It is a specialty of Brussels cuisine, historically associated with working-class cooking, and is served as a main course, often accompanied by bread or potatoes.

Indigirka is a traditional raw fish salad originating from Yakutia in northeastern Russia, prepared with frozen, diced raw fish—typically whitefish such as chir or nelma—combined with raw onion, vegetable oil, and salt. It is typically served immediately after cutting the frozen fish into cubes, consumed as a cold dish often at gatherings and celebrations in Siberian and Yakut culinary tradition.

Banga rice is a Nigerian dish prepared by cooking rice in a broth made from palm fruit extract, combined with ingredients such as onions, crayfish, dried fish or meat, and spices including beletete and oburunbebe sticks. It is commonly served as a main course and is particularly associated with the Niger Delta region of Nigeria, where it is a traditional staple.

Kerupuk puli is an Indonesian cracker made from cooked rice that is ground into a paste, shaped into flat rounds or strips, sun-dried, and then deep-fried until crisp and puffed. It is a common snack and accompaniment in Javanese cuisine, often served alongside rice dishes, soups, or street food as a textural element.

Bambara groundnut is a legume-based dish prepared from the seeds of Vigna subterranea, which are boiled, roasted, or ground into flour and incorporated into soups, stews, porridges, or snack preparations. It is consumed across sub-Saharan Africa and parts of Asia, commonly served as a protein-rich accompaniment to grains or vegetables, or eaten as a standalone boiled or roasted snack.

Danwake is a Nigerian dish made from bean flour and other ingredients such as ground kuli-kuli (peanut cake) shaped into small boiled dumplings. It is commonly served with a sauce or dressing containing peanut oil, tomatoes, onions, and chili peppers, and garnished with sliced cucumber and egg.

Achicha Ede is a Nigerian Igbo dish made from dried, shredded cocoyam (taro) that is rehydrated and cooked with palm oil, crayfish, dried fish or meat, and leafy vegetables such as uziza or oha leaves. It is typically served as a main meal and is particularly associated with the Igbo people of southeastern Nigeria, often prepared during the dry season when dried cocoyam is readily available.

Ekpang Nkwukwo is a Nigerian dish, particularly associated with the Efik and Ibibio peoples of Cross River and Akwa Ibom states, made by grating water yam and wrapping small portions in leaves before cooking them in a pot layered with palm oil, crayfish, onion, and salt. The dish is typically prepared for communal gatherings and celebrations, and the leaf wrapping — often from cocoyam or similar plants — gives the dumplings their distinctive texture during steaming and simmering.

Ukpo Ogede is a Nigerian steamed pudding made primarily from unripe or overripe cooking bananas, which are mashed and mixed with ingredients such as palm oil, crayfish, and seasonings before being wrapped in banana leaves and steamed. It is commonly eaten as a snack or light meal and is particularly associated with Igbo-speaking communities in southeastern Nigeria.

Kubang boyo is a traditional Indonesian dish consisting of rice cooked and wrapped in banana leaves, typically shaped into a form resembling a crocodile's back, giving the dish its name ('kubang boyo' translates roughly to 'crocodile wallow'). It is commonly prepared for ceremonial occasions and communal gatherings, using glutinous or regular rice as the base ingredient, and is served as part of traditional feasts in certain regions of Indonesia.

Bok l'hong is a Cambodian green papaya salad made by pounding shredded unripe papaya with ingredients such as tomatoes, long beans, dried shrimp, peanuts, garlic, chilies, lime juice, and fish sauce using a mortar and pestle. It is typically served as a side dish or light meal, often accompanied by sticky rice or grilled meats.

Rastrojo soup is typically a rustic, brothy preparation whose name evokes the Spanish word for 'stubble' or field remnants, suggesting a dish built from simple, seasonal vegetables, legumes, and leftover greens simmered together with herbs and aromatics. It is commonly served hot as a starter or light main course, often accompanied by crusty bread and a drizzle of olive oil.

Yaroa is a Dominican street food dish made by layering seasoned ground or shredded meat over a base of mashed or fried plantain or yuca, then topped with melted cheese and condiments such as ketchup and mayonnaise. It is typically assembled in a cup or container and served as a filling snack or informal meal, commonly found at street food stalls and casual eateries throughout the Dominican Republic.

Shucky beans are dried green beans, traditionally prepared by stringing fresh beans on thread and hanging them to air-dry, a preservation method common in Appalachian and Southern American cooking. They are typically rehydrated and slow-cooked, often with cured pork such as fatback or ham hocks, and served as a side dish.

Big Mac Bao is a fusion dish that combines the components of a McDonald's Big Mac—beef patties, special sauce, lettuce, cheese, pickles, and onions—served inside steamed Chinese bao buns instead of a sesame seed bun. It is typically offered as a novelty or limited-edition menu item, presented in the style of traditional Chinese steamed buns split to hold the layered burger fillings.

Mextlapique is a traditional Mexican preparation in which fish, typically small freshwater species, are wrapped in corn husks or leaves along with chili peppers, epazote, and other aromatics, then cooked directly over coals or on a comal. It is commonly associated with lake communities in central Mexico, particularly around the State of Mexico and Michoacán, where it is served as a main dish reflecting pre-Hispanic culinary traditions.

Paofan is a Chinese rice dish in which cooked rice is submerged in hot broth or soup, resulting in a porridge-like consistency distinct from congee, as the rice grains remain largely intact rather than being cooked from raw in the liquid. It is commonly served in bowls accompanied by side dishes such as pickled vegetables, braised meats, or preserved eggs, and is eaten as a casual meal or comfort food across various regions of China.

Soup and bouilli is a traditional preparation in which beef or other meat is simmered in water with vegetables and aromatics to produce both a broth and boiled meat as separate components. The resulting soup is typically served as a first course, with the boiled meat (bouilli) plated separately as the main course, often accompanied by vegetables cooked in the same broth.

Dali ni horbo is a traditional Batak dish made from buffalo milk that is cooked and curdled, often combined with spices such as andaliman (Sichuan pepper), shallots, garlic, and chilies to produce a firm, cheese-like curd. It is commonly served as a side dish in Batak ceremonial meals and everyday cuisine in the Lake Toba region of North Sumatra, Indonesia.

Li Liangui Smoked Meat Flatbread is a traditional dish originating from Harbin, China, consisting of sliced smoked pork layered inside or alongside a baked flatbread (bing). It is associated with the Li Liangui brand, which dates to the Qing dynasty, and is commonly served as a street food or casual meal.

Olive loaf is a cold cut made from finely ground pork that is mixed with whole or sliced green olives and formed into a loaf shape, then cooked and sliced for serving. It is typically served cold as a deli meat in sandwiches or on charcuterie and deli platters.

Pagpag is a dish originating in the Philippines, made from discarded meat and bones retrieved from fast-food restaurant garbage, which is then washed, re-cooked, and seasoned or fried to make it edible. It is typically consumed by people in extreme poverty in urban areas such as Manila, often purchased from informal street vendors at very low cost.

Shahan ful is an Ethiopian and Eritrean dish made from cooked fava beans (broad beans) that are mashed or left whole and combined with ingredients such as clarified butter (niter kibbeh), berbere or other spices, onions, and sometimes tomatoes or eggs. It is typically served in a clay or ceramic bowl, often accompanied by injera or bread, and is commonly eaten as a breakfast or main meal.

Venetian sauce is a classic French sauce made by reducing white wine and tarragon vinegar with shallots, then incorporating green herbs such as tarragon, chervil, and parsley into a velouté or butter-based foundation. It is typically served with fish, poultry, or egg dishes and is considered a derivative of the sauce family related to béarnaise preparations.

Rumpuć is a traditional Polish peasant dish made by combining sauerkraut, potatoes, carrots, and bacon, which are cooked together until the ingredients are tender and the flavors meld. It is typically served as a hearty side dish or simple main course, particularly associated with rural and working-class cuisine in Poland.

Placenta cake is an ancient Roman baked dish made from layers of thin pastry sheets (tracta) filled with a mixture of fresh cheese and honey, flavored with bay leaves, and cooked in a crust of pastry dough. It is referenced in the agricultural writings of Cato the Elder (circa 160 BCE) and was typically prepared as an offering or for special occasions rather than everyday consumption.

KoToPo is typically a mixed platter combining elements whose name often stands for chicken (Ko), pork (To), and potato (Po), commonly grilled or pan-seared and seasoned with a blend of international spices. It is often served as a shareable main course accompanied by a side sauce, fresh vegetables, or bread, making it suitable for casual dining.

Sütlü Nuriye is a Turkish dessert made by soaking layers of shredded phyllo dough (tel kadayıf) in a milk-based syrup rather than the sugar syrup used in classic kadayıf, then topping it with crushed pistachios. It is typically served chilled or at room temperature as a lighter-textured alternative to traditional syrup-soaked pastries, and is commonly found in Turkish pastry shops and dessert restaurants.

Pulut lepa is a Malaysian dish made from glutinous rice mixed with a seasoned fish filling that typically includes onion and paprika, wrapped in banana leaves and grilled over charcoal. It is commonly served as a snack or street food, particularly in coastal regions of Malaysia such as Terengganu and Kelantan.

Alu diye bacha machher jhal is a Bengali fish curry made by cooking bacha fish (Eutropiichthys vacha, a small freshwater catfish) with potatoes in a spiced mustard-based sauce, typically incorporating mustard oil, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard paste. It is commonly served as a main course alongside steamed rice in Bengali households across Bangladesh and West Bengal.

Bacha machh bhaja is a Bengali fried fish dish made by coating small freshwater fish, typically bata or similar carp varieties, with spices such as turmeric and chili, then shallow- or deep-frying them in oil until crispy. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish alongside steamed rice and dal in traditional Bengali meals.

Bacha machher tel jhal is a Bengali preparation made with bacha fish (Eutropiichthys vacha), cooked in a spiced mustard oil-based gravy typically seasoned with turmeric, dried red chilies, and panch phoron (a five-spice blend). The dish is prepared by frying the fish pieces and then simmering them in the pungent, oil-rich sauce, and is commonly served with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Boroli machh bhaja is a Bengali fried fish dish made by coating small boroli fish (Ambassis ranga, a type of glassy perchlet) in spiced turmeric and salt before shallow- or deep-frying until crisp. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish alongside rice and dal in traditional Bengali meals.

Amudi machher jhal is a spiced Bengali fish curry prepared by cooking amudi fish (rohu or a similar freshwater fish) with mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and oil, resulting in a pungent, sharp gravy. It is typically served with steamed white rice as a main course in Bengali household meals.

Dhela machher tel jhal is a Bengali preparation made from dried, compressed fish (dhela mach) cooked in a spiced mustard oil base, typically incorporating mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and nigella seeds. It is a traditional dish from the Bengal region, commonly served with steamed rice as part of an everyday meal.

Darkina machh bhaja is a fried fish dish from the Odia culinary tradition of eastern India, prepared by marinating fish pieces in spices such as turmeric, chili, and salt before shallow or deep frying in oil until crisp. It is commonly served as a side dish or starter alongside rice and dal in everyday meals.

Chanda machher jhal is a Bengali fish curry prepared by cooking chanda fish (moonfish/Parambassis species) in a pungent, spiced gravy typically made with mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. The dish is commonly served with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Chanda machh bhaja is a Bengali fried fish dish prepared by marinating thin, round chanda fish (moonfish or Parambassis species) with turmeric and salt, then shallow or deep frying in mustard oil until crisp. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish alongside steamed rice and dal in traditional Bengali meals.

Chanda machher tok is a Bengali sour fish curry made with chanda fish (Parambassis species, a small freshwater fish), cooked in a tangy tamarind-based sauce along with mustard, green chilies, and spices. It is typically prepared as a light, sharp-flavored dish and served with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Lau patay shingi machh vapa is a Bengali steamed dish in which stingray catfish (shingi machh) is cooked with bottle gourd leaves (lau pata) using a steaming method that preserves the natural flavors of the fish. It is typically prepared with mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil, and served as part of a traditional Bengali meal alongside rice.

Kajoli machh bhaja is a Bengali preparation of kajoli fish (Ailia coila), a small freshwater catfish, that is marinated with turmeric and salt, then shallow- or deep-fried in mustard oil until crisp. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Deshi sorpunti dopeyanja is a Bengali-style preparation, typically featuring sorpunti (puti) fish cooked in a spiced onion-based gravy known as do-pyaza, which is characterized by a generous use of onions added in two stages. It is commonly simmered with mustard oil, green chilies, turmeric, and aromatic spices, and is often served hot with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Mola machh bhaja is a Bengali fried fish dish made by coating small mola carplet fish (Amblypharyngodon mola) in spices such as turmeric and chili, then shallow or deep frying them until crisp. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice in Bengali households and is a traditional preparation in both Bangladesh and the West Bengal region of India.

Kajoli machh bhapa is a Bengali steamed fish dish made with kajoli (Ganges river fish, also known as Corica soborna), typically coated in a paste of mustard, green chili, coconut, and turmeric before being wrapped and steamed. It is commonly served with steamed rice and is a traditional preparation found in West Bengal and Bangladesh.

Kuche machher jhal is a Bengali spiced fish curry prepared by cooking small fish, typically dried or fresh, with mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil, resulting in a pungent and moderately spicy gravy. It is commonly served with steamed white rice as a main course in Bengali households.

Mourola machher shukto is a Bengali dish prepared by cooking mourola fish (small freshwater fish, Amblypharyngodon mola) with a mixture of bitter and other vegetables such as bitter gourd, drumstick, and raw banana in a lightly spiced, mildly bitter gravy. It is typically served as a first course alongside steamed rice in traditional Bengali meals, following the customary practice of beginning a meal with a bitter preparation.

Sankar machher rosa is a Bengali fish curry prepared by cooking sankar (silver carp or a similar freshwater fish) in a spiced gravy made with mustard paste, turmeric, green chilies, and mustard oil. It is typically served with steamed white rice as a main course dish in Bengali household meals.

Sankar machher jhal is a Bengali fish curry prepared by cooking sankar (a type of catfish) in a mustard oil-based sauce seasoned with mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and nigella seeds. It is typically served with steamed white rice as a staple home-style meal in Bengali households of West Bengal and Bangladesh.

Ritha machher bhuna is a Bengali dry-cooked fish preparation in which ritha fish (Aorichthys aor, a freshwater catfish) is sautéed and slow-cooked with mustard oil, onions, ginger, garlic, turmeric, red chili, and aromatic spices until the moisture reduces and the masala coats the fish in a thick, concentrated layer. It is typically served as a main course alongside steamed rice in Bengali households and regional restaurants.

Tilapia machh bhaja is a Bengali-style fried fish dish in which tilapia fillets or whole fish are marinated with turmeric, chili, and salt before being shallow- or deep-fried in mustard oil until the exterior is crisp. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice and dal in everyday Bengali meals.

Magaj is a South Asian dish prepared by cooking brain, typically from goat or lamb, with spices such as cumin, coriander, turmeric, chili, and onion, often in a masala-style preparation involving oil or ghee. It is commonly served as a dry or semi-dry curry accompaniment with bread such as naan or roti, and is found in both home cooking and restaurant settings across India and Pakistan.

Titaina is a traditional Valencian dish made by sautéing canned or salted tuna with tomatoes, roasted red peppers, pine nuts, and onion, typically seasoned with olive oil and salt. It is commonly served as a tapa or spread on bread, and is associated with the fishing communities of Valencia, Spain.

Babuko is a Turkish stuffed bread in which leavened dough is filled with cheese, garlic, and peppercorns, then baked until the crust is firm and the filling is melted. It is typically served as a savory snack or appetizer, often eaten warm alongside meals or as a standalone street food.

Tırşik çorbası is a sour soup from eastern Turkey, particularly associated with the Elazığ region, made with dried or fermented tarhana-like ingredients, chickpeas, and bulgur cooked together in a broth seasoned with dried mint and red pepper flakes. It is traditionally served hot as a first course, especially during winter months, and is commonly prepared using fermented grain and yogurt mixtures that give the soup its characteristic tangy flavor.

Aqît is a Central Asian dried dairy product made by straining and drying fermented milk or yogurt, typically formed into small balls or pieces and left to harden in the air or sun. It is commonly consumed as a preserved food or snack, and is a traditional staple among nomadic cultures across Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, and neighboring regions.

Abunuabunu is a Ghanaian green soup prepared by blending cocoyam leaves with ingredients such as tomatoes, onions, pepper, and aromatics, then simmering the mixture with fish, meat, or other proteins. It is typically served with starchy accompaniments like fufu, banku, or boiled yam as a main course.

Albert the Magic Pudding is typically a whimsical dessert inspired by the classic Australian children's story, commonly prepared as a steamed or boiled pudding featuring ingredients such as suet or butter, flour, dried fruits, and warming spices like cinnamon and nutmeg. It is often served warm with custard, cream, or a sweet sauce, making it a comforting choice on international menus that celebrate storybook-themed fare.

Bîstan is a Kurdish dish made with purslane (Portulaca oleracea), chili pepper, and onion, typically combined and cooked together as a vegetable preparation. It is commonly served as a side dish or main vegetable course in the cuisines of the Kurdish-speaking regions of the Middle East.

Nasi paprik is a Malaysian rice dish in which cooked rice is stir-fried with a spicy sauce made from dried chilies, fresh chilies, and paprika along with ingredients such as chicken or seafood, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and vegetables including long beans and kaffir lime leaves. It is typically served on a plate as a single-dish meal and is common at Malaysian mamak stalls and casual restaurants.

Harşo is a Georgian dish made by combining melted cheese with beaten eggs and onion, cooked together in oil until the mixture sets into a soft, unified mass. It is typically served as a simple everyday meal or side dish, prepared in a pan over direct heat.

Meftune is a slow-cooked stew prepared with meat, tomatoes (Lycopersicon), and garlic, typically simmered together until the ingredients meld into a thick, cohesive dish. It is served as a main course, often accompanied by bread or grains, and is associated with traditional home cooking contexts.

Hevrîşk is a traditional Kurdish dish made by breaking or crumbling flatbread into small pieces and combining it with oil, sometimes clarified butter, to create a simple, dense preparation. It is typically served as a modest everyday food or as part of a rural meal, often eaten by hand.

Kalbûnî is a traditional Kurdish dish prepared with lamb chops cooked alongside rice and almonds, combining the richness of meat with the subtle nuttiness of the almonds. It is typically served as a main course in Kurdish cuisine, with the rice absorbing the flavors of the braised or slow-cooked lamb during preparation.

Nasi bogana is a Javanese rice dish, originating from Tegal in Central Java, in which steamed white rice is served with a combination of side dishes including opor ayam (chicken in coconut milk), tempe, tofu, egg, and sambal. It is traditionally wrapped in banana leaves and commonly sold as a packaged meal, often consumed at breakfast or as an affordable everyday dish.

Owho soup is a traditional Urhobo dish from the Niger Delta region of Nigeria, prepared by cooking dried or fresh fish, meat, and crayfish in a base of palm fruit extract, with the addition of owho seeds (Ricinodendron heudelotii) that are fermented or dried and ground to thicken and flavor the soup. It is typically served with starchy accompaniments such as eba (cassava fufu), pounded yam, or starch, and is a staple at both everyday meals and communal gatherings among the Urhobo people.

African Salad is a Nigerian dish, particularly associated with the Igbo people, prepared by combining sliced oil bean seeds (ukazi or ugba) with palm oil, crayfish, ground pepper, onions, ogiri (fermented locust beans), and salt, then mixing thoroughly to allow the flavors to meld. It is commonly served as a snack or appetizer alongside garden egg sauce and is found at roadside food stalls and social gatherings across southeastern Nigeria.

Tuwon dawa is a stiff porridge made from millet flour, prepared by cooking the flour in boiling water and stirring continuously until it reaches a thick, firm consistency. It is a staple food in northern Nigeria and parts of the Sahel region, typically served with soups or stews such as miyan kuka or miyan taushe.

Sesame soup is a warm liquid dish prepared by blending or simmering sesame seeds or sesame paste with water or broth, often combined with ingredients such as sugar, glutinous rice flour, or other grains depending on regional variation. It is commonly served as a dessert or snack in various Asian culinary traditions, typically presented in a bowl either sweet or savory in flavor profile.

Nachos consist of tortilla chips topped with melted cheese and commonly served with additional toppings such as jalapeños, sour cream, guacamole, and salsa. The dish is a staple of Mexican-American cuisine and is typically served as a shared appetizer or snack.

Boli is a West African dish, particularly associated with Nigeria, consisting of roasted or grilled plantain (cooking banana) served alongside roasted or grilled fish and a peanut-based sauce or whole roasted peanuts. It is commonly sold as street food and eaten as a snack or light meal, with the plantain cooked directly over an open flame or charcoal until the skin is charred and the interior is soft.

Cowpeas (black-eyed peas) are legumes commonly prepared by boiling dried or fresh beans until tender, often cooked with aromatics such as onions, garlic, and spices, and sometimes combined with tomatoes, leafy greens, or smoked meats depending on regional tradition. They are served as a main dish, side dish, or stew across cuisines in Africa, the Americas, and Asia, and are a significant source of plant-based protein.

Bhetki rongpuri is a Bengali fish preparation typically made with bhetki (barramundi or Asian sea bass) fillets cooked in a rich, spiced gravy that commonly includes onions, ginger, garlic, green chilies, and a blend of aromatic spices characteristic of Bengali cuisine. It is often served with steamed rice or pulao as part of a traditional meal, and may appear at celebratory occasions and family gatherings.

canonicalName: rice with chicken a la chorrera
displayName: Rice with chicken a la chorrera
aliases: rice with chicken a la chorrera
arroz con pollo a la chorrera
description: Arroz con pollo a la chorrera is a Cuban rice and chicken dish cooked with a higher ratio of liquid than standard arroz con pollo, resulting in a loose, slightly soupy consistency described by the Spanish phrase 'a la chorrera,' meaning flowing or runny. It is prepared with chicken pieces, short- or medium-grain rice, tomatoes, onion, garlic, bell pepper, beer or white wine, saffron or bijol for color, and peas, and is typically served as a main course in Cuban households and restaurants.

Aam diye parse machher jhal is a Bengali fish curry prepared by cooking parse fish (a small freshwater fish common in Bengal) with raw or ripe mango pieces in a spiced mustard-based gravy, typically seasoned with turmeric, green chilies, and mustard oil. The dish is commonly served with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal, with the mango imparting a characteristic sour or tangy flavor to the sauce.

Begun bori diye sorse parse is a Bengali fish preparation in which parshey (Gangetic halfbeak or similar small river fish) is cooked in a mustard-seed paste sauce along with eggplant (begun) and fried lentil dumplings (bori). The dish is typically prepared by simmering the fish and eggplant in a pungent mustard gravy seasoned with green chilies and turmeric, with the bori added to absorb the sauce, and is served with steamed rice as a main course.

Aam pabda is a Bengali fish curry in which pabda (butterfish or Ompok pabda), a small freshwater catfish, is cooked with raw mango (aam) pieces in a mustard-based sauce along with green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. It is typically served as a main course alongside steamed rice, and the sourness of the raw mango balances the pungency of the mustard in the dish.

Bata machh fry is a Bengali preparation of bata fish (Labeo bata), a small freshwater carp, marinated with spices such as turmeric, red chili, and salt, then shallow- or deep-fried in oil until crisp. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish alongside rice and dal in Bengali households and restaurants.

Bata machher tel jhal is a Bengali fish preparation in which small fish, typically ground or paste-form bata fish (a type of carp), are cooked in mustard oil with mustard paste, green chilies, and spices to produce a pungent, oil-based sauce. It is traditionally served with steamed rice as part of a standard Bengali meal.

Aam kasundi pabda is a Bengali fish dish in which pabda (butter catfish, Ompok pabda) is cooked in a sauce made with aam kasundi, a pungent condiment combining raw mango and mustard. The dish is typically prepared by marinating or simmering the fish in the aam kasundi mixture along with mustard oil, green chilies, and turmeric, and is served with steamed rice.

Aam diye latha machher tok is a Bengali sour dish prepared by cooking latha fish (a type of small, slender freshwater fish) with raw or sour mango, mustard, and spices such as turmeric and green chili. It is typically served as a tangy side dish alongside steamed rice, and the sourness of the mango (aam) is central to its characteristic flavor profile.

Doi pabda is a Bengali fish dish in which pabda (butterfish or Ompok pabda) is cooked in a sauce made primarily from doi (yogurt), along with mustard paste, turmeric, green chilies, and mustard oil. It is typically served with steamed rice as part of a multi-course Bengali meal.

Dudh pabda is a Bengali fish dish in which pabda (butterfish, Ompok pabda) is cooked in a sauce of milk and mustard paste, typically with green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. It is commonly served with steamed white rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Dahi machher jhal is a Bengali fish curry in which pieces of fish, typically rohu or similar freshwater varieties, are cooked in a sauce made with yogurt (dahi), mustard paste, green chilies, and spices such as turmeric and cumin. It is typically served with steamed white rice as part of a multi-course Bengali meal.

Hing diye pabda machh is a Bengali fish preparation in which pabda (Ompok pabda), a small freshwater catfish, is cooked with asafoetida (hing) along with mustard oil, green chilies, turmeric, and nigella seeds in a light, pungent gravy. The dish is typically served with steamed white rice and is a traditional home-style preparation common in Bengal and Bangladesh.

Parse bhapa is a Bengali steamed preparation in which hilsa fish (ilish) is cooked in a paste of mustard seeds, green chilies, mustard oil, and turmeric, traditionally wrapped in banana leaves or prepared in a sealed container to retain steam and flavor. It is typically served with plain steamed rice as a main course dish.

Malai pabda is a Bengali fish dish in which pabda (butter catfish, Ompok bimaculatus) is cooked in a rich sauce made with malai (fresh cream) along with spices such as green chili, ginger, and sometimes mustard or coconut milk. It is typically served with steamed rice and is a common preparation in Bengali home cooking and restaurants across West Bengal and Bangladesh.

Gijda is a type of bread, typically prepared from a simple dough of flour, water, salt, and a leavening agent, then baked until the crust is firm and golden. It is commonly served as an accompaniment to main dishes, used for scooping stews and dips, or enjoyed with butter, cheese, or spreads.

Rouyot is a type of baked good, typically prepared by combining flour-based dough with fats and leavening agents before being oven-baked until golden. It is commonly served as a snack or accompaniment to meals, and may be enjoyed plain or alongside sweet or savory toppings depending on regional preferences.

Lebu pata diye mourala machh is a Bengali preparation in which small mourala fish (Amblypharyngodon mola) are cooked with fresh lemon leaves (lebu pata), which impart a citrusy aroma to the dish. It is typically prepared as a light curry or dry preparation and served with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Latha machher tel jhal is a Bengali fish curry prepared by cooking latha fish (a type of small river fish) in mustard oil with mustard paste, green chilies, and turmeric, resulting in a pungent, oil-based sauce characteristic of tel jhal preparations. It is typically served with steamed white rice as part of a multi-course Bengali meal.

Lau diye shol machher jhal is a Bengali preparation of snakehead fish (shol maach) cooked with bottle gourd (lau) in a spiced gravy typically made with mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. The dish is simmered until the gourd is tender and the fish absorbs the pungent, moderately spicy sauce, and is traditionally served with steamed white rice as a everyday meal in Bengali households.

canonicalName: sorse posto diye bata machher jhal
displayName: Sorse posto diye bata machher jhal
aliases: sorse posto diye bata machher jhal
description: Sorse posto diye bata machher jhal is a Bengali fish curry in which fish, typically a freshwater variety, is cooked in a pungent paste made from ground mustard seeds (sorse) and poppy seeds (posto), combined with green chilies, mustard oil, and turmeric. The dish is prepared by simmering fish pieces in this thick, spiced paste-based gravy and is commonly served with steamed white rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Pui metuli diye mourala machh is a Bengali dish prepared by cooking small mourala fish (Amblypharyngodon mola) with the tender shoots and leaves of pui (Malabar spinach) along with spices such as mustard, turmeric, and green chilies. It is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice and is a traditional home-style preparation common in West Bengal and Bangladesh.

Topse fry is a Bengali preparation in which topse fish (Polynemus paradiseus, also known as mango fish or paradise threadfin) are coated in a spiced batter—typically made with gram flour, turmeric, chili, and salt—and deep-fried until crisp. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish, particularly during the Bengali new year and festive occasions, often accompanied by rice or as part of a multi-course meal.

Topse sorse is a Bengali preparation in which topse fish (Mango fish, Polynemus paradiseus) are coated in a mustard-based paste made from ground mustard seeds, green chilies, and turmeric, then typically shallow-fried or cooked in mustard oil. It is commonly served as a main course alongside steamed rice in Bengali households and restaurants.

Topse machher tel jhal is a Bengali preparation of topse fish (Polynemus paradiseus, also known as mango fish or paradise threadfin) cooked in a mustard oil-based sauce with mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and salt, resulting in a pungent, spicy dish. It is typically served with steamed white rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Bele machher tel jhal is a Bengali preparation made with bele fish (sand goby) cooked in a spiced mustard oil-based gravy, typically incorporating mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and nigella seeds. It is a traditional dish from the Bengal region, commonly served with steamed rice as part of a everyday or ceremonial meal.

Gang mourala machher jhal is a Bengali fish preparation made with gang mourala, a small freshwater fish, cooked in a spiced mustard-based sauce along with green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. It is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Small sausage in large sausage (大腸包小腸) is a Taiwanese street food consisting of a grilled sticky rice sausage sliced open and used as a bun to encase a smaller grilled pork sausage. It is commonly served with condiments such as garlic, pickled vegetables, mustard, and scallions, and is widely sold at night markets throughout Taiwan.

La bouillie is a thick porridge made by cooking a starchy flour, commonly millet, sorghum, maize, or wheat flour, in water or milk until it reaches a dense, smooth consistency. It is a staple dish consumed across many African, Caribbean, and French-speaking regions, typically served as a breakfast or weaning food for young children, sometimes sweetened with sugar or flavored with local spices.

Possum pie is an American dessert made with layers of cream cheese, whipped topping, and pudding—typically chocolate or vanilla—set over a buttery pecan or pretzel crust and chilled until firm. It is served cold, often at potlucks, holiday gatherings, and casual family meals, and contains no actual possum despite its name.

Kyur is a traditional Kalmyk dish typically prepared from boiled or slow-cooked meat, often lamb or beef, reflecting the pastoral culinary heritage of the Kalmyk people. It is commonly served in a communal setting, sometimes accompanied by broth, flatbreads, or traditional milk tea, and holds significance as a national dish for festive and family gatherings.

Snirkel is a type of Danish pastry, typically made from a laminated, yeast-leavened dough that is rolled, twisted, or coiled into a spiral shape before baking. It is commonly enjoyed as a breakfast item or afternoon treat alongside coffee, and may be finished with sugar glaze, icing, or a filling such as remonce, cinnamon, or marzipan.

Yin yang fried rice is a Hong Kong dish consisting of a plate of fried rice topped with two contrasting sauces — typically a white cream- or egg-based sauce on one side and a red tomato- or curry-based sauce on the other — arranged to resemble the yin-yang symbol. It is commonly served as a main course in Hong Kong-style Chinese restaurants and cha chaan tengs, using day-old rice stir-fried with eggs, vegetables, and sometimes meat or seafood as the base.

Wolgwa-chae is a Korean dish made from thinly sliced or julienned muskmelon (참외, chamoe), typically dressed with a seasoned sauce and sometimes combined with other vegetables or garnishes. It is served as a side dish (banchan) or light salad course in Korean cuisine.

Tshaka Madesu is a Congolese dish made by cooking beans with cassava, bay leaf, and sweet paprika powder until the ingredients are tender and combined into a hearty stew. It is commonly served as a main dish in the Democratic Republic of Congo, where beans and cassava are staple foods.

Lamaniets is a traditional Belarusian sweet pastry made from wheat flour dough that is rolled, filled with a mixture of ground poppy seeds and honey, then baked with vegetable oil. It is typically served as a festive or everyday dessert, sliced into portions that reveal the layered poppy seed and honey filling.

Sembîsek is a Kurdish fried pastry filled with a mixture of minced meat, onion, and egg, shaped into a triangular or pocket form before cooking. It is commonly served as a snack or appetizer and is associated with festive occasions and everyday street food in Kurdish culinary tradition.

Cabrito is a stew made from young goat meat, slow-cooked with aromatics such as onions, garlic, tomatoes, and spices until the meat is tender. It is commonly served as a main dish in African cuisine, often accompanied by rice, bread, or starchy side dishes.

Crab jujube is a Teochew dish made by mincing crab meat and mixing it with pork or other fillings, then shaping the mixture into small oval forms resembling jujube fruits before frying or steaming. It is typically served as part of a multi-dish Teochew meal and represents a traditional method of transforming crab into a bite-sized, structured preparation.

Bakeapple pie is a fruit pie made with bakeapples (cloudberries, Rubus chamaemorus), wild berries native to subarctic and boreal regions of Canada, particularly Newfoundland and Labrador, where they are harvested in late summer. The berries are typically combined with sugar and encased in a pastry crust, then baked, and the pie is commonly served as a dessert in Newfoundland cuisine.

Cabbage pie is a baked dish consisting of a pastry or dough crust filled with cooked cabbage, often combined with ingredients such as eggs, onions, and seasonings. It is served as a main course or side dish and appears in various forms across Eastern European, Russian, and other culinary traditions.

Taweşeht is a Kurdish lamb dish in which lamb chops are cooked with onion, cooking oil, and salt, typically prepared by frying or slow-cooking the meat until tender. It is a simple, meat-centered preparation commonly served as a main course in Kurdish households and traditional settings.

The New Mexico roll is a sushi-style roll prepared with seasoned rice and New Mexico chile, combining Japanese rolling techniques with Southwestern American flavors. It is typically served as part of a fusion appetizer or entrée selection, often accompanied by soy sauce and wasabi in the manner of conventional maki rolls.

Kharkivske is a Ukrainian biscuit-based confection consisting of sponge cake layers combined with waffle elements and powidl, a thick, unsweetened plum jam used as a filling. It is typically served as a sliced pastry or individual portion dessert, representing a regional Ukrainian bakery tradition from the Kharkiv area.

Pastel and Fataya are fried pastry snacks common in West African street food contexts, filled with a spiced mixture of flaked tilapia, onion, tomato, garlic, chili pepper, and capsicum, seasoned with table salt and vinegar, and cooked in peanut oil. They are typically served as handheld snacks or light meals, with the pastel being a folded dough pocket and the fataya a similar triangular or half-moon shaped fried dumpling.

Posekunchiks are small, bite-sized dumplings, typically made from thin unleavened dough filled with a mixture of minced meat, onions, and seasonings, then pan-fried or deep-fried until golden and crisp. They are commonly served hot as an appetizer or snack, often accompanied by sour cream, vinegar-based dipping sauces, or a light broth.

Möpkenbrot is a traditional German dark rye bread, dense in texture and made primarily from rye flour, water, and salt, often using a sourdough fermentation process. It is typically sliced thin and served with butter, cold cuts, cheese, or savory spreads as part of a traditional German breakfast or open-faced sandwich.

Tonno del Chianti is a traditional Tuscan preparation in which pork (typically loin or other cuts) is slow-cooked and preserved in olive oil, resulting in a texture and appearance reminiscent of canned tuna, which gives the dish its name. It is commonly served cold or at room temperature as an antipasto, sliced and accompanied by bread, beans, or vegetables.

Tirtlan are traditional fried or baked pastries from the Ladin-speaking communities of the Dolomite valleys in northern Italy, made from a thin dough typically filled with either spinach and ricotta or sauerkraut. They are folded into a half-moon or rectangular shape and cooked in a pan or oven, commonly served as a main course or side dish in Alpine regional cuisine.

Marmita de atún, or tuna pot, is a traditional Cantabrian stew prepared by slow-cooking chunks of fresh tuna with potatoes, onion, tomato, green pepper, and olive oil in a clay or iron pot. It is a staple of the fishing communities along the Cantabrian coast of northern Spain and is typically served as a hearty main course.

Monkey brains is a dish documented in various culinary traditions, particularly in parts of China and Southeast Asia, in which the brain of a monkey is consumed either raw, baked, or cooked while still in the skull. It is considered a rare and controversial delicacy, historically associated with elaborate banquet contexts, and has been the subject of significant ethical, legal, and public health scrutiny due to concerns about prion diseases and wildlife protection laws.

Udong is a Korean-style udon dish made with thick wheat flour noodles served in a mild, savory broth typically seasoned with soy sauce, dried anchovies, or kelp, and topped with ingredients such as green onions, fish cake, and sometimes a soft-boiled egg. It is commonly served as a warm noodle soup at casual restaurants and food stalls, and is a popular everyday meal in South Korea.

Gyūdon with 3 Cheeses is a Japanese-style beef bowl dish in which thinly sliced beef and onions are simmered in a seasoned broth of soy sauce, mirin, and dashi, then served over steamed white rice and topped with three varieties of melted cheese. The dish is an international fusion adaptation of traditional gyūdon, combining the savory-sweet umami base of the Japanese original with the richness of cheese, typically served in a bowl as a standalone meal.

Tianshui noodles (天水呱呱 or Tianshui mifen) are a specialty of Tianshui, Gansu province, China, made from buckwheat or pea starch noodles that are cooked and then dressed with a spiced chili oil sauce, vinegar, garlic, and sesame paste. The dish is typically served at room temperature or cold as a street food or casual meal, and is characterized by its numbing-spicy flavor profile common to northwestern Chinese cuisine.

Chengdu egg pancake (Dan Jian Bing) is a street food from Sichuan province made by spreading a thin wheat or mixed-grain batter on a hot griddle, cracking one or more eggs directly onto the surface, and folding the cooked pancake around fillings such as scallions, chili sauce, and crispy crackers. It is commonly sold by street vendors and eaten as a quick breakfast or snack.

Daetong-bap is a Korean dish in which seasoned rice, along with ingredients such as vegetables, nuts, and sometimes meat, is cooked inside a hollowed section of green bamboo, which imparts a subtle fragrance to the rice. It is typically served by splitting or opening the bamboo segment at the table, and is commonly found at traditional Korean meals and specialty restaurants.

Mimi-udon is a regional Japanese noodle dish from Yamagata Prefecture in which the udon dough is hand-shaped into small, flat, ear-like pieces ("mimi" meaning "ear") rather than cut into long noodles. The dough pieces are simmered in a soy-based broth together with vegetables and sometimes meat, and the dish is traditionally served as a home-style or local specialty meal, particularly during winter.

Jjamjjamyeon is a Korean-Chinese hybrid dish that combines jajangmyeon (noodles in black bean paste sauce) and jjamppong (spicy seafood noodle soup) by splitting a serving bowl into two compartments, each containing one of the two preparations. The dish is typically served at Korean-Chinese restaurants, allowing diners to eat both noodle dishes in a single order.

Khoyar polau is a Kashmiri rice dish prepared by cooking long-grain rice with dried kokum (khoyar) and aromatic spices, which gives the rice a distinctive sour flavor and a reddish hue. It is commonly served as part of traditional Kashmiri meals, often accompanied by meat or vegetable side dishes.

Momento Coffee is typically a specialty coffee beverage, likely prepared using espresso-based methods common in international café culture, and may be served hot or iced depending on the variation. It is often accompanied by milk, sugar, or flavored syrups and commonly enjoyed as a morning drink or after a meal.

Puddis is a German-style dessert, typically prepared as a creamy, milk-based pudding in flavors such as vanilla, chocolate, or caramel. It is commonly served chilled in small cups or bowls, often enjoyed as a light sweet course after a meal or as a snack.

Daler muri ghonto is a Bengali dish made by cooking dried lentils, typically masoor or mung dal, together with fish heads—most commonly rohu—along with mustard oil, whole spices, ginger, and green chilies until the mixture forms a thick, cohesive preparation. It is traditionally served as a side dish with steamed rice in Bengali households, often as part of a multi-course meal.

Bandhakopir moshla kopta are Bengali spiced cabbage dumplings made by combining finely chopped or grated cabbage with spices such as cumin, coriander, green chili, and ginger, then shaping the mixture into small balls that are shallow- or deep-fried until golden. They are typically served as a snack or side dish, often accompanied by a dipping chutney or as part of a larger Bengali meal.

Badhakopir paturi is a Bengali dish in which cabbage is mixed with mustard paste, mustard oil, green chili, and spices, then wrapped in banana leaves and cooked by steaming or slow-cooking, allowing the leaf to impart a subtle aroma to the filling. It is typically served as a side dish or light main course, following the traditional paturi technique commonly used in Bengali cuisine for vegetables and fish.

Gondhoraj Ghol-er Pulao is a Bengali rice dish prepared by cooking basmati or long-grain rice with ghol (a spiced, thinned yogurt drink) and flavored with Gondhoraj lime, a variety of kaffir-like citrus native to Bengal known for its intense floral aroma. The dish is typically served as part of a multi-course Bengali meal, where the aromatic lime zest or juice imparts a distinctive fragrance to the finished rice.

Albap is a Korean dish consisting of cooked rice (bap) served with roe, typically fish roe such as flying fish roe, mixed together so the roe coats the grains of rice. It is commonly served as a simple rice dish, sometimes accompanied by seaweed, soy sauce, or other condiments, and is eaten as a light meal or snack.

Rebanira itame is a Japanese stir-fried dish made with thinly sliced beef liver (reba) and garlic chives (nira), cooked together over high heat with soy sauce, sake, mirin, and sesame oil. It is commonly served as a main dish alongside steamed rice in Japanese home cooking and casual restaurants.

Cenil is an Indonesian chewy snack made from tapioca starch or cassava flour, shaped into small cylindrical or ball-like pieces, boiled until cooked, and then coated or rolled in grated coconut and colored with natural food dyes. It is commonly served as a street food or traditional market snack (jajan pasar) in Java, often accompanied by a drizzle of palm sugar syrup.

Yakisaba-somen is a Japanese dish in which thin somen noodles are stir-fried together with mackerel (saba), vegetables, and savory seasonings such as soy sauce. It is typically served as a casual meal and represents a regional preparation that combines the delicate texture of somen with the robust flavor of grilled or pan-cooked mackerel.

Cimol is an Indonesian snack made from tapioca flour (aci) formed into small balls and deep-fried until the exterior is crispy while the inside remains chewy. It is typically served as a street food snack, seasoned with spicy, savory, or flavored powder toppings such as chili, cheese, or barbecue seasoning.

Enokoro meshi is a Japanese rice dish made by cooking rice together with foxtail millet (Setaria italica), a grain historically common in rural Japanese cuisine. It is a simple, rustic preparation typically served as an everyday staple, reflecting traditional agricultural foodways in Japan.

Dojo doufu is a Japanese dish in which live loach fish (dojo) are placed in cold water with tofu, then slowly heated so the fish burrow into the tofu as the water warms, resulting in the loach cooked inside the tofu block. The dish is then simmered in a seasoned broth, often with miso or dashi-based sauce, and served as a hot preparation in traditional Japanese cuisine.

Hatto is a traditional dish from the Tohoku region of Japan, made by mixing wheat flour with water to form a dough that is torn or cut into irregular flat pieces and simmered in a miso or soy-based broth with vegetables such as daikon, carrots, and mushrooms. It is typically served as a hearty one-pot meal during colder months and is considered a form of regional home cooking, particularly associated with Iwate and surrounding prefectures.

Kani-jiru is a Japanese soup prepared by simmering crab pieces in a dashi or miso-based broth, often combined with vegetables such as daikon, burdock root, and green onions. It is commonly served as a warming dish in coastal regions of Japan, particularly in Hokkaido, where crab is abundant, and is typically presented in a bowl as part of a traditional Japanese meal.

Halawat Nahar Khuz is an Iraqi confectionery that is typically prepared from a sweetened dough or semolina base, often enriched with butter or ghee and flavored with ingredients such as rose water, cardamom, or sugar syrup. It is commonly served in small portions as a dessert or accompaniment to tea and coffee, particularly during family gatherings, holidays, and special occasions.

Dazhu gansi is a Huaiyang cuisine dish from Yangzhou consisting of firm tofu (dried tofu curd) that is sliced into fine shreds and simmered in a rich broth, typically made with chicken stock, ham, and shrimp. It is served as a warm dish and is a representative example of the meticulous knife skills and delicate flavoring associated with Huaiyang cooking.

Saffron pancakes are made by incorporating saffron-infused liquid into a standard pancake batter, giving the finished pancakes a distinctive golden-yellow color and a mild floral flavor. They are typically served as a breakfast or dessert item, often accompanied by sweet toppings such as syrup, fruit, or cream.

Tjälknöl is a Swedish dish made by slow-roasting a frozen cut of meat — in this case moose (Alces alces) — directly from its frozen state in a low-temperature oven, typically around 70–125°C, until the desired internal temperature is reached. The resulting roast is commonly sliced thin and served cold or at room temperature, often accompanied by pickled vegetables, sour cream, and rye bread.

Ungui are pig's trotters (feet) prepared by slow-cooking or braising until the collagen-rich skin and cartilage become tender, commonly seasoned with herbs, chiles, and spices in Mexican culinary tradition. They are typically served as a main dish or antojito, often accompanied by salsas, pickled vegetables, or incorporated into stews.

Swedish mackerel soup is a traditional fish soup prepared by simmering mackerel with vegetables such as onion, leek, and potato in a broth, often seasoned with dill and sometimes enriched with cream. It is typically served as a main course in Swedish home cooking, making use of mackerel caught in Scandinavian waters.

Sakata ramen is a regional style from Sakata City in Yamagata Prefecture, Japan, characterized by a light, clear broth typically made from seafood stock, particularly using local fish such as flounder or shellfish, combined with chicken or pork. It is served with thin, straight noodles and commonly topped with ingredients such as chashu pork, menma (bamboo shoots), and negi (green onions), and is often eaten as an everyday meal in the Sakata area.

Timphan is a traditional Acehnese steamed cake from Aceh, Indonesia, made from a dough of glutinous rice flour and ripe banana wrapped in young banana leaves and filled with sweetened grated coconut or srikaya (coconut egg custard). It is commonly prepared and served during Islamic holidays such as Eid al-Fitr and Eid al-Adha, functioning as a customary celebratory food in Acehnese households.

Bacem tofu is a Javanese preparation in which tofu is simmered in a spiced coconut water broth with palm sugar, coriander, galangal, bay leaves, and other aromatics until the liquid is nearly absorbed, resulting in a dark, subtly sweet, and savory flavor. After braising, the tofu is typically shallow-fried or grilled briefly to develop a caramelized exterior, and it is commonly served as a side dish alongside rice and other accompaniments in everyday Indonesian meals.

Bulayak satay is an Indonesian dish from Lombok consisting of grilled or skewered meat served alongside bulayak, a type of rice cake wrapped in woven coconut leaves and cooked until firm. It is typically accompanied by a spiced peanut or offal-based sauce and is commonly sold as street food or at traditional markets in West Nusa Tenggara.

Nasi gurih is an Indonesian steamed rice dish cooked in coconut milk along with aromatics such as lemongrass, pandan leaves, and bay leaves, giving it a rich, savory flavor. It is commonly served as a base for various accompaniments including fried chicken, tempeh, tofu, sambal, and hard-boiled eggs, and is frequently found at breakfast or as part of ceremonial and everyday meals across Indonesia.

Sodd is a traditional Norwegian boiled meat soup prepared by simmering mutton or lamb, and sometimes beef, in water to produce a clear broth, which is then served with the cooked meat alongside meatballs, carrots, and potatoes. It is considered a Norwegian national dish and is commonly served at festive occasions and celebrations such as baptisms and weddings.

Miyar kuka is a Nigerian soup made primarily from baobab leaf powder (kuka), which gives the dish its distinctive dark green color and slightly mucilaginous texture, combined with meat or offal, ground crayfish, palm oil, and dried pepper. It is a staple of northern Nigerian cuisine, particularly among the Hausa people, and is commonly served with tuwo shinkafa (rice flour porridge) or other stiff porridges.

Caluk noodles are a traditional Indonesian noodle dish originating from Aceh, prepared by tossing boiled noodles with a sauce made from mashed, roasted peanuts mixed with palm sugar, chili, and fermented shrimp paste. The dish is typically served as a street food snack or light meal, often garnished with fried shallots and accompanied by fresh vegetables.

Lyvzæ is typically prepared as a hearty dish featuring slow-cooked ingredients, often combining meats, aromatic spices, and seasonal vegetables in a rich, savory base. It is commonly served warm as a main course, frequently accompanied by bread, rice, or a simple side salad to balance the flavors.
