Mouri parse is a Bengali preparation that typically features fennel seeds (mouri) as a defining aromatic element, often incorporated into a lightly spiced gravy or tempered dish alongside vegetables or fish. It is commonly served as part of a traditional Bengali meal with steamed rice, offering a mildly sweet and fragrant counterpoint to other courses.

Grilletta is an Italian sandwich made with a grilled meat patty, typically beef, served inside a soft bread roll along with toppings such as lettuce, tomato, and condiments. It is commonly associated with Italian fast food culture and is served as a quick meal, functioning as Italy's own version of a hamburger.

Grenadiermarsch is a simple pan-fried dish made from cooked pasta and potatoes combined with sautéed onions, typically seasoned with salt, pepper, and paprika. It is a common everyday meal in Hungarian and Austrian cuisine, often served as a budget-friendly main course or side dish.

Häckerle is a traditional German dish made from finely chopped or minced offal, typically including heart, lung, and liver, often mixed with onions, marjoram, and other seasonings before being cooked together. It is commonly served as a hearty main course, sometimes accompanied by bread or potatoes, and is particularly associated with regional home cooking in southern Germany.

Jin deui, known in Indonesia as onde-onde, are deep-fried glutinous rice flour balls filled with sweetened mung bean or lotus paste and coated in sesame seeds. They are commonly served as a street food snack or dessert, with a crispy exterior and chewy, hollow interior formed by steam expansion during frying.

Yam with cod is a dish combining boiled or roasted yam, a starchy tuber, with salt cod that is typically soaked to reduce salinity and then cooked, often sautéed or stewed with onions, tomatoes, peppers, and oil. It is commonly served as a main course and appears in various forms across West African and Atlantic cuisines where both ingredients have long been staple foods.

Kembung Bertelor is an Indonesian dish featuring female kembung fish (Indian mackerel) that carry roe, typically cooked by frying or grilling after being seasoned with spices such as turmeric, salt, and chili. It is commonly served as a main dish alongside steamed rice, sambal, and vegetables in everyday Indonesian home cooking and warung settings.

The Whopperrito is a fast food item created by Burger King that wraps the deconstructed contents of a Whopper burger — including flame-grilled beef, lettuce, tomato, onion, pickles, and sauce — inside a flour tortilla in the style of a burrito. It was offered as a limited-time menu item in the United States, served as a handheld wrap intended as an alternative format to the standard Whopper sandwich.

Botok masin is a Balinese steamed dish made by wrapping salted fish with grated coconut, shallots, garlic, chilies, and various spices in banana leaves before steaming until cooked through. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Balinese or Indonesian meal.

Gulai tekuyung is an Indonesian curry dish made by simmering river or land snails (tekuyung) in a spiced coconut milk broth containing turmeric, galangal, lemongrass, chilies, and other aromatics typical of gulai-style preparations. It is commonly served with steamed rice and is found particularly in Minangkabau and other Sumatran culinary traditions.

Takjil refers to a category of small sweet snacks and drinks traditionally consumed to break the fast during Ramadan in Indonesia, typically including items such as kolak (fruit in coconut milk syrup), es buah (fruit punch), dates, and various rice-based sweets. These bite-sized foods and beverages are prepared using ingredients such as coconut milk, palm sugar, banana, sweet potato, and tapioca pearls, and are served in the late afternoon before the main iftar meal.

Stuffed velvet crab is a dish in which whole velvet crabs (Necora puber) are cooked, then the meat is extracted, mixed with a seasoned filling that typically includes the crab's own flesh, onion, tomato, and sometimes béchamel or breadcrumbs, and the mixture is packed back into the cleaned crab shell. It is commonly served as a starter or tapa in Spanish coastal cuisine, often finished under a grill or in the oven to brown the top.

Boh puniaram is a traditional Acehnese fried snack made from a batter of rice flour, coconut milk, sugar, and grated coconut, shaped into small round balls and deep-fried until golden. It is commonly served as a street food or snack in Aceh province, Indonesia, and is often found at traditional markets and during festive occasions.

Happy pizza is a Cambodian dish based on a flatbread or pizza-style base topped with tomato sauce, cheese, and various ingredients such as vegetables or meat, and prepared with the addition of cannabis as a key ingredient. It is commonly served in restaurants and street food establishments in Cambodia, particularly in Phnom Penh and Sihanoukville, and is associated with tourist-oriented food culture in the country.

Cuore d'Abruzzo is a traditional sweet from the Abruzzo region of central Italy, typically prepared as a heart-shaped confection that often incorporates regional ingredients such as almonds, chocolate, or candied fruit. It is commonly served as a dessert or offered as a gift during festive occasions and celebrations, reflecting the pastry traditions of the area.

Smash is a Nigerian instant mashed potato product made from dehydrated potato flakes that are rehydrated by mixing with hot water or warm milk to produce a smooth, creamy mash. It is commonly served as a side dish or base alongside stews, soups, and protein dishes in everyday Nigerian household and restaurant meals.

Koleh kacang is a Malaysian steamed or baked kuih made primarily from ground peanuts, sugar, eggs, and coconut milk, resulting in a dense, sweet confection with a prominent roasted peanut flavor. It is typically molded into small portions and served as a snack or dessert, commonly found at markets and festive gatherings across Malaysia and Singapore.

Gula tarik is a pulled sugar confection made by repeatedly stretching and folding hot cooked sugar until it develops a glossy, aerated texture with a slightly chewy consistency. It is typically formed into thin strands or molded shapes and served as a sweet snack or dessert, often enjoyed on its own or used as a decorative element in other sweets.

Gyudon with 3 Cheeses is a Japanese-style beef rice bowl in which thinly sliced beef and onions are simmered in a dashi-based sauce seasoned with soy sauce, mirin, and sugar, then topped with three varieties of melted cheese before being served over steamed white rice. The dish combines the traditional Japanese gyudon preparation with an international influence, and is typically served as a single-portion main course in a bowl.

Lenja pita is a Bosnian-style savory pie made with layers of thin phyllo-like dough filled with a mixture of eggs, sour cream, and sometimes cheese or other dairy ingredients. It is typically baked in a round pan and served as a main dish or side dish, often cut into wedge-shaped portions.

The Enormous Omelet Sandwich is a breakfast sandwich offered by Burger King, consisting of a large folded egg omelet layered with sausage, bacon, ham, American cheese, and a special sauce, served on a hoagie-style bun. It was introduced in the United States in 2005 and is typically consumed as a morning meal, notable for its substantial size relative to standard fast-food breakfast items.

Krentenwegge is a Dutch elongated bread roll made from a yeast-leavened grain dough enriched with raisins (krenten), typically baked until the crust is firm and the interior is soft and studded throughout with dried fruit. It is commonly served sliced, often with butter, and is traditionally associated with birth celebrations in parts of the Netherlands, particularly in Groningen and Friesland.

Mirliton de Rouen is a small French pastry originating from Rouen in Normandy, made from a short pastry shell filled with a mixture of eggs, sugar, butter, and ground almonds, typically flavored with vanilla or bitter almond. It is baked until golden and served as an individual tart, traditionally associated with the confectionery heritage of the Normandy region.

Kuchen de quesillo is a Chilean-style tart made with a pastry base filled with quesillo, a soft fresh cheese, combined with eggs and sugar to form a smooth, custard-like filling that is baked until set. It is typically served as a dessert or afternoon snack, often accompanied by tea or coffee, and reflects the German-influenced kuchen tradition established by settlers in southern Chile.

Trenza de Almudévar is a traditional braided sweet bread originating from the town of Almudévar in the Huesca province of Aragon, Spain, made from a enriched dough containing flour, eggs, sugar, oil or butter, and anise, twisted into a characteristic braid shape. It is typically consumed as a pastry or confection, commonly associated with local festivals and everyday baking in the region.

Guagua de pan is a sweet bread shaped in the form of a human figure, traditionally made from a dough of flour, sugar, eggs, butter, and anise, then baked until golden. It is a traditional Ecuadorian preparation most commonly prepared and consumed during Día de los Difuntos (Day of the Dead) celebrations on November 2nd, often accompanied by colada morada, a thick purple fruit drink.

Maghzi Halva is an Iranian confection made by cooking animal brain (typically lamb or beef brain) with butter, sugar, saffron, and rosewater until a thick, smooth paste forms. It is traditionally served warm as a dessert or restorative dish, often garnished with cinnamon or additional saffron.

Branston Piccalilli is a British condiment made from chopped vegetables such as cauliflower, gherkins, and onions pickled in a bright yellow mustard- and turmeric-spiced vinegar sauce. It is typically served as an accompaniment to cold meats, cheese, sandwiches, and ploughman's lunches.

Khao Jee Pâté is a Lao and Vietnamese-influenced sandwich made with a toasted baguette spread with pork liver pâté and filled with ingredients such as sliced pork, pickled daikon and carrots, cucumber, cilantro, and chili. It is commonly sold as street food in Laos, particularly in Vientiane, and is typically eaten as a breakfast or snack item.

Squiccia is an Italian dessert that is typically prepared using simple regional ingredients such as flour, sugar, eggs, and often a flavoring like citrus zest, vanilla, or liqueur, reflecting the rustic traditions of home-style Italian baking. It is commonly served in small portions at the end of a meal or alongside coffee, and may be dusted with powdered sugar or accompanied by seasonal fruit depending on local custom.

A Biscoff sundae is assembled by layering vanilla ice cream with Lotus Biscoff cookie butter spread, crushed Biscoff speculoos biscuits, and whipped cream. It is typically served in a tall glass or bowl as a dessert, often finished with additional biscuit pieces and a drizzle of caramelised biscuit sauce.

Mosaic cake is a no-bake dessert prepared by combining crushed plain biscuits with a mixture of melted butter, sugar, cocoa powder, and eggs, then shaping the mixture into a log or block and refrigerating it until firm. When sliced, the embedded biscuit pieces create a mosaic-like cross-section pattern, and the cake is typically served cold as a tea-time or after-dinner treat.

Rainbow cake is a layered cake consisting of multiple sponge cake layers, each dyed a different color of the visible spectrum using food coloring, stacked and bound together with frosting such as buttercream or cream cheese icing. It is typically served as a celebration or novelty dessert, cut into slices to reveal the multicolored interior layers.

The McBaguette is a burger served on a brown baguette-style roll, filled with a beef patty, Emmental cheese, and seasoned with black pepper (Piper nigrum). It is a menu item sold at McDonald's France, designed to reflect French culinary references within a fast-food format.

Cola de mono ice cream is a frozen dessert based on the Chilean holiday drink cola de mono, which combines aguardiente (or pisco), coffee, milk, cloves, and cinnamon into a spiced, lightly alcoholic mixture that is then churned into ice cream. It is typically served as a dessert, particularly during the Christmas season in Chile, reflecting the cultural tradition of consuming cola de mono during festive gatherings.

The Gatsby is a South African submarine-style sandwich originating in Cape Town, made with a large bread roll filled with ingredients such as slap chips (soft french fries), meat or fish (commonly polony, calamari, or masala steak), and various condiments including atchar and sauces. It is typically sold as street food or takeaway, and a single sandwich is large enough to be shared among multiple people.

Omoyu is a thin Japanese rice porridge made by simmering a small amount of rice in a large quantity of water until the grains break down into a soft, diluted gruel with a water-to-rice ratio typically around 10:1 or higher. It is commonly served as a light, easily digestible food for infants, the elderly, or individuals recovering from illness, and is often eaten plain or with minimal seasoning.

Meihohamkatsu Curry is a regional specialty from Meiho, Gujo City, Gifu Prefecture, Japan, consisting of a breaded and deep-fried ham cutlet (hamkatsu) served over or alongside Japanese-style curry sauce and rice. The dish was developed as a local B-grade gourmet (B-kyu gourmet) item, utilizing ham as the central protein rather than the more common pork loin or chicken cutlet found in standard katsu curry preparations.

A watermelon bar is a chilled dessert preparation in which fresh watermelon is cut into a rectangular or bar-shaped portion, sometimes coated with ingredients such as chocolate, lime juice, or tajín seasoning, and served on a stick or skewer. It is commonly offered as a handheld refreshment at outdoor events, markets, and casual dining settings during warm weather.

Mbongo is a Cameroonian stew prepared by slow-cooking fish or meat in a dark sauce made from mbongo spice (the charred seed of Afrostyrax lepidophyllus), along with ingredients such as onions, garlic, hot peppers, and various local aromatics. It is typically served over boiled plantains, cassava, or rice as a main course.

Satti is a traditional dish from Oman and the wider Arabian Peninsula consisting of rice or bread served with a meat-based broth, typically made from slow-cooked lamb or chicken. It is commonly eaten as a breakfast or early meal, with the bread or rice soaked in the flavorful soup and the meat served alongside.

Sekba is an Indonesian-Chinese braised dish made by slow-cooking pork belly and offal in a seasoned soy sauce broth with spices such as five-spice powder, garlic, and sometimes tofu. It is commonly served as a street food or casual meal, accompanied by steamed rice or noodles, and is particularly associated with the Betawi and Hokkien Chinese communities in Indonesia.

Ikok is a Central African dish, particularly associated with Cameroon, prepared by wrapping Gnetum africanum leaves (known locally as eru or okazi) with other ingredients such as meat or fish in banana leaves and cooking them together. It is typically served as a main dish and shares similarities with other leaf-wrapped preparations found across the Congo Basin region.

Pecel pitik is a Balinese dish made from shredded chicken mixed with grated young coconut, shallots, chili, and a spiced sauce, typically including turmeric and galangal, then wrapped in banana leaves and briefly grilled or steamed. It is traditionally prepared and consumed during religious ceremonies in Banyuwangi, East Java, particularly among the Osing people, where it holds cultural and ritual significance.

Jenamani is an Indian dessert that is typically prepared using traditional sweet-making techniques, commonly incorporating ingredients such as milk solids, sugar, ghee, and aromatic spices like cardamom. It is often served in small portions at the end of a meal or during festive occasions and celebrations.

Madhubhat is typically a sweet rice preparation, with the name suggesting a combination of honey or jaggery ('madhu') with cooked rice ('bhat'), often slow-cooked until the grains absorb the sweetened liquid. It is commonly served as a dessert or ceremonial dish, sometimes garnished with nuts, raisins, or aromatic spices such as cardamom.

Skin crackers, known in Indonesian as kerupuk kulit, are made from cowhide that is cleaned, boiled, sun-dried, and then deep-fried in hot oil until they puff into light, crispy pieces. They are commonly served as a snack or as an accompaniment to main dishes such as rice, noodles, or meat-based preparations across Indonesia.

Klenyem is a traditional Javanese snack made from grated cassava mixed with palm sugar and grated coconut, then wrapped and steamed or grilled until cooked through. It is commonly sold as a street food or market snack in Central and East Java, Indonesia, and is typically served warm.

Kuah beulangong is a traditional Acehnese curry dish from Aceh, Indonesia, prepared by slow-cooking beef or goat meat with grated coconut and a blend of spices including turmeric, galangal, lemongrass, and chili in a large communal pot called a beulangong. It is traditionally prepared in large quantities for communal gatherings, religious celebrations, and festivals such as Eid, with the meat cooked until tender in the rich, spiced coconut-based broth.

Kuluban is an Indonesian dish made by mixing grated or shredded coconut with various vegetables, herbs, and spices, often including ingredients such as lemongrass, galangal, and kaffir lime leaves. It is typically served as a side dish or accompaniment to rice-based meals, and is associated with traditional Javanese or Central Indonesian culinary traditions.

Kuwah Pliëk U is a traditional Acehnese curry-style stew from Aceh, Indonesia, prepared by slow-cooking vegetables such as young jackfruit, eggplant, long beans, and melinjo leaves in a richly spiced broth made with pliëk u (fermented coconut dregs), coconut milk, and aromatic spices including galangal, turmeric, and lemongrass. The dish is typically served as a communal meal alongside steamed rice and is a staple preparation associated with Acehnese ceremonial and everyday dining.

Shpundra is a traditional Ukrainian pork dish in which fatty cuts of pork, typically ribs or belly, are braised together with beets and sometimes beet kvass, producing a dark, tangy broth. It is typically served as a hearty main course, often accompanied by the cooking liquid as a sauce or light soup.

Petatou is a French appetizer consisting of sliced potatoes topped with goat cheese, olives, and cream, typically assembled in layers and baked or grilled until the cheese softens. It is commonly served as a starter or canapé in French cuisine.

Bir pletok is a traditional Betawi herbal drink made by simmering a blend of spices including ginger, lemongrass, pandan leaves, secang wood (sappanwood), cinnamon, cardamom, and cloves in water, which gives the beverage its characteristic reddish color. It is served both hot and cold and is commonly found at traditional Betawi gatherings and street food settings in Jakarta, Indonesia.

Green caviar (Caulerpa lentillifera) is a species of green seaweed harvested primarily in the coastal waters of Southeast Asia and the Pacific, consisting of small, grape-like spheres on thin stems that burst with a briny, slightly salty flavor when eaten. It is typically served fresh or lightly rinsed as a salad, accompaniment to seafood dishes, or appetizer, and is commonly found in Filipino, Japanese, and other Asian cuisines.

Celimpungan is a South Sumatran dish made from fish paste mixed with sago or tapioca flour, shaped into flat oval cakes, and cooked by simmering in a rich coconut milk-based curry sauce seasoned with spices such as turmeric, lemongrass, and galangal. It is commonly served as a main dish in Palembang, South Sumatra, and is closely associated with local culinary traditions alongside other fish-based preparations from the region.

Dengke Mas Naniura is a traditional Batak dish from North Sumatra, Indonesia, made from raw carp (mas) that is cured without heat using the acidity of andaliman (Sichuan pepper-like spice) and asam jungga (a local citrus fruit), which effectively 'cook' the fish through a marinating process. The dish is typically seasoned with a spice paste including shallots, garlic, ginger, and turmeric, and is traditionally served at ceremonial occasions and important gatherings in Batak culture.

Fritanga is a mixed platter of fried or grilled meats common in Latin American cuisines, typically including cuts such as pork ribs, chorizo, blood sausage, and offal, often accompanied by fried plantains, potatoes, and corn. It is commonly served as a shared meal at informal gatherings, street food stalls, and family-style restaurants across countries including Colombia, Ecuador, and Nicaragua.

Dolceriso del Moro is typically a sweet rice-based dessert, likely prepared by simmering rice in milk with sugar and flavorings such as vanilla, cocoa, or dark chocolate, which would account for the 'Moro' (dark) element in its name. It is commonly served chilled or at room temperature as a dessert course, sometimes garnished with candied fruit, almonds, or a dusting of cocoa powder.

Zrazy à la Nelson are beef roulades made by pounding thin slices of beef, filling them with sautéed onions and hard-boiled eggs, then braising the rolled portions in a sauce made from meat stock and flour. The dish is traditionally served with a side of buckwheat porridge or mashed potatoes and is a classic preparation of Russian cuisine.

Kekefia is a Nigerian dish made by cooking plantains (cooking bananas) in palm oil with crayfish, onion, Capsicum peppers, and salt. It is typically served as a side dish or light meal and is associated with Niger Delta culinary traditions.

Otong soup is a Nigerian soup prepared with sliced okra (often called 'otong' in Efik and Ibibio culinary tradition), palm oil, assorted meats or seafood, crayfish, and traditional seasonings such as iru (locust beans). It is typically served with starchy accompaniments like fufu, eba, or pounded yam as part of a main meal in Nigerian households.

The Amtrak Natural Angus Burger is a beef patty made from natural Angus beef, served on a brioche bun with cheese, lettuce (Lactuca sativa), tomato, and red onion. It is accompanied by potato chips and is offered as a meal option aboard Amtrak passenger rail services.

Miyar zogale is a Nigerian soup prepared by cooking moringa leaves with beef, tomato, peanut (groundnut), and palm oil, commonly associated with Hausa cuisine in northern Nigeria. It is typically served over tuwo shinkafa (rice flour porridge) or other starchy staples as a complete meal.

Sroto Kriyik is a variant of sroto (soto) from the Banyumas region of Central Java, Indonesia, distinguished by the addition of kriyik, which are crispy fried pork or chicken skin crackling pieces served on top of the broth. The dish consists of a clear, lightly spiced broth with vermicelli noodles, shredded meat, bean sprouts, and sliced boiled eggs, typically accompanied by ketupat (compressed rice cake) and sambal.

cuisine: cuisine of the region of murcia
tags: cuisine of the region of murcia
description: Jallullo is a traditional bread-based dish from the Region of Murcia made by combining flour, olive oil, salt, and rendered fat or drippings from cured meats such as blood sausage and longaniza, seasoned with paprika and peppercorn. It is typically prepared by mixing the dough with the cooking juices of the sausages and onion, then baked or cooked on a griddle, and served as a rustic accompaniment or standalone dish in the context of traditional Murcian home cooking.

Pundut nasi is a traditional Indonesian dish in which cooked rice is wrapped in banana leaves and then steamed or grilled, resulting in a compact, aromatic parcel. It is commonly served as a staple accompaniment to various side dishes or consumed as a portable meal during everyday occasions and festive events.

Tamago yaki fry is a Japanese-influenced dish in which a rolled or formed egg preparation (tamagoyaki) is coated in breadcrumbs and deep-fried until golden and crispy. It is typically served as a snack or appetizer, sometimes accompanied by a dipping sauce such as tonkatsu sauce or mayonnaise.

Fio Fio is a West African dish made by cooking pigeon peas with cocoyam, onions, capsicum, and palm oil, seasoned with salt to produce a thick, stew-like preparation. It is typically served as a main dish or side, often accompanied by other staple foods in the regions where it is prepared.

Snail pepper soup is a West African broth-based dish prepared by simmering cleaned land snails with a blend of ground crayfish, hot peppers, traditional spice blends such as ehuru and uziza leaves, and seasoning in water or light stock. It is commonly served hot as a standalone dish or light meal, and is found at pepper soup joints and restaurants across Nigeria and neighboring countries.

Chikuwa salad is a Japanese-influenced dish combining sliced chikuwa (a tubular fish cake made from surimi) with potato salad, which is typically made from boiled mashed or cubed potatoes mixed with mayonnaise. It is commonly served as a side dish or appetizer, and the chikuwa may be stuffed with the potato salad or mixed together and presented chilled.

Hojas de parra are grape leaves typically stuffed with a mixture of rice, meat, and seasoned fillings, then rolled and cooked by simmering or baking. In the Basque Country of Spain, they are served as a starter or main dish, often accompanied by tomato sauce or a light broth.

Chanclas poblanas are a traditional Mexican sandwich from Puebla made with a soft, flat oval bread roll (the 'chancla') that is split and filled with seasoned meats such as chicken or pork in mole or salsa, along with beans, avocado, and chili peppers. They are typically served as street food or casual fare, often garnished with cream, cheese, and pickled jalapeños.

Dom chitor is a Bengali dish whose name suggests a slow-cooked preparation ('dom' referring to the dum-style of sealed, low-heat cooking) typically featuring spiced ingredients simmered in their own steam with mustard oil, onions, and traditional Bengali spices such as panch phoron, ginger, and green chilies. It is commonly served warm as a main course alongside steamed rice, luchi, or paratha in a home-style or festive meal setting.
Widerbergare is a Swedish open-faced sandwich consisting of sliced roast beef served on a baguette. It is typically assembled cold and served as a light meal or snack in cafés and lunch restaurants.

Soup meatball is a dish consisting of seasoned ground meat formed into balls, typically made from beef, pork, or a combination, and simmered directly in a broth-based soup alongside vegetables, herbs, or pasta. It is commonly served as a starter or main course in various international cuisines, with the meatballs absorbing the flavors of the surrounding broth during cooking.

Beguner cutlet is a Bengali-style fried snack made from slices of eggplant (brinjal) coated in a spiced batter, typically incorporating besan (chickpea flour), turmeric, chili, and other seasonings, then shallow- or deep-fried until crisp. It is commonly served as a tea-time snack or appetizer, often accompanied by mustard sauce or chutney.

The SpongeBob SquarePants Popsicle is a molded ice cream bar shaped in the likeness of the SpongeBob SquarePants cartoon character, typically featuring vanilla or fruit-flavored frozen dairy or water-based ice cream with a gumball or candy piece serving as the character's eyes. It is a licensed novelty frozen treat sold by ice cream vendors and supermarkets, intended for individual consumption as a handheld dessert on a wooden stick.

Lau patay musoor dal bhapa is a Bengali steamed dish made with red lentils (musoor dal) and bottle gourd leaves (lau pata), combined with mustard paste, green chilies, and mustard oil, then wrapped and cooked using steam. It is typically served as a side dish with plain steamed rice in Bengali households.

Yusanseul (유산슬) is a Korean-Chinese stir-fried dish consisting of pork, squid, shrimp, and various vegetables such as mushrooms, bamboo shoots, and carrots, bound together with a starch-thickened sauce. It is typically served as a main course in Korean-Chinese (jungshik) restaurants, often accompanied by steamed rice or noodles.

Lapek bugih is a traditional Minangkabau steamed cake made from glutinous rice flour mixed with grated coconut and palm sugar, then wrapped in banana leaves before being steamed until firm. It is a common snack and ceremonial food in Padang cuisine, typically served at traditional gatherings, markets, and as an everyday sweet treat in West Sumatra, Indonesia.

Biapong is a steamed or baked filled bun of Chinese-Indonesian origin, made from a soft wheat flour dough encasing a savory or sweet filling such as seasoned pork, chicken, or red bean paste. It is commonly sold as a street food snack or breakfast item in Indonesian Chinese communities, particularly in cities like Medan and Pontianak.

Carang mas is a traditional Javanese sweet snack made from grated cassava or sweet potato mixed with palm sugar and coconut, shaped into small portions and deep-fried until crispy and golden. It is commonly served as a snack or dessert during festive occasions and celebrations in Indonesia.

Injo-gogi-bap is a North Korean dish consisting of steamed rice (bap) served with injo-gogi, a textured soy protein product developed as a meat substitute during periods of food scarcity in North Korea. The soy-based protein is typically prepared to mimic the texture of meat and served alongside or mixed with the rice, making it a staple dish associated with everyday meals in North Korea.

Fish biryani is a layered rice dish in which marinated fish, typically firm-fleshed varieties such as king fish or tilapia, is cooked with long-grain basmati rice, whole spices (including cardamom, cloves, and bay leaves), saffron, fried onions, and fresh herbs using the dum method, where the pot is sealed and slow-cooked to allow the flavors to meld. It is commonly served as a main course accompanied by raita, a yogurt-based condiment, and may be garnished with caramelized onions and boiled eggs depending on regional variation.

Memoni biryani is a spiced rice dish originating from the Memon community of the Gujarat-Sindh region, prepared by layering parboiled basmati rice with meat—typically mutton or chicken—cooked with yogurt, fried onions, tomatoes, and a blend of whole and ground spices including dried plums and minimal food coloring, distinguishing it from other biryanis by its reduced use of saffron and its notably spicier, drier masala. It is typically served as a main course at festive gatherings and family meals, often accompanied by raita or a simple salad.

Cube toast is a thick, hollow square block of bread with the top removed and the interior scooped out, then deep-fried or baked until the shell is crispy. It is typically filled with savory or sweet ingredients such as scrambled eggs, ham, vegetables, or fruit with cream, and served as a breakfast or brunch item, particularly popular in Taiwan and parts of East and Southeast Asia.

Khanom phra phai is a traditional Thai dessert that typically consists of thin, crispy wafer-like layers, commonly prepared from a batter of rice flour, coconut milk, and sugar, often flavored with pandan or sesame and cooked on a hot griddle or mold until delicate and golden. It is usually served in small bite-sized pieces as a sweet snack or accompaniment to tea, and is often found at local markets and during festive occasions.

Taichan satay is an Indonesian grilled chicken skewer dish in which the meat is marinated without sweet soy sauce or peanut sauce, keeping it plain white after cooking. It is typically served with a spicy sambal made from raw chili and lime juice, which provides the primary seasoning, and is commonly sold as street food in Jakarta.

Egyptian Martabak is an Indonesian stuffed pancake made from a thin, folded dough filled with a savory mixture of minced meat, egg, onion, and spices, then pan-fried until crispy on the outside. It is commonly sold as a street food snack and served cut into portions, often accompanied by a vinegar-based dipping sauce with sliced cucumber and shallots.

Geonjin noodles are a Korean dish made with dried, uncooked noodles that are eaten in their raw, crunchy state rather than boiled, often seasoned or flavored with accompanying sauces or seasonings. They are commonly consumed as a snack, with the dry noodles serving as the primary ingredient without the addition of broth or cooking liquid.

Okoho is a traditional soup from the Idoma people of Benue State, Nigeria, prepared using the mucilaginous stem of the okoho plant (Cissus populnea), which is pounded or soaked in water to release a thick, viscous consistency that forms the base of the dish. It is typically cooked with meat or fish, palm oil, and various seasonings, and is served as a main meal alongside starchy accompaniments such as fufu or pounded yam.

Pasta all'Ortolana is an Italian vegetarian pasta dish prepared by sautéing a variety of seasonal vegetables—commonly including zucchini, eggplant, bell peppers, tomatoes, and onions—in olive oil, then tossing them with cooked pasta such as penne or rigatoni. It is typically served as a first course and reflects the cucina povera tradition of using garden vegetables ('ortolana' derives from 'orto,' meaning vegetable garden).

Kutsuzoko no nitsuke is a Japanese simmered dish made from the flatfish sole (kutsuzoko), cooked in a seasoned broth of soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar until the liquid reduces and the fish is glazed. It is typically served as a main course alongside steamed rice, with the braising liquid spooned over the fish as a sauce.

Koi pla is a raw freshwater fish dish from northeastern Thailand (Isan) and Laos, prepared by finely mincing or grinding fresh fish and mixing it with lime juice, fish sauce, roasted rice powder, shallots, lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, and dried chili. It is typically served at room temperature as part of a shared meal alongside sticky rice and fresh vegetables.

Stuffed pineapple is prepared by hollowing out a fresh pineapple half or whole fruit and filling the cavity with a mixture that typically includes diced pineapple flesh combined with ingredients such as rice, seafood, chicken, or fruit salad depending on the regional variation. It is commonly served as both a main course and a dessert presentation, with the pineapple shell functioning as an edible bowl at the table.

Korokke soba is a Japanese noodle dish consisting of buckwheat soba noodles served in a dashi-based broth topped with a korokke, a breaded and deep-fried potato croquette. It is typically served as a hot dish, with the croquette placed directly on the broth and noodles, where it partially softens from the steam and liquid.

Dalca is a Southeast Asian curry-based dish common in Indonesian and Malaysian cuisine, made by simmering vegetables such as eggplant, potatoes, and carrots with lentils or split peas in a spiced coconut milk or tamarind broth along with meat such as mutton or chicken. It is typically served alongside rice, roti, or biryani, and is commonly prepared for festive or communal occasions.

Tagine malsouka is a North African-inspired dish in which malsouka (thin, translucent pastry sheets similar to brik or warka) are used to encase or accompany a slow-cooked tagine preparation of meat and vegetables. It is typically served as a main course, combining the crispy or pliable texture of the pastry with the spiced, braised filling common to tagine-style cooking.

Bannu Pulao is a rice dish originating from the Bannu region of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan, prepared by slow-cooking tender beef or mutton with rice in a broth seasoned with whole spices, fat, and salt, resulting in a distinctively rich and greasy texture. It is traditionally served at communal gatherings, weddings, and celebratory meals, often accompanied by raita or salad.

Asaro is a West African dish, particularly common in Yoruba cuisine, made by boiling and partially mashing yam (Dioscorea) with palm oil, onion, and capsicum until it reaches a thick, porridge-like consistency. It is typically served as a main meal and may be accompanied by fried fish, eggs, or stewed vegetables.

Camarão no Bafo is a traditional dish from Amapá, Brazil, prepared by steaming fresh shrimp (Melicertus kerathurus, a species of penaeid shrimp) in a covered pot, allowing the shellfish to cook in their own moisture and natural juices. The dish is typically served as a simple, minimally seasoned preparation that highlights the natural flavor of the shrimp, often accompanied by regional staples such as rice or farofa.

Paper covered bone is typically a popular snack featuring a meat-wrapped bone, often pork or beef rib, encased in edible rice paper or parchment and then deep-fried or baked to seal in the marinade and juices. It is commonly served hot as a shareable appetizer or street-food item, with diners unwrapping the paper to enjoy the seasoned meat directly from the bone.

Kuzhimandi is a Kerala-style preparation in which marinated meat, typically lamb or chicken, is slow-cooked and then roasted or smoked in a pit or deep vessel, a method derived from the Arabian mandi tradition adapted to South Indian flavors. It is seasoned with a blend of spices including cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and black pepper, and is commonly served over long-grain or basmati rice alongside raita and salad.

Jepara grilled opor is a dish from Jepara, Central Java, in which chicken or meat is first cooked in opor—a coconut milk-based braise seasoned with spices such as galangal, lemongrass, candlenut, coriander, and turmeric—and then finished over a charcoal grill to impart a smoky, charred exterior. The dish combines the rich, creamy flavors of traditional opor with the texture and aroma characteristic of grilling, and is typically served with steamed rice and accompanied by sambal or other condiments.

Olgaengiguk is a Korean soup made by simmering loach fish (Misgurnus anguillicaudatus) with doenjang (fermented soybean paste), vegetables such as zucchini and green onions, and seasonings including garlic and chili. It is traditionally served hot as a main or side dish in Korean home cooking and is associated with rural and regional cuisine.

Rempah Jepara is a fried patty dish originating from Jepara, Central Java, made from a spiced mixture of grated coconut, minced meat or fish, and aromatic herbs such as galangal, lemongrass, and kaffir lime leaves, bound together and pan-fried until golden. It is typically served as a side dish or snack, often accompanied with steamed rice and sambal as part of a larger Indonesian meal.

canonicalName: pui metuli diye amudi macher jhal
displayName: Pui Metuli Diye Amudi Macher jhal
aliases: pui metuli diye amudi macher jhal
description: Pui Metuli Diye Amudi Macher Jhal is a Bengali fish curry prepared by cooking climbing perch (amudi/koi fish) with pui shak (Malabar spinach stems and leaves) and metuli (a type of bean or legume), seasoned with mustard paste, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. The dish is typically simmered until the fish absorbs the pungent, spiced gravy and is served with steamed rice as a traditional Bengali everyday meal.

Bánh dày is a Vietnamese glutinous rice cake made by steaming sticky rice and then pounding it into a smooth, dense, elastic dough, which is shaped into round, flat discs. It is commonly served as a savory accompaniment filled or paired with giò lụa (Vietnamese pork sausage), or eaten as a sweet snack, and holds cultural significance in Vietnamese tradition, particularly during Tết and ceremonial occasions.

Hot and sour soup noodles is a Japanese-Chinese fusion dish consisting of noodles served in a broth seasoned with vinegar and chili, typically combined with ingredients such as tofu, mushrooms, bamboo shoots, and egg to create a tangy, spicy flavor profile. It is commonly served as a single-bowl meal in casual dining contexts, drawing on the Chinese hot and sour soup tradition adapted within Japan's chūka ryōri (Japanese Chinese cuisine) style.

canonicalName: ritual christmas prianyky of sloboda ukraine
displayName: Ritual Christmas prianyky of Sloboda Ukraine
aliases: ritual christmas prianyky of sloboda ukraine
description: Ritual Christmas prianyky of Sloboda Ukraine are spiced honey-dough cookies traditional to the Sloboda Ukraine region, prepared by mixing rye or wheat flour with honey, spices such as cinnamon and cloves, and leavening agents, then shaping and baking the dough into decorative forms. They are made and exchanged during Christmas and winter holiday celebrations as part of regional Ukrainian ritual and gift-giving customs.

10 Won Bread (십원빵) is a South Korean street food shaped to resemble the 10-won coin, made from a thin, crispy batter typically containing flour, sugar, and butter, cooked in a specially engraved mold that imprints the coin's design. It is commonly sold by street vendors and at markets, often served plain or filled with sweet fillings such as red bean paste or cream.

Huatape is a thick Mexican broth or stew traditionally made with a base of ground dried chiles, epazote, and masa (corn dough) used as a thickener, typically combined with seafood such as shrimp or fish. It is commonly served as a main course in a bowl, particularly in the coastal regions of Veracruz, where it is associated with traditional indigenous and mestizo cooking.

Madumongso is a traditional Javanese fermented sweet made from black glutinous rice (ketan hitam) that is cooked, fermented for several days, then combined with coconut milk and palm sugar to produce a thick, tangy, dark-colored confection. It is typically wrapped in banana leaves or plastic packaging and served as a snack or traditional treat, commonly found during festive occasions and celebrations in Java, Indonesia.

Manuk Napinadar is a Batak dish from North Sumatra, Indonesia, consisting of chicken cooked in a spiced blood sauce made with pork or chicken blood, andaliman (Sichuan pepper native to the Batak region), shallots, garlic, ginger, galangal, and chili. The chicken is typically grilled or roasted before being combined with the thick, aromatic sauce, and the dish is traditionally served at ceremonial occasions and adat (customary) gatherings among the Batak people.

Ranga is a traditional Bolivian dish typically prepared as a hearty stew featuring beef tripe or offal simmered with potatoes, peanuts, and a blend of local spices such as aji and cumin. It is commonly served hot with rice or boiled potatoes, often accompanied by llajua, a spicy Bolivian salsa, as a comforting home-style meal.

Nasi tempong is a Balinese rice dish served with steamed or blanched vegetables such as long beans, spinach, and cassava leaves, accompanied by fried or grilled protein options including fish, tofu, or tempeh. It is defined by its intensely spicy sambal tempong, a raw chili-based condiment, and is commonly eaten as an everyday meal in the Banyuwangi region of East Java.

Oromo is a steamed Central Asian dish made by rolling a thin dough sheet around a filling of minced meat (typically lamb or beef) mixed with onions and sometimes pumpkin or other vegetables, then rolling it into a log shape and cooking it in a steamer. It is commonly served sliced into rounds and accompanied by a broth or sauce, and appears in Kazakh, Uzbek, and other Central Asian cuisines as a main course.

Jenang krasikan is a traditional Javanese sweet porridge made from glutinous rice flour, palm sugar, and grated coconut, cooked together until the mixture thickens into a dense, sticky consistency. It is commonly sold as a street food or snack in Central Java, often wrapped in banana leaf or served alongside other traditional jenang varieties.

Polish croquettes are made by filling thin, rolled crêpes with a savory mixture, typically of braised sauerkraut and mushrooms or minced meat, then rolling them into cylinders, coating in breadcrumbs, and pan-frying until golden and crisp. They are commonly served as an appetizer or side dish in Polish cuisine, often accompanied by clear beet soup (barszcz czerwony).

Chartreuse is a French molded dish in which cooked vegetables—and sometimes meat or game—are arranged in a decorative pattern lining a mold, then packed with a filling and unmolded to reveal a colorful geometric exterior. It is traditionally served as a formal first course or side dish, with the vegetable layers typically including cabbage, carrots, turnips, or green beans shaped and fitted carefully around the central filling.

Lentog is a Javanese dish originating from Kudus, Central Java, consisting of young jackfruit (nangka muda) and melinjo leaves cooked in coconut milk-based sauce, served over lontong (rice cake wrapped in banana leaves). It is typically eaten as a morning meal, accompanied by tempe, tofu, and sambal, and is a well-known street food breakfast in the Kudus region.

Masam keueueng is a traditional Acehnese sour and spicy fish curry from Aceh, Indonesia, prepared by simmering fish—commonly skipjack tuna or other local varieties—in a tamarind-based broth seasoned with turmeric, chili, and aromatic spices. The dish is typically served with steamed rice and is a staple of everyday meals in Acehnese households.

Schnepfendreck is a traditional Swiss preparation made from the intestines and innards of the woodcock (Schnepfe), typically mixed with butter, salt, and pepper, and spread on toast or bread. It is usually served as a small appetizer or accompaniment alongside the roasted woodcock, and is considered a classic element of European game cuisine.

Pork tail is prepared from the caudal appendage of the pig, which contains skin, cartilage, and small bones surrounded by collagen-rich connective tissue; it is commonly braised, boiled, smoked, or grilled depending on regional culinary tradition. The dish appears across multiple international cuisines, often served as a standalone appetizer, added to stews and soups for body, or split and grilled until the skin crisps while the interior remains tender.

Gazelle ankles, known in Arabic as 'kaʿb al-ghazāl' or 'chevilles de gazelle' in French, are a crescent-shaped Moroccan pastry made from a thin dough filled with almond paste flavored with orange blossom water and cinnamon. They are typically dusted lightly with powdered sugar or left plain and served as a sweet alongside mint tea at celebrations, gatherings, or as a dessert confection.

Ablo is a steamed rice cake originating from West Africa, particularly associated with Togolese and Beninese cuisines, made from fermented corn or rice flour formed into soft, spongy rounds. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside stews, sauces, or grilled meats and fish.

The Dagobert is a Belgian open-faced sandwich consisting of a thick slice of bread topped with a steak (typically minute steak or entrecôte), a fried egg, and often accompanied by French fries. It is commonly served as a hearty main course in Belgian brasseries and cafés, typically garnished with condiments such as mayonnaise or a sauce.

Pom is a Surinamese baked casserole dish made from grated malanga (Xanthosoma sagittifolium) corms combined with chicken and tomato, seasoned with citrus juice and spices, then oven-baked until set. It is a traditional dish closely associated with the Surinamese Jewish community and is commonly served at festive meals and family gatherings.

Ume suishō is typically a Japanese-style dessert or confection featuring ume (Japanese plum), often prepared as a jelly or crystal-like sweet using agar or kuzu starch to achieve a translucent, glass-like appearance reminiscent of its name, which references crystal (suishō). It is commonly served chilled in small portions as a seasonal treat, particularly in warmer months, and may accompany green tea or conclude a traditional meal.

Ħobż biż-żejt is a Maltese sandwich made from traditional ftira bread that is split and filled with tomatoes, olives, capers, and olive oil, sometimes with added anchovies or tuna. It is typically served as a casual lunch or snack, eaten cold or at room temperature.

Shiroi Koibito is a Japanese confection produced by Ishiya Co., Ltd. in Hokkaido, consisting of thin, crisp langue de chat butter cookies sandwiched around a layer of white chocolate. It is widely sold as a regional souvenir (omiyage) from Hokkaido and is typically packaged in gift boxes for individual consumption or sharing.

Kiyomemochi is a Japanese confection made from pounded glutinous rice (mochi) shaped into small cakes, typically flavored or coated with ingredients such as sweetened bean paste, kinako (roasted soybean flour), or similar traditional toppings. It is associated with Shinto purification rituals and is commonly offered at shrines or served during ceremonial occasions in Japan.

Glögg is a mulled wine beverage prepared by heating red wine with spices such as cinnamon, cloves, cardamom, and ginger, often combined with spirits like vodka or aquavit and sweetened with sugar. It is traditionally served hot in small cups, frequently garnished with raisins and blanched almonds, and is commonly consumed during the winter holiday season in Estonia and other Nordic and Baltic countries.

Kenpi is a traditional Japanese confection made by coating thin strips of gobo (burdock root) or sweet potato in a hard sugar glaze, typically achieved by cooking the strips in sugar syrup until the coating crystallizes. It is commonly served as a snack or side dish and is particularly associated with Kochi Prefecture in Japan, where burdock root kenpi is a regional specialty.

Steam Pot Chicken (汽锅鸡) is a traditional Yunnan dish prepared by slow-steaming a whole chicken with ingredients such as ginger, Sichuan pepper, and sometimes medicinal herbs like goji berries or gastrodia orchid tuber in a specially designed clay pot called a qiguo, which uses an internal steam vent to cook the chicken in its own condensed juices. The resulting broth is clear and concentrated, and the dish is typically served as a centerpiece at formal or family meals in Yunnan cuisine.

Cadbury Snack is a chocolate biscuit product manufactured by Cadbury, consisting of a shortcake or wafer biscuit base topped or coated with milk chocolate. It is commonly sold as an individually wrapped bar or in multipacks and is typically consumed as a snack or served alongside hot beverages.

The Cadbury Spira is a chocolate bar consisting of two cylindrical tubes of milk chocolate formed by winding a continuous ribbon of chocolate into a hollow spiral shape. It was manufactured by Cadbury and typically eaten as a hand-held confectionery snack, and was also commonly used as a straw for drinking beverages such as milk or hot chocolate due to its hollow core.

Nisholda is an Iranian confection made by whipping egg whites with a syrup of water and sugar, often stabilized with citric acid and beaten with the dried root of soap root (Acanthophyllum) to produce a dense, white, meringue-like foam. It is traditionally served as a sweet spread or dessert, particularly during Persian New Year (Nowruz), and is commonly eaten with bread or alongside other sweets.

Talkhan is a Central Asian preparation made from roasted and ground grain, typically wheat or barley, sometimes combined with walnuts and other ingredients to form a dry flour or paste. It is traditionally consumed as a portable, energy-dense food, eaten dry or mixed with water, oil, or other liquids, and is common in Afghanistan and neighboring regions as a simple staple or travel food.

Badhakopir kopta is a Bengali dish made from cauliflower or cabbage (badhakopi) that is grated or finely chopped, mixed with spices and binding ingredients such as chickpea flour, and formed into small balls that are then deep-fried. The koftas are typically served as a standalone snack or incorporated into a spiced gravy as part of a main course meal.

Koraisuntir dalna is a Bengali vegetarian curry made with green peas (koraishuti) and drumsticks (sojne data/moringa pods) cooked in a spiced gravy typically based on ginger, cumin, turmeric, and tomato. It is commonly prepared as a dry or semi-dry curry and served as a side dish with steamed rice or flatbreads such as luchi or roti.

Fulkopir porota is a South Asian flatbread made by stuffing whole wheat dough with a spiced cauliflower (fulkopi) filling, then rolling it flat and cooking it on a hot griddle with oil or ghee. It is typically served as a main meal accompanied by yogurt, pickle, or a complementary curry.

Kochi aamer ombol is a Bengali sour relish made from raw green mangoes cooked with mustard, dried red chilies, sugar or jaggery, and spices to produce a tangy, sweet-and-sour sauce. It is typically served as a palate-cleansing condiment or side dish alongside rice-based meals, particularly during the summer season when raw mangoes are in season.

Dumbleng is a traditional Indonesian snack typically prepared from cassava or glutinous rice flour, shaped into small rounds or ovals and often cooked by steaming or frying until tender. It is commonly sweetened with palm sugar and coated in grated coconut, and is generally served as a light afternoon treat alongside tea or coffee.

Fulkopir anondivog is a Bengali vegetarian dish made by cooking cauliflower florets with spices such as cumin, turmeric, ginger, and green chilies, often combined with potatoes and finished with ghee or mustard oil. It is typically served as a side dish alongside rice or flatbreads in South Asian household meals and festive occasions.

Enchorer kopta is a Bengali vegetarian dish made from grated raw jackfruit that is boiled, mashed, mixed with spices and often chickpea or gram flour, then shaped into balls and fried before being simmered in a spiced onion-tomato gravy. It is typically served as a main course accompaniment to steamed rice, pulao, or luchi.

Dim bhorta is a Bangladeshi dish made from hard-boiled eggs that are mashed and mixed with finely chopped onions, green chilies, mustard oil, and salt, sometimes with coriander or fried garlic added. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice and dal as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Pepe muger ghonto is a Bengali dish made by cooking diced raw papaya with split mug (mung) dal, tempered with whole spices such as cumin, bay leaf, and dried red chili, and seasoned with turmeric, ginger, and ghee. It is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice during a traditional Bengali meal.

Dim vapa is a Bengali dish made by steaming eggs in a mixture of mustard paste, green chilies, mustard oil, and sometimes grated coconut or onion until the custard-like preparation sets. It is typically served with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Dim Kosha is a Bengali dish in which hard-boiled eggs are simmered in a thick, heavily reduced gravy made from onions, garlic, ginger, tomatoes, and a blend of spices such as turmeric, cumin, coriander, and garam masala, often with potatoes added. It is typically served hot with steamed rice, pulao, luchi, paratha, or roti as a main course.

Nasi buluh is a rice dish prepared by cooking glutinous rice with coconut milk and salt inside hollow bamboo tubes lined with banana leaves, which are then roasted or grilled over an open fire until the rice is cooked and infused with the aroma of the bamboo. It is commonly served during harvest festivals and communal gatherings among Dayak and other indigenous communities in Borneo, typically accompanied by meat, fish, or vegetable side dishes.

Euneobap is a Korean rice dish prepared by cooking rice together with sweetfish (eun-eo), typically in an earthenware or stone pot, often with soy sauce, sesame oil, and aromatics added during or after cooking. It is commonly served as a main course with the fish mixed into the rice at the table, accompanied by banchan side dishes.

Shankalur payesh is a Bengali rice pudding prepared by simmering grated shankhalu (yam bean or jicama) in milk that has been reduced and sweetened with sugar or jaggery, often flavored with cardamom, bay leaf, and garnished with raisins and nuts such as cashews or pistachios. It is typically served chilled or at room temperature as a dessert during festive occasions and religious celebrations in Bengali households.

Peyanj posto is a Bengali dish made by cooking a paste of ground poppy seeds with sliced onions, green chilies, and mustard oil until the mixture thickens into a soft, textured preparation. It is typically served with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Enchor er kima is a Bengali dish made from raw green jackfruit that is boiled, finely chopped or minced to resemble ground meat, then cooked with onions, ginger, garlic, green chilies, and a blend of spices such as cumin, turmeric, and garam masala. It is typically served as a side dish with rice, luchi, or roti in Bengali home-style meals.

Amrar dal is a Bengali lentil dish prepared by cooking split lentils, typically masoor or moong dal, with pieces of green, unripe mango to impart a tangy flavor, tempered with mustard seeds, dried red chilies, and panch phoron in mustard oil. It is commonly served as a summer accompaniment to steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Pat shak er jhol is a Bengali light stew prepared by simmering jute leaves (pat shak) with minimal spices such as panch phoron, green chilies, and sometimes potatoes or small fish in a thin broth. It is typically served with steamed rice as part of a home-style meal in West Bengal and Bangladesh.

Gandal Patar Jhol is an Assamese light curry prepared by simmering the leaves and tender stems of the gandal (jute mallow) plant in water with ingredients such as garlic, green chilies, and mustard oil, sometimes thickened with a little rice flour or paired with small fish or potatoes. It is typically served warm with steamed rice as part of a traditional home-style meal in Assam.

Piyazmaş is a Kurdish dish made from thinly sliced onions tossed with lemon juice and crushed garlic, often seasoned with salt and sumac or other spices. It is typically served as a cold side dish or salad accompanying grilled meats, kebabs, and rice-based main courses.

Mashi rice is typically a stuffed rice preparation, with the name likely derived from the Arabic word 'mahshi,' referring to vegetables such as bell peppers, zucchini, tomatoes, or grape leaves filled with seasoned rice and herbs. It is commonly simmered in a tomato-based broth and often served warm as a main course, accompanied by yogurt or fresh bread.

Alu kopta is a dish made from boiled and mashed potatoes shaped into balls, often mixed with spices, herbs, and sometimes gram flour or breadcrumbs for binding, then fried and simmered in a spiced tomato- or yogurt-based gravy. It is typically served hot with rice, roti, naan, or paratha as a main course in South Asian meals.

Byśki are small figurative breads from the Kurpie region of Poland, shaped by hand from wheat dough into forms representing domestic and wild animals before being baked. They are traditionally prepared for Christmas, placed on the holiday table or hung as decorations, and later given to children and livestock as symbols of prosperity.

Galbo is a Japanese confection produced by Meiji, consisting of a biscuit core coated in chocolate, giving it a crunchy interior and smooth outer shell. It is sold in small bite-sized pieces within individual bags and is typically consumed as a snack or accompaniment to tea or coffee.

Chomchom of Belakoba is a Bengali milk-based sweet made by curdling milk to form chhena, shaping it into oval pieces, and simmering them in sugar syrup until they absorb the sweetness and develop a dense, chewy texture. It is typically served at room temperature as a dessert during festivals, weddings, and religious occasions, often garnished with grated khoya or desiccated coconut.

Bonbon la rouroute is a traditional confection from Réunion made from the starch of the rouroute plant (arrowroot, Maranta arundinacea), which is mixed with sugar and water, cooked, and then shaped and dried into small sweets. It is typically consumed as a childhood treat or household candy, often associated with Creole culinary heritage.

Sguta is a traditional Sardinian unleavened flatbread made from durum wheat flour, water, and salt, rolled thin and baked on a hot stone or griddle. It is typically served alongside meals, accompanying cheeses, cured meats, or stews in rural Sardinian dining.

A Mountain Bar is a confection consisting of a peanut-studded nougat-like center coated in milk chocolate, produced in a short, thick cylindrical shape. It is typically sold individually wrapped as a packaged candy bar and eaten as a snack or dessert.

Smint is a small compressed mint pellet manufactured by pressing sugar substitutes such as sorbitol, maltitol, or isomalt together with mint or fruit flavorings into tablets. It is dispensed from a small plastic click-top container and consumed as a sugar-free breath freshener rather than served as part of a meal.

Zours are sour-flavored gummy candies produced by the Dutch confectioner Red Band, made from a gelatin or starch base combined with sugar, glucose syrup, fruit acids, and fruit flavorings, then coated with a sour sugar crystal mixture. They are sold in individual packets as a confectionery snack and are typically consumed on their own rather than as part of a meal.

Tehane is a Moroccan preparation in which seasoned kofta (spiced minced meat) is encased in or wrapped with lamb or beef spleen, then grilled or skewered over charcoal. It is typically served as street food or as part of a shared meal, often accompanied by bread, cumin, salt, and harissa.

Lumblija is a traditional spiced cake from the island of Korčula in Croatia, made with flour, sugar, honey, nuts such as almonds and walnuts, raisins, dried figs, and aromatic spices including cinnamon, cloves, and nutmeg, often enriched with prošek or another sweet wine. It is typically baked for All Saints' Day and other autumn and winter holidays, and served in slices alongside coffee or dessert wine.

Cortadillo is a traditional Spanish sweet pastry from Andalusia, made from a dough of flour, lard, sugar, and often flavored with cinnamon, wine, or anise, which is rolled, filled or topped with ingredients such as cabello de ángel (pumpkin jam) or almonds, and baked before being dusted with powdered sugar. It is typically served as a dessert or accompaniment to coffee, and is especially common during Christmas and other festive occasions.

Ox eye, known in Switzerland as Ochsenauge or Spiegelei, is a fried egg prepared sunny-side up in butter or oil until the white is set while the yolk remains runny, resembling an eye. It is typically served for breakfast or as a topping on dishes such as Rösti, spinach, or minced meat preparations.

Verwurelter are Luxembourgish fried pastries made from a sweet yeast or egg-based dough that is rolled, cut into strips, and twisted or knotted before being deep-fried and dusted with sugar. They are traditionally served during Carnival and other festive occasions, often accompanied by coffee.

Abekatekonto soup is prepared by simmering meat with palm nut pulp and ground peanuts to form a thick, oily base, seasoned with Capsicum peppers for heat. It is typically served hot as a main course alongside starches such as rice, fufu, or boiled tubers.

Pouteille is a traditional stew from the Lozère region of France, made by slow-cooking salted pork trotters and tail with potatoes, onions, carrots, garlic, and red wine, often thickened with a roux and flavored with herbs. It is typically served hot as a hearty main course during winter, particularly around Christmas and New Year celebrations.

Kruska is a Swedish porridge-like dish prepared by simmering rolled oats and wheat bran with water and raisins until softened and thickened. It is typically served warm for breakfast, often accompanied by milk.

Murki is a Bengali sweet snack made from puffed rice (muri) coated with a hardened sugar or jaggery syrup, formed into small crunchy clusters or balls. It is typically served as a tea-time snack or festive treat, particularly during occasions such as Lakshmi Puja and Poush Sankranti.

Şambaba is a Turkish dessert made from semolina or flour-based sponge cake that is baked, cut into portions, and then soaked in a hot sugar syrup flavored with lemon. It is typically served at room temperature, often topped with clotted cream (kaymak) or chopped pistachios, as a traditional ending to a meal.

Bokpe is a Kazakh flatbread made from a dough of flour and cow's milk, which is rolled thin and fried or baked until golden. It is typically served as an accompaniment to meals, often alongside tea, soups, or meat dishes.

Orrisa is a traditional dish prepared by slow-cooking meat with barley until the grains soften and absorb the broth, producing a thick, porridge-like consistency. It is typically served hot as a main course, often during communal meals or festive gatherings.

Wassa-wassa is a West African couscous-like dish from Togo and Benin, made by moistening millet or cassava flour and steaming it until the grains form small, fluffy pellets. It is typically served with a sauce or stew containing meat, fish, or vegetables as a staple accompaniment at family meals and communal gatherings.

Girella is a spiral-shaped sponge cake roll filled with a layer of cream, jam, or chocolate, produced by spreading the filling over a thin sheet of baked sponge and rolling it into a log before slicing into individual portions. It is typically served as a snack or dessert, often accompanying coffee or tea, and is commonly found as an industrially packaged single-serving item.

Natinombur is a Batak dish from North Sumatra consisting of grilled or fried fish, typically mujair or carp, topped with a spice paste made from andaliman pepper, shallots, garlic, chilies, and roasted candlenuts. It is commonly served with steamed rice as part of a traditional Batak meal.

Sangsang is a Batak dish from North Sumatra made from minced pork or buffalo meat cooked with a paste of shallots, garlic, lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, candlenuts, and andaliman pepper, often enriched with coconut milk and the animal's blood. It is traditionally served with rice at Batak ceremonial gatherings such as weddings and funerals.

Gui is a Korean method of preparing food by grilling or roasting over direct heat, commonly applied to marinated meats such as beef (bulgogi), pork (samgyeopsal), or fish, as well as vegetables. It is typically served with rice, dipping sauces like ssamjang, and side dishes (banchan), and is often cooked at the table on a built-in grill for communal dining.

Jagannath Ballabha is an Odia sweet prepared by combining grated coconut, chhena (fresh curd cheese), sugar, and ghee, which are cooked together and flavored with cardamom before being shaped into small portions. It is traditionally offered as bhog to Lord Jagannath at the Puri temple and distributed as prasad to devotees, particularly during religious festivals.

Rondho royal, also known as misro, is a Javanese fried snack made from grated cassava shaped around a filling of palm sugar (gula jawa), then deep-fried until the exterior is crisp and the sugar inside melts. It is typically served warm as a teatime or afternoon snack in Indonesia, commonly accompanied by coffee or tea.

Kashi dofu is a Japanese preserved tofu made by freezing tofu and then drying it, resulting in a dense, spongy texture that rehydrates when simmered. It is typically reconstituted in dashi-based broth with seasonings such as soy sauce, mirin, and sugar, and served as part of traditional Buddhist temple cuisine or home-style simmered dishes.

Boyal paturi is a Bengali dish in which boyal (a freshwater catfish) is marinated in a paste of mustard, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil, then wrapped in banana leaves and steamed or lightly pan-cooked until the fish absorbs the flavors and the leaf imparts its aroma. It is typically served with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Tangra posto is a Bengali fish preparation in which tangra (a small freshwater catfish) is cooked in a paste of ground white poppy seeds, often with green chilies, mustard oil, turmeric, and onion or nigella seeds. It is typically served with steamed rice as part of a main meal in Bengali households.

Pan Galo Plaza is typically a bread-based preparation, with 'pan' suggesting a loaf or roll that is commonly baked and may incorporate ingredients such as flour, eggs, butter, and sometimes cheese or herbs. It is often served as an accompaniment to main courses or enjoyed on its own as part of a light meal, and the 'Galo Plaza' designation suggests a signature or namesake variation offered in an international dining context.

Nabada is a traditional Portuguese dish typically prepared as a sweet confection or dessert, commonly featuring ingredients such as eggs, sugar, and turnip or pumpkin, slow-cooked to achieve a rich, jam-like consistency. It is often served in small portions as a dessert or accompaniment to coffee, and may appear during festive occasions or regional celebrations.

A rice-saving dish is a strongly flavored, often salty or savory preparation designed to be eaten in small quantities alongside a larger portion of plain rice, common in East and Southeast Asian culinary traditions. Typical examples include salted or fermented vegetables, pickles, preserved fish, and intensely seasoned meat or bean preparations served as side dishes at everyday meals.

Koba is a traditional Malagasy cake made from a paste of ground peanuts, rice flour, and sugar, wrapped in banana leaves and slowly boiled or steamed for several hours until firm. It is typically sliced into rounds and served as a snack or dessert, often sold by street vendors in Madagascar.

Kasuami is a traditional Southeast Sulawesi dish made from grated cassava that is squeezed to remove its liquid, then shaped into a cone and steamed until cooked. It is commonly served as a staple carbohydrate alongside fish dishes such as parende or ikan bakar, particularly in the Buton and Wakatobi regions of Indonesia.

Shapla chingri is a Bangladeshi dish made by cooking the tender stems of the water lily (shapla) with small shrimp (chingri), onions, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard or other cooking oil. It is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Kue muso is a steamed Indonesian layered cake from South Sumatra, made with a bottom layer of glutinous rice and a top layer consisting of a custard-like mixture of eggs, coconut milk, sugar, flour, and pandan or cocoa powder for coloring. It is typically served as a sweet snack or takjil during Ramadan to break the fast.

Lavariya is a Sri Lankan sweet made by pressing a dough of rice flour through a string-hopper mould into nest-shaped coils, which are then steamed and filled with a mixture of grated coconut cooked with treacle or jaggery and flavoured with cardamom. It is typically served as a dessert or festive sweet, particularly during celebrations such as the Sinhala and Tamil New Year.

Padamaran is a traditional Malay kuih from Riau, Indonesia, made from rice flour batter mixed with pandan juice, steamed in small cups lined with banana leaves, and topped with a sauce of palm sugar (gula merah) and coconut milk. It is typically served as a sweet snack or dessert, particularly during Ramadan as a dish for breaking the fast.

Oondees are small steamed dumplings from the Konkani cuisine of coastal Karnataka, made from rice flour dough that is shaped into balls and cooked over boiling water or in a steamer. They are typically served hot with coconut chutney, curries, or a drizzle of ghee and jaggery as a breakfast or light meal.

Uih sepit is a traditional Indonesian snack made from wheat flour dough that is shaped and pressed between heated iron molds, producing thin, crisp wafer-like pieces. It is commonly served as a dry snack accompanying tea or coffee, and is often prepared in larger quantities for festive occasions and celebrations.

Bora is a Meitei fritter made by mixing chopped vegetables, herbs, or pulses with a batter of rice flour or gram flour seasoned with salt and chilli, then deep-frying the mixture in small rounds until crisp. It is typically served as a side dish or snack alongside rice and accompaniments such as ooti, kangshoi, or chutneys in a traditional Manipuri meal.

Sînor is a traditional Romanian pastry made by rolling thin sheets of dough with a filling of fresh cheese and quark, then coiling the filled rolls into a spiral shape and baking them. It is typically served as a dessert or accompaniment to tea and coffee, often dusted with sugar.

Sombe is a stew prepared by pounding and simmering cassava leaves with palm oil, often cooked together with fish or meat until the greens are tender and the sauce is thickened. It is commonly served as a main course alongside starches such as rice, fufu, or cassava-based staples.

Bamba is a puffed corn snack made by extruding corn grits under heat and pressure, then coating the resulting puffs with peanut butter. The strawberry variant referenced here incorporates a strawberry-flavored cream filling inside the puff, and it is typically served as a packaged snack eaten at room temperature.

Wani ryori is a traditional dish from the San'in region of Japan, particularly Hiroshima and Shimane prefectures, consisting of shark meat (called 'wani' locally) served raw as sashimi, thinly sliced and accompanied by soy sauce and wasabi. The shark's natural ammonia content acts as a preservative, historically making it a staple protein in inland mountainous areas, and it is commonly served during New Year celebrations and festive occasions.

Seto dyed rice is typically a colorful rice dish inspired by the Seto region, commonly prepared by tinting steamed rice with natural ingredients such as vegetable juices, herbs, or spices to achieve vibrant hues. It is often served as a main or side component alongside proteins and pickled vegetables, offering a visually striking presentation suited to shared dining settings.

Amsterdam onions are small silverskin onions pickled in a sweetened, spiced white vinegar brine, often flavored with ingredients such as turmeric, mustard seed, and sugar, which gives them a pale yellow color and a sweet-sour taste. They are typically served as a condiment or garnish alongside cheese platters, cold cuts, bar snacks, and bitterballen.

Tarana is prepared by mixing flour, eggs, milk, butter, and salt into a stiff dough, which is then grated or crumbled into small granules and dried. The dried granules are typically cooked in boiling water or broth to make a soup, served warm as a first course.

Tumact me tulez is a handmade pasta dish from the Arbëreshë communities of Basilicata, consisting of long, hand-rolled noodles typically dressed with a sauce of toasted breadcrumbs, walnuts, and sometimes anchovies or honey. It is traditionally served on Christmas Eve and other feast days as a first course.

Ping On Bun is a steamed Chinese bun made from wheat flour dough, traditionally stamped with a red seal bearing the characters for 'peace' (ping on). It was historically distributed on Cheung Chau Island in Hong Kong during the annual Cheung Chau Bun Festival, where towers of these buns are displayed and later shared with residents for good fortune.

Grassins are small French pastries made from shortcrust dough combining flour, butter, sugar, and a pinch of salt, rolled out, cut into shapes, and baked until golden. They are typically served as a sweet accompaniment to coffee, tea, or dessert courses.

Shinoda-don is a Japanese rice bowl dish consisting of steamed rice topped with simmered aburaage (deep-fried tofu pouches) that have been cooked in a seasoned broth of dashi, soy sauce, and mirin, often combined with egg and chopped scallions or onions. It is typically served as a quick, inexpensive meal in casual Japanese eateries and at home.

Coca garlanda is a traditional Catalan ring-shaped sweet flatbread made from a yeasted dough enriched with eggs, sugar, and lard or oil, typically decorated with candandied fruits and pine nuts before baking. It is commonly served during festive occasions, particularly associated with patron saint celebrations in Catalonia, and is often accompanied by sweet wine or coffee.

Julienne is a hot appetizer of mushrooms and often chicken, sautéed with onions and bound in a sour cream or béchamel sauce, topped with grated cheese and baked until golden. It is traditionally served in small individual metal cups called kokotnitsa, typically as a starter course.

Gulai Balak is a Lampung curry made by simmering beef or buffalo meat with coconut milk and a spice paste that typically includes shallots, garlic, turmeric, ginger, galangal, chili, lemongrass, and candlenut. It is traditionally served with steamed rice at ceremonial gatherings and family feasts in Lampung, Sumatra.

Akar Pinang is an Indonesian deep-fried sweet snack made from a batter of rice flour, eggs, sugar, and coconut milk, which is pressed through a mold with a flower- or lattice-shaped nozzle directly into hot oil to form intricate crisp shapes. It is commonly served during festive occasions such as Eid al-Fitr and other family gatherings, often presented alongside other traditional kue in jars or on cookie trays.

Bubur gunting is a sweet dessert soup from Pontianak, West Kalimantan, consisting of pieces of cakwe (Chinese-style fried dough) cut with scissors into a bowl, then topped with a warm, thickened syrup made from water, sugar, and mung bean starch or cornstarch. It is typically served warm as a breakfast dish or afternoon snack, often accompanied by tea or coffee.

Randang porridge is a rice porridge cooked with the spiced coconut and beef mixture of Minangkabau rendang, combining ingredients such as shredded beef, coconut milk, lemongrass, galangal, turmeric leaf, chili, and shallots simmered until thickened. It is commonly served as a breakfast or light meal in Indonesia, often garnished with fried shallots, crackers, and sliced scallions.

Ukanshin ryori is a formal Ryukyuan court cuisine historically served to Chinese envoys (sapposhi) visiting the Ryukyu Kingdom, featuring multi-course meals that combine Chinese culinary techniques with local Okinawan ingredients such as pork, seafood, tofu, and seasonal vegetables. The dishes are prepared through methods including steaming, simmering, and deep-frying, and are traditionally presented on lacquerware in a succession of small plates at ceremonial banquets.
