Fried rice is prepared by stir-frying cooked rice in a wok or large pan with oil, vegetables, proteins such as eggs, meat, or seafood, and seasonings including soy sauce and garlic. It is typically served as a main course or side dish in Asian cuisine and is commonly found throughout East and Southeast Asia.

Spaghetti alle vongole is prepared by tossing spaghetti with a sauce made from clams, garlic, white wine, and olive oil, sometimes finished with parsley. The dish is typically served as a main course in Italian coastal cuisine, with the clams either left in their shells or removed depending on regional variation.

Jollof rice is prepared by cooking rice in a tomato-based sauce made from tomatoes, palm oil, and seasonings including garlic, onions, peppercorns, and crayfish, typically served as a one-pot dish. It is a staple West African rice dish commonly served at celebrations and communal meals, often accompanied by protein or vegetable sides.

Ilish macher paturi is a Bengali preparation where hilsa fish is marinated in a spiced paste of mustard, turmeric, and chili, then wrapped in banana leaves and steamed or grilled until cooked through. The dish is traditionally served as a main course in Bengali cuisine, with the banana leaf imparting a subtle flavor to the tender, flaky fish.

primaryIngredients: Pisum sativum subsp. sativum convar. axiphium
description: Drunken noodles is a Lao stir-fried noodle dish made with shahe fen (wide rice noodles) combined with chicken, snap peas, and holy basil, seasoned with a savory sauce of soy sauce, fish sauce, oyster sauce, chili pepper, and sugar. The dish is typically served as a main course, with the noodles and protein stir-fried together until the sauce coats the ingredients evenly.

Dry curry is a spiced dish prepared by cooking meat, vegetables, or legumes with aromatic spices, coconut milk or yogurt, and minimal liquid until the sauce reduces and coats the ingredients. The dish is typically served with rice or bread and features a concentrated flavor profile from spices such as turmeric, cumin, coriander, and chili.

Arem-arem is an Indonesian dish consisting of rice and vegetables wrapped in banana leaves and steamed until cooked through. It typically contains seasoned rice mixed with ingredients such as eggs, meat, and various vegetables, and is commonly served as a breakfast item or light snack throughout Indonesia.

Sate Madura is an Indonesian skewered meat dish from Madura island, made by marinating meat in a spice paste containing turmeric, garlic, and other aromatics before grilling over charcoal. It is typically served with a peanut sauce and accompaniments such as rice cakes or cucumber, commonly found as street food and in casual dining contexts.

Chestnut pie is a baked pastry dessert with a filling made from roasted and pureed chestnuts, often combined with sugar, butter, and sometimes eggs or cream to create a smooth paste. The filling is enclosed in a pastry crust and baked until golden, typically served as a dessert or sweet course, particularly popular in European cuisines during autumn and winter months.

Involtini siciliana consists of thin slices of meat, typically beef or fish, rolled with a filling of breadcrumbs, pine nuts, raisins, and sometimes anchovies or sardines, then secured with toothpicks or string. The rolls are browned in oil and finished in tomato sauce, traditionally served as a main course in Sicilian cuisine.

aliases: artes culinarias recetas sopa de lentejas
description: Lentil soup is prepared by simmering dried or fresh lentils with aromatic vegetables such as onions, carrots, and celery, typically seasoned with herbs and spices until the legumes are tender and partially break down to create a thick consistency. The dish is commonly served hot as a starter or light meal, often with bread for dipping, and appears across Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, and South Asian cuisines with regional variations in spices and additional ingredients.

Isterband is a Swedish sausage made from pork meat, pork fat, and barley or rye, traditionally seasoned with pepper and spices, then smoked and dried. It is typically sliced and pan-fried, served warm as a main course or appetizer, often accompanied by potatoes, lingonberry jam, and pickled cucumber.

Moros y Cristianos is a Cuban rice and bean dish made by cooking black beans and white rice together or separately and then combining them, typically seasoned with garlic, onions, and spices. This staple dish is traditionally served as a side course or main component of Cuban meals, often accompanied by meat, plantains, and other traditional sides.

Tsybryki are Belarusian fried pastries made from a dough of flour and potato that is rolled thin, filled with various ingredients such as potato or meat, and then deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crispy. These savory pastries are typically served hot as a main dish or appetizer in Belarusian cuisine.

Gingerbread man is a spiced cookie made from a dough of flour, butter, sugar, eggs, and spices including ginger, cinnamon, and cloves, shaped into a human figure and typically baked until firm. The cookies are traditionally decorated with icing or candied features and served as a festive treat during winter holidays or as a children's dessert.

Karah Parshad is a sweet halwa prepared by cooking whole-wheat flour, ghee, and sugar together until the mixture reaches a thick, porridge-like consistency. It is traditionally served in Sikh gurdwaras as a blessed communal offering (prashad) distributed to worshippers at the end of religious services.

Żurek is a traditional Polish sour rye soup made with a fermented rye flour base (żur) combined with potatoes, pearl barley, and often pork or kielbasa, creating a distinctive sour and hearty broth. The soup is typically served hot, traditionally with a hard-boiled egg and rye bread, and is considered a classic Polish comfort dish often eaten during Easter or winter months.

Faschierte Laibchen are Austrian and German meatballs made from ground meat, typically pork or beef, mixed with breadcrumbs, eggs, and seasonings, then formed into oval patties and pan-fried until golden brown. They are traditionally served warm with boiled potatoes and lingonberry jam or a creamy sauce as a main course.

Sol over Gudhjem is a Danish open-faced sandwich traditionally made with smoked herring fillets arranged in a circular pattern on rye bread, topped with a raw egg yolk in the center and garnished with chopped onions and capers. The dish is typically served as a lunch or light dinner item and originates from the fishing village of Gudhjem on the island of Bornholm.

aliases: sandwich au beurre de cacahuete et a la confiture
сэндвич с арахисовым маслом и желе
sanduiche de manteiga de amendoim e geleia
сэндвич с арахисовым маслом и джемом
description: A peanut butter and jelly sandwich is prepared by spreading peanut butter and jelly on sliced bread, typically with both spreads applied to separate slices before pressing them together. This sandwich is commonly served as a quick lunch or snack, often cut diagonally, and pairs well with beverages such as milk or juice.

Potée berrichonne is a traditional French rustic stew from the Berry region made with pork (typically including different cuts such as shoulder, belly, and bones), cabbage, potatoes, and root vegetables simmered together in broth until tender. The dish is served as a hearty one-pot meal, traditionally eaten as a main course in family settings or casual dining contexts.

Halawet el Jibn is a Syrian dessert made from cheese (typically white cheese or halloumi), semolina, and sugar, which are mixed together and rolled into cylindrical shapes. The rolls are typically fried or baked until golden, then drizzled with simple syrup and often topped with chopped pistachios, and served warm as a sweet treat.

Edo black soup is a traditional Nigerian dish prepared by simmering fish and beef with bitter leaf, palm fruit juice, and palm oil, seasoned with salt and Capsicum. The soup is typically served as a main course with starch-based accompaniments such as fufu, garri, or rice.

Prawns are steamed with garlic and served over rice vermicelli, allowing the seafood to retain its natural flavor while absorbing the aromatic garlic seasoning. This dish is typically presented as a light main course or appetizer in international seafood cuisine.

Shuizhu roupian is a Sichuan dish consisting of thinly sliced pork that is briefly poached in spiced broth infused with chili oil, Sichuan peppercorns, and aromatics like garlic and ginger. The dish is typically served in a bowl with the poaching liquid and garnished with scallions and cilantro, often accompanied by rice.

Musur dal er cutlet is a Bengali cutlet made from red lentils (musur dal) that are cooked, mashed, and mixed with spices and onions before being shaped into patties and shallow-fried until golden. It is typically served as an appetizer or snack, often accompanied by chutney or as part of a meal.

aliases: bacon lettuce avocado and tomato sandwich
description: A BLAT is a sandwich composed of bacon, lettuce, avocado, and tomato on bread, typically toasted, with mayonnaise spread on the bread. It is served as a lunch or light meal, often cut diagonally and accompanied by side dishes such as chips or pickles.

Beef biryani is a rice dish prepared by layering marinated beef with partially cooked basmati rice, aromatics such as onions and garlic, and spices including cumin, cinnamon, and cardamom, then cooking together in a sealed pot using the dum pukht method. The dish is traditionally served as a complete meal, often accompanied by raita (yogurt sauce) and pickles.

Nasi minyak is an Indonesian rice dish prepared by cooking rice in butter or ghee along with spices such as cinnamon, cloves, and cardamom, often with the addition of meat or chicken. The dish is traditionally served during special occasions and celebrations, typically accompanied by side dishes such as pickled vegetables, eggs, or meat curries.

aliases: abak soup is an indigenous soup among the ibibio people of nigeria it features atama leaves and palm fruit concentrate the latter of which makes the dish resemble banga soup
description: Abak soup is a Nigerian dish prepared by simmering fish and meat with crayfish, periwinkles, and peppered ponmo in a broth seasoned with palm fruit, onions, and salt. The soup is traditionally served with fufu, garri, or other starchy accompaniments as a main course.

Pasta salad is prepared by cooking pasta, cooling it, and combining it with a vinaigrette or creamy dressing along with vegetables, proteins, and herbs. It is typically served cold as a side dish or light main course at casual meals, picnics, and buffets.

Chana masala is a chickpea curry prepared by simmering cooked chickpeas in a spiced tomato-based sauce seasoned with masala spices, typically including cumin, coriander, and turmeric. The dish is commonly served with rice or Indian breads such as roti or naan.

Chawanmushi is a Japanese steamed egg custard made by whisking eggs with dashi broth and combining with ingredients such as chicken, shrimp, mushrooms, and vegetables before steaming in a covered cup until set. It is typically served hot as an appetizer or light course in Japanese cuisine, eaten with a small spoon directly from the cup.

Dressed herring is a Russian appetizer made with pickled or salted herring layered with diced vegetables such as potatoes, beets, onions, and sometimes apples, bound together with mayonnaise or sour cream. The dish is typically served cold as a traditional zakuska (appetizer) and often garnished with egg, herbs, or additional mayonnaise.

Zosui is a Japanese rice soup prepared by simmering cooked rice in dashi broth with vegetables, eggs, and often seafood or chicken until the mixture becomes a porridge-like consistency. It is traditionally served as a light meal or comfort food, frequently eaten for breakfast, as a way to use leftover rice, or as a recovery dish.

Túrógombóc are Hungarian dumplings made from curd cheese (túró), breadcrumbs, eggs, and sugar, formed into balls and boiled until they float to the surface. They are typically served warm with sour cream and sometimes dusted with powdered sugar or cinnamon, commonly appearing as a dessert or main course in Hungarian cuisine.

Chicken inasal is a grilled chicken dish from the Iloilo region of the Philippines, marinated in a mixture of calamansi juice, vinegar, garlic, ginger, and spices before being cooked over charcoal. It is typically served with rice and a dipping sauce made from vinegar and spices, and is commonly eaten as a casual meal or street food.

Beer can chicken is prepared by inserting an open can of beer into the cavity of a whole chicken, which is then roasted upright on a grill or in an oven until the skin is crispy and the meat is cooked through, with the beer providing moisture and steam during cooking. The dish is typically served as a main course with the chicken carved at the table, often accompanied by side dishes such as vegetables or grains.

Lau shak posto vate is a Bengali dish made by cooking bottle gourd leaves (lau shak) with poppy seed paste (posto) and serving it alongside plain steamed rice (vate). The preparation typically involves sautéing the greens with mustard oil, green chilies, and a paste of soaked white poppy seeds, resulting in a simple, mildly flavored vegetarian dish common in everyday Bengali home cooking.

Frittata di luvertin is an Italian frittata made with luvertin, the young shoots of black bryony (Tamus communis), a wild plant harvested in early spring, combined with eggs and cooked in a pan. It is a traditional dish from the Veneto and Friuli regions of northeastern Italy, typically served as a seasonal appetizer or main course when the foraged shoots are available.

Fulkopir ghonto is a Bengali dry-style vegetable dish made by cooking cauliflower florets with potatoes, peas, and a blend of spices including cumin, turmeric, ginger, and garam masala, often with the addition of coconut or green chilies depending on the regional variation. It is prepared by sautéing the vegetables with the spice mixture until tender and relatively dry, and is typically served as a side dish alongside rice or flatbreads such as roti in Bengali households.

Narkel diye kochu dantar ghonto is a Bengali dry-style dish prepared by cooking taro stem (kochu danta) with grated coconut (narkel), along with spices such as turmeric, cumin, and green chilies, typically finished with a tempering of mustard seeds or panch phoron. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Pat shak er bora are fritters made from jute leaves (pat shak), which are chopped and mixed with chickpea flour, spices such as cumin and chili, and sometimes onion before being deep-fried until crisp. They are a traditional Bengali preparation typically served as a snack or side dish, particularly popular in Bangladesh and the Indian state of West Bengal.

Mulo koraishutir ghonto is a Bengali dry-style vegetable dish made by cooking grated or chopped white radish (mulo) together with green peas (koraishuti) in a spiced mixture typically including mustard oil, cumin, turmeric, and green chilies. It is prepared by sautéing the vegetables until the moisture evaporates to achieve a semi-dry consistency, and it is commonly served as a side dish alongside rice and dal in a traditional Bengali meal.

Dre-si is a traditional Tibetan sweet rice dish prepared by cooking rice with butter, dried fruit such as raisins, and often dzi or other sweeteners, sometimes with the addition of cheese curds. It is commonly served during auspicious occasions and festivals, including Losar (Tibetan New Year), as a celebratory food.

Nasi lemuni is a Malaysian rice dish cooked with the leaves of the lemuniherb (Vitex trifolia), which imparts a distinctive bluish-green color and aromatic flavor to the grains. It is traditionally prepared by steaming or boiling rice together with the lemuni leaves and is commonly served with accompaniments such as fried fish, salted egg, and sambal, particularly during Ramadan and festive occasions in the northern states of Malaysia.

Capsicum er chop is a Bengali-origin snack in which a whole green capsicum (bell pepper) is stuffed with a spiced filling of mashed potato, sometimes combined with minced meat or vegetables, then coated in breadcrumbs and deep-fried until golden. It is typically served as a street food or appetizer, often accompanied by kasundi (mustard sauce) or chutney.

Enchor Alur Torkari is a Bengali vegetarian dish made by cooking raw jackfruit (enchor) and potatoes (alur) together in a spiced gravy, typically prepared with mustard oil, turmeric, cumin, bay leaves, and green chilies. It is commonly served as a side dish with steamed rice or flatbreads such as roti, and is a staple preparation in Bengali households across Bangladesh and West Bengal.

Amchur korola dolma is a stuffed bitter melon dish in which hollow bitter gourd (korola) pieces are filled with a spiced mixture seasoned with amchur (dried mango powder), then cooked until tender. It is served as a vegetable side dish, with the tartness of amchur balancing the natural bitterness of the gourd.

Gondhoraj cha er sorbot is a chilled Bengali beverage made by combining the juice of gondhoraj lime (Citrus x limon 'Gondhoraj'), a highly aromatic lime variety native to Bengal, with sugar, water or soda, and sometimes black salt or chaat masala. It is typically served as a cold drink, particularly during warm weather, and is valued for the gondhoraj lime's distinctive floral fragrance that sets it apart from standard lime-based beverages.

Lempuk is an Indonesian confection made by cooking durian flesh with sugar over low heat for an extended period until the mixture thickens and solidifies into a dense, chewy paste. It is a traditional sweet associated particularly with the Riau region of Sumatra and is typically shaped into blocks or wrapped portions, commonly given as a gift or souvenir.

Şambali is a semolina-based cake common across Middle Eastern and North African cuisines, made by combining coarse semolina with sugar, yogurt or milk, and fat, then baking until set and soaking the result in a simple sugar syrup. It is typically cut into squares or diamond shapes and often garnished with almonds or coconut, served at room temperature as a dessert or sweet snack.

canonicalName: alu peyajkoli diye sankar machher jhol
displayName: Alu peyajkoli diye sankar machher jhol
aliases: alu peyajkoli diye sankar machher jhol
description: Sankar mach (hybrid carp) is cooked in a light, spiced broth along with potatoes (alu), spring onions or onion greens (peyajkoli), and whole spices such as cumin, turmeric, and green chilies to produce a thin, soupy jhol. This Bengali fish stew is typically prepared by briefly frying the fish before simmering it with the vegetables in water or a light sauce, and is commonly served over steamed rice as an everyday meal in Bengali households.

Pui metuli chingri diye is a Bengali dish made with Malabar spinach (pui shak) stems and tender tips cooked with shrimp (chingri) in a light mustard oil-based preparation, often seasoned with turmeric, green chilies, and nigella seeds. It is typically served as a vegetable side dish alongside steamed rice in a traditional Bengali meal.

Beguner malaikari is a Bengali dish in which slices or pieces of eggplant (begun) are cooked in a rich sauce made with coconut milk (malai), along with spices such as mustard, turmeric, and green chili. It is typically served as a vegetarian main course alongside steamed rice, drawing on the same coconut milk-based sauce technique used in traditional prawn malaikari.

Kanchakolar khosa bata is a Bengali preparation made by grinding or mashing the peels (khosa) of raw green banana (kanchakol), typically combined with mustard oil, green chilies, and spices such as mustard paste or panch phoron. It is served as a side dish or accompaniment in traditional Bengali meals, often alongside rice.

Uchchhe lau diye tetor dal is a Bengali bitter lentil dish prepared by cooking dal (typically masoor or moong lentils) with bitter melon (uchchhe) and bottle gourd (lau), seasoned with mustard, dried red chilies, and a tempering of panch phoron. It is traditionally served as the first course of a Bengali meal, consumed with plain rice to stimulate digestion through its characteristic bitter flavor.

Lau shak chorchori is a Bengali dry-style stir-fry made from the leaves and tender stems of bottle gourd (lau shak), cooked with mustard oil, dried red chilies, and spices such as turmeric and nigella seeds. It is typically prepared as a simple everyday vegetable dish and served alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Kajoli machher chorchori is a Bengali dry-style fish preparation made with kajoli (Ganges river sprat or similar small fish), cooked with mustard oil, turmeric, green chilies, and a combination of vegetables such as potatoes and eggplant in a minimal-liquid, stir-fry technique. It is typically served as a side dish alongside steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali multi-course meal.

aliases: pan de la provincia de valladolid
description: Valladolid bread (pan de Valladolid) is a traditional Spanish bread originating from Valladolid, Castile and León, made from wheat flour, water, salt, and yeast, and characterized by its thick, crisp crust and dense, chewy crumb resulting from a slow fermentation process. It is typically baked in round or elongated loaves and served as a table bread accompanying meals, commonly found in the Castilian culinary tradition.

Méture is a traditional cornbread from the Landes and Basque regions of southwestern France, made by mixing cornmeal (maize flour) with wheat flour, eggs, and fat, then baked in a loaf or round form. It is typically served as an accompaniment to soups, stews, or cured meats, and is a staple of rustic Gascon and Basque country cuisine.

Pan cateto is a traditional Spanish rustic bread, typically made from wheat flour, water, salt, and sourdough or yeast, shaped into a round or elongated loaf with a thick, hard crust and a dense, chewy crumb. It originates from rural Andalusia and is commonly served sliced alongside meals, used as a base for dishes such as gazpacho or salmorejo, or eaten with olive oil and tomato.

canonicalName: piece of sorghum bread contained in basket s 283 from the grave goods of child mummy s 278
displayName: Piece of sorghum bread contained in basket S. 283, from the grave goods of child mummy S. 278
aliases: piece of sorghum bread contained in basket s 283 from the grave goods of child mummy s 278
pane di sorgo contenuto nel cesto s 283 fa parte del corredo funerario infantile della mummia s 278
description: This is a piece of ancient bread made from sorghum, an African cereal grain, likely prepared by grinding the grain and baking or drying the dough. It was recovered as a grave good from basket S. 283, found alongside child mummy S. 278 at an archaeological site, reflecting funerary food offerings practices of the culture that produced it.

Bogurar doi is a thick, creamy sweetened yogurt originating from Bogura (formerly Bogra) in Bangladesh, made by fermenting full-fat milk with a yogurt culture in traditional clay pots, which absorb excess moisture and contribute to its characteristic dense texture. It is typically served chilled as a dessert or accompaniment, and is widely recognized as a distinct regional dairy product with protected geographical significance in Bangladesh.

Dawama is a traditional Arab slow-cooked meat dish, typically prepared with lamb or beef simmered in a spiced broth or sauce seasoned with ingredients such as onion, tomato, and a blend of warm spices including cumin and coriander. It is commonly served over rice or with flatbread as a main course in Levantine and broader Arab cuisine.

Sultsina is a thin unleavened flatbread from Karelian cuisine, made from rye or wheat dough rolled flat and cooked on a dry griddle or pan, then spread with a filling of cooked millet or rice porridge mixed with butter. It is typically folded or rolled around the filling and served warm, often as a snack or light meal, and is closely related to other Karelian pastry traditions such as the Karelian pasty.

Bicciolani di Vercelli are traditional dry spiced cookies from Vercelli in Piedmont, Italy, made from a dough of flour, butter, sugar, eggs, and a distinctive blend of spices that typically includes black pepper, cinnamon, cloves, and nutmeg. They are baked until firm and crisp, and are commonly served as a snack or dessert, often accompanied by wine or sweet liqueurs.

Keukarah is a traditional crispy fried cracker from Aceh, Indonesia, made from a batter of rice flour and coconut milk that is shaped into thin, lacy rounds before being deep-fried until golden and brittle. It is commonly served as a snack or accompaniment during festive occasions and celebrations in Acehnese culture.

Note shaager bora are fritters made from note shak (Amaranthus spinosus, or spiny amaranth leaves) combined with gram flour (besan), chopped onion, green chili, and spices, mixed into a batter and deep-fried until crisp. They are a traditional Bengali preparation typically served as a snack or side dish, often accompanied by steamed rice or enjoyed on their own.

Jukti Phooler Bora is a Bengali fritter made by coating edible flowers — typically a mix of blossoms such as those from the shimul (silk cotton) tree — in a seasoned chickpea flour (besan) batter and deep-frying them until crisp. The dish is traditionally prepared as a seasonal snack or side dish, served with rice or as a standalone appetizer, and reflects the Bengali culinary practice of cooking with foraged or garden flowers.

Angsle is a warm Javanese street food dessert made from a combination of ingredients including white bread, rice flour cakes (putu mayang), peanuts, melinjo crackers, and kolang-kaling (palm fruit), all served in a sweet coconut milk broth. It is typically sold by street vendors at night and consumed as a warming snack or light dessert in Central and East Java, Indonesia.

Snail caviar consists of small, round eggs harvested from land snails, typically of the species Helix aspersa or Helix pomatia, which are cleaned and cured with salt before packaging. The pale white to ivory pearls are served as a luxury garnish or standalone appetizer, often accompanied by blini, toast, or neutral crackers to highlight their earthy, mushroom-like flavor.

Corn crab soup is a smooth, thickened soup made by combining sweet corn kernels or creamed corn with crab meat in a seasoned broth, typically thickened with starch and finished with beaten egg whites stirred in to create fine, silky strands. It is commonly served as a starter in Chinese-American and international Chinese restaurant cuisine, often garnished with green onions or a drizzle of sesame oil.

Westfälische Götterspeise is a German dessert composed of layers of pumpernickel bread and whipped or creamy cream, assembled in a mold or glass and typically chilled before serving. The dish takes its name from the traditional Westphalian rye bread, which contrasts in texture with the cream layers, and is served cold as a dessert in German cuisine.

Torta nera di San Secondo is a traditional savory pie from the town of San Secondo Parmense in the Emilia-Romagna region of Italy, made with a shortcrust or lard-based pastry shell filled with a mixture of eggs, spinach or chard, cheese, and cured meats such as spalla cotta. It is typically baked in the oven and served as an antipasto or main course, particularly during local festivals and celebrations.

Koraishunti diye bhetki machh is a Bengali fish dish in which bhetki (barramundi or Asian sea bass) is cooked with fresh green peas (koraishunti) in a lightly spiced gravy typically incorporating ginger, cumin, and green chilies. It is commonly served with steamed rice as part of a multi-course Bengali meal.

Tilapia sorshe jhal is a Bengali preparation in which tilapia fish is cooked in a pungent mustard-based sauce made from ground mustard seeds, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil. The dish is typically served with steamed white rice and is part of the broader tradition of 'jhal' (spicy) fish curries common in Bengali and Bangladeshi cuisine.

Mouth-watering Chicken (口水鸡, kǒushuǐ jī) is a Sichuan cold dish made from poached chicken that is chilled, sliced, and dressed with a sauce combining chili oil, Sichuan peppercorns, sesame paste, soy sauce, garlic, and vinegar. It is typically served at room temperature as an appetizer or starter in Sichuan cuisine, and is characterized by its numbing, spicy, and savory flavor profile.

Mokthuk is a Nepali soup dish that combines momo-style dumplings filled with meat or vegetables in a seasoned broth, typically made with ingredients such as ginger, garlic, and spices. It is commonly served as a warming meal, with the dumplings cooked directly in the broth rather than steamed or fried separately.

Crispy Thighs consists of chicken thighs that are seasoned and cooked—typically by frying, roasting, or baking—until the skin achieves a firm, golden exterior while the meat remains juicy. The dish is commonly served as a main course and appears across a wide range of international culinary traditions, often accompanied by sides such as vegetables, rice, or potatoes.

Turrón de vino is a Spanish confection made by combining turrón (a nougat base of honey, egg whites, and nuts, typically almonds) with wine, which is incorporated during cooking to flavor and sometimes color the mixture. It is typically served in sliced or block form as a sweet, often during festive occasions or as a dessert accompaniment.

Pagnotta di San Martino is a traditional Italian bread associated with the feast of Saint Martin (November 11), typically made from wheat flour, water, yeast, and salt, shaped into a round loaf and baked in a wood-fired or conventional oven. It is customarily prepared and distributed in central Italy, particularly in Umbria and Tuscany, as part of the Saint Martin's Day celebrations, often accompanied by newly pressed olive oil or local wine.

canonicalName: peyajkoli diye tengra machh er jhal
displayName: Peyajkoli diye tengra machh er jhal
aliases: peyajkoli diye tengra machh er jhal
alu peyajkoli diye tengra machh er jhal
description: Peyajkoli diye tengra machh er jhal is a Bengali fish preparation in which tengra (mystus catfish) is cooked in a spiced sauce with peyajkoli (onion stalks/scallion greens), mustard oil, green chilies, turmeric, and other whole spices to produce a pungent, moderately hot curry. The dish is typically served with steamed white rice as part of a multi-course Bengali meal.

A spaghetti taco consists of a crunchy taco shell filled with cooked spaghetti pasta typically tossed in tomato-based meat sauce, combining elements of Italian-American pasta dishes with the format of a Mexican taco. The dish gained widespread recognition after being featured on the Nickelodeon television series iCarly and is commonly prepared as a novelty meal, particularly popular among children.

Pukala is a traditional Filipino dish made by deep-frying or pan-frying water buffalo (carabao) meat until it becomes crispy and dry, often after being boiled or marinated beforehand. It is typically served as a main course or pulutan (appetizer/bar food) and is commonly found in the Ilocos and Pampanga regions of the Philippines.

Nasi langgi is an Indonesian dish consisting of cooked rice served with accompaniments that typically include a boiled or fried egg, sambal, and various side dishes such as shredded coconut, fried tempeh, and dry-cooked meat. It is commonly found in Central Javanese cuisine, particularly in the Yogyakarta and Solo regions, and is often served as a complete individual portion wrapped in banana leaf or presented on a plate.

Sorputi machh vapa is a Bengali steamed fish preparation in which sorputi (Puntius sarana, a freshwater carp) is coated with a paste of mustard seeds, green chilies, turmeric, and mustard oil, then wrapped and steamed until cooked through. It is traditionally served with steamed rice and is a common dish in Bengali households of Bangladesh and West Bengal.

Pui patay ilish paturi is a Bengali preparation in which hilsa fish (ilish) is marinated with mustard paste, green chili, turmeric, and oil, then wrapped in pui (Malabar spinach) leaves and steamed or cooked in a covered vessel. This method of steaming fish within leaves is a traditional Bengali technique called paturi, which allows the fish to absorb the flavors of the marinade, and the dish is typically served with steamed rice.

Rumford's Soup is a historical Bavarian dish developed by Count Rumford in the late 18th century as a low-cost nutritious meal for the poor, made primarily from pearl barley, dried peas, potatoes, and vinegar, slow-cooked into a thick porridge-like soup. It was originally served as institutional fare in Munich workhouses and is regarded as an early example of scientifically formulated mass catering.

canonicalName: rosun o longkar achare chingri machh
displayName: Rosun o longkar achare chingri machh
aliases: rosun o longkar achare chingri machh
description: Rosun o longkar achare chingri machh is a Bengali prawn dish prepared by marinating and cooking shrimp in a pickling-style sauce made with garlic (rosun), cloves (long), mustard, vinegar or tamarind, and a blend of spices including turmeric and chili. It is typically served with steamed rice as part of a multi-course Bengali meal, and the tangy, pungent flavor profile is characteristic of achar (pickle)-inspired cooking traditions from the Bengal region.

Fruit bat soup is a traditional dish prepared by boiling a whole fruit bat (from the family Pteropodidae) in water or coconut milk with aromatics such as onion and coconut, often leaving the animal largely intact including fur and wings. It is consumed in parts of Micronesia and Southeast Asia, typically served as a broth-based soup.

Rendang jariang is a West Sumatran dish made by slow-cooking stink beans (petai, Parkia speciosa) together with beef or buffalo meat in a spiced coconut milk sauce containing chilies, lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, and kaffir lime leaves until the liquid is fully reduced and the ingredients are coated in a dry, caramelized spice paste. It is a regional variation of rendang that incorporates the distinctive bitter, pungent flavor of stink beans, and is typically served with steamed rice as part of a Minangkabau meal.

Desperation pie is a category of simple American pies historically made during times of scarcity, using pantry staples such as sugar, eggs, butter, and vinegar or citrus juice as the primary filling ingredients in place of fresh fruit. Common regional varieties include vinegar pie and transparent pie, which are baked in a standard pastry shell and typically served as a dessert at home or community gatherings.

Pallubasa is a traditional beef soup from Makassar, South Sulawesi, Indonesia, prepared by simmering beef or buffalo meat and offal in a spiced broth made with toasted coconut, galangal, turmeric, coriander, and other aromatics. It is typically served in a bowl over rice or with a raw egg cracked into the hot broth, and is commonly eaten as a breakfast dish at dedicated Pallubasa eateries in Makassar.

Ban machh kosha is a Bengali dry-style curry prepared by slow-cooking wild fish with onions, garlic, ginger, mustard oil, and a blend of spices such as turmeric, cumin, and red chili until the moisture is largely reduced and the masala coats the fish pieces. It is typically served as a main course alongside steamed rice in Bengali households and traditional restaurants.

Kajoli machher tel jhal is a Bengali fish dish prepared by cooking kajoli fish (Ailia coila, a small freshwater catfish) in a mustard oil-based sauce seasoned with green chilies, nigella seeds, and turmeric. It is typically served as a main course alongside steamed rice, and the preparation emphasizes the pungency of mustard oil combined with the delicate texture of the small fish.

canonicalName: kochur loti kachki shutki diye vorta
displayName: Kochur loti kachki shutki diye vorta
aliases: kochur loti kachki shutki diye vorta
description: Kochur loti kachki shutki diye vorta is a Bengali mashed dish made from taro stem (kochur loti) combined with kachki shutki, a dried form of small freshwater Ganges river sprat (Corica soborna), typically prepared by cooking the taro stems until soft and then mashing them together with the pungent dried fish along with mustard oil, green chilies, and aromatics. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside plain steamed rice in Bengali households, particularly in Bangladesh and West Bengal.

Paciencias de Almazán are small, round, hard biscuits originating from Almazán in the Soria province of Castile and León, Spain, made from a simple dough of flour, egg, and aniseed, which are piped or dropped in small rounds and baked until firm and dry. They are traditionally served as a snack or accompaniment to coffee or tea, and are associated with the local confectionery tradition of the region.

Pringsewu fish pindang is a sour and spicy Indonesian fish soup originating from the Pringsewu region of Lampung, Sumatra, prepared by simmering freshwater fish with tamarind, galangal, lemongrass, shallots, chilies, and salt in a seasoned broth. It is commonly served as a main course alongside steamed rice, reflecting the pindang cooking tradition of using acidic and aromatic ingredients to both flavor and preserve the fish.

Posto bata diye parse machh is a Bengali preparation of parshe fish (Ambassis species, a small freshwater fish) cooked with a paste of posto (white poppy seeds), typically combined with mustard oil, green chilies, and salt to form a thick coating or sauce. The dish is commonly prepared by simmering or lightly cooking the fish in the poppy seed paste and is served as a side dish with steamed rice in traditional Bengali households.

Pabda machh er tel jhal is a Bengali preparation made with pabda fish (Ompok pabda), cooked in a pungent, oil-based sauce typically featuring mustard oil, green chilies, nigella seeds (kalonji), and turmeric. The dish is characterized by its spicy, sharp flavor profile derived from the mustard oil base and is commonly served with steamed white rice as a traditional everyday meal in Bengali households of West Bengal and Bangladesh.

Cocido is a slow-cooked Portuguese stew traditionally made with chickpeas, white beans (Phaseolus vulgaris), potatoes, carrots, various cuts of meat, cured cold cuts, and fatback (salo), all simmered together in a seasoned broth. It is typically served in multiple courses, with the broth presented first as a soup followed by the meats, sausages, and vegetables arranged on a platter.

Ofe akwụ is a traditional Igbo palm fruit soup prepared by boiling and pounding fresh palm fruits to extract a thick, rich red palm sauce, which is then cooked with assorted meats, stockfish, crayfish, and leafy vegetables such as uziza or bitter leaf. It is a staple dish in Igbo cuisine from southeastern Nigeria, typically served with starchy accompaniments such as pounded yam, fufu, or eba.

Kaoru Kingoma Tan Tan Men is a Japanese-style tantanmen (担々麺) ramen dish featuring a sesame-based broth seasoned with fragrant black sesame (黒胡麻, kingoma), typically enriched with spicy ground meat, chili oil, and noodles. It is served as a hot noodle soup, commonly garnished with green onions, sesame paste, and a soft-boiled egg, and is associated with restaurant ramen menus in Japan.

Sada polau is a plain steamed rice dish prepared by cooking long-grain aromatic rice, typically basmati or kalijeera, with ghee, salt, and sometimes whole spices such as bay leaf, cardamom, and cloves until each grain is separate and fragrant. It is commonly served as a neutral accompaniment to meat curries, lentils, or vegetable dishes in South Asian, particularly Bengali, cuisine.

Savory rice pudding (鹹粿/碗粿) is a Taiwanese dish made by steaming a mixture of rice flour batter with savory ingredients such as pork, dried shrimp, shiitake mushrooms, and soy sauce until it sets into a soft, pudding-like consistency. It is typically served in the bowl in which it was steamed, often topped with garlic sauce, soy-based condiments, and sometimes a garnish of cilantro, and is commonly eaten as a street food or breakfast item.

Okosama lunch is a Japanese children's meal (yōshoku style) typically served on a single plate and arranged to include a small portion of curry rice or ketchup-seasoned rice molded into a dome or flag-topped mound, alongside items such as a hamburger patty, spaghetti napolitan, fried shrimp, and potato salad. It is a standard menu item at Japanese family restaurants and department store dining halls, designed as a sampler-style meal for young children combining multiple Western-influenced Japanese dishes in one serving.

Taiwanese apple bread (蘋果麵包) is a soft, lightly sweetened bun made from enriched dough that is shaped and scored to resemble an apple, typically filled or flavored with apple-based ingredients such as apple jam or fresh apple pieces. It is commonly found in Taiwanese bakeries and served as a snack or breakfast item.

Harees is a slow-cooked porridge made from whole or coarsely ground wheat and meat, typically lamb or chicken, which are simmered together for several hours until the mixture breaks down into a smooth, homogeneous consistency. It is traditionally served at communal gatherings, religious occasions, and festive meals across Armenian and broader Middle Eastern and Caucasian culinary traditions.

Cocktail salad is a chilled dish typically combining diced or mixed fruits, and sometimes vegetables or seafood such as shrimp, bound with a mayonnaise- or cream-based dressing and often incorporating ingredients like celery, nuts, or marshmallows depending on the regional variation. It is commonly served as a starter, side dish, or light meal at buffets, parties, and casual dining occasions.

Kashmir chicken curry is a slow-cooked dish in which chicken pieces are simmered in a spiced sauce made with yogurt, whole spices such as cardamom, cloves, and cinnamon, dried Kashmiri red chilies, and fennel powder, producing a deep red, mildly flavored gravy. It is typically served with steamed rice or flatbreads such as naan or roti.

Hadler Hochzeitssuppe is a traditional wedding soup from the Hadeln region of Lower Saxony, Germany, typically prepared as a clear beef or chicken broth enriched with egg custard dumplings (Eierstich), small meatballs, asparagus pieces, and sometimes semolina dumplings or noodles. It is a celebratory dish historically served as the first course at wedding feasts in that region.

Dishwasher salmon is a method of cooking salmon by sealing the fish in foil or a plastic bag and placing it in a dishwasher, where the heat of the wash and dry cycles gently poaches the fish to a finished internal temperature. The technique uses the dishwasher as a low-heat cooking environment, typically producing a moist, lightly cooked fillet that is served as a main course, sometimes accompanied by sauces or seasonings added before sealing.

Yellow tail fish balls (bakso ikan ekor kuning) are an Indonesian dish made by grinding Caesionidae (yellow tail fish) meat with tapioca starch, garlic (Allium sativum), shallots (Allium ascalonicum), and salt to form a smooth paste that is shaped into balls and boiled until firm. They are typically served in broth alongside rice vermicelli or noodles and vegetables, forming a common street food or home-cooked meal across Indonesia.

Doclang is a Sundanese dish from West Java consisting of lontong (rice cakes), tofu, potatoes, bean sprouts, and hard-boiled eggs served with a thick peanut sauce seasoned with garlic, chili, and kecap manis (sweet soy sauce). It is commonly eaten as a breakfast or street food dish and is closely related to ketoprak, distinguished by its use of potatoes and the specific composition of its peanut sauce.

canonicalName: buta kara jjigae teishoku machikado ya
aliases: buta kara jjigae teishoku machikado ya
description: Buta Kara Jjigae Teishoku is a Japanese set meal (teishoku) featuring buta kara jjigae, a spicy stewed dish combining pork and vegetables in a Korean-style jjigae (stew) seasoned with gochujang or similar chili-based paste. The set meal is typically served with steamed white rice, miso soup, and small side dishes, following the standard teishoku format common in Japanese casual dining establishments such as Machikado-ya.

Es Goyang is an Indonesian frozen treat made by pouring a sweetened, flavored liquid mixture—commonly made with coconut milk, pandan, or fruit flavorings—into small molds attached to sticks, which are then shaken or rocked back and forth while being frozen using a salt-ice mixture to create a smooth texture. It is typically sold as a street food snack, often by mobile vendors, and is commonly consumed as a refreshing treat in warm weather.

Sparrow satay is an Indonesian dish in which whole sparrows or sparrow meat are skewered and grilled over charcoal, typically marinated with a blend of spices such as garlic, coriander, and sweet soy sauce before cooking. It is served with accompaniments such as peanut sauce, rice cake (lontong), or steamed rice, and is found as a street food or specialty dish in certain regions of Indonesia.

Evil jungle prince is a Thai-influenced dish consisting of protein or vegetables stir-fried or simmered in a coconut milk-based sauce seasoned with chili pepper, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, and fresh basil, often incorporating cabbage. It is typically served over rice and appears on Hawaiian restaurant menus as part of the local adaptation of Southeast Asian culinary traditions.

Menjes is a traditional Javanese dish made from ground or mashed tempeh mixed with tofu, spices, and sometimes offal or soy-based ingredients, then fried or sautéed until cooked. It is commonly served as a side dish or snack in East Java, often accompanied with rice or eaten alongside other street food items.

Sago bread is a traditional bread made primarily from sago starch, extracted from the pith of sago palm trees, which is dried and processed into a flour used as the main binding ingredient. It is commonly prepared by mixing sago starch with water and sometimes salt, then baking or cooking the mixture into flat or loaf-style portions, and is typically served as a staple carbohydrate accompaniment to savory dishes in parts of Indonesia and the broader Pacific region.

Idada is a steamed Gujarati snack made from a fermented batter of rice and urad dal, prepared similarly to idli but typically spread into a flat, spongy cake rather than molded into rounds. It is commonly served with green chutney and sometimes topped with a tempering of mustard seeds, sesame seeds, and curry leaves.

Catànies are a traditional confection from Vilafranca del Penedès in Catalonia, Spain, consisting of whole Marcona almonds coated in a layer of praline and then enrobed in dark chocolate. They are typically served as a sweet treat or gift confection, often packaged in small boxes and consumed as a dessert or accompaniment to coffee.

Nonta dalia is a savory Bengali porridge made from broken wheat (dalia) cooked with water or broth, seasoned with salt, mustard oil, and spices such as cumin and green chili, sometimes enriched with vegetables or small amounts of meat or fish. It is typically served as a light meal or breakfast dish, offering a savory alternative to the sweet versions of dalia common in South Asian cuisine.

Chhanchi kumror bichir bori is a Bengali preparation made by grinding pumpkin seeds (kumro bichi) into a paste, which is then shaped into small dried lentil-like dumplings called bori and sun-dried until firm. These boris are typically deep-fried or added to vegetable dishes and curries, serving as a protein-rich accompaniment in traditional Bengali household cooking.

Dhenros chorchori is a Bengali vegetable dish made by stir-frying or slow-cooking okra (dhenros) with minimal spices such as mustard seeds, turmeric, green chilies, and sometimes panch phoron, often finished with a small amount of mustard oil. It is typically served as a side dish with steamed rice and dal as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Nabadwip's Red Curd (Lal Doi) is a sweetened yogurt from Nabadwip in West Bengal, prepared by boiling milk with sugar until it caramelizes to a reddish-brown color, then setting it with a yogurt culture in earthenware pots where slow fermentation develops its characteristic color and flavor. It is typically served chilled as a dessert, often at the end of a meal or during festive and religious occasions in Bengali households.

Torta de Guimarães is a Portuguese pastry from the city of Guimarães, made with a thin puff pastry dough filled with a mixture of squash jam (doce de abóbora), almonds, and cinnamon, then baked until golden. It is typically served at room temperature as a dessert or accompaniment to coffee, and is often sold individually in local pastry shops.

Sharots is an Armenian confection made by threading walnuts onto a string and repeatedly dipping them into a thickened syrup of grape or fruit molasses (often mixed with flour), allowing each coat to dry before the next layer is added. It is typically dried into sausage-shaped strands, sliced into rounds, and served as a sweet snack or dessert, particularly during autumn and winter holidays.

Gazpacho pastor is a traditional shepherd's dish from the mountainous regions of southeastern Spain, particularly Albacete, Cuenca, and Murcia, prepared by simmering game meats such as rabbit, hare, or partridge with garlic, tomato, and aromatic herbs, then combining the stew with broken pieces of unleavened flatbread known as torta de gazpacho, which absorb the cooking juices. It is typically served hot in a communal pan or earthenware dish as a hearty main course, often during hunting season or at rural gatherings.

Manuk labakh is a traditional Karo Batak dish from North Sumatra consisting of chicken cooked with grated coconut, coconut milk, and a blend of spices including lemongrass, turmeric, ginger, candlenut, and chilies, often combined with the bird's blood for richness. It is typically served with rice at ceremonial gatherings and family celebrations in Karo communities.

Amich is an Armenian festive dish prepared by stuffing poultry, typically chicken or turkey, with a filling of rice and dried fruits before roasting or baking until the meat is cooked through and the stuffing has absorbed the juices. It is traditionally served during New Year and Christmas celebrations, often presented whole at the center of the holiday table and sliced for guests.

Otona-sama Lunch is an adult version of the Japanese okosama lunch, typically presented on a compartmentalized plate featuring items such as fried shrimp, hamburg steak, spaghetti, curry or pilaf rice, and a small salad, often topped with a decorative flag. It is served in family restaurants and cafes as a nostalgic meal aimed at adult customers who wish to enjoy the assorted children's plate format.

Nimpatar shukto is a Bengali vegetable dish prepared by cooking a mix of vegetables such as bitter gourd, raw banana, eggplant, drumstick, and radish with neem leaves, tempered with spices like panch phoron, ginger, and mustard, and often finished with milk or a paste of mustard and poppy seeds. It is traditionally served at the start of a Bengali meal alongside steamed rice, valued for its bitter flavor profile that opens the palate.

Slemang is a traditional Malaysian dish that typically incorporates sugar and flour as base ingredients, suggesting a sweet preparation that is often steamed, baked, or pan-cooked in the style of local kuih or confections. It is commonly served as a snack or dessert alongside tea or coffee, and may be enjoyed during casual meals or festive occasions.

Kirdo is a traditional Kurdish dish, typically prepared using regional staples such as grains, legumes, or slow-cooked meats seasoned with herbs and warm spices common to the cuisine. It is often served as a hearty main course, commonly accompanied by flatbread, yogurt, or rice alongside fresh vegetables.

Puçuko is typically a small, bite-sized preparation drawn from Turkish culinary traditions, likely featuring a dough or pastry base filled or topped with ingredients such as cheese, minced meat, or vegetables seasoned with regional spices. It is commonly served as a meze or snack, often accompanied by yogurt-based sauces, fresh herbs, or tea in a casual dining context.

Qopo is a Kurdish dish typically prepared with seasoned minced meat and garlic, often shaped into small patties or dumplings and cooked in a seasoned broth or sauce. It is commonly served as a hearty main course, sometimes accompanied by rice, flatbread, or yogurt as part of a traditional meal.

Miyabiya is typically presented as an international fusion dish, likely drawing on Japanese-inspired elements given its name, and is commonly prepared with a balance of fresh seasonal ingredients, delicate seasonings, and refined plating techniques. It is often served as a main course in a small-to-medium portion, accompanied by rice, vegetables, or light broth, making it suitable for diners seeking a globally influenced dining experience.

Tel parse is a traditional Bengali preparation, typically consisting of parboiled or soaked rice served in a pool of fragrant mustard oil, often accompanied by green chilies, sliced onions, and salt. It is commonly eaten as a simple breakfast or light meal in rural Bengal, sometimes alongside fried vegetables, boiled eggs, or small fried fish.

Rangila parse is a traditional Bengali fish preparation, typically made with parse (mullet) fish cooked in a spiced gravy featuring mustard oil, turmeric, green chilies, and aromatic spices characteristic of Bengali cuisine. It is commonly served hot alongside steamed rice as part of a main course meal.

Pastificio typically refers to a dish centered on freshly made pasta, often produced in-house using simple ingredients such as flour, eggs, water, and salt. It is commonly served with a variety of sauces ranging from tomato-based preparations to butter, cream, or meat ragù, and is usually presented as a main course accompanied by grated cheese.

Acewwaḍ is a traditional Algerian dish, likely of Berber (Kabyle) origin, typically prepared as a rustic porridge or stew based on semolina, barley, or wheat flour cooked slowly with water, olive oil, and local seasonings. It is commonly served warm in a communal bowl, often accompanied by buttermilk, fresh herbs, or vegetables, and traditionally enjoyed during family gatherings or seasonal celebrations.

Hause of Cakes refers to a pastry establishment-style offering that typically features an assortment of baked goods such as layered sponge cakes, cupcakes, meat pies, and sweet pastries commonly prepared with flour, sugar, eggs, butter, and flavorings like vanilla or chocolate. These items are often served fresh as snacks, celebration desserts, or accompaniments to tea and coffee, reflecting the international bakery traditions popular in Nigerian urban centers like Sabon Tasha, Kaduna.

Ciaurrina is a traditional Sicilian pastry, typically prepared with a sweet dough that is often filled or enriched with ingredients common to the island's baking tradition, such as ricotta, candied fruit, almonds, or honey. It is commonly served as a dessert or accompaniment to coffee, particularly during festive occasions and local celebrations.

Singûsîr is a traditional Kurdish dish that is typically prepared using regional ingredients such as bulgur or rice, legumes, and seasonal vegetables, often flavored with herbs and spices common to Kurdish cooking like sumac, mint, or dried peppers. It is commonly served as a home-style meal, usually accompanied by flatbread, yogurt, or pickled vegetables in a communal setting.

Ortigara cake is a traditional dessert from the Asiago plateau in northern Italy, typically prepared as a layered sponge or shortcrust cake filled with cream, often flavored with local ingredients such as honey, hazelnuts, or berries that reflect the alpine region's produce. It is commonly served in slices as a dessert or with coffee, and its name and shape often evoke Mount Ortigara, a notable peak in the area.

This refers to a style of bite-sized croissants popularized by Taiwanese bakery brands, typically made from laminated butter dough that is baked until golden and crisp on the outside while remaining light and flaky inside. They are commonly offered in a variety of flavors such as original butter, chocolate, matcha, or seaweed, and are often served as a snack, breakfast item, or takeaway treat packaged in small boxes or bags.

Panspermia is typically a grain-based dish inspired by the ancient concept of mixed seeds, commonly prepared by simmering a variety of whole grains, legumes, and pulses such as wheat berries, barley, lentils, and chickpeas until tender. It is often served warm or at room temperature, seasoned with olive oil, herbs, or spices, and presented as a wholesome shared plate or side dish in a communal dining context.

Pumpum soup is a Turkish-style soup typically prepared by simmering seasonal vegetables or legumes in a seasoned broth, often finished with butter, lemon, or a touch of red pepper flakes in keeping with common Turkish soup traditions. It is commonly served hot as a starter alongside fresh bread, accompanying a larger meal or enjoyed on its own as a light course.

Otchahoi is a Japanese noodle dish typically prepared by simmering or stir-frying noodles with a savory broth or sauce, often incorporating ingredients such as vegetables, soy-based seasonings, and occasionally meat or seafood. It is commonly served hot in a bowl as a main course, reflecting the comforting and umami-rich character of traditional Japanese noodle preparations.

Mami btakan is typically a noodle-based soup dish, commonly featuring wheat noodles served in a savory broth with sliced meat such as chicken, beef, or pork, and often garnished with green onions, boiled egg, and fried garlic. It is usually served hot as a main course or a hearty snack, accompanied by condiments like soy sauce, calamansi, or chili on the side.

Magokoro tsutsumi is typically a wrapped or parcel-style dish, with the name suggesting a preparation made with care, often featuring seasoned fillings enclosed in a thin wrapper such as dough, rice paper, or a delicate egg or crepe layer. It is commonly served as an appetizer or small plate, sometimes accompanied by a dipping sauce or light broth depending on regional influences within its international fusion context.

Sroki is a traditional Sorbian dish typically prepared as a rustic preparation rooted in the rural Lusatian cuisine of the Sorbs, commonly featuring hearty ingredients such as potatoes, grains, or regional vegetables combined with simple seasonings. It is often served as a warming main course or side, accompanied by bread, quark, or local meats in the style of Sorbian home cooking.

Taro with yellow sauce is typically prepared by boiling or steaming taro root until tender, then coating or accompanying it with a yellow sauce that commonly includes ingredients such as turmeric, mustard, curry spices, or egg yolk depending on regional interpretation. It is often served warm as a side dish or light main course, sometimes alongside rice, bread, or grilled proteins.

Sambal salabance is an Indonesian dish that typically features a spicy sambal-based preparation, commonly made with a blend of chilies, shallots, garlic, and aromatic spices characteristic of Indonesian cuisine. It is often served as a flavorful accompaniment alongside steamed rice and other traditional sides, providing a bold and piquant element to the meal.

Masnovines is typically a Spanish-style dish that likely features seasonal ingredients prepared in a traditional Mediterranean manner, often incorporating olive oil, garlic, and regional herbs. It is commonly served as a shared plate or light main course, accompanied by crusty bread and suited to a casual dining setting.

Bánh nhúng is a Vietnamese-style crispy fritter, typically made by dipping a patterned mold into a thin batter of rice flour, wheat flour, coconut milk, and sesame seeds, then deep-frying it until golden and lacy. It is commonly served as a sweet snack or street-food treat, often enjoyed on its own or alongside tea.

Tepung torak is a traditional Malaysian kueh, typically prepared from a dough or batter based on flour (tepung), often rice or sago flour, which is shaped into small pieces and cooked by steaming or boiling. It is commonly served as a snack or dessert, often accompanied by grated coconut and a sweet sauce made from palm sugar (gula melaka).

Kuih pepena is a traditional Malaysian kueh, a category of bite-sized sweet or savory snacks that are typically steamed, boiled, or baked using ingredients such as glutinous rice, rice flour, coconut milk, and palm sugar. It is commonly served as a tea-time treat or festive snack, often enjoyed alongside coffee or tea in markets, homes, and celebratory gatherings.

Salad Pan is typically a mixed salad assembled and served in a shallow pan or skillet, commonly featuring a base of fresh leafy greens along with vegetables such as tomatoes, cucumbers, onions, and bell peppers, often accompanied by proteins like grilled chicken, tuna, or cheese. It is usually dressed with a light vinaigrette or creamy dressing and served as a shareable starter or a light main course in casual international dining settings.

Mokary is typically prepared as a savory dish featuring a blend of seasoned ingredients, often combining proteins, vegetables, and aromatic spices in a method common to international fusion cooking. It is commonly served as a main course, frequently accompanied by rice, bread, or a light side salad depending on regional adaptation.

Tubby Custard is typically a thick, pourable custard-style dessert, commonly prepared from milk, egg yolks, sugar, and cornstarch or custard powder, gently heated until smooth and creamy. It is often served warm in a bowl or cup as a sweet treat, sometimes accompanied by biscuits or fruit on the side.

Gata is a traditional Armenian sweet pastry typically made from layered or folded dough filled with a crumbly mixture of flour, butter, and sugar known as khoriz, then baked until golden. This version, billed as the largest of its kind, is commonly prepared in an oversized format for festive or communal occasions and is usually sliced into portions and served with coffee or tea.

Glico Wings Frost Bite is a commercially produced ice cream novelty, typically made with a frozen dairy or milk-based core and often coated in a crisp chocolate shell that delivers a contrasting crunch. It is commonly served pre-packaged and eaten directly from its wrapper as a chilled dessert or snack, making it a convenient option in warm weather or after a meal.

Godrogodro is typically a rice cake, commonly prepared by cooking rice until soft and then shaping or steaming it into a dense, sliceable form, sometimes sweetened with sugar or coconut depending on regional variations. It is often served as a snack or dessert, and may be accompanied by tea, coffee, or fresh fruit.

Kinper is typically prepared as a savory dish drawing on international culinary techniques, often combining seasoned proteins or vegetables with a blend of aromatic spices and a complementary sauce or broth. It is commonly served warm as a main course, frequently accompanied by rice, flatbread, or a fresh side salad depending on regional interpretation.

To-beram is a traditional Chechen dish typically prepared with simple, hearty ingredients common to the region's mountain cuisine, often featuring a base of meat, dairy, or cornmeal combined with aromatic herbs and spices. It is commonly served warm as part of a communal meal, often accompanied by flatbread and a garlic-based sauce that is characteristic of Chechen culinary traditions.

Da jiu-jia is a Chinese dish whose name suggests it is commonly enjoyed as an accompaniment to alcoholic beverages, typically prepared with savory, flavorful ingredients suited to pairing with wine or spirits. It is often served as part of a shared table setting, alongside other small plates in the traditional Chinese dining style.

Nkôno ngond is a traditional Cameroonian dish typically prepared with egusi (ground pumpkin or melon seeds), which are cooked into a thick, savory sauce often flavored with smoked fish, meat, palm oil, and local spices. It is commonly served warm alongside starchy accompaniments such as plantains, cassava, or boiled tubers, making it a hearty centerpiece of a shared family-style meal.

Kelan Antep is a traditional Indonesian dish, likely originating from regional home-style cooking, typically prepared as a spiced stew or curry-like preparation featuring locally available ingredients such as meat or vegetables simmered in a seasoned broth. It is commonly served with steamed rice and accompanied by sambal or other traditional condiments, often enjoyed as part of a shared family meal.

Tao kuish is a Chinese dish that is typically prepared using traditional cooking methods such as stir-frying, steaming, or braising, and commonly includes ingredients like vegetables, meat or seafood, and aromatic seasonings such as soy sauce, ginger, and garlic. It is often served hot alongside steamed rice or noodles as part of a shared meal.

Hormigo is a traditional Basque dish typically prepared with a flour-based batter or dough, sometimes incorporating beet for color and subtle sweetness. It is commonly served as a rustic accompaniment or simple main course, reflecting the hearty, home-style character of northern Spanish country cooking.

Mebakbeka is typically prepared as a savory dish with an international flair, likely combining sautéed or simmered ingredients such as vegetables, spices, and a protein or grain base. It is commonly served as a main course, often accompanied by bread, rice, or a light side salad depending on regional interpretation.

Brayo is a traditional dish from Central Java, Indonesia, typically prepared using locally sourced ingredients and seasoned with a blend of regional spices common to Javanese cooking, such as shallots, garlic, and coconut. It is often served as part of a communal meal alongside steamed rice and other accompaniments, reflecting the rustic culinary heritage of the region.

Bawin sauce is a traditional dish from western Côte d'Ivoire, typically prepared with grated or pounded cassava root cooked into a thick, stew-like sauce, often seasoned with local spices, palm oil, and sometimes smoked fish or meat. It is commonly served as a communal main course alongside staples such as rice, attiéké, or boiled tubers.

Vieta is a traditional Chechen preparation made from flax seeds, which are typically roasted and then ground into a coarse meal or paste, sometimes combined with butter, honey, or sugar to form a dense, nourishing mixture. It is commonly served as a rustic snack or accompaniment to tea, valued historically as a hearty, energy-rich food in mountain households.

Chocolate lobster is a Spanish dish that typically features lobster served with a rich, dark chocolate-infused sauce, reflecting the traditional Catalan practice of pairing seafood with small amounts of chocolate in savory preparations. It is commonly prepared by sautéing the lobster and simmering it in a sauce often built from onions, tomatoes, brandy, almonds, and grated dark chocolate, and is usually served as a main course with bread to soak up the sauce.

Edo traditional food typically refers to hearty preparations centered on spicy soups and thick stews, often simmered slowly with a combination of meats, leafy greens, and ground seeds or nuts to achieve a rich, textured consistency. These dishes are commonly served with starchy accompaniments such as pounded yam, fufu, or rice, and are usually enjoyed as a communal main course during family meals or gatherings.

Paensae is a North Korean holiday dish typically prepared for festive occasions and family gatherings, often featuring seasonal ingredients characteristic of Korean celebratory cooking such as rice, vegetables, and marinated meats or seafood. It is commonly served alongside an assortment of banchan (side dishes) and is associated with traditional Korean holidays, reflecting regional culinary customs of the northern peninsula.

Bloke kavla is a traditional dish from the Bloke plateau region of southwestern Slovenia, typically prepared as a hearty buckwheat or barley-based porridge-like preparation that reflects the rustic mountain cuisine of the area. It is commonly served as a warming main course or side, often accompanied by cured meats, sauerkraut, or dairy products typical of Slovenian alpine fare.

Cirdingis is a traditional Chechen dish typically prepared as a baked or pan-cooked flatbread-style preparation, commonly made from simple ingredients such as cornmeal or flour combined with dairy products like butter, cheese, or cream. It is often served warm as part of a shared family meal, usually accompanied by broth, meat dishes, or tea in the customary Chechen style.

Bhoi is a traditional Indonesian sweet cake, typically prepared by baking or steaming a batter made from rice flour, eggs, sugar, and coconut milk until it develops a soft, spongy texture. It is commonly served as a snack or dessert alongside tea or coffee, and often appears during festive gatherings and ceremonial occasions.

Kacimuih is a traditional Indonesian dish, commonly associated with Minangkabau cuisine from West Sumatra, typically prepared as a salad-like mixture of steamed or boiled vegetables such as cassava leaves, long beans, or bean sprouts combined with grated coconut seasoned with chili, garlic, and other spices. It is often served at room temperature as a side dish alongside rice and other main courses in a traditional Indonesian meal.

Tmmyan is a Taiwanese dish that is typically prepared as a savory preparation featuring regional staples such as rice, noodles, or braised meats, often seasoned with soy sauce, rice wine, sesame oil, and aromatics like garlic, ginger, and scallions. It is commonly served hot as part of a shared meal or as a street-food-style portion, frequently accompanied by pickled vegetables or a light broth on the side.

Koyabu is a regional Indonesian dish that typically features a combination of locally sourced ingredients, often prepared with aromatic spices, coconut, or chili-based seasonings characteristic of the archipelago's culinary traditions. It is commonly served with steamed rice and may be accompanied by sambal, fresh vegetables, or crackers as part of a shared meal.

Bubur ayam is an Indonesian-style savory rice porridge, typically prepared by slowly simmering rice in broth until it reaches a smooth, creamy consistency, then topped with shredded poached chicken, fried shallots, scallions, soybeans, and crispy crackers (kerupuk). It is commonly served for breakfast or as a light meal, often accompanied by condiments such as sweet soy sauce (kecap manis), sambal, and a drizzle of chicken broth or yellow kuah.

Topi waja is a traditional Indonesian dish typically prepared as a slow-cooked stew of beef or offal simmered in a rich, spiced broth that commonly includes shallots, garlic, ginger, and aromatic spices such as nutmeg, clove, and cinnamon. It is often served warm with steamed rice, emping crackers, or sliced tomato and celery as a hearty main course.

Gandasturi is a traditional Banjarese dish from South Kalimantan, Indonesia, typically prepared using locally sourced ingredients and seasonings characteristic of the region's cuisine, which often features rice, fish, or spiced meats cooked with aromatic herbs. It is commonly served as part of a larger meal alongside steamed rice and complementary side dishes, reflecting the everyday culinary traditions of the Banjar people.

Kayu jabuk is a traditional Banjarese dish from South Kalimantan, Indonesia, typically prepared as a savory rice porridge or stew-like preparation that reflects the region's cooking traditions. It commonly includes local spices and aromatics, and is often served as a comforting meal during family gatherings or communal occasions.

Tumpiangin is a traditional Banjarese dish from South Kalimantan, Indonesia, typically prepared using locally available ingredients and reflecting the region's characteristic use of aromatic spices and coconut-based seasonings. It is commonly served as part of a home-style meal alongside steamed rice, and may appear in several regional variations depending on the cook and occasion.

Avellanate is a traditional Spanish preparation whose name derives from 'avellana' (hazelnut), and it is typically made with ground or crushed hazelnuts combined with other pantry staples such as bread, garlic, or spices to form a rich sauce or sweet. It is commonly served as an accompaniment to meats, poultry, or fish in savory versions, or presented as a dessert-like confection depending on the regional recipe.

Adjidjat is a traditional Algerian dish typically prepared as a savory egg-based preparation, often cooked with a base of sautéed onions, tomatoes, and warming spices such as cumin, paprika, and black pepper. It is commonly served hot alongside crusty bread or flatbread, making it a popular choice for breakfast or a light main course.

Nanbanbeccho appears to be a fusion-style dish drawing on the Japanese 'nanban' tradition, which typically refers to preparations influenced by early Portuguese and Spanish trade, often featuring ingredients marinated or fried and dressed with a tangy vinegar-based sauce along with onions and chili. It is commonly served as a main course or shared plate, paired with steamed rice or pickled vegetables to balance the sweet, sour, and savory flavors.

Herazushi is a style of sushi typically made by pressing vinegared rice and assorted toppings such as seafood, vegetables, or egg into a flat, spatula-shaped form, with the name referring to the 'hera' or wooden paddle used in its preparation. It is commonly served sliced into portions as part of a shared meal or bento, often accompanied by soy sauce, pickled ginger, and wasabi.

Enchorer kima is a Bengali dish typically prepared with minced or finely chopped raw jackfruit (enchor), slow-cooked with onions, ginger, garlic, green chilies, and a blend of spices such as cumin, turmeric, and garam masala to resemble a keema-style preparation. It is commonly served as a vegetarian main course alongside steamed rice, luchi, or roti, often featured in traditional Bengali home-style meals.

Katoya data chorchori is a traditional Bengali vegetable dish typically prepared by slow-cooking jute plant stems (katoya data) with a blend of mustard oil, panch phoron (five-spice mix), green chilies, and sometimes additional vegetables like pumpkin or potato. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice and dal, often enjoyed as part of a home-style Bengali meal.

Koi pathri is a Bengali dish typically prepared with koi fish (climbing perch), which is commonly marinated with turmeric and salt before being cooked in a spiced gravy featuring mustard oil, green chilies, and aromatic spices. It is often served with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal.

Topser horogouri is a traditional Bengali preparation that typically features topshe (mango fish), a small, delicate river fish prized in Bengal, cooked in a spiced gravy often made with mustard oil, turmeric, green chilies, and a blend of aromatic spices. It is commonly served with steamed rice as part of a traditional Bengali meal, usually accompanying lentils and vegetable dishes.

Panžakaj is a traditional Erzyan dish, typically prepared as a baked or pan-cooked preparation rooted in the rural culinary customs of the Erzya people of the Volga region, commonly incorporating simple local ingredients such as flour, eggs, milk, and sometimes potatoes or curd. It is often served warm as a hearty everyday meal or during festive family gatherings, usually accompanied by butter, sour cream, or other dairy-based condiments.

Sari pengantin is a traditional Banjarese dish from South Kalimantan, Indonesia, typically prepared as a festive specialty often associated with wedding celebrations, as suggested by its name meaning 'essence of the bride.' It commonly features a combination of rice or glutinous rice with rich, spiced accompaniments, and is usually served during ceremonial gatherings alongside other regional delicacies.

Nobabi motorsunti is a Bengali-style dish typically prepared with motorsunti (green peas or dried peas), often simmered in a spiced gravy featuring onions, ginger, garlic, and a blend of traditional South Asian spices such as cumin, turmeric, and garam masala. It is commonly served warm alongside rice, luchi, or paratha as part of a larger meal, and the 'nobabi' designation suggests a richer, Nawabi-inspired preparation with added ghee, cream, or nuts for a more indulgent flavor.
